A topological pseudo-monoid is a topological space with a partially defined multiplication obtained by embedding it in an ambient topological abelian group and restricting the group law to products that remain inside the embedded subspace.

IUT I §0monoids

Divisible and cyclotomic cases

Divisibility is defined by the existence and compatibility of roots in the ambient group. Cyclotomic pseudo-monoids carry a torsion subgroup isomorphic to Q/Z and stable under multiplication with the pseudo-monoid.

IUT I §0cyclotomic

Category-isomorphism convention

Mochizuki uses "isomorphism of categories" to mean an isomorphism class of equivalences of categories. This convention matters whenever later pages speak about full poly-isomorphisms between categories or Hodge-theater components.

IUT I §0categories

Poly-morphism and full poly-isomorphism

A poly-morphism is a collection of morphisms between two objects; a poly-isomorphism is a collection of isomorphisms. The full poly-isomorphism is the collection of all isomorphisms. Composites ignore multiplicity and retain the set of all pairwise composites.

IUT I §0poly

Capsule of objects

A capsule is a finite indexed family of objects in a category. Morphisms between capsules use injections of index sets and component morphisms; capsule-full poly-isomorphisms use bijections of the index sets.

IUT I §0capsules

Finite etale covering categories

For a connected noetherian algebraic stack of the stated type, B(X) denotes finite etale coverings over X; B(X)_0 denotes the connected-object subcategory. These categories feed the anabelian side of the later Hodge-theater definitions.

IUT I §0coverings

Connected temperoid

Btemp(Pi) is the category of countable discrete sets with continuous action by a tempered topological group Pi. For Galois-countable cases, the group Pi can be reconstructed from the category up to inner automorphism.

IUT I §0temperoids

Temperoid morphisms

Morphisms of connected temperoids are represented contravariantly by functors preserving finite limits and countable colimits. For suitable tempered groups, this matches continuous outer homomorphisms between the groups.

IUT I §0outer homs

Abstract equivalence

Two functors are abstractly equivalent when they differ by isomorphisms of their source and target categories. The wiki treats this as a warning that many later comparisons are invariant up to the specified categorical equivalence, not up to literal equality.

IUT I §0equivalence

Valuations and curve notation

Section 0 fixes abbreviations such as NF, MLF, CAF, valuations V(F), completions F_v, residue characteristics, and the curve/orbicurve terminology used by the initial theta data.

IUT I §0notation
Formalization use. These conventions justify why the Lean and wiki layers should represent collections of possible isomorphisms, capsule indices, and reconstructed groups explicitly instead of replacing them by single chosen maps or definitional equalities.
Sources: IUT I Related: IUT I atlas prime strips and bridges theta/log links anabelian background species and mutations

Pseudo-Monoids in IUT I §0

IUT I begins by allowing monoid-like objects that are only partially closed under multiplication. A pseudo-monoid is represented inside an ambient abelian group, and multiplication is defined only when the product remains inside the chosen subset. This is the source convention behind later rational-function pseudo-monoids and coric function packages.

Partial multiplication inside an ambient group IUT I §0, monoids and categories
TermSource meaningDownstream role
Pseudo-monoid A subset embedded in an ambient topological abelian group, with multiplication defined by the ambient law only when the product remains in the subset. Models later partially closed function and monoid packages.
Divisible Root extraction is compatible with membership: an element belongs exactly when its relevant powers belong, in the ambient group. Prepares the divisibility conditions used in coric rational functions.
Cyclotomic The ambient group may be chosen with torsion subgroup isomorphic to Q/Z, stable under multiplication with the pseudo-monoid. Connects pseudo-monoids to cyclotomic rigidity and Kummer data.
Formalization consequence. A pseudo-monoid should not be encoded as an ordinary monoid unless the source has supplied global closure. The ambient group and the membership condition are part of the structure.
Sources: IUT I §0 Related: notation primitives kappa-coric functions Frobenioids/log-shells

Poly-Isomorphisms in IUT I §0

A poly-morphism is a set of morphisms between two objects. A poly-isomorphism is a set of isomorphisms, and the full poly-isomorphism is the set of all isomorphisms. Composing poly-morphisms means taking all pairwise composites and ignoring multiplicity. This convention is essential in theta links, bridge morphisms, and Hodge-theater comparisons.

Sets of possible maps, not chosen maps IUT I §0
ConventionDefinitionGuardrail
Poly-morphism A collection of morphisms A -> B. Do not replace by one selected morphism unless the source chooses one.
Poly-isomorphism A poly-morphism all of whose members are isomorphisms. The data is a set of possible isomorphisms.
Full poly-isomorphism The collection of all isomorphisms between the two objects. This is common in theta-link and bridge statements.
Composition All composites g_j o f_i, with multiplicity ignored. Set-level composition changes proof obligations in Lean.
Failure mode. Many incorrect simplifications of IUT-style interfaces start by replacing a full poly-isomorphism with a canonical-looking single isomorphism. The source convention explicitly avoids that move.
Sources: IUT I §0 Related: theta links D-ThetaNF bridges symmetries/processions

Capsules and Temperoids in IUT I §0

Section 0 defines two categorical devices that recur throughout IUT I: capsules, which are finite indexed families of objects, and connected temperoids, which encode countable discrete sets with continuous action by a tempered topological group. These conventions control later prime-strip capsules and anabelian reconstruction interfaces.

Finite indexed families and reconstructible action categories IUT I §0
DeviceSource definitionDownstream role
Capsule A finite indexed family {A_j}_{j in J} of objects of a category. Used for labeled families of D-prime-strips and Hodge-theater components.
Capsule morphism An injection of index sets plus component morphisms. Index movement is explicit; labels are not erased.
Capsule-full poly-isomorphism A capsule-full poly-morphism whose index injection is a bijection. Controls later processions and full bridge comparison data.
Btemp(Pi) Countable discrete sets with continuous Pi-action. Provides the category-theoretic language for tempered fundamental groups.
Connected temperoid A category equivalent to Btemp(Pi) for a tempered topological group. For Galois-countable cases, Pi is reconstructible up to inner automorphism.
Formalization consequence. Capsule index sets and temperoid reconstruction hypotheses are part of the mathematical interface. Later pages should link here whenever they use labeled capsules or recovered topological groups.
Sources: IUT I §0 Related: D-prime strips and labels symmetries/processions Galois-countability repairs

IUT I Section 1: Orbicurve Cover Package

IUT I begins by fixing a cyclic covering package attached to a once-punctured elliptic curve and a distinguished cusp. The point is not only to have curves, but to have coverings, quotient orbicurves, cusp labels, and fundamental groups that can later be reconstructed group-theoretically.

Section 1 Source Package cyclic covers, quotient orbicurves, cusps, and reconstruction
Source itemConstruction or statementWiki/formalization consequence
Setup before Definition 1.1 Starting from a hyperbolic orbicurve built from a once-punctured elliptic curve, the paper forms finite etale cyclic coverings and quotient orbicurves. The displayed covering diagram has Galois groups Z/ell Z, Z/2Z, and Z/2ell Z in the indicated layers. The base curve data carries a rigid cyclic-cover hierarchy before theta data is packaged.
Definition 1.1 Defines hyperbolic orbicurves of type (1,ell-tors arrow) and (1,ell-tors arrow)^{±} by arising, up to isomorphism, from the constructed X-arrow or C-arrow package. These are source-defined curve types, not informal labels for arbitrary elliptic-curve covers.
Remark 1.1.1 The arrow notation records ordered archimedean labels 1,2,... determined by the distinguished epsilon on the relevant plus-minus orbits. Label order enters already at the orbicurve-cover layer; later label discipline is not introduced only at Gaussian or log-theta stages.
Remark 1.1.2 The constructed X-arrow and C-arrow are determined, up to k-isomorphism, by (X/k, C, epsilon). The cover package is canonical relative to the chosen source data.
Corollary 1.2 For k an NF or MLF, there is a functorial group-theoretic algorithm to reconstruct the indicated fundamental groups, together with cusp decomposition-group conjugacy classes, from the top-level group. The reconstructed groups are viewed as subgroups of the relevant automorphism group. The later anabelian interface has an explicit reconstruction theorem behind it: groups and cusp classes are recovered from group structure.
Remark 1.2.1 The automorphism groups of the constructed covers identify with the corresponding cyclic Galois groups. The cover automorphism data is exactly the cyclic symmetry data used in the package.
Remark 1.2.2 Corrects background cusp-reconstruction algorithms by characterizing cusp inertia through cyclic pro-ell coverings that are totally ramified at a cusp. Cusp recovery requires the repaired total-ramification test, not a vague assertion that cusps are visible.
Remark 1.2.3 Repairs related combinatorial anabelian graph-cover arguments, including characterizations of cuspidal and nodal edge-like subgroups and verticial subgroups by ramification properties. The graph-of-anabelioids input to tempered covering theory relies on corrected recognizers for cusps, nodes, and vertices.

Cyclic cover hierarchy

Section 1 organizes the elliptic curve, its quotient orbicurve, and cyclic covers into a hierarchy whose Galois groups include Z/ell Z, Z/2Z, and Z/2ell Z.

IUT I §1cyclic covers

Ordered cusp labels

The arrow in the notation records ordered labels determined by the chosen cusp data. This is an early source of the label discipline that later appears in processions and theta-value labels.

Remark 1.1.1labels

Group-theoretic reconstruction

Corollary 1.2 reconstructs the lower fundamental groups and cusp decomposition classes from the top group, functorially with respect to topological-group isomorphisms.

Corollary 1.2anabelian

Cusp inertia test

Remark 1.2.2 repairs the cusp algorithm by using cyclic coverings whose degree-ell quotient is totally ramified at a cusp. The total-ramification condition is then group-theoretic.

Remark 1.2.2repair

Edge and vertex recognizers

Remark 1.2.3 repairs the combinatorial graph-cover background by replacing several informal descent and ramification claims with explicit group-theoretic characterizations of cuspidal, nodal, and verticial data.

Remark 1.2.3CombGC

Formalization duty

A faithful formalization should eventually expose cyclic cover maps, Galois group identifications, cusp decomposition classes, and repaired total-ramification recognizers. A bare record saying that the cover exists would miss the Section 1 content used downstream.

Lean targetsource interface
The role of this page is foundational: it records the first anabelian reconstruction mechanism in the series. Later Hodge theaters should be read as carrying this repaired curve/cover/cusp recognition layer, not as untyped copies of elliptic-curve data.
Sources: IUT I, §1 Related: anabelian background IUT I atlas tempered covering complements initial theta data Hodge theaters

IUT I Atlas: From Initial Data to Hodge Theaters

IUT I builds the starting universe of the theory. Its central move is not yet the Corollary 3.12 comparison, but the construction of typed theaters, prime strips, label classes, and Frobenioid-theoretic links that make later alien comparisons possible.

Punctured elliptic curves and orbicurves

The curve X_F is a once-punctured elliptic curve over a number field. The quotient by the involution gives the orbicurve C_F. These curves provide the hyperbolic objects whose fundamental groups and coverings are reconstructed anabelianly.

IUT I §1initial data

Tempered coverings

IUT I records complements on tempered coverings and decomposition subgroups. This is part of the absolute anabelian input: local and global curve data must be recoverable from group-theoretic information.

IUT I §2anabelian

Initial theta data components

Definition 3.1 fixes an algebraic closure Fbar/F, a once-punctured elliptic curve, a prime l >= 5, a cover C_K, valuation sets, bad reduction places, and a distinguished cusp. It also imposes torsion, Galois, and mod-l image conditions.

Definition 3.1input package

Bad and good valuation sets

The distinction between V_bad and V_good controls where multiplicative reduction, theta evaluation, local logarithms, and log-volume estimates behave in different ways.

valuationslocal data

Frobenioids at bad primes

At bad nonarchimedean places, Frobenioids encode base-field and divisor-like arithmetic data. Later log-volume estimates read this material through monoids, units, realifications, and Kummer-compatible actions.

IUT I §3Frobenioids

Global realified Frobenioids

Realification passes Frobenioid data into a form where degrees and log-volumes become real-valued. This is one reason the theory must track exactly which real-valued quantity is being compared.

realificationdegree

Theta-Hodge theater

Definition 3.6 defines a theta-Hodge theater as valuation-indexed local categories, Frobenioid/Kummer data, and global modular Frobenioid data attached to the fixed initial theta data. It is a miniature arithmetic world built category-theoretically.

Definition 3.6theater

Theta-link

Corollary 3.7 gives full poly-isomorphisms between theta-Hodge theaters. The point is that the link is Frobenioid-theoretic and does not live inside ordinary ring/scheme morphisms.

Corollary 3.7horizontal motion

Frobenius-picture chains

Corollary 3.8 arranges theta-Hodge theaters in integer-indexed chains. These chains are the first visible form of the later lattice-like world of mutually alien but structurally linked copies.

IUT I §3chains

D-prime-strips

A holomorphic base-prime-strip is a valuation-indexed collection of local objects. Its mono-analyticization produces a D⊢-prime-strip, a recurring interface for stripping away holomorphic history.

Definition 4.1prime strips

Label classes of cusps

IUT I attaches F_l-torsor structures to cusp label classes. These labels are later essential when Gaussian and j^2-weighted theta data are averaged or descended.

Proposition 4.2labels

Base-NF and base-theta bridges

Model base bridges describe how prime-strips and label classes are moved while preserving only the structures allowed at that stage. They are early prototypes of the later bridge discipline needed for Corollary 3.12.

IUT I §4bridges
Sources: IUT I Related: initial theta data Hodge theaters theta links

Tempered Covering Complements

IUT I Section 2 supplies group-theoretic control over tempered covering subgroups before the Hodge-theater construction starts. The point is to recognize decomposition and inertia data inside tempered and profinite fundamental groups, so that later anabelian reconstruction has a rigid source interface.

Tempered subgroup control from sub-semi-graphs to arithmetic exact sequences
Source itemStatement shapeWiki consequence
Setup before Proposition 2.1 The tempered fundamental group Pi_G^tp embeds naturally in the pro-hat Sigma completion hat Pi_G. A connected sub-semi-graph H subset G gives decomposition subgroups Pi_H^tp and hat Pi_H. Decomposition subgroups are not informal labels; they are subgroups inside the ambient tempered/profinite group system.
Proposition 2.1 If a profinite conjugate of a nontrivial compact subgroup of Pi_G^tp lands back in Pi_G^tp, then the conjugating element already lies in Pi_G^tp. This prevents hidden profinite motion from producing a new tempered realization indistinguishable at the compact-subgroup level.
Proposition 2.2 The decomposition subgroups associated to sub-semi-graphs are commensurably terminal in the corresponding ambient groups. Commensurators do not enlarge the subgroup beyond the subgroup itself; this is the rigidity used by later reconstruction arguments.
Corollary 2.3 For a stable hyperbolic curve over an MLF, sub-semi-graphs determine subgroups Delta_X,H and Pi_X,H, their profinite closures, and exact sequences over G_k under the stated slimness hypotheses. The curve-level arithmetic subgroup is tied to the graph-level component data, so later local prime-strip inputs can be read group-theoretically.
Corollary 2.3(vi) A cusp inertia group lies in a conjugate of the subgroup attached to H exactly when the corresponding cusp of the stable model meets a component contained in H. Inertia data detects geometric incidence with selected components; it is not merely external decoration on the group.
Proposition 2.4 The profinite-conjugacy rigidity of Proposition 2.1 has arithmetic analogues for the geometric and arithmetic tempered fundamental groups of X/k. The same rigidity principle survives the passage from graph coverings to arithmetic curve fundamental groups.
Corollary 2.5 In the full profinite case, a profinite decomposition or inertia group is contained in the tempered fundamental group exactly when it is the corresponding tempered decomposition or inertia group. A profinite conjugate of the tempered group containing such a subgroup must equal the tempered group. This is the theorem-level form of Theorem B from the introduction. Later applications use especially the cusp-inertia portion.
Theorem 2.6 A discrete analogue is proved for groups virtually free or orientable surface groups, with a stronger conclusion when the relevant intersection is nonabelian. The section records both the tempered arithmetic result and the classical group-theoretic shadow behind it.

Profinite conjugacy rigidity

Proposition 2.1 is the central local rigidity lemma of the section. It says that a profinite conjugator that sends a nontrivial compact subgroup back into the tempered group is already a tempered element.

Proposition 2.1compact subgroup

Commensurably terminal subgroups

Proposition 2.2 turns the conjugacy rigidity into commensurator control for decomposition subgroups attached to connected sub-semi-graphs. This keeps the subgroup boundary sharp under profinite completion.

Proposition 2.2decomposition

Sub-semi-graph subgroups

Section 2 treats a connected sub-semi-graph as determining compatible tempered and pro-completed subgroups. These are the group-theoretic form of selecting components in the stable reduction graph.

IUT I §2 setupsubgraph H

Arithmetic compact conjugates

Proposition 2.4 repeats the rigidity phenomenon for arithmetic compact subgroups inside the tempered fundamental group of a hyperbolic curve over a mixed-characteristic local field.

Proposition 2.4MLF curve

Tempered decomposition recognition

Corollary 2.5 identifies which profinite decomposition and inertia groups actually come from the tempered fundamental group. It also states that a profinite conjugate of the tempered group containing one such subgroup is already the original tempered group.

Corollary 2.5Theorem B

Inertia cusp test

Corollary 2.3(vi) gives the useful incidence test: membership of a cusp inertia group in the selected subgroup is equivalent to the corresponding stable-model cusp meeting a component contained in the selected subgraph.

Corollary 2.3(vi)cusps

Discrete analogue

Theorem 2.6 isolates a parallel statement for groups with finite-index free or orientable surface subgroups. Its role in the wiki is diagnostic: the tempered statement has a recognizable classical group-theoretic analogue, but the IUT application uses the tempered arithmetic version.

Theorem 2.6free/surface groups
Formalization note: this page should eventually become a library interface for subgroup recognition and terminality lemmas. It should not be replaced by an opaque field saying that decomposition subgroups exist, because Section 2 proves how they behave inside the ambient completed groups.
Sources: IUT I, §2 Related: IUT I atlas IUT I coverings/symmetries anabelian background prime strips and bridges

IUT I Propositions 2.1-2.2: Tempered Terminality

Propositions 2.1 and 2.2 are the first precise rigidity layer of IUT I Section 2. They say that a profinite motion that returns nontrivial compact tempered subgroup data to the tempered world is already a tempered motion, and hence that the relevant decomposition subgroups have no larger commensurator inside the completed group.

From compact subgroup test to commensurable terminality IUT I, Propositions 2.1-2.2
Source itemPrecise roleDo not weaken to
Setup before Proposition 2.1 The tempered fundamental group Pi_G^tp is regarded as a subgroup of its pro-Sigma completion Pi_G. For a connected sub-semi-graph H, the groups Pi_H^tp and Pi_H are decomposition subgroups, well-defined up to conjugacy. A mere label saying “the subgroup associated to H exists.” The ambient inclusions and completions are part of the statement.
Proposition 2.1 If Lambda is a nontrivial compact subgroup of Pi_G^tp, gamma in Pi_G, and gamma Lambda gamma^-1 subset Pi_G^tp, then gamma in Pi_G^tp. A general claim that all profinite conjugators are tempered. The compact subgroup hypothesis is the detector.
Proof of Proposition 2.1 The proof passes through universal tempered and pro-Sigma semi-graphs. Compact subgroups lie in verticial subgroups; the nontrivial intersection after conjugation forces the corresponding vertices to be equal or adjacent; temperedness of the conjugated vertex forces the conjugator to be tempered. A black-box conjugacy principle detached from graph vertices and adjacency.
Proposition 2.2 Pi_H and Pi_H^tp are commensurably terminal in the corresponding ambient groups, and Pi_G^tp is commensurably terminal in Pi_G. Normalizer control only. The source statement is commensurator control, hence stronger in the intended subgroup boundary sense.
Remarks 2.2.1-2.2.2 Mochizuki records generality beyond the displayed PSC-type case and notes that, for all primes, the proof gives an alternative route to normal terminality of finitely generated free groups in their profinite completions. Treating Section 2 as a disposable side lemma. It is a reusable rigidity mechanism behind later arithmetic recognition.

Tempered vertex detector

The source proof detects whether a pro-Sigma vertex is tempered by comparing it with images of vertices from the universal tempered covering. This is the geometric content behind the compact-subgroup test.

Prop. 2.1 proofvertices

Nontrivial compact subgroup test

The subgroup Lambda must be nontrivial and compact. This lets the proof place both Lambda and its conjugate inside verticial subgroups before invoking the equal-or-adjacent vertex criterion.

Prop. 2.1compactness

Commensurator chain collapse

Proposition 2.2 uses Proposition 2.1 over open subgroups of verticial subgroups, then combines the resulting terminality with the pro- Sigma analogue of the subgroup equality C(Pi_H)=Pi_H.

Prop. 2.2commensurator
Sources: IUT I, §2, Propositions 2.1-2.2 Related: tempered covering complements subgraph arithmetic groups 2.3 arithmetic recognition 2.4-2.5

Compact Conjugator Test in IUT I Proposition 2.1

Proposition 2.1 is the local detector behind Section 2. If a nontrivial compact subgroup of the tempered fundamental group is moved by a profinite element and lands back inside the tempered group, then the moving element was already tempered. The proof reads compact subgroup data through verticial subgroups in universal tempered and pro-Sigma covering graphs.

Landing back in the tempered group forces a tempered conjugator IUT I Proposition 2.1
HypothesisMeaningUsed for
Nontrivial compact subgroup Lambda subset Pi_G^tp is compact and not the trivial subgroup. Places Lambda inside a verticial subgroup of the tempered covering.
Profinite conjugator gamma in Pi_G and gamma Lambda gamma^-1 subset Pi_G^tp. Creates two verticial subgroups whose images intersect nontrivially.
Vertex criterion The corresponding pro-Sigma vertices are equal or adjacent. Shows the conjugated vertex is tempered, hence gamma is tempered.
The theorem is not a generic conjugacy-closedness statement. Its detector is the nontrivial compact subgroup and the proof depends on verticial subgroup containment in the universal covering graphs.
Sources: IUT I Proposition 2.1 Related: tempered terminality commensurable terminality arithmetic tempered recognition

Commensurable Terminality in IUT I Proposition 2.2

Proposition 2.2 turns the compact conjugator test into commensurator control. For a connected sub-semi-graph H, the associated decomposition subgroups Pi_H and Pi_H^tp are commensurably terminal in their ambient completed and tempered groups.

Commensurator chains collapse to the subgroup itself IUT I Proposition 2.2

Stronger than normalizer control

The proposition controls commensurators, not only normalizers. This matters because the subgroup boundary is being tested up to finite-index overlap.

Subgroup source discipline

Pi_H^tp and Pi_H are not arbitrary subgroups; they come from a connected sub-semi-graph and are treated as decomposition subgroups inside Pi_G.

Sources: IUT I Proposition 2.2 Related: compact conjugator test subgraph arithmetic groups tempered covering complements

IUT I Corollary 2.3: Sub-Semi-Graph Arithmetic Subgroups

Corollary 2.3 transports the Section 2 terminality mechanism from an abstract semi-graph of anabelioids to the tempered and profinite fundamental groups of a hyperbolic curve over a mixed-characteristic local field. It is the page where a connected sub-semi-graph H becomes the arithmetic subgroup data used by later local reconstruction.

Sub-semi-graph H as an arithmetic subgroup interface IUT I, Corollary 2.3
AssertionContentLater use
Definitions in the statement Delta_X,H^tp and Delta_X,H are fiber products over Pi_G^tp and Pi_G. They encode the subgroup of the curve fundamental group selected by H. Later “selected component” statements should point to these fiber-product subgroups, not to an informal subgraph label.
Corollary 2.3(i)-(ii) The closed subgroups are commensurably terminal, the G_k outer action restricts to them, and the closure of the tempered subgroup inside the pro-Sigma group is the pro-Sigma subgroup. This is the exact point where graph terminality becomes arithmetic subgroup terminality.
Corollary 2.3(iii)-(iv) Under either the auxiliary-prime hypothesis or the all-primes hypothesis, Delta_X,H is slim. This yields exact sequences over G_k and commensurable terminality of the corresponding arithmetic images. The hypotheses are part of the theorem. A formal interface must carry which slimness route is being used.
Proof of slimness Mochizuki gives two routes: an elementary route using a prime l outside the relevant sets, and a route using Tama's theorem in the all-primes case. Both establish an infinite nonabelian intersection condition for vertex and selected-subgraph data. This is not a cosmetic proof detail; it is what makes center-free exact sequences available.
Corollary 2.3(v)-(vi) The tempered/profinite intersection behaves as expected, and cusp inertia lies in a conjugate of the selected subgroup exactly when the corresponding stable-model cusp meets a component contained in H. The cusp-incidence test is the IUT-relevant recognizer: it turns geometric component incidence into group-theoretic membership.

Fiber-product subgroups

The source defines Delta_X,H^tp and Delta_X,H by pulling back the graph subgroup data along Delta_X^tp -> Pi_G^tp and Delta_X -> Pi_G. This is the clean model for a future Lean record.

Cor. 2.3fiber product

Slimness routes

The two proof routes for slimness should remain distinguishable: auxiliary-prime arguments and the all-primes argument using Tama's theorem have different dependencies.

Cor. 2.3(iii)slim

Cusp incidence

Assertion (vi) is the operational recognizer. A cusp inertia subgroup is in the selected subgroup precisely when the stable-model cusp meets an irreducible component contained in H.

Cor. 2.3(vi)inertia
Sources: IUT I, §2, Corollary 2.3 Related: tempered terminality 2.1-2.2 arithmetic recognition 2.4-2.5 cyclic cover reconstruction

IUT I Proposition 2.4 and Corollary 2.5: Arithmetic Tempered Recognition

Proposition 2.4 is the arithmetic analogue of the compact-subgroup rigidity lemma. Corollary 2.5 then identifies exactly when profinite decomposition and inertia groups associated to points or cusps are already tempered. Mochizuki explicitly notes in Remark 2.5.2 that later IUT applications only need the cusp-inertia part, which uses Proposition 2.4(i).

Arithmetic analogue and cusp-inertia recognition IUT I, Proposition 2.4 and Corollary 2.5
Source itemStatementFormalization checkpoint
Proposition 2.4(i) A profinite conjugator in Pi_X that sends a nontrivial pro-Sigma compact subgroup of Delta_X^tp back into Delta_X^tp lies in Pi_X^tp. This is the route used for cusp inertia in later IUT applications.
Proposition 2.4(ii) When Sigma is the set of all primes, the same conclusion holds for compact subgroups of Pi_X^tp whose image in G_k is open. This part is used for decomposition groups in Corollary 2.5, but Mochizuki says later IUT use does not require it.
Proposition 2.4(iii) Delta_X^tp and Pi_X^tp are commensurably terminal in their profinite counterparts. This is the arithmetic terminality output of the proposition.
Corollary 2.5 For all primes, a profinite decomposition or inertia group is contained in Pi_X^tp exactly when it is the corresponding tempered decomposition or inertia group. A profinite conjugate of Pi_X^tp containing such a group must equal Pi_X^tp. The result is a recognition theorem, not merely an existence statement for decomposition or inertia groups.
Remark 2.5.2 Later IUT papers only apply the cusp-inertia portion of Corollary 2.5, whose proof uses Proposition 2.4(i), essentially as a formal consequence of Proposition 2.1. This narrows the formalization priority: cusp inertia should be modeled before the full closed-point decomposition statement.

Arithmetic analogue

Proposition 2.4 repeats the compact-subgroup conjugacy test inside the arithmetic curve setting, using characteristic open subgroups and stable model special fibers to reduce back to Proposition 2.1.

Prop. 2.4arithmetic

Cusp-inertia priority

Remark 2.5.2 is a useful implementation guide: the IUT series later needs the cusp-inertia part, and that part avoids the more elaborate open-image argument of Proposition 2.4(ii).

Remark 2.5.2IUT use

Weak section-conjecture reading

Remark 2.5.1 describes Corollary 2.5 as a very weak section-conjecture type statement: certain sections visible profinitely are geometric in the tempered setting.

Remark 2.5.1anabelian
Sources: IUT I, §2, Proposition 2.4 and Corollary 2.5 Related: tempered terminality subgraph arithmetic groups Galois-countability repairs

Cusp Inertia Recognition in IUT I Corollary 2.5

Corollary 2.5 recognizes when a profinite inertia group associated to a cusp is already a tempered inertia group. Remark 2.5.2 then narrows the later IUT dependency: the series only needs this cusp-inertia portion, whose proof uses Proposition 2.4(i), hence ultimately the compact conjugator test of Proposition 2.1.

The later IUT dependency is the cusp-inertia branch IUT I Corollary 2.5 and Remark 2.5.2
StatementContentPriority
Corollary 2.5, inertia branch A profinite inertia group associated to a cusp is contained in Pi_X^tp exactly when it is the corresponding tempered inertia group. Recognizes cusp inertia internally in the tempered/profinite interface.
Conjugate criterion A profinite conjugate of Pi_X^tp containing such a cusp inertia group must be equal to Pi_X^tp. Prevents a different profinite copy from carrying the same cusp inertia signal.
Remark 2.5.2 Later IUT use requires the cusp-inertia portion, whose proof only uses Proposition 2.4(i). Formalize this branch before the full closed-point decomposition branch.
Sources: IUT I Corollary 2.5 Related: arithmetic tempered recognition compact conjugator test cusp/edge/vertex recognizers

IUT I Theorem 2.6-Lemma 2.7-Corollary 2.8: Discrete and Topological Analogues

The final part of IUT I Section 2 records a classical group-theoretic analogue of the tempered recognition statements. It is not a replacement for the arithmetic theorem, but it explains the shape of the conjugator conclusion in terms of virtually free groups, orientable surface groups, and topological fundamental groups of complex hyperbolic curves.

Classical shadow of the tempered conjugator theorem IUT I, Theorem 2.6 through Corollary 2.8
ItemContentWhy it is included
Theorem 2.6 For F containing a finite-index free or orientable-surface subgroup G, an element of the profinite completion that conjugates an infinite subgroup H back into F lies in F . N(H_G). If H_G is nonabelian, it lies in F. This is the classical analogue of the tempered conjugator theorem.
Abelian case If H_G is abelian, Lemma 2.7 makes it cyclic and a normalizer term remains. Remark 2.6.1 stresses that the stronger tempered-style equality need not hold here. Prevents importing a stronger conclusion into the discrete analogue.
Nonabelian case Lemma 2.7 supplies finite-index subgroups and powers whose images generate a rank-two free abelian subgroup in abelianization; comparing the two cyclic normalizers forces the conjugator into the discrete group. Explains why nonabelianity removes the normalizer ambiguity.
Lemma 2.7 The lemma records subgroup freeness, residual-finite separation into abelianizations, cyclicity of abelian subgroups, normal terminality of suitable procyclic closed subgroups, and triviality of certain profinite centralizers. These are the discrete ingredients replacing the graph-of-anabelioids machinery used in Propositions 2.1 and 2.4.
Corollary 2.8 For a complex hyperbolic curve, the same conclusion applies to the usual topological fundamental group inside its profinite completion; Mochizuki notes that the IUT series only needs the non-proper cusp-inertia case. Gives the topological-curve form of the Section 2 analogy.

Normalizer ambiguity

Theorem 2.6 gives gamma in F . N(H_G) in general. Only under nonabelianity of H_G does the normalizer ambiguity disappear.

Theorem 2.6normalizer

Abelianization toolkit

Lemma 2.7 packages the finite-index and abelianization facts needed to distinguish cyclic from nonabelian behavior in free and surface groups.

Lemma 2.7free/surface groups

Topological curve form

Corollary 2.8 translates the discrete theorem to complex hyperbolic curves. The wiki keeps it separate from the arithmetic theorem because its conclusion and hypotheses are not identical to the tempered setting.

Corollary 2.8complex curves
Sources: IUT I, §2, Theorem 2.6-Lemma 2.7-Corollary 2.8 Related: arithmetic tempered recognition Galois-theoretic context anabelian background

Galois-Countability Repairs

IUT I Remark 2.5.3 records a technical correction to the background theory of tempered fundamental groups in SemiAnbd. The issue is not a new IUT construction: universal tempered covering pro-objects require countability hypotheses so compatible basepoints exist. Mochizuki then checks that the examples used in IUT remain unaffected.

Why countability enters tempered groups IUT I Remark 2.5.3

Galois-countable tempered group

Remark 2.5.3 defines a tempered group as Galois-countable when its topology admits a countable basis. Connected temperoids, quasi-temperoids, and families of these objects inherit corresponding Galois-countability conditions.

Remark 2.5.3(T1)tempered

Galois-countable semi-graph

A semi-graph of anabelioids is Galois-countable when it is countable and has a countable cofinal family of finite etale coverings that split after base change over each component. Strictly coherent countable examples are Galois-countable.

T2-T4semi-graphs

Basepoint obstruction

Mochizuki explains the need for countability through universal covering pro-objects: countable index sets make compatible systems of basepoints available, while without them an obstruction analogous to a nonabelian inverse-limit obstruction may appear.

Remark 2.5.3basepoints

No effect on IUT examples

The examples of SemiAnbd used in IUT are declared unaffected: topological subquotients of absolute Galois groups of countable fields are Galois-countable, and the semi-graphs in the cited examples are strictly coherent.

E1-E5no effect

SemiAnbd §3 repair

Remark 2.5.3 states that selected definitions, propositions, theorems, and corollaries in SemiAnbd §3 should assume the tempered groups, temperoids, and semi-graphs involved are Galois-countable. The rest of the cited uses are then covered by the examples above.

E7repair

Other SemiAnbd corrections

The same remark lists further textual corrections: a false automorphism assertion involving Dehn-twist counterexamples, strengthened component-wise conditions for actions on semi-graphs, and small phrase changes in several SemiAnbd statements.

Remark 2.5.3(iii)-(vi)errata

Formalization rule

A formal interface for tempered fundamental groups should carry the countability hypotheses needed to define the reconstructed group and its basepoint choices. The hypotheses may often be discharged automatically for IUT's source examples, but they should not be silently omitted.

Lean guardrailhypotheses
Sources: IUT I Related: tempered covering complements connected temperoid basepoints/inter-universality anabelian background

IUT II Atlas: Theta Evaluation and Gaussian Structures

IUT II supplies the theta-value and Kummer-theoretic machinery that feeds the horizontal direction of the log-theta lattice. It is also where radial/coric language and multiradiality become explicit formal principles.

SectionMain contentWiki route
§1 Multiradial mono-theta environments, interior/exterior cyclotomes, cyclotomic rigidity, constant-multiple rigidity. mono-theta environments, mono-theta rigidity, multiradiality
§2 Galois-theoretic theta evaluation at torsion labels and conjugate synchronization. Galois theta evaluation, distinguished subgraph, theta-value labels
§3 Tempered Gaussian Frobenioids: theta monoids, Gaussian monoids, Kummer/Galois comparison, post-anabelian forgetting. Tempered Gaussian monoids, Gaussian Frobenioid categories, Gaussian Frobenioids, Frobenioids/log-shells
§4 Global Gaussian Frobenioids and the modified theta-link variants Theta×μ and Theta×μ_gau. Good-prime Gaussian monoids, Global Gaussian Frobenioids, Theta±ell Gaussian lift, ThetaNF Fl symmetry, ThetaNF Frobenioid localization, F×μ Kummer prime-strips, Theta×μ Frobenius-pictures, D-Theta etale-pictures, theta/log links, log-theta lattice
IUT II §1 source item Construction Later role
Definition 1.1(i) A mod-N mono-theta environment MTheta algorithmically yields Pi_Y, Pi_X, G, the geometric kernels Delta, Delta_Y, Delta_X, the subquotient l DeltaTheta, and the exterior subgroup Pi_mu. These are the group-theoretic pieces from which the theta-environment rigidity statements are formed.
Definition 1.1(ii) l DeltaTheta is the interior cyclotome and Pi_mu is the exterior cyclotome. The source constructs a cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism between their mod- N reductions. This is the first IUT II rigidity comparison between theta-section data and roots-of-unity data.
Remark 1.1.1 The cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism is reconstructed by comparing the theta-section subgroup with an algebraic subgroup produced from a commutator map; the relevant construction is stable under the F_l^± symmetry. The rigidity is not an arbitrary isomorphism: it is tied to the internal mono-theta construction and its symmetry properties.
Propositions 1.2-1.3 Mono-theta environments may be reconstructed either from the tempered fundamental group or from the tempered Frobenioid. The resulting cyclotomic rigidity agrees with the corresponding MLF-Galois pair rigidity. This links the group-theoretic and Frobenioid-theoretic routes used later in theta evaluation and Gaussian Frobenioids.
Example 1.7 A radial environment is a functor Phi : R -> C from radial data to coric data. It is multiradial when Phi is full; otherwise it is uniradial. Multiradiality is a precise fullness condition, not a slogan about many copies. It controls whether gluing in coric data can be lifted to radial data.
Corollary 1.10 The mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism l DeltaTheta(Pi) -> Pi_mu(MTheta*(Pi)) determines a multiradially defined functor relative to the G O×mu(G) coric environment. This is the clean multiradial rigidity input: roots of unity are quotiented away in the coric unit-quotient object.
Corollary 1.12 Pointed inversion automorphisms and theta subsets give direct-product splittings after quotienting by torsion. The resulting assignment is multiradially defined. This is the constant-multiple rigidity input that prevents uncontrolled scalar ambiguity in later theta-value comparison.
IUT II §2 source item Theta-evaluation construction Later role
Proposition 2.1 The tempered-covering diagram Pi_Ydd -> Pi_Y -> Pi_X, together with the corresponding lower-level objects, is reconstructed functorially from the local tempered group at a bad place. This supplies the connected covering on which theta values are evaluated.
Corollary 2.4 Cusp-label classes, two-torsion translation by mu-, and decomposition groups D_{t,mu-} are reconstructed with the relevant F_l^± symmetry. Evaluation points are not chosen analytically; they are recovered from group-theoretic cusp and torsion-point data.
Corollary 2.5 Restricting etale theta functions to the subgraph and then to D_{t,mu-} gives mu_{2l}- and mu-orbits of theta values indexed by |t| in |F_l|. This is the group-theoretic theta-evaluation algorithm used before passage to mono-theta and Frobenioid forms.
Remark 2.5.1 Relative to the standard cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism, the theta-value orbit for a label j is the orbit of the value with exponent j^2. This is the source of the square-weight phenomenon that reappears in the Corollary 3.12 discussion.
Remark 2.6.1 All theta values for |t| in |F_l| are computed relative to one basepoint, one local Galois group, and one cyclotome. Mochizuki calls this conjugate synchronization. Simultaneous Kummer theory for all theta values requires this single shared basepoint/cyclotome.
Corollary 2.8 Replacing l DeltaTheta by Pi_mu via mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity gives mono-theta-theoretic theta values and zero-label splittings compatible with Corollary 1.12. This is the route that connects group-theoretic evaluation to Frobenioid-theoretic theta functions.
Corollary 2.9, Remark 2.9.1 Replacing l DeltaTheta by base-field cyclotomes mu_Z(G_v) gives a useful evaluation route, but as stated it is only uniradial; IUT III log-shells later modify it into a multiradial form with mild indeterminacy. This marks an explicit source boundary: base-field numerical access is useful, but it is not automatically multiradial.

Mono-theta environment

A mono-theta environment packages tempered fundamental groups, theta-section data, and cyclotomic subquotients. IUT II studies what can be recovered functorially from this environment.

Definition 1.1MΘ

Mono-theta reconstruction

IUT II Proposition 1.2 records two construction routes for MTheta: from a topological group isomorphic to the tempered fundamental group, and from the corresponding tempered Frobenioid category.

Proposition 1.2group/Frobenioid

Interior and exterior cyclotomes

IUT II distinguishes an interior cyclotome from an exterior cyclotome and constructs rigidity isomorphisms between their reductions. This controls how roots of unity and theta sections are compared.

cyclotomesrigidity

Theta-section commutator

Remark 1.1.1 explains the cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism by comparing the theta-section subgroup with a subgroup produced from a commutator map. This is the source-level reason the isomorphism is tied to the mono-theta environment, not simply chosen.

Remark 1.1.1commutator

Radial and coric data

Radial data is source-side data for a functorial algorithm; coric data is the target data shared after applying the algorithm. Multiradiality means this passage does not lose the relevant rigidity.

Example 1.7radial/coric

Fullness condition

In Example 1.7, multiradiality is the fullness of Phi : R -> C. This lets isomorphisms of coric data lift to isomorphisms of radial data, which is why simultaneous execution across spokes becomes meaningful.

Example 1.7(ii),(v)parallel transport

Tautological multiradialization

When ordinary radial data is not multiradial, IUT II describes a tautological multiradialization by remembering a full poly-isomorphism to the coric target. This clarifies the difference between real multiradiality and multiradiality obtained by adding bookkeeping.

poly-isomorphismrigidity

Cyclotomic rigidity

Corollary 1.10 proves multiradial mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity isomorphisms. These are central because the later theta-value evaluation depends on comparing cyclotomic structures across alien viewpoints.

Corollary 1.10multiradial

Constant multiple rigidity

Corollary 1.12 gives multiradial constant multiple rigidity. In the series, this rigidity controls the scalar ambiguity that would otherwise undermine theta-value comparison.

Corollary 1.12scalar control

Pointed inversion automorphism

Corollary 1.12 uses pointed inversion automorphisms to isolate invariant theta subsets modulo torsion. These invariants produce the splittings that express constant-multiple rigidity in a multiradial form.

Corollary 1.12(i)-(ii)splitting

Galois-theoretic theta evaluation

Section 2 reconstructs theta-evaluation data from Galois-theoretic and mono-theta information, including theta values, theta sections, and the compatibility of torsion labels.

IUT II §2theta values

Evaluation decomposition groups

Corollary 2.4 reconstructs decomposition groups for cusp translates by the two-torsion point mu-. These groups are the local targets to which theta functions are restricted for evaluation.

Corollary 2.4torsion translate

Theta-value orbits

Corollary 2.5 produces mu_{2l}- and mu-orbits of theta values theta_|t| indexed by |F_l|. Remark 2.5.1 identifies the source of the j^2 exponent.

Corollary 2.5j²

Conjugate synchronization

Remark 2.6.1 requires all label-indexed theta values to be computed with one Galois group and one cyclotome. This is what makes simultaneous Kummer extraction across all theta values meaningful.

Remark 2.6.1single basepoint

Mono-theta theta evaluation

Corollary 2.8 transports theta evaluation through mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity, replacing l DeltaTheta by Pi_mu and preserving the zero-label splittings.

Corollary 2.8MTheta

Base-field theta evaluation

Corollary 2.9 instead uses base-field cyclotomes mu_Z(G_v). Remark 2.9.1 records the price: this route gives numerical access but is uniradial until the later log-shell modification.

Corollary 2.9uniradial

Theta monoids

Theta monoids are constructed in mono-theta, Frobenioid-theoretic, and group-theoretic forms. Their comparison is one of the mechanisms by which theta values are turned into algebraic objects suitable for transport.

IUT II §3monoids

Gaussian monoids

Gaussian monoids encode theta-value distributions with the Gaussian structure that Mochizuki repeatedly compares to the classical Gaussian integral. They prepare the later LGP monoids of IUT III.

Corollary 3.5Gaussian

Global conjugate synchronization

The global Gaussian Frobenioid theory requires synchronization among conjugates. The point is to compare many local pieces without pretending they arise from one fixed holomorphic history.

IUT II §4global Gaussian

Theta-value labels

The nonzero labels in F_l/{±1} index where theta evaluations are taken. Their square weights later become visible in the Scholze-Stix discussion and in finite formalization models.

labelsj²

Theta link source

The horizontal theta links of IUT III depend on the Hodge-Arakelov theta-evaluation machinery of IUT II. The link is not merely a named arrow; it packages the evaluation of theta functions at torsion points.

theta linkHodge-Arakelov
Sources: IUT II Related: theta links multiradiality indeterminacies

IUT III Atlas: Log-Theta Lattice to Corollary 3.12

IUT III is the conceptual culmination of the series. It builds the log-theta lattice, constructs multiradial theta and logarithmic Gaussian procession monoids, introduces log-volume for packets and processions, and states the proof-critical Corollary 3.12 route.

Paper route What it contributes Wiki route
IUT III §1 Log-link, log-shells, non-Gaussian and Gaussian log-theta lattices, vertical cores, and the basic failure of commutativity between horizontal theta links and vertical log links. log-theta lattice, theta/log links, log-shells
IUT III §2 Multiradial theta monoids, coric unit portions, cyclotomic rigidity compatibility, and the preparation for log-shell-theoretic Gaussian monoids. Gaussian Frobenioids, multiradiality, indeterminacies
IUT III §3 Local and global tensor packets, LGP monoids, log-Kummer correspondences, packet/procession log-volume, and the pilot-object comparison used in Theorem B. LGP monoids, log-Kummer correspondence, tensor/log-volume corridor
Theorem A/B Theorem A packages the multiradial representation of LGP data under indeterminacies; Theorem B turns this into the signed log-volume comparison stated as Corollary 3.12. multiradial algorithms, upper semi-compatibility, Corollary 3.12

F-prime-strip

An F-prime-strip is the local valuation-indexed setting in which log-links act. It carries Frobenioid, unit, group-action, and log-shell data in the form needed for later Kummer and log-volume arguments.

Definition 1.1prime strip

Tautological log-link

At a nonarchimedean place, the local p-adic logarithm turns the unit part into log-shell data. A log-link from one Frobenioid to another is obtained by composing this tautological log-link with a Frobenioid poly-isomorphism.

local logarithmlog-shell

Pre-log-shell and log-shell

The pre-log-shell is built from invariant units; the log-shell is a scaled version of it. Log-shells are codomain-side objects of log-links, determined by selected domain-side unit data.

IUT III §1units

Gaussian log-theta lattice

Definition 1.4 gives non-Gaussian and Gaussian log-theta lattices. The Gaussian version uses Theta^{×μ}_{gau}-links and is the setting for LGP monoids and Corollary 3.12.

Definition 1.4lattice

Noncommutative squares

IUT III stresses that log-theta lattice squares are far from commutative. Horizontal theta links and vertical log links are incompatible with local ring structures in different ways.

warninghistory

Vertical cores and coricity

Because horizontal motion obligates subsequent vertical motion, meaningful multiradiality is obtained through objects invariant under vertical translation symmetries. These are the vertical cores.

Theorem 1.5coric

LGP monoids

Logarithmic Gaussian procession monoids are built by applying local packet Frobenioid constructions through log-links and processions. They are the monoid layer where labels, packets, and Gaussian structure meet.

Proposition 3.4LGP

Log-Kummer correspondence

Proposition 3.5 describes a correspondence between units/splitting monoids over a vertical column and actions on invariant log-shell data. It is a key bridge between Kummer theory and log-link compatibility.

The correspondence is deliberately not a single Kummer isomorphism: the source statement says such a single isomorphism fails compatibility with the log-links, so one must use the totality of Kummer isomorphisms pre-composed with log-link iterates.

Proposition 3.5Kummer

Kummer detachment

In the proof discussion for Corollary 3.12, Kummer detachment is the passage from Frobenius-like objects needed for the Theta×μ-LGP link to the etale-like objects used in the multiradial representation. It depends on compatibility with Ind1, Ind2, Ind3, and the full log-Kummer correspondence, not on a single-column Kummer isomorphism.

Fig. I.8Step (xi)

Processions and capsules

A procession organizes capsules indexed by label sets such as S^{±}_{j+1}. Procession-normalized quantities must use compatible averaging across overlapping label components.

capsulesaveraging

Packet-normalized log-volume

Proposition 3.9 normalizes nonarchimedean packet log-volume so multiplying by p_v adds -log(p_v), and normalizes archimedean radial log-volume so multiplying by e adds 1.

Proposition 3.9log-volume

Procession-normalized log-volume

IUT III explains that arbitrary weighted averages are not allowed here: compatibility with overlapping capsule labels forces the ordinary averaged normalization used by the procession.

normalizationlabels

Holomorphic hull

In the Corollary 3.12 route, possible images of a theta-pilot object are placed inside a holomorphic hull. The determinant/log-volume of this hull is where the final signed upper ray is read.

Step (xi)hull

Upper semi-compatibility

The third indeterminacy supplies an upper-semi comparison rather than equality. This directionality is essential for any faithful formalization of the Theorem 3.11 to Corollary 3.12 route.

In Proposition 3.5 this means containment of images in the vertically coric target modules or balls, after allowing the relevant log-link iterates; it is not a symmetric identification of packet regions.

Ind3one-sided

Theta-pilot and q-pilot

The theta-pilot and q-pilot are compared only after passing through the allowed weakening, hull, determinant, log-volume, and real-line charting operations. Treating them as already living in one concrete history is the wrong interface.

pilotscomparison

Remark 3.12.2

Remark 3.12.2 is a guide to the intended weakening of data, the distinction between q/theta labels, and the asymmetry of columns. It is an important checkpoint for formalization decisions around the disputed passage.

Remark 3.12.2audit point
Sources: IUT III Related: log-theta lattice Corollary 3.12 dispute

IUT IV Atlas: Estimates, Diophantine Output, and Species

IUT IV turns the log-volume machinery into Diophantine consequences and then gives a formal language for inter-universal movement. It is both the application paper and the foundational paper of the four-part series.

Paper route What it contributes Wiki route
IUT IV §1 Local nonarchimedean tensor-product estimates, archimedean radial estimates, prime-distribution bounds, weighted averages, elliptic-curve input, and Theorem 1.10's explicit theta-pilot log-volume estimate. tensor products, local estimates, prime/averaging input, elliptic torsion input, elliptic torsion, arithmetic divisors, theta-pilot estimate, estimate pipeline
IUT IV §2 Reduction from general elliptic-curve and hyperbolic-curve data to suitable initial theta data, then extraction of Vojta, abc, and Szpiro-type consequences from the log-volume estimates. suitable initial theta data, lambda-line setup, auxiliary prime choice, Diophantine inequalities, applications
IUT IV §3 Species, mutations, cores, mutation histories, and model-free specimens: the formal language Mochizuki uses to describe movement between set-theoretic realizations of object types. species, mutations, species/mutations page
Theorem A States the final bounded-discrepancy inequality for hyperbolic curves; Mochizuki records Vojta for hyperbolic curves, abc, and Szpiro as special cases. Diophantine inequalities, source library
Explicit Estimates μ6-modified theta theory and numerical effective versions of the IUT IV Diophantine estimates, including mono-complex ABC/Szpiro statements and Fermat-type applications. μ6 theory, effective ABC/Szpiro, FLT application

Multiple tensor products and differents

Proposition 1.1 considers tensor products of rings of integers R_i in finite extensions of Q_p. If R_I is the full tensor product and (R_I)~ its normalization, then R_I ⊆ (R_I)~ and a power of p determined by the different orders sends the normalization back into R_I.

This is the first local estimate behind the later packet log-volume bounds.

Proposition 1.1local fields

p-adic logarithm estimates

Proposition 1.2 compares log_p(R_i^×) with explicit fractional ideals in R_i. For automorphisms preserving the tensor-product logarithm submodule, it gives inclusions into shifted copies of (R_I)~.

In the unramified odd-prime case the estimate simplifies to preservation of the normalized tensor-product lattice.

Proposition 1.2log_p

Different estimates

Proposition 1.3 bounds the different order of a finite extension k_i/k_0. The lower bound is sharp in the tame case; the upper bound applies when k_i is Galois over a tame intermediate field and records the contribution of the p-primary part of the degree.

Proposition 1.3ramification

Nonarchimedean log-volume estimates

Proposition 1.4 normalizes log-volume on compact open subsets of finite extensions of Q_p so that μlog(R_i)=0 and μlog(pR_i)=-log(p). It then applies the previous different and logarithm estimates to bound transformed tensor-product log-shell regions.

The torsion subgroup of local units enters explicitly through the formula for μlog(log_p(R_i^×)).

Proposition 1.4p-adic

Archimedean metric estimates

Proposition 1.5 treats the complex archimedean case. The Chinese-remainder isomorphism C⊗_R C ≃ C⊕C changes the metric by a factor of 2; for multiple tensor products, the direct-sum metric is 2^(|I|-1) times the tensor-product metric.

The resulting integral structure is preserved by the primitive automorphisms generated by conjugation and multiplication by fourth roots of unity.

Proposition 1.5archimedean

Weighted averages

Proposition 1.7 supplies elementary weighted-average tools. These are used when translating local bounds and label-indexed data into global estimates.

Proposition 1.7averaging

Elliptic torsion input

Proposition 1.8 records the elliptic-curve facts used later in the global estimate: automorphisms of a polarized abelian variety inject into their action on l-torsion for l >= 3, models of an elliptic curve are controlled by sections of the automorphism-to-Galois sequence, and rational torsion constrains descent and reduction.

The final parts supply the Legendre-form descent and the good/multiplicative reduction ramification statements used when bounding the distinguished primes in Theorem 1.10.

Proposition 1.8torsion/descent

Prime-number estimate

Proposition 1.6 uses the prime number theorem to choose a constant η_prm such that a weighted reciprocal-log prime sum is at least one for all sufficiently large bounds. This is the analytic input later used in Theorem 1.10.

Proposition 1.6PNT

Arithmetic divisor bookkeeping

Definition 1.9 defines real arithmetic divisors on a number field as finite formal sums over all valuations. Finite valuations are weighted by log(q_v), archimedean valuations by 1, and the normalized degree is divided by [F:Q].

The same definition isolates the portion supported over a fixed set E of valuations and gives normalized E-degrees. These degrees are stable under finite base extension, which is why the later estimates can move between Fmod, F_tpd, F, and K.

Definition 1.9degrees

Theta-pilot log-volume estimate

Theorem 1.10 gives explicit log-volume estimates for theta-pilot objects in the situation of IUT III Corollary 3.12. It is the bridge from the abstract corridor to numerical inequalities.

Its inputs include the tripodal field F_tpd = Fmod(E[2]), the extension obtained by adjoining sqrt(-1) and the 3- and 5-torsion, different and conductor divisors, and the q-divisor supported at bad multiplicative places.

Theorem 1.10theta-pilot

Distinguished valuations

In the proof of Theorem 1.10, distinguished nonarchimedean valuations are those that extend to valuations of K ramified over Q. The proof identifies this condition with divisibility by 2*3*5*l or membership in the support of the q- and different-divisors.

This is the sieve separating places with nonzero local correction terms from non-distinguished places, where the local upper bound is zero.

Theorem 1.10 (iii)ramified primes

Local upper-bound computation

For a fixed rational valuation and a fixed procession index, the proof estimates the component of the holomorphic hull of possible theta-pilot images in the relevant tensor-product log-shell space. At distinguished nonarchimedean valuations it applies Proposition 1.4; at nondistinguished valuations the bound is zero; at archimedean valuations it applies Proposition 1.5.

Theorem 1.10 (iv)-(vii)local bounds

Final Theorem 1.10 bound

After weighted averaging over valuation choices and procession averaging over the label index, the local bounds give an explicit admissible value of CTheta. Combining this value with Corollary 3.12 yields the stated inequality bounding (1/6) log(q) by different, conductor, torsion, and prime-counting terms.

Theorem 1.10 (viii)CTheta

Tripodal intermediate field

The field F_tpd is obtained by adjoining the 2-torsion data of a Legendre model. It is used in IUT IV to organize suitable initial theta data for Diophantine applications.

F_tpdLegendre

Suitable initial theta data

Corollary 2.2 constructs initial theta data from points on the thrice punctured projective line, outside an exceptional finite set, with controlled q-parameter and height behavior.

The constructed data satisfies the hypotheses of Theorem 1.10 after choosing the auxiliary prime l, the field F_tpd, the bad-place set, the covering data, the valuation section, and the distinguished cusp.

Corollary 2.2application input

Well-known estimates

Proposition 2.1 supplies two elementary inputs for §2: the inequality log(x) <= x for x >= 1, and a prime-number-theorem bound for theta(x)=sum_{p<=x} log(p). The latter is used to find a prime outside a finite forbidden set with an explicit size bound.

Proposition 2.1PNT

Compact lambda-line setup

Corollary 2.2 specializes to X=P1_Q with D={0,1,infty}, viewed as the lambda-line for Legendre elliptic curves. The compactly bounded set K_V must include the prime 2 in its support and satisfy a boundedness condition on the 2-adic j-invariant image.

Corollary 2.2lambda-line

q-parameter and height comparison

Corollary 2.2(i) identifies, up to bounded discrepancy on K_V, the q-parameter degree away from 2, the total q-parameter degree, the height associated to the divisor at infinity on the compactified elliptic moduli stack, and the height attached to omega_X(D) with the factor 1/6.

Corollary 2.2(i)BD classes

Auxiliary prime choice

In Corollary 2.2(ii), after discarding a finite exceptional set, one chooses a prime l of size roughly the square root of the total q-height, with logarithmic and degree factors. The chosen l avoids local height divisibility obstructions, avoids cyclic subgroup obstructions, leaves a nonempty bad-place set, and forces a large l-torsion Galois image.

Corollary 2.2(ii)l selection

Exceptional sets

Corollary 2.2 constructs finite sets of exceptional algebraic points depending on the compact set, degree bound, and epsilon parameters. On the complement, the resulting error term epsilon_E can be made at most the prescribed epsilon, while the exceptional points have explicitly bounded q-height.

Corollary 2.2(ii)-(iii)finite exceptions

Diophantine inequalities

Corollary 2.3 applies the log-volume estimates to Vojta-type inequalities for hyperbolic curves; this is the route by which the series claims abc and Szpiro-type consequences.

The statement bounds the height attached to omega_X(D) by (1+epsilon) times the sum of log-different and log-conductor, up to bounded discrepancy on points of degree at most d.

Corollary 2.3Vojta/abc

GenEll reduction

The proof of Corollary 2.3 invokes the equivalence in GenEll Theorem 2.1: it is enough to prove the inequality on compactly bounded subsets of the thrice punctured projective line. Corollary 2.2 supplies exactly that input, and Belyi-map analytic continuation carries the result back to general hyperbolic curves.

Corollary 2.3GenEll

Prime 2 restriction

Remark 1.10.6 explains why bad multiplicative places over residue characteristic 2 are excluded from Vbad_mod: the etale theta-function rigidity used for multiradiality depends on odd residue characteristic, and the relevant integral structures separate at p=2.

Remark 1.10.6odd primes

μ6 theory

The Explicit Estimates paper replaces the original 2-torsion-centered theta setup by a μ6-version. Its purpose is to make the etale theta-function machinery work uniformly at arbitrary bad places, including bad places over the prime 2.

Explicit Estimates §3-5μ6

Effective Theorem 5.1

Theorem 5.1 is the μ6 analogue of IUT IV Theorem 1.10. Under the large-prime hypothesis l >= 10^15, it gives a sharpened explicit CTheta estimate and a final bound with constants such as 4.0881.

Theorem 5.1effective CTheta

Effective initial theta data

Corollary 5.2 is the μ6 effective analogue of IUT IV Corollary 2.2. It constructs suitable μ6-initial theta data outside finite exceptional sets and removes the earlier compact-bounded-set restrictions in the mono-complex case.

Corollary 5.2effective exceptions

Effective ABC/Szpiro

Theorem 5.3 states effective ABC/Szpiro inequalities over mono-complex number fields, i.e. Q and imaginary quadratic fields. Theorem 5.4 specializes the resulting Szpiro-type inequality over Q.

Theorems 5.3-5.4mono-complex

Fermat application

Corollary 5.8 applies the effective inequalities to Fermat-type equations, giving a stated lower-bound threshold for prime exponents and then combining with classical numerical results to obtain the paper's claimed alternative route to Fermat's Last Theorem.

Corollary 5.8application

Species

A species is a type of mathematical object specified by set-theoretic formulas, not by one fixed set-theoretic realization. This is Mochizuki's formal language for inter-universal movement.

Definition 3.1foundations

Mutation

A mutation sends objects and morphisms of one species to another by formulas. Mutation histories organize sequences of such changes along an oriented graph.

Definition 3.3species

Model-free object

A model-free specimen is described independently of the ZFC model in which it is interpreted. This explains how the word "inter-universal" is tied to rules and formulas rather than to one ambient universe.

ZFCGuniverses

Set-theoretic copies

IUT IV's foundational section is relevant to the dispute because it explains why distinct copies and mutation histories are not meant to be identified simply because they have isomorphic ordinary output.

copy disciplinehistory
Theorem 1.10 computation route how IUT IV §1 converts the Corollary 3.12 corridor into numbers
Sources: IUT IV Related: IUT IV consequences formalization dispute

Initial Theta Data

Initial theta data is the input package for the four-paper series. IUT I, Definition 3.1 fixes an algebraic closure over a number field, a once-punctured elliptic curve, a prime l >= 5, a torsion-cover/orbicurve package, a valuation section, a nonempty set of bad multiplicative places, and a distinguished cusp. The point of the definition is not only to name constants, but to create the environment in which the later anabelian, Frobenioid, Hodge-theater, and theta-link constructions can run.

Initial theta data package the source input before Hodge theaters are built
Definition 3.1 layer Data fixed Why it matters later
Arithmetic base F/F, the field of moduli Fmod, and the l-torsion field K. Provides the Galois and field-extension environment for all Hodge theaters and prime-strips.
Curve/orbicurve base A once-punctured elliptic curve XF ⊂ EF, its quotient CF, and the hyperbolic orbicurve CK of type (1,l-tors)±. Supplies the anabelian and theta-function source geometry.
Valuations A section V over Vmod = V(Fmod), with nonempty Vbad_mod of odd-residue-characteristic multiplicative places. Controls local prime-strips, log-links, bad-place theta evaluation, and global product-formula constraints.
Cusp and local covers A cusp epsilon of CK, with bad-place compatibility to the canonical generator determined by the torsion quotient. Fixes the label/cusp data used by later theta, Kummer, and Frobenioid constructions.
Group-theoretic recovery The open subgroup Pi_XK ⊂ Pi_CK and the function field of XK are reconstructed through the theta-approach to absolute anabelian algorithms. Prevents the input from being a bare geometric tuple; the etale/tempered group side is part of the source machinery.

Field and moduli layer

The data includes a number field with algebraic closure, the field of moduli Fmod of the elliptic curve, and a finite Galois extension K tied to the l-torsion representation.

IUT I Def. 3.1Fmod/K

Elliptic curve layer

The curve X_F is a once-punctured elliptic curve inside E_F. The quotient by the involution gives the orbicurve C_F, and the prime l controls the torsion and label symmetries.

elliptic inputl-torsion

Cover and quotient layer

The orbicurve C_K over K has C_F ×_F K as its core and determines the associated cover X_K. This is where the later label-class and cusp geometry starts.

orbicurvescovers

Valuation section

The subset V of valuations of K is chosen as a section over the valuations of Fmod. It separates archimedean/nonarchimedean and bad/good places for the local prime-strip and log-volume constructions.

Vlocalization

Bad multiplicative places

Vbad_mod is a nonempty set of nonarchimedean places of odd residue characteristic where the elliptic curve has bad multiplicative behavior. These places anchor the Tate-curve and theta evaluation side of the theory.

bad placesTate curve

Distinguished cusp

The cusp epsilon is a nonzero cusp of C_K. At bad places it is required to match the canonical generator determined by the relevant torsion quotient.

cuspgenerator

Dupuy-Hilado decomposition

Dupuy-Hilado split the construction into pre-theta data (F,l,E,S) and auxiliary data (V,M,epsilon) that simulates a global multiplicative subspace and generator.

expositionglobal multiplicative subspace

Pre-theta data

In Dupuy-Hilado's unpacking, pre-theta data consists of (F,l,E,S) with a non-arithmetic punctured elliptic curve, large mod-l Galois image, torsion conditions including sqrt(-1) and 30-torsion over F, and a nonempty bad multiplicative set satisfying congruence exclusions.

DH Definition 2.4.1hypotheses

Fricke involution

The paper rewrites part of Mochizuki's cover data through the classical modular curve Y0(l): a cyclic subgroup M <= E[l] is sent by the Fricke involution to (E/M, E[l]/M). This gives a concrete modular handle on the auxiliary subgroup appearing in initial theta data.

DH §2.2Y0(l)

Fake global multiplicative subspace

Dupuy-Hilado replace the orbicurve notation by an auxiliary subgroup M that simulates the global multiplicative subspace and global canonical generator. They stress that this is a bookkeeping simplification for constructing examples, not a replacement for Mochizuki's later Hodge-theater machinery.

DH §§2.7-2.8example machinery

Non-arithmetic condition

The pre-theta data requires the punctured complex elliptic curve E_C \ {o} not to be an arithmetic Riemann surface. Dupuy-Hilado verify this condition in their explicit route using Sijsling's classification of the exceptional j-invariants.

DH §2.3-2.4Sijsling

Computable examples

Their 2025 paper proves an algorithm for generating initial theta data from elliptic-curve databases over Q and works out the case of the Cremona curve 11a1.

11a1algorithm

Formalization role

In Lean, this object should begin as a structured input record but should not remain a mere label: the long-term target is to replace fields such as valuation sections, covers, torsion quotients, and cusp compatibility by explicit constructions or cited theorem interfaces.

Lean interfacesource obligations
Sources: IUT I Dupuy-Hilado initial theta data Related: Hodge theaters IUT I atlas Corollary 3.12

Initial Theta Field and Moduli Layer

Definition 3.1 begins with arithmetic base data. The number field F, its algebraic closure, the field of moduli Fmod, and the ell-torsion field K set the Galois environment in which all later Hodge-theater objects are built.

Arithmetic base for initial theta data IUT I Definition 3.1(a)-(c)
LayerDefinition 3.1 dataDownstream use
Base field F is a number field with sqrt(-1) in F; an algebraic closure is fixed. Supplies absolute Galois groups and the arithmetic base of the theater.
Moduli field Fmod is the field of moduli of the once-punctured elliptic curve. Provides the valuation base and later stack/Frobenioid constructions.
Torsion field K is determined by the kernel of the ell-torsion representation, whose image contains SL2(F_ell). Controls the torsion-cover and Galois symmetry layer used in theta data.
Sources: IUT I Definition 3.1(a)-(c) Related: initial theta data valuation layer cusp/covers layer

Initial Theta Valuation Layer

Definition 3.1 chooses a subset V of valuations of K that is a section over the valuations of Fmod. This turns the global field data into valuation-indexed local data and separates bad multiplicative places from good places before Frobenioids and prime-strips are built.

Valuation section and bad/good split IUT I Definition 3.1(b),(e)

Section over moduli valuations

The choice V subset V(K) induces a bijection with Vmod. Later local objects are indexed by this chosen representative set.

Bad nonarchimedean places

Vbad_mod is nonempty, nonarchimedean, of odd residue characteristic, and consists of places where the elliptic curve has multiplicative reduction.

Bad-place theta models

At bad places, the paper invokes the EtTh construction, described informally as extracting an ell-th root of the theta function, to obtain the local torsion-theta orbicurve models.

Sources: IUT I Definition 3.1(b),(e) Related: initial theta data bad nonarch Frobenioids theta evaluation sections

Initial Theta Cusp and Cover Layer

The cover and cusp part of Definition 3.1 ties the Section 1 arrow-cover machinery to the initial theta package. The orbicurve C_K determines X_K, local covers and fundamental groups, and a distinguished nonzero cusp epsilon whose local specializations become the label source for later theta and Kummer data.

Cover package plus distinguished cusp IUT I Definition 3.1(d)-(f)
ComponentDefinition 3.1 roleGuardrail
C_K and X_K The signed torsion orbicurve over K determines the associated cover and profinite group inclusions. Do not model initial theta data as just an elliptic curve and prime.
Distinguished cusp epsilon is a nonzero cusp of C_K; at bad places it matches the canonical generator up to sign. Cusp compatibility is part of the source data for labels and theta values.
Arrow cover output The data determines X_K arrow and C_K arrow of Definition 1.1 type. This ties Definition 3.1 back to the Section 1 cyclic-cover machinery.
Sources: IUT I Definition 3.1(d)-(f) Related: arrow torsion types valuation layer theta approach theta evaluation sections

IUT I Remark 3.1.2: The Theta Approach to Anabelian Reconstruction

Remark 3.1.2 explains how the absolute anabelian reconstruction algorithm is used in IUT I Section 3. Instead of reconstructing directly from the full orbicurve group Pi_CK, Mochizuki routes the reconstruction through the group-theoretically recoverable open subgroup Pi_XK. This is the “theta approach” to AbsTopIII Theorem 1.9.

Why reconstruction is routed through X_K IUT I, Remark 3.1.2
Source checkpointContentWiki/formalization consequence
Remark 3.1.1 For nonarchimedean v, the local profinite groups also have corresponding tempered fundamental groups whose profinite completions recover the profinite groups. The geometric tempered group Delta^tp depends a priori on v. Local reconstruction interfaces must distinguish profinite and tempered inputs and should not assume the tempered group is globally constant.
Remark 3.1.2(i) The open subgroup Pi_XK subset Pi_CK is constructed group-theoretically from Pi_CK. The subgroup Delta_X is characterized by an explicit algorithm, and nonzero cusp decomposition groups determine the image of Pi_XK in Pi_CK / Delta_X. Pi_XK should be modeled as reconstructible data, not as an arbitrary chosen open subgroup.
Remark 3.1.2(ii) The reconstruction algorithm of AbsTopIII Theorem 1.9 is applied to Pi_XK to reconstruct the function field of X_K, equipped with the natural Gal(X_K/C_K) action. This is the theta approach: the reconstruction is routed through the torsion cover rather than through the full orbicurve group directly.
Local versions The same approach is used for the good nonarchimedean local groups Pi_Cv, Pi_Xv, Pi_Cv with arrows, and for bad places using tempered groups together with Corollary 1.2 and EtTh Proposition 2.4. The wiki should keep the valuation-stratum-specific local routes separate from the global Pi_CK route.
Remark 3.1.2(iii) The tautological extension 1 -> Delta^Theta -> Delta_X^Theta -> Delta_X^ell -> 1 gives an isomorphism between the cyclotome M_X and l · Delta^Theta. This is the stated significance of the theta approach. The cyclotome is not decorative. It is the target through which the later bad-place theta/Kummer theory reads the reconstruction.
Remark 3.1.2(iv) Mochizuki compares the role of the covering X for large l to the universal covering of a complex elliptic curve by the complex plane in holomorphic reconstruction. This analogy belongs on the orientation layer only; the formal content is the subgroup reconstruction and cyclotome identification above.

Open subgroup Pi_XK

Remark 3.1.2(i) says Pi_XK is recovered from the abstract topological group Pi_CK. The recovery uses the subgroup Delta_X and the group-theoreticity of nonzero cusp decomposition groups.

Remark 3.1.2(i)reconstructible

Function-field reconstruction

Applying AbsTopIII Theorem 1.9 through Pi_XK reconstructs the function field of X_K together with the natural Gal(X_K/C_K)-action.

Remark 3.1.2(ii)AbsTopIII

Cyclotome M_X

The extension class of the tautological extension identifies the cyclotome M_X from AbsTopIII with l · Delta^Theta. Mochizuki names this as the significance of the theta approach.

Remark 3.1.2(iii)cyclotome

Local theta approach

At good nonarchimedean and bad nonarchimedean places the same convention is applied to local group inputs, with the bad-place route passing through tempered groups. This is why later local Frobenioid pages are valuation sensitive.

Remark 3.1.2(ii)local groups

Large-l covering analogy

The comparison with the universal cover of a complex elliptic curve is an analogy for why the covering X is useful. It does not replace the precise group-theoretic construction of Pi_XK.

Remark 3.1.2(iv)orientation

Formalization duty

A faithful interface needs fields for: reconstructibility of Pi_XK, applicability of the AbsTopIII algorithm, the resulting Gal(X_K/C_K)-action, and the M_X ≃ l · Delta^Theta cyclotome comparison.

Lean guardrailnot a label
Sources: IUT I, Remarks 3.1.1-3.1.2 Related: initial theta data cyclic cover reconstruction kappa-coric functions IUT I Frobenioid package
