Pi_XK Recovery in the Theta Approach

Remark 3.1.2(i) says that the open subgroup Pi_XK subset Pi_CK is constructed group-theoretically from the topological group Pi_CK. The recovery uses the subgroup Delta_X and the decomposition groups at nonzero cusps.

Recover the torsion-cover group before applying AbsTopIII IUT I Remark 3.1.2(i)
The source does not assume Pi_XK as an arbitrary chosen subgroup. Its group-theoretic recoverability is part of the theta approach interface.
Sources: IUT I Remark 3.1.2(i) Related: theta approach function-field recovery cusp recognizers

Function-Field Recovery in the Theta Approach

After Pi_XK has been recovered, Remark 3.1.2(ii) applies the reconstruction algorithm of AbsTopIII Theorem 1.9 to Pi_XK. The output is the function field of X_K equipped with its natural Gal(X_K/C_K)-action.

Reconstruction routed through the torsion cover IUT I Remark 3.1.2(ii)
RouteInputOutput
Global theta approach Pi_XK subset Pi_CK Function field of X_K with the natural Gal(X_K/C_K)-action.
Good local route Local groups such as Pi_Cv and arrow-cover variants. Valuation-local reconstruction compatible with the global convention.
Bad local route Tempered groups at bad nonarchimedean places. The local input for theta/Kummer and Frobenioid constructions.
Sources: IUT I Remark 3.1.2(ii) Related: Pi_XK recovery cyclotome comparison valuation layer

Cyclotome Comparison in the Theta Approach

Remark 3.1.2(iii) identifies the stated significance of the theta approach: the tautological extension of geometric fundamental groups identifies the cyclotome M_X arising from the anabelian reconstruction with ell · Delta^Theta, the cyclotomic object used later in the bad-place theory.

Theta approach target: a cyclotomic identification IUT I Remark 3.1.2(iii)
This page is a guardrail against treating the theta approach as only a reconstruction convention. The source explicitly points to the cyclotome comparison as the reason this route matters for the later series.
Sources: IUT I Remark 3.1.2(iii) Related: function-field recovery kappa-coric functions bad nonarch Frobenioids

Kappa-Coric Functions

IUT I Remark 3.1.7 introduces κ-coric rational functions on the C_Fmod model. These functions are designed for Kummer-theoretic reconstruction: their zero/pole pattern avoids critical points, their values at strictly critical points split constants up to roots of unity, and their ∞κ and ∞κ× variants form divisible cyclotomic pseudo-monoids.

Kummer-oriented rational functions IUT I Remark 3.1.7

The C_Fmod model

Remark 3.1.7 starts from the unique model of C_F over the field of moduli Fmod. Its coarse space is isomorphic to the affine line, so rational functions on the orbicurve can be read through affine-line polynomial geometry.

Remark 3.1.7(i)Fmod

Strictly critical points

Critical points are closed points lying over the compactification points that arise from 2-torsion on the elliptic curve. Strictly critical points are the non-cuspidal critical points; they are treated as canonical non-cuspidal test points.

Remark 3.1.7(i)2-torsion

κ-coric definition

A rational function f is κ-coric if, when nonconstant, it has one pole and at least two zeroes, its zero/pole divisor is defined over a number field and avoids critical points, and its restrictions at strictly critical points are roots of unity.

Remark 3.1.7(i)Kummer

Constant splitting up to roots of unity

The strictly critical value condition gives a canonical splitting, up to roots of unity, of nonzero constant multiples into κ-coric functions and constants. In particular, if both f and c f are κ-coric, then c is a root of unity.

Remark 3.1.7(i)constants

∞κ and ∞κ× pseudo-monoids

An element is ∞κ-coric if some positive power is κ-coric, and ∞κ×-coric if a unit multiple is ∞κ-coric. Multiplication gives pseudo-monoid structures; for the ∞κ and ∞κ× versions these pseudo-monoids are divisible and cyclotomic.

Remark 3.1.7(ii)pseudo-monoids

Values at noncritical points

For the relevant algebraically closed or solvably closed fields, every element appears as the value of a κ-coric rational function at a noncritical rational point. This is a supply theorem for the later Kummer theory of rational functions.

Remark 3.1.7(ii)values

κ-solvable elements

Over Fmod, an element is κ-solvable if it is an F_sol^x-multiple of an ∞κ-coric element. The field generated by these elements is denoted L_C(κ-sol) in the source discussion.

Remark 3.1.7(iii)F_sol

Linear disjointness with the C_K field

The perfectness of SL_2(F_l), together with the critical-point avoidance in ∞κ×-coric functions, yields linear disjointness between the field generated by the C_K embeddings and the κ-solvable field over the stated base.

Remark 3.1.7(iii)SL2

κ-sol automorphisms

Remark 3.1.7(iv) defines κ-sol-open subgroups and the corresponding κ-sol automorphism and outer-automorphism groups. The conjugation diagram built from the exact sequence of Galois groups is cartesian because the kernel is center-free.

Remark 3.1.7(iv)outer actions

Formalization rule

A formal model should not treat κ-coricity as a boolean tag. It has geometric content: pole/zero constraints, critical-point avoidance, root-of-unity splitting, pseudo-monoid operations, and Galois-theoretic automorphism control.

Lean guardrailstructure
Sources: IUT I Related: initial theta data pseudo-monoid ThetaNF Frobenioid lift Frobenioids/log-shells log-Kummer correspondences

C_Fmod Model and Critical Points

Remark 3.1.7 starts from the model C_Fmod of C_F over the field of moduli. Its coarse space is the affine line, and its compactification carries critical points coming from the 2-torsion points of the elliptic curve. The strictly critical points are the non-cuspidal ones.

Affine-line model with critical-point tests IUT I Remark 3.1.7(i)
ObjectSource definitionRole
C_Fmod The unique model of C_F over Fmod, obtained from Definition 3.1 data. Base curve for κ-coric rational functions.
Critical point A closed point over a compactification point arising from a 2-torsion point. Forbidden support for zero/pole divisors.
Strictly critical point A critical point not mapping to the compactification point arising from the unique cusp. Root-of-unity value tests split constants up to roots of unity.
Sources: IUT I Remark 3.1.7(i) Related: kappa-coric functions κ-coric definition theta field/moduli layer

Kappa-Coric Rational Functions

A rational function f on C_L is κ-coric when its divisor and critical-point behavior satisfy the three conditions in Remark 3.1.7(i). The κ is explicitly mnemonic for Kummer: the functions are designed as Kummer-compatible rational functions on the reconstructed affine-line model.

Three source conditions for κ-coricity IUT I Remark 3.1.7(i)

One-pole condition

For nonconstant f, the source requires precisely one pole of unrestricted order and at least two distinct zeroes.

Critical-point avoidance

The divisor of zeroes and poles is defined over a number field and avoids the critical points from the 2-torsion geometry.

Root-of-unity values

At every strictly critical point, f restricts to a root of unity; this later controls constants up to roots of unity.

κ-coricity is not a boolean label in the source. It packages geometric support conditions, a preferred polynomial orientation, and root-of-unity normalization data.
Sources: IUT I Remark 3.1.7(i) Related: C_Fmod critical points ∞κ pseudo-monoids log-Kummer correspondences

Infinity-Kappa Pseudo-Monoids

Remark 3.1.7(ii) extends κ-coric functions by allowing positive powers and unit multiples. These ∞κ and ∞κ× classes are not ordinary monoids; under multiplication they form pseudo-monoids, and the ∞κ and ∞κ× versions are divisible and cyclotomic in the Section 0 sense.

Root closure and unit-twist closure IUT I Remark 3.1.7(ii)
ClassDefinitionExtra structure
κ-coric Directly satisfies the divisor, critical-point, and root-of-unity conditions. Pseudo-monoid under multiplication.
∞κ-coric Some positive power is κ-coric. Divisible and cyclotomic pseudo-monoid.
∞κ×-coric Some unit multiple is ∞κ-coric. Divisible and cyclotomic pseudo-monoid; strict-critical values test whether it is ∞κ.
Sources: IUT I Remark 3.1.7(ii) Related: pseudo-monoids κ-coric definition κ-solvable disjointness

Kappa-Solvable Elements and Linear Disjointness

Remark 3.1.7(iii) specializes to L = Fmod and defines κ-solvable elements by allowing F_sol^x multiples of ∞κ-coric elements. The same paragraph records the Galois-theoretic payoff: a linear disjointness statement between the field generated by C_K and the κ-solvable field.

κ-solvability feeds a Galois disjointness statement IUT I Remark 3.1.7(iii)
The linear disjointness argument uses both group theory, especially the perfectness of SL2(F_ell), and the critical-point avoidance built into ∞κ×-coricity.
Sources: IUT I Remark 3.1.7(iii) Related: ∞κ pseudo-monoids theta field/moduli layer κ-sol automorphisms

Kappa-Sol Automorphism Control

Remark 3.1.7(iv) defines κ-sol-open subgroups and the associated κ-sol automorphism and outer-automorphism groups. These groups carry profinite topologies from their finite quotients, and the conjugation diagram attached to the Galois exact sequence is cartesian because the kernel is center-free.

Outer automorphism control from κ-sol-open subgroups IUT I Remark 3.1.7(iv)
TermMeaning in Remark 3.1.7(iv)Reason it matters
κ-sol-open Intersection with Gal(L_C/L_C(κ-sol)) of a normal open subgroup of Gal(L_C/L_C). Defines the finite quotient system used for the profinite topology.
κ-sol automorphism An automorphism preserving every κ-sol-open subgroup. Gives compatible maps to automorphism groups of all κ-sol-open quotients.
Cartesian conjugation diagram The natural Galois-to-automorphism square is cartesian. Depends on center-freeness of the kernel, proved via genus-zero curve groups and Galois injectivity.
Sources: IUT I Remark 3.1.7(iv) Related: κ-solvable disjointness function-field recovery anabelian background

IUT I Frobenioid Package

IUT I Examples 3.2-3.5 construct the local and global Frobenioid data that Definition 3.6 packages into a theta-Hodge theater. The split is valuation sensitive: bad nonarchimedean, good nonarchimedean, and archimedean places have different local constructions, while the global realified Frobenioids act as currency-exchange devices for the local divisor monoids.

Examples 3.2-3.5 into Definition 3.6 local strata, theta versions, global realification

Bad nonarchimedean Frobenioids

For v in V_bad, Example 3.2 starts from the hyperbolic curve X_v and constructs a tempered Frobenioid F_v over the base category D_v of connected tempered coverings. The subcategory D_v^⊢ is pulled back from the base field and has a left-adjoint inclusion into D_v.

Example 3.2(i)tempered

Frobenioid-theoretic theta function

Example 3.2(ii) recalls the birationalization F_v^div and a Frobenioid-theoretic ell-th root of the theta function on the covering Y_v. Its reciprocal Theta_v is reconstructed only up to mu_2ell and ell Z indeterminacies.

Example 3.2(ii)theta value

Bad-prime split Frobenioids

The bad-place package forms C_v^⊢ from the monoid generated by the logarithmic divisor of the chosen q_v^(1/2ell), and forms C_v^Theta from theta values on products with Y_v. The two split Frobenioids are naturally equivalent.

Example 3.2(iv)-(v)split

Category-theoretic reconstruction

Example 3.2(vi) lists what is reconstructible from the relevant categories: the base categories, the theta-side category, the tempered Frobenioid, and the split Frobenioids. Remark 3.2.1 stresses that these reconstructions should be read algorithmically, not as choices from a fixed scheme model.

Example 3.2(vi)mono-anabelian

Tempered-meromorphic correction

Remark 3.2.4 records a correction to the earlier EtTh discussion of tempered Frobenioids: for the present series, "tempered Frobenioid" is read with the stronger root-existence condition needed for arbitrary positive roots. Theta functions lie in the required range.

Remark 3.2.4EtTh

Good nonarchimedean Frobenioids

For v in V_good cap V_non, Example 3.3 builds p_v-adic Frobenioids from valuation monoids ord(O_L^triangleright) and the absolutely primitive submonoid generated by p_v. The theta version replaces log(p_v) by the formal symbol log(p_v) log(Theta).

Example 3.3good places

Archimedean Frobenioids

For archimedean v, Example 3.4 replaces p-adic categories by Aut-holomorphic orbispaces and an archimedean Frobenioid representing O_Kv^triangleright. The split topological monoid separates the compact unit factor from the noncompact R_ge0 factor.

Example 3.4(i)-(ii)CAF

Archimedean Kummer structure

The Kummer structure is the inclusion kappa_v : O^triangleright(C_v) -> A_Dv. Remark 3.4.2 explains that it is equivalent to an Aut-holomorphic structure on the groupification plus a co-holomorphicization; this is the part compatible with the logarithm operation.

Remark 3.4.2log-compatible

Local theta versions

At good and archimedean places, the theta-side split Frobenioid is obtained by adjoining the formal factor log(Theta) to the generator log(p_v). At bad places, the actual theta value Theta_v supplies the corresponding divisor data.

Examples 3.2-3.4Theta version

Global realified Fmod

Example 3.5 constructs the realified Frobenioid C_mod^real associated to Fmod. Its prime set is naturally identified with V_mod, and each local prime component maps to the realification of C_v^⊢ by a comparison isomorphism rho_v.

Example 3.5(i)realification

Global theta version

The theta version C_tht^real is an isomorphic copy generated by a formal log(Theta). At bad places, rho_v^Theta uses the quotient log_Phi(Theta_v) / log_Phi(q_v); at good places it uses the formal log(p_v) log(Theta).

Example 3.5(ii)theta currency

D/log-shell version

Example 3.5(iii) constructs F_D^real from a copy of the global realified Frobenioid and the D_v^⊢ data. Mochizuki explicitly says this may be viewed as the log-shell version of the global package.

Example 3.5(iii)log-shell

Currency-exchange role

Remark 3.5.1 describes the global Frobenioids as devices for currency exchange between the local divisor-monoid currencies at the various valuations. This is the source-level reason the wiki keeps the local packages and the global realified comparison maps separate.

Remark 3.5.1(ii)local/global

Definition 3.6 theater package

A theta-Hodge theater is the collection ({F_v}_{v in V}, F_mod^real). At nonarchimedean places F_v is a category equivalent to the local Frobenioid; at archimedean places it is a triple with an Aut-holomorphic orbispace and Kummer structure; globally it carries the prime bijection, split local Frobenioids, and maps rho_v.

Definition 3.6theater input

Input for the first theta link

Definition 3.6 also constructs F_tht^real and F_D^real algorithmically from F_mod^real. Corollary 3.7 then defines the theta link as the full poly-isomorphism from the theta-side global package of one theater to the mod-side global package of another.

Corollary 3.7theta link
Formalization warning: this package should not be collapsed into one generic "local Frobenioid" field. The source construction depends on the valuation stratum, the split Frobenioid, the theta version, and the global realified comparison maps.
Sources: IUT I Related: initial theta data kappa-coric functions Hodge theaters theta/log links Frobenioids/log-shells

IUT I Example 3.2: Frobenioids at Bad Nonarchimedean Primes

Example 3.2 builds the bad-prime local Frobenioid package from the Tate-curve theta-function theory of EtTh. The source construction separates the tempered Frobenioid F_v, the base-field-theoretic hull C_v, the split Frobenioid F_v^⊢, and the theta split Frobenioid F_v^Theta.

Bad-place theta function as a split Frobenioid package IUT I, Example 3.2
Source itemConstructionReason to keep separate
Example 3.2(i) The curve X_v determines a tempered Frobenioid F_v over connected tempered coverings D_v. The base-field subcategory D_v^⊢ = B(K_v)^0 embeds fully and has a left-adjoint projection from D_v. This is the local tempered category, not just a p-adic monoid.
Example 3.2(ii) The birationalization F_v^div and the covering Y_v carry the Frobenioid-theoretic l-th root of the theta function. Its reciprocal Theta_v is determined up to mu_2l and lZ indeterminacies. The indeterminacy belongs to the source object and must not be erased.
Example 3.2(iv) The value of Theta_v at -q_v is q_v^(1/2l), up to mu_2l. This produces the submonoid generated by log_Phi(q_v) and the split Frobenioid F_v^⊢. The q_v normalization links the Tate parameter to the divisor monoid.
Example 3.2(v) Products with Y_v define D_v^Theta. The monoid generated by powers of Theta_v gives C_v^Theta and F_v^Theta, naturally equivalent to F_v^⊢. This is the bad-prime source of the theta-side split Frobenioid.
Example 3.2(vi), Remarks 3.2.1-3.2.4 The relevant base categories, split Frobenioids, and theta-side data are reconstructible from the category structure; “reconstructible” should be read algorithmically. Remark 3.2.4 also strengthens the tempered meromorphic condition needed for arbitrary roots. Records both the construction and the correction guarding the range of EtTh used here.
Sources: IUT I, Example 3.2 Related: IUT I Frobenioid package theta approach global realified theater

Bad-Place Frobenioid Base Category

Example 3.2(i) begins the bad nonarchimedean construction with the tempered Frobenioid F_v determined by the hyperbolic curve X_v. Its base category D_v is the connected tempered-covering category, while D_v^⊢ is pulled back from Spec(K_v).

Tempered curve coverings and base-field coverings IUT I Example 3.2(i)
ComponentExample 3.2(i) roleFormalization guardrail
F_v Tempered Frobenioid determined by X_v. Depends on the bad-place tempered category, not a generic p-adic Frobenioid field.
D_v Category of connected tempered coverings of X_v. Keep the curve-covering base separate from base-field coverings.
D_v^⊢ Full subcategory pulled back from B(K_v)^0, with a left-adjoint projection from D_v. The inclusion/projection relation is part of the source structure.
Sources: IUT I Example 3.2(i) Related: bad nonarch Frobenioids theta value indeterminacy tempered covering complements

Theta Value and Indeterminacy at Bad Places

Example 3.2(ii) recalls the Frobenioid-theoretic ell-th root of the theta function. The source uses its reciprocal Theta_v inside the birationalized Frobenioid and records that it is determined only up to mu_2ell and ell Z indeterminacies.

Bad-place theta value before split Frobenioids IUT I Example 3.2(ii)
These are not later audit labels. Example 3.2(ii) attaches the mu_2ell and ell Z indeterminacies directly to the reconstruction of Theta_v.
Sources: IUT I Example 3.2(ii) Related: bad-place base category bad split Frobenioids theta links

Bad-Place Split Frobenioids

Example 3.2(iv)-(v) constructs two split Frobenioids at a bad place: F_v^⊢, built from the divisor monoid generated by the normalized Tate parameter q_v^(1/2ell), and F_v^Theta, built from powers of Theta_v. The source gives a natural equivalence between them.

q-normalized side and theta side IUT I Example 3.2(iv)-(v)
SideConstructionMeaning
q-normalized side q_v^(1/2ell) defines N · log_Phi(q_v) and C_v^⊢. Reads the Tate parameter into a divisor-monoid generator.
Theta side Powers of Theta_v on products with Y_v define C_v^Theta. Reads the theta function into the Frobenioid monoid.
Equivalence The two split Frobenioids are naturally equivalent and characteristic splittings correspond. Connects the q-normalized and theta-normalized descriptions at bad places.
Sources: IUT I Example 3.2(iv)-(v) Related: theta value indeterminacy bad Frobenioid reconstruction global realified theater

Bad Frobenioid Reconstruction and Corrections

Example 3.2(vi) and Remarks 3.2.1-3.2.4 explain what is intrinsic in the bad-place Frobenioid package. The source lists category-theoretic reconstruction steps and clarifies that these reconstructions should be read algorithmically, while Remark 3.2.4 corrects the range of the tempered Frobenioid theory needed for arbitrary roots.

Intrinsic reconstruction and tempered-meromorphic correction IUT I Example 3.2(vi), Remarks 3.2.1-3.2.4
Source noteContentWiki/formalization consequence
Example 3.2(vi) Lists category-theoretic reconstruction of D_v^⊢, D_v^Theta, D_v, F_v^Theta, and F_v^⊢. These are theorem interfaces, not arbitrary record fields.
Remark 3.2.1 “Reconstructed category-theoretically” is read mono-anabelianly as explicit algorithms from category structure. Do not rely on a hidden reference scheme model when explaining reconstruction.
Remark 3.2.4 The needed function class is strengthened so roots of all positive orders exist over tempered coverings; theta functions satisfy the stronger condition. Record the correction before using EtTh tempered Frobenioids downstream.
Sources: IUT I Example 3.2(vi), Remarks 3.2.1-3.2.4 Related: bad split Frobenioids bad-place base category mono-anabelian transport

IUT I Examples 3.3-3.4: Good Nonarchimedean and Archimedean Frobenioids

Examples 3.3 and 3.4 give the non-bad local Frobenioid strata. Good nonarchimedean places use p-adic Frobenioids built from valuation monoids and a formal log(Theta) factor. Archimedean places use Aut-holomorphic orbispaces, split topological monoids, and Kummer structures compatible with the logarithm operation.

Non-bad local strata before global realification IUT I, Examples 3.3-3.4
Source itemConstructionDistinction
Example 3.3(i) At good nonarchimedean places, valuation monoids ord(O_L^triangleright) and ord(Z_pv^triangleright) define C_v and C_v^⊢. Good places use a formal p-adic generator, not a Tate theta value.
Example 3.3(ii)-(iii) Replacing log(p_v) by log(p_v) log(Theta) defines F_v^Theta. The element p_v is recovered through the algorithmic field structure on Kummer images. The theta symbol is formal at good nonarchimedean places.
Example 3.4(i)-(iii) At archimedean places, F_v is the triple (C_v,D_v,kappa_v). The split monoid separates the compact unit factor from the noncompact R_ge0 factor, and the theta version again uses the formal log(Theta) generator. Archimedean local data is not a p-adic Frobenioid.
Remark 3.4.2 The Kummer structure kappa_v is equivalent to an Aut-holomorphic structure on the groupification plus the induced co-holomorphicization. The co-holomorphicization is compatible with logarithms; the monoid structure is not. This is a central warning for later log-link compatibility.
Sources: IUT I, Examples 3.3-3.4 Related: bad nonarch Frobenioids global realified theater theta/log links

IUT I Example 3.3(i): Good Nonarchimedean Base Frobenioids

At a good nonarchimedean valuation v in V_good cap V_non, the local Frobenioid is not built from a Tate theta value. It is built from valuation monoids over the tempered base category of the curve and its base-field subcategory.

Good nonarchimedean base construction IUT I, Example 3.3(i)
Source itemDefinition in Example 3.3(i)Use in the wiki model
D_v B(X_v)^0, with D_v^⊢ = B(K_v)^0 embedded as a full subcategory by pullback from Spec(K_v). Records the same curve/base-field separation used at bad primes.
Phi_Cv For Spec(L) in D_v^⊢, Spec(L) |-> ord(O_L^triangleright)^pf, pulled back from D_v^⊢ to D_v. Provides the divisor monoid for the ambient p-adic Frobenioid.
Phi_Cv^⊢ Spec(L) |-> ord(Z_pv^triangleright), an absolutely primitive submonoid of the ambient valuation monoid. Provides the mono-analytic split side.
F_v^⊢ The element p_v in Z_pv^triangleright determines the characteristic splitting tau_v^⊢ on C_v^⊢. Creates the non-theta split Frobenioid to be compared with the theta version.
Source: IUT I, Example 3.3(i) Related: good formal theta bad-place base category global realified theater

IUT I Example 3.3(ii)-(iii): Formal Theta at Good Nonarchimedean Primes

Good nonarchimedean primes use a formal theta generator. The construction replaces log(p_v) by log(p_v) log(Theta), obtains a split Frobenioid isomorphic to the non-theta split Frobenioid, and then records that the ingredients are category-theoretically recoverable from F_v.

Formal theta replacement at good primes IUT I, Example 3.3(ii)-(iii)
ClaimSource contentWhy it matters later
Formal theta is not a theta value Example 3.3(ii) introduces N log(p_v) log(Theta) and keeps O^x(C_v^Theta) := O^x(C_v^⊢). Prevents importing bad-prime theta-value indeterminacy into good primes.
Split equivalence The resulting F_v^Theta is naturally equivalent to F_v^⊢ as a split Frobenioid. Supplies the good-prime local input for global theta-side packages.
Recoverability Example 3.3(iii) lists category-theoretic reconstructions of D_v^⊢, D_v^Theta, D_v, C_v^⊢, and finally F_v^⊢, F_v^Theta from F_v. Marks which good-prime structures may be transported without choosing coordinates.
Source: IUT I, Example 3.3(ii)-(iii), Remarks 3.3.1-3.3.2 Related: good base Frobenioids bad theta-value indeterminacy Kummer-ready/blind

IUT I Example 3.4(i): Archimedean Aut-Holomorphic Frobenioids

At an archimedean valuation, Mochizuki replaces the p-adic local Frobenioid picture by an Aut-holomorphic orbispace and a complex archimedean topological field. The local object is the triple F_v := (C_v,D_v,kappa_v).

Archimedean local Frobenioid data IUT I, Example 3.4(i)
ObjectExample 3.4(i) contentInterpretation
Aut-holomorphic input The hyperbolic orbicurves at v determine Aut-holomorphic orbispaces and corresponding CAFs A. Archimedean local geometry is encoded topologically/holomorphically.
C_v An archimedean Frobenioid over the one-morphism base category determined by Spec(K_v). Represents the topological monoid O_Kv^triangleright.
kappa_v The topological isomorphism K_v ≃ A_Dv restricts to a monoid inclusion O(C_v) -> A_Dv. This is the archimedean Kummer structure in the local Frobenioid triple.
Source: IUT I, Example 3.4(i) Related: arch Kummer/log good nonarch Frobenioids Frobenioid prime-strips

IUT I Example 3.4(ii)-(iii): Archimedean Splitting, Kummer, and Log Compatibility

The archimedean split side uses the category TM^⊢ of split topological monoids. The noncompact factor is written additively, log(p_v) is a generator, and the theta version again replaces it by a formal log(p_v) log(Theta). Remark 3.4.2 explains why this Kummer structure must be viewed through co-holomorphicization when logarithms are involved.

Archimedean split monoid and log-compatible Kummer reading IUT I, Example 3.4(ii)-(iii), Remark 3.4.2
LayerSource contentPrecision point
TM^⊢ Objects split a topological monoid into a compact unit factor isomorphic to S^1 and a noncompact factor isomorphic to R_ge0. The archimedean split object is a topological monoid object, not a p-adic valuation monoid.
Formal theta log(p_v) generates the noncompact factor; replacing it by log(p_v) log(Theta) defines F_v^Theta, isomorphic to F_v^⊢ after forgetting the formal symbol. This parallels Example 3.3 but in the archimedean topological setting.
Kummer/co-holomorphic reading Remark 3.4.2 identifies kappa_v with an Aut-holomorphic structure on O(C_v)^gp plus a co-holomorphicization to A_Dv. The co-holomorphicization is the log-compatible part; the monoid structure is not.
Archimedean theta approach Remark 3.4.3 views A_Xv = A_Dv through cuspidal inertia and holomorphic elliptic/Belyi cuspidalization. Explains how archimedean reconstruction enters later prime-strip algorithms.
Source: IUT I, Example 3.4(ii)-(iii), Remarks 3.4.2-3.4.3 Related: arch Aut-holomorphic data theta/log links Frobenioid prime-strips

IUT I Example 3.5 and Definition 3.6: Global Realified Data and Theta-Hodge Theaters

Example 3.5 constructs the global realified Frobenioids that compare local divisor-monoid “currencies” across valuations. Definition 3.6 packages the local data and global realified data into a theta-Hodge theater.

Global currency exchange into a theta-Hodge theater IUT I, Example 3.5 and Definition 3.6
Source itemConstructionPurpose
Example 3.5(i) C_mod is the realification of the global Frobenioid over F_mod. Its prime set is identified with V_mod, and each component maps to the realified local divisor monoid by rho_v, with normalization factor [K_v:(F_mod)_v]^-1. Provides the mod-side global comparison currency.
Example 3.5(ii) C_tht is an isomorphic theta-version generated by formal log(Theta). At good places, rho_v^Theta uses formal log(p_v) log(Theta); at bad places, it uses the quotient involving log_Phi(Theta_v) and log_Phi(q_v). Produces F_tht, the source of the theta-link.
Example 3.5(iii), Remark 3.5.1 D_mod and the objects D_v^⊢ form the F_D package, the log-shell version. Mochizuki describes C_mod and C_tht as currency-exchange devices among local divisor monoids. Separates the D/log-shell side from Frobenioid divisor monoids.
Definition 3.6 A theta-Hodge theater is ({F_v}_{v in V}, F_mod). Nonarchimedean local entries are categories equivalent to the source local Frobenioids; archimedean entries are triples (C_v,D_v,kappa_v); the global entry carries C_mod, its prime bijection, local split Frobenioids, and rho_v. This is the first complete theta-Hodge theater object in IUT I.
Sources: IUT I, Example 3.5 and Definition 3.6 Related: bad nonarch Frobenioids good/arch Frobenioids theta/Frobenius pictures Hodge theaters

IUT I Example 3.5(i): Global Realified C_mod

The first global Frobenioid in Example 3.5 is the realification C_mod attached to F_mod and the trivial Galois extension. It packages the local realified divisor monoids into a single global comparison device whose primes are identified with V_mod.

Global mod-side currency exchange IUT I, Example 3.5(i)
ComponentExample 3.5(i) contentWiki consequence
C_mod The realification of the Frobenioid associated to F_mod and the trivial Galois extension. This is the mod-side global Frobenioid used before theta-linking.
Prime indexing Prime(C_mod) is naturally bijective with V_mod, and each component Phi_Cmod,v is a realified local valuation monoid. Global comparison is organized valuation-by-valuation.
rho_v The restriction functor C rho_v : C_mod -> (C_v^⊢)^rlf is determined by a topological monoid isomorphism Phi_Cmod,v -> Phi_Cv^⊢,rlf. Local divisor monoids receive a normalized global currency.
Volume normalization log_mod^⊢(p_v) maps to [K_v:(F_mod)_v]^-1 log_Phi(p_v). The degree factor is part of the data, not an after-the-fact scaling convention.
Source: IUT I, Example 3.5(i) Related: global theta version global realified overview IUT I Frobenioid package

IUT I Example 3.5(ii): Global Theta Version C_tht

The theta-side global Frobenioid C_tht is an isomorphic copy of C_mod generated by a formal log(Theta). Its local restriction maps distinguish good and bad valuations: good places use the formal generator, while bad places use the theta-value quotient from Example 3.2.

Theta-side global currency IUT I, Example 3.5(ii)
CaseExample 3.5(ii) contentRole
Global formal copy Phi_Ctht := Phi_Cmod log(Theta), producing C_tht, a natural equivalence C_mod -> C_tht, and a prime bijection. Creates the theta-side global Frobenioid.
Good valuations rho_v^Theta sends the formal generator to [K_v:(F_mod)_v]^-1 log_Phi(p_v) log(Theta). Matches the formal local theta construction of Example 3.3.
Bad valuations rho_v^Theta uses log_Phi(Theta_v), scaled by log_Phi(p_v)/log_Phi(q_v) and the same local degree factor. Imports the bad-prime theta-value normalization from Example 3.2.
F_tht (C_tht, Prime(C_tht) -> V, {F_v^Theta}, {rho_v^Theta}), naturally isomorphic to F_mod. This package is the source side of the theta link in Corollary 3.7.
Source: IUT I, Example 3.5(ii), Remark 3.5.1(i) Related: global Cmod realification theta links theta value indeterminacy

IUT I Example 3.5(iii): Global D/Log-Shell Version

The D-version is a log-shell version of the same global comparison architecture. It replaces the local Frobenioid divisor monoids by algorithmically reconstructed topological monoids (R_ge0^⊢)_v, with a distinguished Frobenius element normalized differently at nonarchimedean and archimedean places.

D-side/log-shell global package IUT I, Example 3.5(iii)
LayerExample 3.5(iii) contentUse
D_mod A copy of C_mod with corresponding Phi_Dmod, prime bijection, and local generators log_Dmod(p_v). Moves the global comparison to the D/log-shell side.
Nonarchimedean local monoid From D_v^⊢, AbsTopIII gives (R_ge0^⊢)_v with a Frobenius element; multiply by the absolute ramification index e_v. Defines log_DPhi(p_v) at nonarchimedean places.
Archimedean local monoid From D_v^⊢ in TM^⊢, AbsTopIII gives (R_ge0^⊢)_v; divide the Frobenius element by 2pi. Defines the archimedean normalization in the same target monoid type.
F_D (D_mod, Prime(D_mod) -> V, {D_v^⊢}, {rho_v^D}), algorithmically reconstructible from {D_v^⊢}. Provides the log-shell-facing package used by later D-side comparisons.
Source: IUT I, Example 3.5(iii), Remark 3.5.1(ii) Related: global Cmod realification theta/log links log-shells

IUT I Definition 3.6: Theta-Hodge Theater

Definition 3.6 packages the local Frobenioid data and the global mod-side Frobenioid data into the first complete theta-Hodge theater object: HT^Theta = ({F_v}_{v in V}, F_mod). The definition is explicitly typed by valuation kind.

The typed object assembled by Definition 3.6 IUT I, Definition 3.6
ClauseDefinition 3.6 contentFormalization guardrail
(a) nonarchimedean For v in V_non, the entry F_v is a category equivalent to the local Frobenioid category from Examples 3.2 or 3.3. The category alone must carry enough structure to reconstruct the listed local companions.
(b) archimedean For v in V_arc, the entry is a triple (C_v,D_v,kappa_v), with D_v an Aut-holomorphic orbispace and kappa_v the Kummer-structure inclusion. Archimedean entries are not forced into the same type as p-adic Frobenioids.
(c) global package F_mod consists of C_mod, Prime(C_mod) -> V, the local split Frobenioids {F_v^⊢}, and the maps {rho_v}. The theater’s global object is not just a label; it carries local comparison maps.
constructed outputs F_tht and F_D are constructed algorithmically from F_mod. Later theta-link and D-side pages should refer to these outputs, not rebuild them ad hoc.
Source: IUT I, Definition 3.6 and Remarks 3.6.1-3.6.2 Related: global Cmod realification global theta version theta links Hodge theaters

Theta Links and Pictures

Corollary 3.7 defines theta links between theta-Hodge theaters as full poly-isomorphisms from the theta-side global package of one theater to the mod-side global package of another. Corollary 3.8 then chains these links into the order-conscious Frobenius-picture, while Corollary 3.9 extracts an etale-picture core that admits arbitrary label permutations.

Order-conscious theta movement versus etale core IUT I Corollaries 3.7-3.9

Corollary 3.7 theta link

Given two theta-Hodge theaters †HT^Theta and ‡HT^Theta for the same initial theta data, the theta link is the full poly-isomorphism †F_tht -> ‡F_mod. It is asserted nonempty by the definitions and the preceding local/global Frobenioid constructions.

Cor. 3.7(i)poly-isomorphism

No distinguished member

Remark 3.7.1 says there are many distinct isomorphisms in the theta-link poly-isomorphism, and none is distinguished in the way the internal F_mod -> F_tht equivalence of Example 3.5 is. This is why the wiki treats theta links as relations, not chosen maps.

Remark 3.7.1no chosen map

D^⊢ preservation

Corollary 3.7(ii) uses tautological identifications D_v^⊢ -> D_v^Theta on both sides, together with the theta link, to obtain a full poly-isomorphism from †D_v^⊢ to ‡D_v^⊢.

Cor. 3.7(ii)mono-analytic

Unit preservation

Corollary 3.7(iii) similarly obtains a full poly-isomorphism between the unit monoids O^x_{†C_v^⊢} and O^x_{‡C_v^⊢}. This preservation is local and mono-analytic; it is not an identification of full ring histories.

Cor. 3.7(iii)units

Frobenius-picture chain

Corollary 3.8 applies the theta link successively to an integer-indexed family of distinct theaters. The resulting oriented graph admits the translation action of Z, but not arbitrary permutations of adjacent vertices.

Cor. 3.8order-conscious

Theta-to-q cross-theater assignment

Remark 3.8.1 identifies the central feature of the Frobenius-picture: at bad places, n Theta_v is sent to (n+1) q_v. The target value belongs to the next theater, not to the same scheme theory in which the source theta function was constructed.

Remark 3.8.1(i)bad primes

Order-conscious structure

The Frobenius-picture is Frobenius-like because its divisor-monoid portion carries an ordered chain structure. Swapping two adjacent theaters while fixing the rest is not an automorphism of the oriented graph.

Cor. 3.8Frobenius-like

Etale-picture core

Corollary 3.9 extracts the diagrams centered on D_v^⊢. Unlike the Frobenius-picture, this etale-picture admits arbitrary permutations of theater labels. Remark 3.9.1 identifies D_v^⊢ as the mono-analytic core.

Cor. 3.9(i)core

Units as temporary invariant

Corollary 3.9(ii) adds local units to the etale-picture. Remark 3.9.2 warns that these units cease to be constant invariants after later enhancements involving the rotation of addition and multiplication in IUT III.

Cor. 3.9(ii)temporary

Teichmuller geodesic analogy

Remark 3.9.3 compares the mono-analytic core to a fixed real-analytic surface with varying holomorphic structures. Units correspond to the fixed dimension, while local value groups undergo theta-dilations along the Frobenius-picture.

Remark 3.9.3analogy

Distinct scheme theories

Remark 3.9.4 distinguishes the theta link from ordinary evaluation of a theta function at a torsion point. The value (n+1) q_v belongs to the next theta-Hodge theater's scheme theory, while n Theta_v was constructed over the preceding theater.

Remark 3.9.4not evaluation

Formalization rule

A formal model should represent the Frobenius-picture and etale-picture as different structures on the same indexed family: one order-conscious and one permutation-symmetric after passing to the mono-analytic core.

Lean guardrailpictures
Source consequence: the theta link is not merely a conventional evaluation map inside one theater. Its target lies in the adjacent theater's scheme theory, while only the mono-analytic core supplies the permutation-symmetric etale-picture.
Sources: IUT I Related: IUT I Frobenioid package Hodge theaters basepoints/inter-universality theta/log links Teichmuller analogy

IUT I Corollary 3.7: Theta Links

Corollary 3.7 is the first explicit horizontal move between distinct theta-Hodge theaters. It starts with two theaters over the same initial theta data, forms a full poly-isomorphism from the theta-side global package of the first theater to the mod-side global package of the second, and records exactly which mono-analytic structures are preserved.

Theta link interface full poly-isomorphism plus mono-analytic preservation
Source itemPaper contentWiki/formalization consequence
Inputs Two theta-Hodge theaters †HT^Theta and ‡HT^Theta attached to the same initial theta data. The page treats the link as a cross-theater construction, not as internal self-map.
Cor. 3.7(i) A nonempty full poly-isomorphism †F_tht ~> ‡F_mod, called the theta link. Use relation/poly-map language; do not choose a privileged representative.
Cor. 3.7(ii) For each v in V, the tautological D_v^⊢ ~ D_v^Theta identifications give a full poly-isomorphism †D_v^⊢ ~> ‡D_v^⊢. The mono-analytic core is the first preserved layer across the link.
Cor. 3.7(iii) The same procedure gives a full poly-isomorphism of unit monoids O^x_{†C_v^⊢} ~> O^x_{‡C_v^⊢}. Unit preservation is local and mono-analytic; it is not a scheme-history equality.
Remark 3.7.1 There are many distinct isomorphisms in the theta-link poly-isomorphism, with no distinguished member analogous to the internal F_mod ~ F_tht equivalence. The wiki marks the theta link as an indeterminate relation rather than a selected function.
Guardrail: a theta link should not be rendered as a conventional function between two ringed spaces. Mochizuki's statement is a full poly-isomorphism between theater packages, with preserved mono-analytic outputs.
Sources: IUT I, Corollary 3.7 and Remark 3.7.1 Related: global realified theater Frobenius-picture 3.8 etale-picture 3.9 Collas category view

IUT I Corollary 3.8: Frobenius-Picture

Corollary 3.8 repeats the theta link along an integer-indexed family of distinct theta-Hodge theaters. The resulting Frobenius-picture is an oriented chain: it admits translation by Z, but not arbitrary permutations of adjacent theaters.

Order-conscious chain of theaters theta at one label maps to q at the next label
Source itemPaper contentWiki/formalization consequence
Cor. 3.8 An integer-indexed family {nHT^Theta}_{n in Z} is linked successively by Corollary 3.7. Represent as a labeled chain, not as an unordered collection of theaters.
Graph The displayed graph has arrows ... -> (n-1)HT^Theta -> nHT^Theta -> (n+1)HT^Theta -> .... Translation by Z is allowed; arbitrary permutation symmetry is not.
Remark 3.8.1(i) At bad places, the theta link maps nTheta_v to (n+1)q_v. The value lives in the next theater's scheme theory.
Remark 3.8.1(ii) The Frobenius-like part comes from divisor monoids, while D_v^⊢ and units are invariant up to isomorphism at this stage. Keep divisor/value-group movement separate from mono-analytic preservation.
Remark 3.8.1(iii)-(iv) Across labels, quotient group or topological-field isomorphs are related by indeterminate isomorphisms that obliterate the ring or holomorphic structure attached to a particular label. The page records the exact place where scheme-history erasure begins.
The Frobenius-picture is order-conscious. The etale-picture of Corollary 3.9 is extracted by passing to the mono-analytic core; its permutation symmetry should not be imported back into the Frobenius-picture.
Sources: IUT I, Corollary 3.8 and Remark 3.8.1 Related: theta links 3.7 etale-picture 3.9 theta-to-q cross-theater assignment mutually alien copies

IUT I Corollary 3.9: Etale-Picture Core

Corollary 3.9 extracts a canonical splitting of the Frobenius-picture by centering the comparison at the mono-analytic object D_v^⊢. This etale-picture admits arbitrary permutations of labels, and its unit-enhanced version is explicitly temporary: IUT III later destroys this invariance when addition and multiplication are rotated.

Permutation-symmetric mono-analytic core D^⊢ hub, optional unit layer, and later warning
Source itemPaper contentWiki/formalization consequence
Cor. 3.9(i) The diagrams centered on D_v^⊢ give an etale-picture that admits arbitrary permutations of the labels n in Z. Encode the etale-picture as the permutation-symmetric core, distinct from the ordered Frobenius-picture.
Cor. 3.9(ii) A second etale-picture includes the pair (D_v^⊢, O^x_{C_v^⊢}). At nonarchimedean places this is a category plus a group-like monoid; at archimedean places it is an object of TM^⊢ plus a topological group. Keep the unit-enhanced core typed by place class.
Remark 3.9.1 D_v^⊢ is identified with the mono-analytic core; the notation ⊢ abbreviates this mono-analytic viewpoint. The page uses “core” only for this mono-analytic layer.
Remark 3.9.2 The unit-group invariance of Corollary 3.9(ii) does not persist in later enhanced Frobenius-pictures of IUT III. Do not treat unit preservation in IUT I as a global invariant of the whole theory.
Remark 3.9.3 Mochizuki compares the situation to a fixed real-analytic surface carrying varying holomorphic structures; value groups move by theta-dilations while units resemble a fixed dimension. The Teichmuller analogy is explanatory, not a substitute definition.
Remark 3.9.4 The assignment nTheta_v -> (n+1)q_v is not ordinary evaluation inside one scheme theory: source theta and target q-value belong to adjacent theaters. This is the core non-identification rule feeding later 3.11-3.12 diagnostics.
Collas's category-theoretic reading is useful here: the etale-picture behaves like a reconstruction-stable categorical container, while the Frobenius-picture retains the ordered multiplicative movement. The source definitions remain Mochizuki's Corollaries 3.7-3.9.
Sources: IUT I, Corollary 3.9 and Remarks 3.9.1-3.9.4 Related: theta links 3.7 Frobenius-picture 3.8 Collas category view 3.12 diagnostics

IUT I Coverings and Symmetries

Before Hodge theaters are built, IUT I constructs a web of finite etale coverings of the initial elliptic-curve and orbicurve data. The important point is that two different finite-field symmetries appear: the multiplicative symmetry F_l^* modulo sign on the orbicurve side, and the additive signed symmetry F_l ⋊ {±1} on the elliptic side. Their mismatch is the source of much of the later bookkeeping.

Coverings before theaters finite etale covers, local covers, and two symmetry sources

From X_F to C_F

The initial elliptic curve gives the once-punctured curve X_F. Quotienting by the natural {±1}-action gives the hyperbolic orbicurve C_F. This is the curve/orbicurve base on which the first Hodge-theater data is built.

IUT I Introorbicurve

Degree-l cover of C_K

The initial data determines a finite etale degree-l cover of C_K. It is controlled by a rank-one quotient of the l-torsion module of E_K, and at bad places it matches the quotient coming from the loop in the special-fiber dual graph.

IUT I §I1l-torsion

Canonical cusp datum

The same construction determines a cusp epsilon of the cover. At bad places it corresponds to the canonical generator, up to sign, of the quotient determined by the unique loop of the special-fiber dual graph.

IUT I Def. 3.1cusp

Bad-place theta cover

For v in V_bad, IUT I obtains a local finite etale cover X_v -> X_v by extracting l-th roots of the theta function. These are the bad-place covers later feeding theta evaluation.

IUT I §I1theta roots

Good-place epsilon cover

For v in V_good, the analogous local cover is determined by the distinguished cusp epsilon. Thus good and bad places enter the same prime-strip machinery through different covering origins.

IUT I §I1good places

Multiplicative symmetry

The quotient F_l^* / {±1} acts as an arithmetic poly-symmetry on C_K. Mochizuki presents it as induced through Galois action on the rank-one quotient, hence suited for descent toward the field of moduli.

F_l^*arithmetic

Additive signed symmetry

The group F_l ⋊ {±1} acts as a geometric poly-symmetry on X_K, through automorphisms of the scheme and its cusps. This side is later used for additive label bookkeeping and conjugate synchronization.

F_l ⋊ ±geometric

Symmetry failure on local covers

IUT I stresses that the global multiplicative symmetry only extends to a trivial remnant on the local covers, while the global additive signed symmetry only extends to a sign remnant. This is why local covers cannot be treated as if they carried the full global symmetry.

Fig. I1.1local loss

Conjugate synchronization

The additive signed symmetry can permute copies of local Galois groups associated to distinct labels without introducing conjugacy indeterminacies. Mochizuki calls this conjugate synchronization and uses it later in Hodge-Arakelov theta evaluation.

IUT I §I1synchronization

Descent-oriented symmetry

The multiplicative symmetry is better suited to descending from K to F_mod, especially for reconstructing the number field from the etale fundamental group of C_K. That descent comes with global Galois permutation and conjugacy ambiguity.

F_moddescent

Finite-field disentangling

Mochizuki treats large l as making arithmetic in F_l roughly approximate arithmetic in Z. The split between additive and multiplicative finite-field symmetries is therefore the first approximation to separating additive and multiplicative ring structures.

IUT I §I1add/mult split

Prime-strip taxonomy source

Figure I1.2 introduces the main prime-strip types: D, D⊢, F, F⊢, later weakened F⊢× and F⊢×μ forms, and realified F⊩ variants. The taxonomy is built from this covering-and-symmetry layer, not added afterward.

Fig. I1.2taxonomy
Formalization warning: the two symmetries should not be encoded as one generic label action. The multiplicative side is arithmetic and descent-oriented; the additive signed side is geometric and supports conjugate synchronization.
Sources: IUT I, Introduction §I1 Related: initial theta data Hodge theaters prime strips bridge theory

Cyclic Cover Reconstruction

IUT I Section 1 constructs arrow-decorated cyclic covers of once-punctured elliptic curves and proves that the relevant covering and cusp data are characteristic in the arithmetic fundamental group. This page records the source theorem interface behind later uses of cusp labels and anabelian reconstruction.

From cusp data to characteristic cyclic covers IUT I §1, Definition 1.1 and Corollary 1.2

Torsion orbicurve setup

Section 1 starts with l >= 5 prime to 6, a type (1,1) hyperbolic curve X, and a type (1,l-tors)^± hyperbolic orbicurve C over a characteristic-zero field k. Their common core produces a type (1,l-tors) cover.

IUT I §1setup

Epsilon quotient exact sequence

A nonzero cusp epsilon of C has two inverse-image cusps epsilon' and epsilon'' on X. Quotienting the geometric fundamental group by the inertia images of the other nonzero cusps gives an exact sequence with inertia term and elliptic l-torsion quotient.

IUT I §1cusps

Plus/minus eigenspace cover

The involution from Gal(X/C) switches the two cusp inertia factors and acts by -1 on the elliptic quotient. Since l is odd, this yields plus and minus eigenspaces and quotient groups J_X and J_C.

IUT I §1eigenspaces

The 2 epsilon section

The cusp 2 epsilon determines a section G_k -> J_C. The condition 2 != ±1 mod l and the triviality hypothesis on the relevant mod-l action make the resulting image normal and place it inside J_X.

IUT I §1section

Arrow torsion types

Definition 1.1 names the resulting covers as type (1,l-tors arrow) and (1,l-tors arrow)^±. The arrow records the ordered-label information determined by the chosen nonzero cusp epsilon.

Definition 1.1arrow labels

Cyclic Galois groups

The construction gives finite etale cyclic coverings with Galois groups Z/lZ, Z/2Z, and Z/2lZ in the indicated curve/orbicurve tower. These finite symmetry packages later become label and cusp data.

IUT I §1cyclic covers

Corollary 1.2 reconstruction

For k an NF or MLF, Corollary 1.2 asserts a functorial group-theoretic algorithm that reconstructs the relevant arithmetic fundamental groups and cusp decomposition classes from the arrow cover's fundamental group.

Corollary 1.2characteristic

Automorphism consequence

Remark 1.2.1 records that the automorphism groups of the arrow covers over k are the expected cyclic Galois groups. This is another way the cover construction becomes rigid enough for later reconstruction.

Remark 1.2.1automorphisms

Formalization rule

A Lean model should separate the cover construction, the section from 2 epsilon, and the Corollary 1.2 reconstruction algorithm. Treating the cusp labels as arbitrary names loses the characteristic group-theoretic content of this section.

Lean guardrailreconstruction
Sources: IUT I Related: IUT I coverings/symmetries cusp/edge/vertex recognizers tempered covering complements initial theta data anabelian background

Epsilon Quotient in IUT I §1

The cyclic-cover construction starts from a nonzero cusp epsilon of the signed torsion orbicurve C. Its two inverse-image cusps on X determine a quotient of the mod- ell geometric fundamental group that isolates the selected cusp pair from the other nonzero cusps.

Selected cusp pair and quotient group IUT I §1 before Definition 1.1
ItemSource roleWhy it matters
Chosen cusp A nonzero cusp epsilon of the type (1,ell-tors)^± orbicurve. It fixes the ordered-label source used by the arrow notation.
Lifted cusps The two cusps epsilon', epsilon'' of X lying over epsilon. Their inertia groups form the retained cusp term in the quotient.
Quotient group The group Delta_epsilon, obtained from Delta_X^{ab} tensor Z/ellZ by inertia from the other nonzero cusps. It is the group on which the involution later yields plus/minus eigenspaces.
The quotient is not a generic mod-ell abelianization. Its definition depends on retaining exactly the two cusp inertia images over the chosen epsilon and killing the other nonzero cusp inertia images.
Sources: IUT I §1 Related: cyclic cover reconstruction iota eigenspaces arrow torsion types

Iota Eigenspaces in IUT I §1

The nontrivial element iota of Gal(X/C) switches the two selected cusp inertia factors and acts as multiplication by -1 on the elliptic ell-torsion quotient. Because ell is odd, this gives a plus/minus decomposition of Delta_epsilon.

Involution action on the epsilon quotient IUT I §1

Plus direction

The selected cusp inertia maps isomorphically to the plus eigenspace. Mochizuki uses this plus quotient to define later quotient groups J_X and J_C.

Minus direction

The elliptic compactification quotient is where the involution acts by -1. This separation depends on ell being odd.

Formalization should record the involution action and the odd-prime hypothesis; the plus/minus splitting is a theorem about this action, not a free decomposition label.
Sources: IUT I §1 Related: epsilon quotient 2 epsilon section cyclic cover reconstruction

The 2 Epsilon Section in IUT I §1

After the plus quotient has been formed, the cusp 2 epsilon of C determines a section G_k -> J_C. The assumptions ell >= 5 and triviality of the relevant mod- ell Galois action ensure that the section image is determined by this cusp and is normal in J_C.

From the doubled cusp to cyclic arrow covers IUT I §1
StepSource constraintOutput
Choose 2 epsilon ell >= 5, hence 2 != +/-1 mod ell. A cusp distinct enough to determine the required section.
Form sigma : G_k -> J_C The relevant outer Galois action is trivial by the standing hypothesis. The image is determined by 2 epsilon and normal in J_C.
Use the section image The section image lies in J_X. Finite etale cyclic arrow-cover diagrams with specified Galois groups.
Sources: IUT I §1 Related: iota eigenspaces arrow torsion types initial theta data

Arrow Torsion Types in IUT I Definition 1.1

Definition 1.1 names the cyclic covers constructed from the preceding epsilon-quotient and section machinery. A hyperbolic orbicurve is of type (1,ell-tors arrow), or signed type (1,ell-tors arrow)^±, when it arises from that construction for some ell and chosen nonzero cusp.

Definition by construction, not by informal decoration IUT I Definition 1.1 and Remarks 1.1.1-1.1.2

Definition 1.1

The phrase "type (1,ell-tors arrow)" refers to arising, up to isomorphism, from Mochizuki's cyclic covering construction.

Remark 1.1.1

The arrow records the archimedean ordered labels 1,2,... on the relevant {+/-1}-orbits, as determined by epsilon.

Remark 1.1.2

The arrow covers are determined, up to k isomorphism, by the source triple (X/k, C, epsilon).

The arrow is not decorative notation. It records ordered label data produced by the chosen cusp and therefore belongs to the typed source object used later by initial theta data and Hodge-theater labels.
Sources: IUT I Definition 1.1 Related: 2 epsilon section cyclic cover reconstruction initial theta data D-prime strips and labels

IUT I Remarks 1.2.2-1.2.3: Cusp, Edge, and Vertex Recognizers

After Corollary 1.2, Mochizuki pauses to repair background anabelian algorithms for recognizing cusps, nodal edges, and vertices. This is not incidental errata: the later Hodge-theater and tempered-covering machinery relies on group-theoretic recognition of precisely these pieces.

Recognizer repair route Remarks 1.2.2 and 1.2.3
Source itemPaper contentWiki/formalization consequence
Remark 1.2.2(i) For cyclic finite etale covers of degree a power of ell, total ramification at some cusp is detected by a degree-ell factor and an inequality involving the number of cusps. The condition extends to pro-ell cyclic covers. Cusp inertia is recognized through a test on coverings, not by naming a cusp externally.
Remark 1.2.2(ii) Decomposition groups of cusps are characterized as maximal closed subgroups isomorphic to Z_ell satisfying the cyclotomic-dimension inequality for characteristic open subgroups. The formal interface should include the maximality condition, the Z_ell type, and the inequality criterion.
Remark 1.2.3(i)-(iii) A Galois finite etale covering is understood as connected. Several unnecessary descent and purely ramified clauses in the background CombGC definitions are removed. Cuspidal total ramification is then characterized by the degree-ell factor inequality. The corrected notion is narrower and cleaner than the earlier background phrasing; the wiki should not retain the discarded clauses.
Remark 1.2.3(iv) Cuspidal edge-like subgroups are maximal closed Z_ell-subgroups such that every characteristic finite quotient gives a cyclic cover totally ramified at a cusp. Vertices are characterized through maximal elementary abelian quotients of the unramified abelianization. Edge and vertex recovery are separate criteria. A later graph-cover page should not collapse both into a single "graph is reconstructible" slogan.
Remark 1.2.3(v) Nodal edge-like subgroups in the noncuspidal case are characterized analogously by cyclic covers that are nodally totally ramified, equivalent to being module-wise nodal. Nodal data has its own ramification recognizer, parallel to but distinct from cuspidal edge recognition.
Remark 1.2.3(vi)-(vii) The corrected arguments for CombGC Theorem 1.6 follow formally from the repaired characterizations of cuspidal edge-like subgroups, unramified verticial subgroups, and the associated total-ramification tests. The repair is intended to close dependency gaps in the background theorem used by later semi-graph and tempered-covering arguments.

Total-ramification recognizer

The operative test is not geometric intuition about a visible puncture. It is a group-theoretic condition on cyclic covers, their degree-ell factor, and the resulting cusp-count inequality.

Remark 1.2.2cyclic covers

Maximal Z_ell condition

Cusp decomposition groups are characterized among maximal closed Z_ell-subgroups. This maximality is part of the theorem interface and should not be omitted in a formal model.

Remark 1.2.2maximality

Connected Galois cover convention

Remark 1.2.3 explicitly says that "Galois" finite etale covering is to be understood as connected in the repaired CombGC discussion.

Remark 1.2.3(i)convention

Cuspidal edge-like subgroup

A cuspidal edge-like subgroup is recovered by checking that characteristic quotients produce cyclic covers that are cuspidally totally ramified. The proof of group-theoretic cuspidality is then formal from this criterion.

Remark 1.2.3(iv),(vi)cusps

Unramified verticial subgroup

Vertex recovery is handled through the unramified abelianization and maximal elementary abelian quotients that isolate one vertex component of the verticial part.

Remark 1.2.3(iv),(vii)vertices

Nodal edge-like subgroup

In the noncuspidal case, nodal edge-like subgroups are recovered by nodally totally ramified cyclic coverings, equivalently by the module-wise nodal condition.

Remark 1.2.3(v)nodes
Formalization warning: any Lean or wiki interface that says only "cusps and vertices are group-theoretically reconstructible" is too coarse for this source block. The paper specifies ramification tests, maximal subgroup criteria, and quotient criteria.
Sources: IUT I, Remarks 1.2.2-1.2.3 Related: cyclic cover reconstruction tempered covering complements Galois-countability repairs anabelian background

ThetaNF Frobenioid Lift

IUT I Section 5 lifts the D-level bridge formalism of Section 4 to Frobenioid-prime-strips. The result is the ThetaNF-Hodge theater: an NF-bridge, a Theta-bridge, and a common capsule of F-prime-strips whose associated D-bridges form the D-ThetaNF-Hodge theater.

From D-bridges to ThetaNF theaters Section 5 adds Frobenioid data without losing the D-level dynamics
IUT I Section 5 item What is added Why it matters
Example 5.1 Group-theoretic reconstruction of global Frobenioid data attached to D_o, including field/unit data and the F_mod subfield obtained by invariants. The number-field side is not inserted as an untyped field copy; it is recovered from the D-side anabelian input and then repackaged Frobenioid-theoretically.
Example 5.4 Cusp-label classes determine delta-valuations and hence localized F-prime-strips F|delta. The same example constructs pivotal distributions for the label j=1. Section 4 label synchronization becomes a localization mechanism for Frobenioids; this is where labels begin to control arithmetic-degree bookkeeping.
Remark 5.4.2 Log-volumes of F_*_J may be read either as averages over labels or as sums, depending on whether one normalizes by the cardinality of the label set. The later height inequalities depend on keeping these normalization conventions explicit instead of silently identifying all arithmetic degrees.
Definition 5.5(i) An NF-bridge is a capsule of F-prime-strips F_J, Frobenioid categories F_o and F_*, and a poly-morphism lifting a D-NF bridge. It is the Frobenioid lift of the number-field bridge. Its associated D-NF bridge remains the controlling lower-level object.
Definition 5.5(ii) A Theta-bridge is a capsule F_J, an F-prime-strip F_> tautologically associated to a Theta-Hodge theater, and poly-morphisms determined by a D-Theta bridge. It lifts theta-side comparison to Frobenioid data while keeping the source of the morphisms in the D-Theta bridge.
Definition 5.5(iii) A ThetaNF-Hodge theater glues an NF-bridge and a Theta-bridge with the same F-prime-strip capsule, requiring the associated D-data to form a D-ThetaNF-Hodge theater. The construction is not a new independent theater type; it is the Frobenioid lift of the Section 4 D-ThetaNF package.
Corollary 5.6 Isomorphisms of Theta-Hodge theaters, NF-bridges, Theta-bridges, and ThetaNF-Hodge theaters are controlled by the associated D-prime-strip or D-bridge isomorphisms; compatible gluings form an F_l-torsor. The Frobenioid lift preserves the functorial dynamics of Section 4. This is the source-level justification for modeling the lifted layer as a controlled extension of D-level bridge data.

Global Frobenioid reconstruction

Example 5.1 reconstructs field and unit data from pi_1(D_o), extends the action to the reconstructed D_*, and obtains the F_mod part by invariants.

Example 5.1anabelian

Kappa-coric functions

Section 5 uses kappa-coric rational-function pseudo-monoids and their nonzero variants to recover function data from the orbicurve side. At strictly critical points, constant multiples split into coric functions and constants up to roots of unity.

Example 5.1coric data

Kummer-ready rigidity

Corollary 5.3 and Remark 5.3.2 state that F-prime-strip isomorphisms are rigid over their D-side data because the Frobenioid package is Kummer-ready. The corresponding mono-analytic weakened strips are Kummer-blind.

Cor. 5.3rigidity

F_mod prime-strip

In the introductory summary of Section 5, the natural realified F-prime-strip F_mod is built from 2l-th roots of bad-place q-parameters and compatible good-place data satisfying the product formula.

Def. 5.2(iv)q-parameters

Theta prime-strip

Replacing those q-parameter roots by reciprocals of l-th roots of the Frobenioid-theoretic theta function Theta_v gives the theta-side F-prime-strip used in the source theta link.

IUT I Intro §I1Theta_v

Theta link source definition

The full poly-isomorphism from the theta-side realified F-prime-strip to the F_mod realified F-prime-strip is the theta link. It relates Frobenioid-theoretic data, not ordinary ring or scheme structures.

Cor. 3.7poly-isomorphism

Unit-level preservation

The theta link also induces a full poly-isomorphism on the associated F⊢× prime-strips. This is the source-level unit/mono-analytic preservation statement, not a preservation statement for full arithmetic holomorphic structures.

Cor. 3.7F⊢×

NF bridge

Definition 5.5(i) packages F_J, F_o, and F_* together with a lifting poly-morphism. The lift is unique over the D-NF bridge in the sense supplied by Corollary 5.3.

Def. 5.5(i)NF side

Theta bridge

Definition 5.5(ii) packages F_J, F_>, and a Theta-Hodge theater. Its constituent poly-morphisms are determined by the corresponding D-Theta bridge.

Def. 5.5(ii)Theta side

ThetaNF-Hodge theater

Definition 5.5(iii) glues the NF and Theta bridges. Morphisms must be compatible in the concrete sense that they induce the same bijection between the two capsule index sets.

Def. 5.5(iii)gluing

Frobenius-picture chain

Iterating theta-linked ThetaNF-Hodge theaters gives the integer-indexed Frobenius-picture. Mochizuki emphasizes that this chain does not admit arbitrary adjacent permutation symmetries among its links.

Cor. 3.8chain

Etale-picture core

Passing to the associated D⊢ prime-strips yields a mono-analytic core that is invariant up to isomorphism. This is the etale-picture setting where spokes can be split and permuted.

IUT I Intro §I1mono-analytic
Formalization warning: Section 5 should not be represented by a single generic “bridge” type. The NF-bridge and Theta-bridge share an F-prime-strip capsule, but their source obligations differ, and Corollary 5.6 reads their isomorphisms through the associated D-level bridges.
Sources: IUT I, Section 5 Related: IUT I coverings/symmetries Hodge theaters Frobenioids/log-shells prime strips theta/log links

Hodge Theaters

A Hodge theater is a miniature arithmetic universe built from the initial data. Mochizuki describes these theaters as models in which the additive and multiplicative dimensions of a number field are disentangled enough to support comparisons unavailable in ordinary scheme theory.

Separated dimensions one theater keeps linked data typed, not globally identified

The phrase "alien arithmetic holomorphic structure" refers to viewing data from the standpoint of a theater related by a non-ring/scheme-theoretic link. This is why the theory has to track histories, labels, and comparison levels explicitly.

Hodge-theater layer Source description Formalization meaning
Theta±ell side Tracks F_l^±-symmetry on labels -l < ... < -1 < 0 < 1 < ... < l using finite etale coverings of XK. Source of additive plus/minus label bookkeeping and conjugate synchronization constraints.
ThetaNF side Tracks F_l-symmetry on labels 1 < ... < l using finite etale coverings of CK. Source of number-field descent and Fmod-reconstruction bookkeeping.
Gluing The combined Theta±ellNF-Hodge theater is obtained by gluing the two sides along D-prime-strips, identifying 0 != ±t with the corresponding j. Prevents additive and multiplicative labels from being treated as one undifferentiated index.
D-Hodge theater The associated D-theater is the system of base categories attached to the Frobenioid system. Provides the etale/mono-analytic comparison layer used by later prime-strip and log-link constructions.
Frobenius and etale pictures Integer-indexed chains of theta-linked theaters form the Frobenius-picture; D-theaters share a mono-analytic core in the etale-picture. The wiki and Lean interfaces must distinguish same-type theaters from same-history theaters.
IUT I source item Theater/link data Why the distinction matters
Definition 3.6(a)-(b) A theta-Hodge theater has local Frobenioid data {F_v}_{v in V}. Nonarchimedean components are categories equivalent to the local Frobenioid categories; archimedean components also carry Aut-holomorphic orbispaces and Kummer structures. Local objects are not bare valuations: they carry the structure from which the D, D⊢, DTheta, F⊢, and FTheta variants are reconstructed.
Definition 3.6(c) The global part Fmod consists of a Frobenioid category Cmod, a prime-to-valuation bijection, the local F⊢_v data, and comparison maps rho_v between global and local realified divisor monoids. This is the global currency-exchange device: it relates local divisor monoids without restoring one global ring-history across all later links.
Definition 3.6(c), Example 3.5 From Fmod one algorithmically constructs Ftht and F_D, the theta-side and D-side objects used in theta-Hodge-theater comparison. The first theta link is an isomorphism between these constructed collections of data, not an isomorphism of schemes.
Corollary 3.7(i) For two theta-Hodge theaters, the full poly-isomorphism Ftht -> Fmod is nonempty and is called the theta link. The link has no distinguished member. This is the first source-level warning that later comparison must track poly-isomorphisms and indeterminacy, not a canonical transport map.
Corollary 3.7(ii)-(iii) The theta link preserves the relevant D⊢ data and the unit groups O^x after composing with the tautological D⊢ <-> DTheta identifications. Preservation lives at the mono-analytic/unit level. It is not preservation of the full arithmetic holomorphic structure.

Theta-Hodge theaters

IUT I Section 3 constructs chains of theta-Hodge theaters from the initial theta data. These are the first theater objects whose scheme-theoretic histories will later be separated by theta links.

Definition 3.6theta theater

Theta links

Corollary 3.7 defines theta links between theta-Hodge theaters. Corollary 3.8 organizes integer-indexed chains of theta-linked theaters into the Frobenius-picture.

Cor. 3.7-3.8Frobenius-picture

Theta-link preservation

The first theta link preserves D⊢ and O^x through the tautological theta-side identifications. It does not identify the two theaters as the same arithmetic holomorphic universe.

Cor. 3.7(ii)-(iii)mono-analytic

Fmod global Frobenioid

The object Fmod packages the global Frobenioid category, its prime set, local mono-analytic Frobenioid data, and the realified comparison maps used to relate local divisor monoids.

Definition 3.6(c)global

Ftht theta target

Ftht is constructed algorithmically from Fmod and serves as the theta-side collection of data in the source theta link Ftht -> Fmod.

Example 3.5theta link

D and D⊢ layers

The D-prime-strip layer records holomorphic base data, while its mono-analyticization D⊢ supplies the coric interface that can be compared without retaining the same ring history.

D-prime-stripsmono-analytic

ThetaNF theaters

IUT I Section 5 glues NF-bridges and theta-bridges into thetaNF-Hodge theaters. This is where Frobenioid-prime-strips and global realified monoid data enter the theater package.

Definition 5.5NF + theta

Theta±ell theaters

Section 6 adds the additive theta-plus-minus and theta-ell structures. The F_l^± torsor language controls the plus/minus cusp-label symmetries needed for the additive side.

Definition 6.11additive

Theta±ellNF theaters

Definition 6.13 glues the theta±ell theater to the thetaNF theater along the compatible bridge data. This combined object is the main Hodge-theater input for the later theta/log-link and log-theta-lattice machinery.

Definition 6.13combined theater
Sources: IUT I Panoramic Overview Related: theta/log links multiplicative/additive bridges dispute

Mutually Alien Copies

In Mochizuki's survey, "alien" means abstract otherness: two copies of the same kind of mathematical world are held apart so that a simultaneous comparison can be made through weaker structures. The phrase is therefore a bookkeeping constraint on comparison, not a claim that the copies have different internal theorems.

Two-copy comparison distinct labels make simultaneous gluing meaningful

Two-copy method

The survey begins from the Gaussian integral: squaring the integral introduces two identical but independent copies. In IUT, the analogous move is to use two copies of conventional scheme theory around the same initial theta data, then compare them through weak common structures.

Alien §1.3two copies

Gaussian integral analogy

The survey aligns the Gaussian calculation with IUT: introduce alien copies, pass through two-dimensional coordinate changes, split value-group and unit-group directions, evaluate the radial portion by quadraticity, and control the angular portion by logarithmic unit geometry.

Alien introradial/angular

q-power model

The introductory model identifies the left-side family q^{j^2} with the right-side q through the theta-link. The desired height bound comes from computing the theta-side data in terms of the q-side, modulo the permitted indeterminacies.

Alien introq^j^2

Mono-anabelian transport

Section 2.7 describes links as walls or filters between mutually alien scheme-theoretic copies. Frobenius-like objects live on one side of a link, while mono-anabelian transport reconstructs selected monoid or group data in forms that can cross the wall.

Alien §2.7transport

Closed-loop prime-strip

Section 3.11 explains that the multiradial algorithm must produce a closed loop on a single weakened F⊢×μ-prime-strip. A mere open path from one non-comparable object type to another would not yield an intrinsic numerical conclusion.

Alien §3.11closed loop

AND versus OR model

Examples 3.11.3-3.11.4 give elementary combinatorial and numerical models of the risk: the theta-link is meaningful as a simultaneous AND relation between distinct labeled copies; replacing it by OR makes the distinct copies look redundant and the final estimate appear unlicensed.

Alien §3.11∧/∨
Alien-copy featureSource roleConnection to other wiki pages
Distinct copies Keep simultaneous theta-link assignments well typed. logical structure, Hodge theaters
Weakened interface Use multiplicative monoids, profinite groups, and prime-strips instead of ring isomorphisms. prime-strips, theta/log links
Value/unit decoupling Separate radial/value-group and angular/unit-group portions. multiplicative/additive bridges, log-theta lattice
Closed loop Return to comparable q-intertwined data before taking log-volume. Corollary 3.12 corridor, indeterminacies
For formalization, the key engineering lesson is that "same mathematical kind" and "same labeled copy" must remain separate. Collapsing those two notions is exactly the simplification that the alien-copy exposition warns against.
Sources: Mutually Alien Copies Panoramic Overview Related: logical structure theta and log links multiradiality Corollary 3.12

IUT I Definition 4.1 and Proposition 4.2: D-Prime Strips and Labels

Section 4 begins by isolating the D-side, or base-category side, of later Hodge-theater enhancements. A D-prime-strip is a valuation-indexed package of local D-data; from each local component Mochizuki reconstructs cusp label classes, then Proposition 4.2 identifies these labels across all valuations.

Local D-data to global label torsor IUT I Definition 4.1 and Proposition 4.2
Source itemPaper contentWiki consequence
Def. 4.1(i) A holomorphic base-prime-strip D = {D_v}_{v in V} has nonarchimedean entries equivalent to the local D-categories and archimedean entries given by Aut-holomorphic orbispaces. Prime-strips are place-indexed local objects, not bare labels.
Def. 4.1(ii) From each D_v, one reconstructs cusp label classes LabCusp(D_v), an F_l^x/{±1}-torsor with a canonical element determined by epsilon_v. The label set is reconstructed from local structure; it is not an external index set.
Def. 4.1(iii)-(iv) A mono-analytic D^⊢-prime-strip is defined separately, and any D-prime-strip has a natural mono-analyticization. The D and D^⊢ layers must remain separately typed.
Prop. 4.2 The local LabCusp(D_v) torsors are canonically identified across valuations by compatibility with epsilon classes and torsor structures, giving LabCusp(D). This is the first Section 4 global label synchronization mechanism.
Remark 4.2.1 If the good-place local object were defined by C_v rather than the arrow-decorated X_v data, these bijections need not exist. The source choice at good places is mathematically active, not cosmetic.
Formalization guardrail: do not collapse Section 4 labels into a generic finite type. The paper reconstructs labels from cusp data and then proves a canonical cross-place torsor identification.
Sources: IUT I, Definition 4.1, Proposition 4.2, Remark 4.2.1 Related: multiplicative combinatorics local analytic sections theta evaluation sections prime strips and bridges

IUT I Definition 4.1(i): D-Prime-Strip

A holomorphic base-prime-strip is the D-side local package used before the Frobenioid enhancements of Section 5. It is a valuation-indexed family D = {D_v}_{v in V}, with different local types at nonarchimedean and archimedean valuations.

Valuation-indexed D-side local data IUT I, Definition 4.1(i)
Valuation classDefinition 4.1(i) contentPrecision point
Nonarchimedean D_v is a category equivalent to the local D_v of Examples 3.2(i) or 3.3(i). The local object is categorical D-side data, not a chosen field embedding.
Archimedean D_v is an Aut-holomorphic orbispace isomorphic to the archimedean source D_v. Archimedean entries retain their own Aut-holomorphic type.
Associated object Each local entry functorially determines a corresponding D_v associated to the curve/orbicurve side, with a natural morphism. This is the local setup used for later prime-strip morphisms.
Source: IUT I, Definition 4.1(i) Related: cusp label torsors D-prime strips overview

IUT I Definition 4.1(ii): Cusp Label Torsors

From each local component D_v, Mochizuki reconstructs cusps and groups them into label classes. These label classes form an F_l^x/{±1}-torsor and contain a canonical class determined by the initial cusp epsilon_v.

Local cusps to label-class torsor IUT I, Definition 4.1(ii)
InputConstructionSource dependence
v in V_non Cusps are reconstructed from cuspidal decomposition groups of pi_1(D_v). Uses AbsTopI Lemma 4.5 as cited in Definition 4.1(ii).
v in V_arc Cusps are reconstructed from connected components of neighborhoods of infinity. Uses the Aut-holomorphic orbispace topology.
LabCusp(D_v) Label classes over a nonzero cusp carry the natural F_l-torsor structure and canonical eta_v. The label is reconstructed from local geometry, not assigned externally.
Source: IUT I, Definition 4.1(ii) Related: label synchronization initial cusp/covers

IUT I Definition 4.1(iii)-(iv): D^⊢-Prime-Strips

The mono-analytic base-prime-strip is a separate typed layer. It is obtained directly by specifying the mono-analytic local objects, and every D-prime-strip has a natural mono-analyticization.

D to D^⊢ IUT I, Definition 4.1(iii)-(iv)
LayerDefinition 4.1 contentGuardrail
D^⊢ at nonarchimedean places Categories equivalent to D_v^⊢ from Examples 3.2(i), 3.3(i). Do not reuse the holomorphic D_v type.
D^⊢ at archimedean places Objects of TM^⊢ isomorphic to Example 3.4(ii). The archimedean mono-analytic layer is a split topological monoid object.
Morphisms/capsules Morphisms are valuation-indexed collections; capsules use the conventions of Section 0. Capsules keep multiple prime-strips visible as a family, not as one product object.
Source: IUT I, Definition 4.1(iii)-(iv) Related: etale-picture core D-prime-strip definition

IUT I Definition 4.1(v)-(vi): Global D and Poly-Morphisms

Definition 4.1 also fixes a global base category D = B(C_K)^0. From a category equivalent to this global object, one reconstructs valuations, archimedean Aut-holomorphic orbispaces, cusp labels, and finally poly-morphisms from prime-strips to the global base.

Local D-prime-strips to global D IUT I, Definition 4.1(v)-(vi)
ObjectDefinition 4.1(v)-(vi) contentRole
D B(C_K)^0; from pi_1(D) one reconstructs the algebraic closure, valuations, and archimedean orbispaces. Provides the global target for model base-NF-bridges.
