LabCusp(D) The global category has cusp label classes with a natural F_l-torsor structure. Aligns the global side with local label torsors.
Poly-morphism A local poly-morphism D_v -> D is a collection of morphisms; a prime-strip poly-morphism is the family over all valuations. Section 4 uses relation-like poly-data, not a single chosen map.
Source: IUT I, Definition 4.1(v)-(vi) Related: local analytic sections label synchronization

IUT I Proposition 4.2: Label Synchronization

Proposition 4.2 synchronizes the local label torsors of a D-prime-strip. For any two valuations, the bijection between label classes is uniquely determined by compatibility with the canonical eta_v classes and the F_l-torsor structures.

Local torsors become one global label torsor IUT I, Proposition 4.2 and Remark 4.2.1
AssertionProposition 4.2 contentUse
Unique local bijections For any v,w in V, LabCusp(D_v) -> LabCusp(D_w) is uniquely determined by eta_v -> eta_w and torsor compatibility. Turns local label classes into one synchronized torsor.
Isomorphism invariance The bijections are preserved by arbitrary isomorphisms of D-prime-strips. The labels are intrinsic to the D-prime-strip structure.
Natural bijection The canonical element and torsor structure identify LabCusp(D) with F_l. Supplies the Section 4 label set used by later bridges.
Remark 4.2.1 If good-place D_v were defined from C_v instead of arrow-decorated X_v, the required bijection system need not exist. The source object choice is active mathematical data.
Source: IUT I, Proposition 4.2 and Remark 4.2.1 Related: cusp label torsors local analytic sections

IUT I Example 4.3: Local Analytic Sections

Example 4.3 constructs model base-NF bridges. Remark 4.3.1 gives the conceptual point: D-prime-strips behave like arithmetic local-analytic sections of Spec(K) -> Spec(F), precisely because no global multiplicative section exists in ordinary scheme theory.

Local sections without global holomorphicity Example 4.3 and Remarks 4.3.1-4.3.2

Automorphisms fixing epsilon

Example 4.3 uses the subgroup Aut_epsilon(C_K) of automorphisms fixing the distinguished cusp. The quotient by this subgroup supplies the F_l^x/{±1} label action on the model bridge.

Example 4.3(i)NF bridge

Local morphisms to the global base

For nonarchimedean and archimedean valuations, Example 4.3 constructs poly-morphisms D_v -> D by composing natural local-to-global morphisms with local automorphisms and the epsilon-fixing global automorphism group.

Example 4.3(ii)-(iii)local/global

Capsule poly-action

Copies D_j of the tautological D-prime-strip form a capsule indexed by j in F_l^x/{±1}. The resulting phi^NF is equivariant for the label poly-action.

Example 4.3(iv)capsule
Source itemPaper contentWiki consequence
Remark 4.3.1(i) The D-prime-strips are interpreted as arithmetic local-analytic sections of Spec(K) -> Spec(F) and as local-analytic global multiplicative subspaces. They simulate the missing global section without becoming a ring-theoretic section.
Remark 4.3.1(ii) These sections fail to extend to ring homomorphisms K -> F, to splittings of prime-decomposition trees, or to sections of global absolute Galois groups. This explains why local prime-strip comparisons cannot be collapsed into ordinary global geometry.
Remark 4.3.2 The desired geometry must have local independence from global structures, globalizability independent of local structures, and etale-like structures that support canonical splittings. This is the source-side motivation for panalocalization and mono-anabelian machinery in later IUT.
Remark 4.3.3 Mochizuki compares mono-anabelian geometry with Frobenius-invariant p-adic Teichmuller structures and with the upper-half-plane metric analogy. The analogy belongs to orientation; formal pages still use the D-prime-strip definitions.
Collas's categorification viewpoint fits this page: IUT replaces a missing scheme-theoretic section by categorical local containers. The decisive source claim, however, is Mochizuki's three-part failure of global holomorphicity in Remark 4.3.1.
Sources: IUT I, Example 4.3 and Remarks 4.3.1-4.3.3 Related: D-prime strips 4.1-4.2 theta evaluation sections Collas category view fundamental group mutation

IUT I Example 4.3(i): NF Automorphism Subgroups

Example 4.3 starts the model base-NF-bridge by isolating automorphisms of C_K that fix the cusp epsilon. Modulo sign, their image inside the torsion action is semi-unipotent inside a Borel subgroup; the quotient supplies the F_l action.

Automorphism subgroups behind the NF bridgeIUT I, Example 4.3(i)
ObjectExample 4.3(i) contentUse
Aut_epsilon(C_K)Subgroup of automorphisms fixing the cusp epsilon.Stabilizes the base NF poly-morphism for label 1.
Images in torsion actionAut_epsilon(C_K) maps to a semi-unipotent subgroup; Aut(C_K) maps to a Borel subgroup.Controls how labels act on the global base.
F_l quotientThe quotient Aut(C_K)/Aut_epsilon(C_K) is naturally identified with F_l.Supplies the label action used in Example 4.3(iv).
Source: IUT I, Example 4.3(i) Related: NF D-capsule action global D poly-morphisms

IUT I Example 4.3(ii)-(iii): Local NF Poly-Morphisms

For each valuation, Example 4.3 constructs a poly-morphism phi_v^NF : D_v -> D. It starts from a tautological local morphism to the global base and then closes under local automorphisms and cusp-fixing global automorphisms.

Local morphism closed under allowed automorphismsIUT I, Example 4.3(ii)-(iii)
ValuationConstructionAllowed variation
NonarchimedeanRestriction along X_v -> C_v -> C_K or its bad-place variant gives phi_*v^NF.Precompose by Aut(D_v); postcompose by Aut_epsilon(D).
ArchimedeanThe tautological map D_v = X_v -> C_v -> C(D,v) gives phi_*v^NF.Same poly-morphism closure under local and cusp-fixing global automorphisms.
Source: IUT I, Example 4.3(ii)-(iii) Related: NF automorphism groups global D poly-morphisms

IUT I Example 4.3(iv): F_l-Indexed D-Capsule

Example 4.3 then copies the tautological D-prime-strip once for each j in F_l. The label j acts by post-composition on the global base, producing a poly-morphism phi_j^NF : D_j -> D and a capsule-level phi^NF : D -> D.

Model base-NF bridge capsuleIUT I, Example 4.3(iv)
LayerExample 4.3(iv) contentUse
D_jFor each j in F_l, take a copy of the tautological D-prime-strip.Creates the label-indexed capsule used by base-NF bridges.
phi_j^NFTransport phi_1^NF using the F_l poly-action on the global base.Keeps labels attached to poly-morphisms rather than to external names.
Equivariancephi^NF is equivariant for the natural F_l poly-action on the data.Prepares Definition 4.6’s D-NF bridge model.
Source: IUT I, Example 4.3(iv) Related: NF local poly-morphisms D-ThetaNF bridges

IUT I Remark 4.3.1: Prime-Strips as Local Analytic Sections

Remark 4.3.1 explains the conceptual meaning of Example 4.3: D-prime-strips behave like arithmetic collections of local analytic sections of Spec(K) -> Spec(F). They simulate local multiplicative subspaces, but fail to be globally arithmetically holomorphic.

Three failures of global holomorphicityIUT I, Remark 4.3.1
Failure modeRemark 4.3.1 contentLater need
No ring sectionThe local analytic sections fail to extend to ring homomorphisms K -> F.Explains why a genuine global multiplicative subspace is unavailable.
Prime decomposition treesA local identification of primes does not extend to an isomorphism of full prime decomposition trees.Connects the obstruction to Neukirch-Uchida-style global arithmetic.
No global Galois sectionLocal absolute Galois group isomorphisms do not extend to a section of global absolute Galois groups.Motivates later mono-anabelian panalocalization.
Source: IUT I, Remark 4.3.1 Related: NF D-capsule action prime strips and bridges

IUT I Examples 4.4-4.5: Theta Evaluation Sections

Example 4.4 constructs model base-theta bridges. At bad places, the bridge is built from evaluation points obtained by translating labeled cusps, and the theta value at label j lies in the mu_{2l}-orbit of q_v^{j^2}. Example 4.5 then transports cusp labels through the NF and theta bridges.

Bad-place theta evaluation route labels, translated cusps, evaluation sections, and label transport
Source itemPaper contentWiki consequence
Example 4.4(i) At v in V_bad, labels in |F_l| label cusps of C_v; translating by mu_- gives evaluation points of X_v. Bad-prime evaluation is label-sensitive before any global bridge is formed.
Theta value The value of Theta_v over the evaluation point of label j lies in the mu_{2l}-orbit of q_v^{j^2}. The squared label is the source-level reason later theta-value bookkeeping is label-arithmetic, not just indexing.
Evaluation sections Points above the evaluation points in the theta cover are K_v-rational and determine sections G_v -> Pi_v, reconstructible from isomorphs of Pi_v. Evaluation sections are part of the anabelian reconstruction interface.
Example 4.4(ii)-(iv) The base-theta bridge D_j -> D_> uses these evaluation sections at bad places and full poly-isomorphisms at good places. The bridge has different local definitions by place class.
Example 4.5 Restriction through phi_j^NF and phi_j^Theta yields phi_j^LC : LabCusp(D) -> LabCusp(D_>), with the epsilon class characterized by evaluation j -> j. Label transport is a theorem-level interface, not a chosen alignment of local labels.
Remark 4.5.1 Independent local/global conjugacy indeterminacies must not be partially synchronized. At bad places, the required factorization is determined without such an arbitrary synchronization. This becomes a direct guardrail for later theta-link and 3.12 corridor modeling.
The bridge must retain the bad-place evaluation-section route. Replacing it by an abstract label map would lose the source connection between j, q_v^{j^2}, and the reconstructed section G_v -> Pi_v.
Sources: IUT I, Examples 4.4-4.5 and Remark 4.5.1 Related: D-prime strips 4.1-4.2 local analytic sections 4.3 theta evaluation route global Kummer noninterference

IUT I Example 4.4(i): Theta Evaluation Points

The model base-Theta bridge begins at bad multiplicative places. Labels in |F_l| identify cusps of C_v; translation by the distinguished two-torsion point mu_- produces evaluation points of X_v. The theta value at label j lies in a mu_{2l}-orbit of q_v^{j^2}.

Bad-place labels become theta values IUT I, Example 4.4(i)
Source datumExample 4.4(i) contentFormalization consequence
Cusp labels At v in V_bad, the labels in |F_l| correspond to cusps of C_v. Evaluation labels are attached to source cusps before any bridge target is chosen.
mu_- The distinguished order-two torsion point whose closure meets the same special-fiber component as the zero cusp translates cusps to evaluation points. The evaluation point is not an arbitrary point of X_v.
Theta value The theta value over label j is in the mu_{2l}-orbit of q_v^{j^2}. The square of the label is source-side arithmetic data that later bookkeeping must preserve.
Source: IUT I, Example 4.4(i) Related: bad-place theta bridge theta evaluation overview IUT II theta evaluation route

IUT I Example 4.4(ii): Bad-Place Theta Bridge

At bad places, the model base-Theta bridge is built from the evaluation sections supplied by Example 4.4(i). For each label j in F_l, the local morphism phi^Theta_{v,j} : D_{v,j} -> D_{>,v} is compatible with the induced outer actions on the geometric tempered fundamental groups.

Evaluation sections define the bad-place bridge IUT I, Example 4.4(ii)
Bridge layerExample 4.4(ii) contentGuardrail
Local map For v in V_bad and j in F_l, the construction gives phi^Theta_{v,j}: D_{v,j} -> D_{>,v}. Bad-place theta bridges are not generic strip isomorphisms.
Evaluation sections The map is obtained from the group-theoretically reconstructible sections G_v -> Pi_v. The anabelian reconstruction route is part of the bridge data.
Compatibility The construction is compatible with outer actions on geometric tempered fundamental groups, with finite ambiguity. Lean pages should model the compatibility and ambiguity separately.
Source: IUT I, Example 4.4(ii) Related: theta evaluation points outer factorization indeterminacy D-ThetaNF bridges

IUT I Example 4.4(iii)-(iv): Good-Place and Global Theta Bridge

At good nonarchimedean and archimedean places, the model base-Theta bridge uses full poly-isomorphisms phi^Theta_{v,j}: D_{v,j} ≃ D_{>,v}. These local components assemble into the global bridge phi^Theta: D_J -> D_>, and the capsule D_J carries the natural F_l permutation poly-action.

Good-place isomorphisms assemble with bad-place evaluation IUT I, Example 4.4(iii)-(iv)
Assembly stepExample 4.4 contentConsequence
Good places For v in V_good, phi^Theta_{v,j} is a full poly-isomorphism. The place class determines whether the bridge is evaluation-built or isomorphic.
Global component The local data assemble to phi^Theta_j : D_j -> D_>. One label gives one global source strip mapped to the theta target.
Capsule bridge The full bridge phi^Theta : D_J -> D_> is compatible with the natural F_l permutation poly-action on the capsule. Label action is structural, not decorative sidebar data.
Source: IUT I, Example 4.4(iii)-(iv) Related: bad-place theta bridge D-ThetaNF bridges Section 4 symmetries

IUT I Example 4.5: Label Transport Through Base Bridges

Example 4.5 transports cusp-label classes through the model base-NF and model base-Theta bridges. For each label j, restrictions through phi^NF_j and phi^Theta_j induce an F_l-torsor isomorphism phi^LC_j : LabCusp(D) -> LabCusp(D_>).

NF and Theta bridges transport the same label class IUT I, Example 4.5
Transport itemExample 4.5 contentUse downstream
phi^LC_j NF and Theta bridge restrictions induce LabCusp(D) -> LabCusp(D_>). This is the label-transport interface used in Proposition 4.7.
Label action phi^LC_j is obtained from the j=1 case by composing with the action by j. The torsor action, not a chosen enumeration, controls label movement.
[epsilon] The unique class [epsilon] in LabCusp(D) is characterized by the condition that evaluation sends phi^LC_j([epsilon]) to j. This becomes the global label class used to identify LabCusp(D) with capsule indices.
Source: IUT I, Example 4.5 Related: D-ThetaNF bridges outer factorization indeterminacy label synchronization

IUT I Remark 4.5.1: Outer Factorization Indeterminacy

Remark 4.5.1 is a guardrail for all later bridge formalization. It analyzes factorizations J -> H -> G known only up to conjugacy and warns that specifying intermediate inclusions independently imposes an artificial partial synchronization of indeterminacies.

Composite data without artificial synchronization IUT I, Remark 4.5.1
CaseRemark 4.5.1 contentWiki guardrail
General warning Choosing the inclusion J -> H independently of the composite J -> G partially deactivates independent conjugacy indeterminacies. Do not formalize bridge compatibility by adding synchronized representatives unless the source supplies them.
Bad places Using Corollary 2.5, the factorization is uniquely determined by the composite J -> G without prior synchronization. This is the strong form needed for Hodge-Arakelov evaluation in IUT II.
Archimedean places Using Corollary 2.8, the H-conjugacy class of the image of J is determined, a weaker property than the bad-place result. Do not transfer the bad-place uniqueness statement unchanged to archimedean places.
This page is directly relevant to the 3.12 corridor: a common-container or comparison model must preserve the source's nonsynchronization discipline instead of silently choosing representatives in one global coordinate system.
Source: IUT I, Remark 4.5.1 Related: bad-place theta bridge label transport 3.12 diagnostics

IUT I Definition 4.6 and Proposition 4.7: D-ThetaNF Bridges

Definition 4.6 packages the Section 4 bridge machinery into D-NF bridges, D-Theta bridges, and D-ThetaNF-Hodge theaters. Proposition 4.7 then proves that the bridge package transports cusp-label torsors and produces the crucial bijection zeta : LabCusp(D) -> J from global cusp labels to capsule indices.

Shared capsule bridge package Definition 4.6 and Proposition 4.7
Source itemPaper contentWiki consequence
Def. 4.6(i) A D-NF bridge is a poly-morphism D_J -> D, modeled on Example 4.3, from a capsule of D-prime-strips to a global base object. It is the global-number-field bridge, not the theta-evaluation bridge.
Def. 4.6(ii) A D-Theta bridge is a poly-morphism D_J -> D_>, modeled on Example 4.4, from a capsule of D-prime-strips to a theta-side target strip. It carries the evaluation-section input from bad places.
Def. 4.6(iii) A D-ThetaNF-Hodge theater is D <- D_J -> D_>; its isomorphisms are bridge morphisms inducing the same bijection on capsule indices. Shared capsule-index compatibility is part of the object definition.
Prop. 4.7(i) The bad-place structure of the D-Theta bridge determines chi : J -> F_l^x/{±1}. The labels of capsule constituents are recovered from the bridge, not chosen freely.
Prop. 4.7(ii)-(iii) Bridge restrictions determine torsor isomorphisms phi_j^LC : LabCusp(D) -> LabCusp(D_>) and a unique epsilon class yielding zeta : LabCusp(D) -> J. This is the source of global cusp labels native to D.
Remark 4.7.2 The inverse of zeta is described as a combinatorial Kodaira-Spencer morphism relating arithmetic capsule indices to geometric cusp labels. The bridge is not only bookkeeping; it is the Section 4 arithmetic/geometric label interface.
The crucial point for later formalization is the shared capsule: the NF and theta bridge morphisms must agree on the capsule-index bijection before the zeta bijection can serve as a global label interface.
Sources: IUT I, Definition 4.6, Proposition 4.7, Remarks 4.7.1-4.7.2 Related: theta evaluation sections symmetries/processions ThetaNF Frobenioid lift tensor/log-volume corridor

IUT I Propositions 4.8-4.11 and Corollary 4.12: Symmetries and Processions

The last part of Section 4 explains why unlabeled output data matters. Proposition 4.9 separates cyclic label symmetry from full symmetric-group symmetry; Definition 4.10 and Proposition 4.11 introduce processions to reduce label indeterminacy; Corollary 4.12 extracts the D-ThetaNF etale-picture.

From labels to comparison data Propositions 4.8-4.11 and Corollary 4.12
Source itemPaper contentWiki consequence
Prop. 4.8 D-NF bridge isomorphisms form an F_l^x/{±1}-torsor; D-Theta bridge and D-ThetaNF theater isomorphism sets have cardinality one; gluing choices again form an F_l^x/{±1}-torsor. There are two different uniqueness/indeterminacy regimes in the same bridge package.
Prop. 4.9(i) Forgetting to the underlying D-NF bridge gives output with cyclic F_l^x/{±1}-symmetry acting simply transitively on capsule indices. Cyclic symmetry preserves the NF bridge's global label structure.
Prop. 4.9(ii)-(iii) Forgetting to the holomorphic or mono-analytic capsule gives full symmetric-group symmetry and forgets the bijection J -> F_l^x/{±1}. Absolute comparison requires forgetting the cyclic structure.
Remark 4.9.1 Non-labeled output data is needed to compare objects, including later log-volumes, constructed relative to different labels. This is a direct upstream source for log-volume comparability in IUT III.
Remark 4.9.2 A single global D synchronizes labels. If each prime-strip had an independent globalization, nonsynchronized labels would be incompatible with the zeta bijection. The one-base-object design is mathematically load-bearing.
Def. 4.10; Prop. 4.11 A procession is a nested capsule diagram P_1 -> ... -> P_n. For Section 4, processions reduce label indeterminacy from (l*)^(l*) to l*!. Processions retain comparison symmetry while minimizing label ambiguity.
Cor. 4.12 The D-ThetaNF link is the full poly-isomorphism between the mono-analyticized targets D_>^⊢. In an infinite chain, these form a constant mono-analytic core with arbitrary permutation symmetries among spokes. This extends the Corollary 3.9 etale-picture pattern to D-ThetaNF theaters.
Later log-volume experiments must distinguish three comparison levels: cyclic F_l^x/{±1} comparison, full symmetric comparison after forgetting labels, and procession comparison with reduced indeterminacy.
Sources: IUT I, Propositions 4.8-4.11 and Corollary 4.12 Related: D-ThetaNF bridges etale-picture 3.9 packet/procession log-volume 3.12 diagnostics

Multiplicative Combinatorial Teichmuller Theory

IUT I Section 4 develops the D-side combinatorics needed before theta evaluation. Its purpose is to control labels F_l^⋇ = F_l^x/{±1}, local prime-strip sections, model NF/Theta bridges, label-class transport, and processions. This is the first source layer where label comparison and label indeterminacy are measured explicitly.

D-side label dynamics IUT I Section 4

F_l^⋇ labels

Section 4 begins with labels in F_l^⋇ = F_l^x/{±1}, of cardinality l⋇ = (l-1)/2. These labels are the torsion labels at which IUT II will evaluate the etale theta function.

§4 openinglabels

D-prime-strips as local sections

Definition 4.1 defines a D-prime-strip as valuation-indexed D-data. Remark 4.3.1 interprets model D-prime-strips as arithmetic local-analytic sections of Spec(K) -> Spec(F), simulating a global multiplicative subspace without a global ring-theoretic section.

Def. 4.1; Rem. 4.3.1local sections

Label classes of cusps

For each local component, Definition 4.1 constructs LabCusp(D_v), an F_l^⋇-torsor with a canonical element from epsilon_v. Proposition 4.2 identifies these local torsors across all valuations of a D-prime-strip.

Def. 4.1(ii); Prop. 4.2LabCusp

Why X_arrow_v, not C_v

Remark 4.2.1 explains that using C_v instead of X_arrow_v at good places breaks the uniform label-class bijections. The obstruction is global and uses decomposition behavior in Gal(K/F).

Remark 4.2.1good places

Model base-NF bridge

Example 4.3 constructs D⋇ -> D° by using the global object D° = B(C_K)^0, the subgroup fixing the cusp epsilon, and the induced poly-action of F_l^⋇ on the capsule.

Example 4.3NF bridge

Model base-Theta bridge

Example 4.4 constructs D⋇ -> D_>. At bad places, the morphisms are built from evaluation sections labeled by j in F_l^⋇; at good places they are full poly-isomorphisms.

Example 4.4Theta bridge

No synchronization shortcut

Remark 4.5.1 analyzes inclusions J -> H -> G known only up to independent conjugacy. At bad places, the desired factorization is determined without imposing an artificial synchronization of local and global Galois indeterminacies.

Remark 4.5.1conjugacy

D-ThetaNF-Hodge theater

Definition 4.6 glues a D-NF bridge and a D-Theta bridge along the same capsule of D-prime-strips. A morphism of D-ThetaNF theaters requires the two bridge morphisms to induce the same bijection on capsule indices.

Definition 4.6bridge pair

Globalizing labels through LabCusp(D°)

Proposition 4.7 constructs a unique class [epsilon] in LabCusp(D°) and an isomorphism from global cusp labels to capsule indices. This replaces fragile local label choices by data native to the global side.

Proposition 4.7global labels

Synchronized labels from one D°

Remark 4.9.2 says label cohesion is an important consequence of using a single global D°. Independent globalizations for each strip would yield nonsynchronized labels and destroy the global bijection of Proposition 4.7.

Remark 4.9.2one basepoint

Cyclic versus symmetric label comparison

Proposition 4.9 distinguishes the cyclic F_l^⋇-symmetry of D-NF bridge output from the full symmetric-group symmetry after forgetting the cyclic structure. The latter is needed for absolute comparison of unrelated D-prime-strips.

Proposition 4.9forgetful functors

Procession indeterminacy count

Proposition 4.11 uses nested capsules P_1 -> ... -> P_l⋇. The source explicitly records the gain: the possible label assignments drop from (l⋇)^(l⋇) to l⋇!.

Prop. 4.11processions

D-ThetaNF etale-picture

Corollary 4.12 defines the D-ThetaNF link by passing a D-ThetaNF theater to the mono-analyticized target strip D_>^⊢. In an infinite chain this becomes a constant mono-analytic core with freely permutable spokes.

Corollary 4.12etale-picture
Formalization warning: Section 4 should not be modeled as a plain finite index set with labels. The source distinguishes global cusp-label torsors, capsule indices, cyclic F_l^⋇-symmetry, full symmetric freedom after forgetting labels, and procession-level reduction of indeterminacy.
Sources: IUT I Related: prime strips and bridges theta/Frobenius pictures multiplicative/additive bridges theta evaluation route tensor/log-volume corridor

Global Frobenioids in IUT I 5.1

Example 5.1 is the first Frobenioid lift of the Section 4 bridge machinery. Starting from the global base object D° of a D-ThetaNF-Hodge theater, it reconstructs field data, global model Frobenioids, κ-coric pseudo-monoids, κ-solvable quotients, and diagonal versus arbitrary distributions over the capsule labels.

From D° to global Frobenioid data IUT I Example 5.1

M* field reconstruction

Example 5.1(i) applies the theta-approach to pi_1(D°) to reconstruct an isomorph of F^x, denoted M*(D°), together with the field structure on M* union {0}. Extending the action to D* and taking invariants gives the Fmod part.

Example 5.1(i)AbsTopIII 1.9

D* and C_Fmod

The same construction recovers a profinite group corresponding to the model C_Fmod. The category D* is the connected-object category for this group and contains D° as an open-subgroup layer.

Example 5.1(i)C_Fmod

Global κ-coric pseudo-monoids

Example 5.1(i) reconstructs the rational function-field Galois group and the pseudo-monoids M_κ*, M_∞κ*, and M_∞κ×*. These are the global analogues of the κ-coric objects introduced in Remark 3.1.7.

Example 5.1(i)κ-coric

κ-solvable quotient

The quotient pi_1^rat(D*) -> pi_1^{κ-sol}(D*) is built from the kernel of the ∞κ action and the maximal solvable quotient over Fmod. The resulting objects include M_κ-sol* and M_sol*.

Example 5.1(i)κ-sol

κ-sol conjugate synchronization

Although the F_l^⋇ poly-action on pi_1^rat(D*) is initially only defined up to conjugacy, the linear-disjointness input from Remark 3.1.7 makes the induced action on the κ-sol outer representation well-defined without conjugacy indeterminacy, and in fact trivial.

Example 5.1(i)synchronization

F* model Frobenioid

Example 5.1(ii)-(iii) reconstructs the valuation set and stack-theoretic arithmetic divisor monoid, then applies Frobenioid theory to obtain the model Frobenioid F*(D°). Its restrictions are F° over D° and F*_mod over terminal objects.

Example 5.1(ii)-(iii)Frobenioid

Birational monoids and decomposition groups

Example 5.1(iv) uses birationalizations of the Frobenioids to recover nonzero field elements, integral submonoids at primes, unit submonoids, and decomposition subgroups Pi_p inside pi_1(D*).

Example 5.1(iv)decomposition

Unique ∞κ structures

Example 5.1(v) defines ∞κ-coric and ∞κ×-coric structures on F* and proves they exist uniquely up to unique isomorphism. Kummer classes identify the relevant cyclotomes and recover M*, M_sol*, and M_mod*.

Example 5.1(v)Kummer

Strict critical test for ∞κ

An ∞κ structure is recovered from an ∞κ× structure by requiring that the Kummer class restricts to torsion at decomposition groups of strictly critical points. This is the Example 5.1 version of the root-of-unity condition in Remark 3.1.7.

Example 5.1(v)critical points

Constant distributions over labels

Example 5.1(vii) forms F*_j for each capsule label and the diagonal category F*_{<J>} inside the product F*_J. Remark 5.1.1 reads this as constant distributions versus arbitrary distributions over the label product.

Example 5.1(vii)distributions

Kummer-ready versus Kummer-blind

Remark 5.1.3 says Kummer theory can be performed with F°, F*, M*, M_sol*, M_∞κ*, M_∞κ×*, and M_κ-sol*, but not with F*_mod, M_mod*, or M_κ* alone.

Remark 5.1.3black-out

Belyi cuspidalization viewpoint

Remark 5.1.4 frames number-field elements as geometrically represented by Belyi maps and the later local logarithm theory as arithmetic analytic continuation. This is explanatory context for why the reconstruction is tied to hyperbolic curve data.

Remark 5.1.4Belyi
Formalization warning: F*_mod should not be treated as a standalone field object with all structure recoverable internally. The source explicitly says its additive/ring structure is recovered only through the extra embedding into the Kummer-ready F* layer.
Sources: IUT I Related: κ-coric functions multiplicative combinatorics ThetaNF Frobenioid lift Frobenioids/log-shells log-Kummer correspondences

IUT I Example 5.1: Global Reconstruction from D

Example 5.1 reconstructs the global number-field and Frobenioid layers from the global D-side base category. It is the source block behind the later F*, F_mod, κ-coric pseudo-monoids, κ-sol quotient, and global-to-local distribution interfaces.

Global reconstruction route Example 5.1(i)-(vii) and Remark 5.1.5
Source itemPaper contentWiki consequence
Example 5.1(i) From pi_1(D), the theta-approach reconstructs an isomorph of F^x, the model C_Fmod group, F_mod invariants, pi_1^rat, and κ-coric pseudo-monoids. The global field layer is an output of anabelian reconstruction, not a primitive field parameter.
κ-sol quotient The quotient pi_1^rat -> pi_1^κ-sol is built using the ∞κ action and a maximal solvable quotient over F_mod. κ-sol data is an intermediate Kummer interface, not a replacement for full anabelian reconstruction.
Example 5.1(ii)-(iii) Valuations and divisor monoids reconstructed from the field structure determine the model Frobenioid F(D), with restrictions to D and terminal objects. This is where field/unit data becomes Frobenioid data.
Example 5.1(iv)-(v) Birational monoids recover decomposition groups, integral submonoids, unique ∞κ/∞κ× structures, and cyclotomic identifications via Kummer classes. The reconstruction depends on Kummer readiness and cyclotome rigidity.
Example 5.1(vii) F_j and diagonal F_<J> are copies of F_mod placed over label-indexed valuation products; Remark 5.1.1 reads these as arbitrary versus constant distributions. This is the first Section 5 distribution layer over the label capsule.
Remark 5.1.5 Mochizuki rejects replacing this machinery by a solvable-Uchida-style ring reconstruction, because such a reconstruction is incompatible with localization operations in D-ThetaNF-Hodge theaters. The wiki marks solvable reconstruction as a prohibited shortcut for IUT's local/global interface.
Guardrail: the source does not allow the global ring structure to be recovered by a solvable shortcut that ignores localization. The reconstruction must stay compatible with D-ThetaNF localizations.
Sources: IUT I, Example 5.1 and Remarks 5.1.1-5.1.5 Related: global Frobenioids overview Kummer-ready/blind local analytic sections delta-valuations

IUT I Remarks 5.1.3 and 5.2.2: Kummer-Ready and Kummer-Blind Interfaces

Mochizuki explicitly distinguishes interfaces where Kummer theory can be performed from interfaces where it cannot. This distinction is a structural rule for the wiki: an F-prime-strip carries Kummer-ready Frobenioid data, while the weakened mono-analytic F^⊢ interface is Kummer-blind.

Where Kummer theory is available Remarks 5.1.3 and 5.2.2
Source itemPaper contentWiki consequence
Remark 5.1.3 Kummer theory may be performed with F, F*, M, M_sol, M_∞κ, M_∞κ×, and M_κ-sol. These are Kummer-ready interfaces.
Remark 5.1.3 Kummer theory cannot be performed with F_mod, M_mod, or M_κ alone. These are Kummer-blind unless embedded into a ready layer.
Additive structure The additive or ring structure on the birational monoid portion of F_mod is recovered only with the additional datum of the embedding F_mod -> F. The wiki must not describe F_mod as self-sufficient field data.
Remark 5.2.2 F-prime-strips are Kummer-ready, whereas F^⊢-prime-strips are Kummer-blind. This distinction controls how mono-analyticization may be used in later comparisons.
The Kummer-blind interface can be useful for mono-analytic comparison, but it cannot supply Kummer roots or recovered ring structure by itself.
Sources: IUT I, Remarks 5.1.3 and 5.2.2 Related: global reconstruction Frobenioid prime-strips log-Kummer correspondences 3.12 diagnostics

Frobenioid Prime-Strips in IUT I 5.2

Definition 5.2 introduces the F-level prime-strip interfaces over the D-prime-strips of Section 4: holomorphic F-prime-strips, mono-analytic split F⊢-prime-strips, globally realified F⊩-prime-strips, and the local ∞κ/∞κ× coric structures. Corollary 5.3 then records the rigidity that lets F-data be controlled by its D-data.

F-prime-strip interfaces over D-prime-strips IUT I Definition 5.2 and Corollary 5.3

F-prime-strip definition

Definition 5.2(i) defines a holomorphic Frobenioid-prime-strip F = {F_v}_{v in V}. At nonarchimedean places the components are categories equivalent to the local Frobenioids C_v; at archimedean places the component includes a category, an Aut-holomorphic orbispace, and a Kummer structure.

Def. 5.2(i)F-strip

F⊢-prime-strip definition

Definition 5.2(ii) defines mono-analytic Frobenioid-prime-strips F⊢. Nonarchimedean components are split Frobenioids equivalent to F_v^⊢; archimedean components are triples with a Frobenioid, an object of TM⊢, and a splitting.

Def. 5.2(ii)mono-analytic

F⊩-prime-strip definition

Definition 5.2(iv) packages global realification: (C⊩, Prime(C⊩) -> V, F⊢, {rho_v}). It is isomorphic to the F_mod^⊩ collection of Example 3.5 and carries the real-valued comparison maps rho_v.

Def. 5.2(iv)realified

Associated D-strips

Remark 5.2.1(i) gives functorial algorithms from F-prime-strips to D-prime-strips, and from F⊢-prime-strips to D⊢-prime-strips. The F-layer lies over the D-layer; it is not independent extra decoration.

Remark 5.2.1(i)over D

Mono-analyticization of F

Remark 5.2.1(ii) constructs F -> F⊢ and also constructs F⊩ from F. The latter uses the simple product-formula structure of the global realified Frobenioid and the rigidity of local divisor monoids.

Remark 5.2.1(ii)functorial

Local ∞κ/∞κ× structures

Definition 5.2(v)-(viii) reconstructs local κ-, ∞κ-, and ∞κ×-coric pseudo-monoids from D-side fundamental-group or Aut-holomorphic data and attaches the uniquely determined ∞κ/∞κ× structures to F-components.

Def. 5.2(v)-(viii)κ-coric

Cyclotome uniqueness

The local construction compares Kummer cohomology classes and divisors of zeroes and poles. The same elementary intersection Q_>0 ∩ Zhat^x = {1} gives the unique cyclotomic identifications needed to recover the pseudo-monoids.

Def. 5.2(vi),(viii)cyclotome

Strict critical torsion test

An ∞κ structure is recovered from ∞κ× by restricting the associated Kummer class to decomposition groups, or systems of strictly critical points, and requiring torsion/root-of-unity behavior. Remark 5.2.3 singles out the bad-place point coming from label zero.

Def. 5.2; Rem. 5.2.3critical points

Kummer-ready versus Kummer-blind

Remark 5.2.2 states the interface rule: F-prime-strips are Kummer-ready, while F⊢-prime-strips are Kummer-blind. Later formalization should therefore not infer Kummer classes from the weakened F⊢ interface alone.

Remark 5.2.2guardrail

Corollary 5.3 rigidity

Corollary 5.3(i)-(ii) says isomorphisms of global Frobenioids and F-prime-strips are determined bijectively by isomorphisms of their base categories or associated D-prime-strips. The F⊢ version is only stated surjectively.

Cor. 5.3(i)-(iii)rigidity

Bad tempered Frobenioid automorphisms

Corollary 5.3(iv) states that for bad places the natural map Aut(F_v) -> Aut(D_v) is bijective. The proof uses Kummer readiness, rational function monoids, theta divisors, and cuspidal/non-cuspidal divisor control.

Cor. 5.3(iv)bad places

Morphisms over D

Remark 5.3.1 extends rigidity to certain D-prime-strip morphisms, not only isomorphisms: after pullback of target Frobenioids, Corollary 5.3 gives a uniquely determined F-arrow lying over the D-arrow.

Remark 5.3.1lifting
Formalization warning: the map from F⊢ isomorphisms to D⊢ isomorphisms is only recorded as surjective in Corollary 5.3(iii), unlike the bijective statements for global Frobenioids and F-prime-strips. Encoding all three as bijections would overstate the source.
Sources: IUT I Related: global Frobenioids 5.1 prime strips and bridges κ-coric functions ThetaNF Frobenioid lift log-Kummer correspondences

Pivotal Distributions in IUT I 5.4

Example 5.4 is the Frobenioid-level passage from the Section 4 D-ThetaNF bridge to localized F-prime-strip data. It constructs labeled copies, theta-side poly-morphisms, delta-valuations, NF-side poly-morphisms, restriction functors, realifications, and finally the pivotal distribution at the label j = 1.

Example 5.4 source route labeled F-strips to pivotal distributions

Labeled F-capsule

Example 5.4(i) starts with labeled copies F_j of the theta-side F_> over the capsule labels j in J. The diagonal object F_<J> maps diagonally to F_J and is fully poly-isomorphic to F_>.

Example 5.4(i)labels

Theta poly-morphisms

Example 5.4(ii) also regards each F_j as related to F_> by a poly-morphism psiTheta_j : F_j -> F_>, uniquely determined by the D-theta arrow phiTheta_j.

Example 5.4(ii)theta side

Delta-valuations

Example 5.4(iii) defines a delta-valuation from a cusp label delta in LabCusp(D). It may be read intrinsically from the D-NF bridge by selecting the unique capsule constituent whose induced cusp label is the label 1.

Example 5.4(iii)cusps

Localized F|delta

Example 5.4(iv) localizes F at the delta-valuations to construct an F-prime-strip F|delta, well-defined only up to isomorphism because different elements above the same model valuation must be related without a canonical choice.

Example 5.4(iv)localization

Poly-morphisms over D

A poly-morphism F' -> F is represented by a full poly-isomorphism F' ~= F|delta for some cusp label. It lies over the induced D-prime-strip poly-morphism and is compatible with the local and global infinity-kappa coric structures.

Example 5.4(iv)over D

Unique NF poly-morphism

Example 5.4(v) applies Corollary 5.3(ii) to obtain a unique poly-morphism psiNF : F_J -> F lying over the D-NF bridge phiNF.

Example 5.4(v)NF side

Restriction functors

Example 5.4(vi) obtains isomorphism classes of restriction functors from F_mod through F_<J> to the local factors. These classes are independent of the chosen poly-morphism among its F_l-conjugates.

Example 5.4(vi)restriction

Pivotal distribution

Example 5.4(vii) names the objects from the restriction construction at the label j = 1 the pivotal distributions, together with their realified versions.

Example 5.4(vii)j = 1

Average versus sum

Remark 5.4.2 records the log-volume convention: one may read F_<J> degrees as averages over J ~= F_l, or, when using Frobenioid isomorphisms, as sums over the labels without dividing by l.

Remark 5.4.2log-volume
Source separation: Remark 5.4.1 says the labeled theta-side constructions use the D-Theta bridge portion, while the delta-valuation, localization, restriction, and pivotal-distribution constructions use the D-NF bridge portion. A formal model should keep these bridge dependencies separate.
Sources: IUT I Related: Frobenioid prime-strips 5.2 global Frobenioids 5.1 ThetaNF Frobenioid lift Corollary 3.12 corridor

IUT I Example 5.4: Delta-Valuations and Localization

Example 5.4 turns the global label interface into localized Frobenioid data. A cusp label delta in LabCusp(D) determines delta-valuations, and localization at those valuations constructs the F-prime-strip F|delta, well-defined up to isomorphism and compatible with local/global ∞κ-coric structures.

From global cusp label to local F-strip Example 5.4(iii)-(iv)
Source itemPaper contentWiki consequence
Example 5.4(iii) A delta-valuation may be defined through the Aut-epsilon orbit mapping delta to [epsilon], or intrinsically through the D-NF bridge and the unique capsule constituent whose label is 1. The valuation is bridge-native; it is not an arbitrary local choice.
Example 5.4(iv) Localizing F at delta-valuations constructs an F-prime-strip F|delta. This is the Frobenioid lift of the D-side poly-morphism interface.
Indeterminacy Since V^un -> V_mod is not injective, different elements above one model valuation cannot be related by a canonical choice; F|delta is well-defined only up to isomorphism. Do not turn localization into a selected function.
∞κ compatibility The poly-morphism is compatible with local and global ∞κ-coric structures via restriction of associated Kummer classes and induced homomorphisms of rational fundamental groups. Localization must preserve the Kummer/coric bookkeeping, modulo the stated indeterminacies.
Example 5.4(v) Corollary 5.3(ii) supplies the unique psi^NF : F_J -> F lying over phi^NF. The NF-side lift is controlled by D-side rigidity.
Delta-localization is a relation up to isomorphism. A page or formal model that chooses one valuation in each fiber silently adds data not present in the paper.
Sources: IUT I, Example 5.4(iii)-(v) Related: pivotal distributions D-ThetaNF bridges global reconstruction packet/procession log-volume

IUT I Definition 5.5 and Corollary 5.6: ThetaNF Bridges

Definition 5.5 is the Frobenioid-theoretic lift of Definition 4.6. It defines NF-bridges, Theta-bridges, and ThetaNF-Hodge theaters over a shared capsule of F-prime-strips. Corollary 5.6 proves that their isomorphism theory is governed by the associated D-level bridges.

Frobenioid lift of D-ThetaNF Definition 5.5 and Corollary 5.6
Source itemPaper contentWiki consequence
Def. 5.5(i) An NF-bridge consists of an F-prime-strip capsule F_J, Frobenioid categories F and F*, the morphism F -> F*, and a poly-morphism lifting a D-NF bridge. This is the Frobenioid lift of the number-field side.
Def. 5.5(ii) A Theta-bridge consists of F_J, F_>, a Theta-Hodge theater, and poly-morphisms determined by a D-Theta bridge. This is the Frobenioid lift of the theta/evaluation side.
Def. 5.5(iii) A ThetaNF-Hodge theater glues the NF and Theta bridges, requiring their associated D-data to form a D-ThetaNF-Hodge theater and requiring morphisms to induce the same bijection on F-capsule indices. The shared capsule condition survives the Frobenioid lift.
Cor. 5.6(i) Isomorphisms of Theta-Hodge theaters are bijective with isomorphisms of their associated D-prime-strips. The underlying D-layer controls theater isomorphism.
Cor. 5.6(ii) Isomorphisms of NF-bridges, Theta-bridges, and ThetaNF-Hodge theaters are bijective with isomorphisms of the associated D-NF, D-Theta, and D-ThetaNF bridge data. The F-level bridge package is rigid over the D-level bridge package.
Cor. 5.6(iii) Compatible gluing poly-isomorphisms between the F-prime-strip capsules of an NF-bridge and Theta-bridge form an F_l^x/{±1}-torsor. Gluing indeterminacy is inherited from Section 4.
Remark 5.6.1 The functorial dynamics of Section 4 have analogues for the Section 5 NF-bridges, Theta-bridges, and ThetaNF-Hodge theaters. Section 5 is a controlled lift, not a separate combinatorial theory.
Encoding rule: every ThetaNF-Hodge theater page should expose the associated D-ThetaNF-Hodge theater. Corollary 5.6 makes that associated D-data the controlling isomorphism layer.
Sources: IUT I, Definition 5.5, Corollary 5.6, Remark 5.6.1 Related: ThetaNF Frobenioid lift D-ThetaNF bridges Section 4 symmetries/processions Theta±ell bridges

Prime Strips and Bridges

Prime-strips are valuation-indexed interfaces extracted from Hodge-theater data. They are not decorative labels: D-prime-strips, their mono-analytic versions, F-prime-strips, and bridge data determine which information is retained when the theory moves between holomorphic and mono-analytic views.

Prime-strip interface holomorphic data, mono-analyticization, and bridges

D-prime-strip

IUT I Definition 4.1 defines a D-prime-strip as a collection {D_v}_{v in V}. At nonarchimedean places these are categories equivalent to the local D-data; at archimedean places they are Aut-holomorphic orbispaces.

Mono-analyticization

The associated D⊢-prime-strip keeps the mono-analytic information obtained by passing to the appropriate subcategories or topological-monoid objects. This is one controlled way to weaken holomorphic history.

Label classes

Label classes of cusps are reconstructed from the prime-strip and carry an F_l-torsor structure together with a canonical class determined by the initial cusp datum.

D-NF bridge

A D-NF bridge is a poly-morphism from a capsule of D-prime-strips to a number-field side D-prime-strip. It tracks the number-field component of a bridge without conflating it with theta evaluation.

D-Theta bridge

A D-Theta bridge is a poly-morphism from a capsule of D-prime-strips to a theta-side D-prime-strip. At bad places it transports the label information used later by theta-value and Gaussian constructions.

D-ThetaNF theater

A D-ThetaNF-Hodge theater combines compatible D-NF and D-Theta bridges over the same capsule. This compatibility is an early instance of the "same interface, different permitted histories" discipline.

Formalization checkpoint: a Lean model should distinguish the strip object, the mono-analyticized strip, the capsule index set, and the two bridge directions. Collapsing these loses exactly the bookkeeping needed later in the log-theta lattice.
Prime-stripBad-prime model in IUT I, Fig. I1.2Interpretive roleLater use
D Pi_v, via tempered coverings at bad places and finite etale coverings at good places. Holomorphic local arithmetic structure attached to the curve data. Base object for D-NF and D-Theta bridges.
D⊢ G_v, via the corresponding Galois-category data. Mono-analyticization of D; keeps the underlying arithmetic-real-analytic information. Processions of D⊢-prime-strips generate tensor packets in IUT III.
F O_Fv^▹ with its natural group action. Frobenioid/monoid interface for divisor-like multiplicative data. Theta links compare F-prime-strips built from theta values and q-parameters.
F⊢ O_Fv^× × q_v^N. Mono-analytic Frobenioid interface retaining unit and q-power data. Input to weakened F⊢× and F⊢×μ variants.
F⊢× O_Fv^×. Unit portion after dropping q-power value data. Appears in the original theta-link preservation statements.
F⊢×μ O_Fv^× / O_Fv^μ. Unit quotient modulo roots of unity. Central weakened portion in Theta×μ, Gaussian, and LGP links.
F⊢×μ + q (O_Fv^× / O_Fv^μ) × q_v^N. Unit quotient together with q-power value data. Tracks the split between unit data and value-group data.

Local analytic sections

Remark 4.3.1 interprets D-prime-strips as arithmetic collections of local analytic sections of Spec(K) -> Spec(F_mod). This is how IUT simulates a global multiplicative subspace and a global canonical generator without an actual global scheme-theoretic section.

Cost of the simulation

The same remark emphasizes that this simulation dismantles the global prime structure into valuation-indexed local pieces. The resulting bookkeeping is not cosmetic: it is why later bridge, label, Frobenioid, and log-shell structures must remain carefully typed.

Single global object

Remark 4.9.2 explains why a D-NF bridge uses one global D-object rather than independent globalizations for each local prime-strip. The single global object synchronizes the capsule index set with the labels 1,...,l* up to the controlled F_l^*/±-action.

Remark 4.9.2synchronization

Why labels must be compared

Remark 4.9.1 says the later theta evaluation produces arithmetic line bundles at different labeled D-prime-strips, and the theory needs to compare their arithmetic degrees as log-volumes. This is the reason for passing to non-labeled output data.

Remark 4.9.1log-volumes

Cyclic versus full symmetry

Proposition 4.9 distinguishes the cyclic F_l^*/±-symmetry retained by the D-NF bridge from the full symmetric-group freedom obtained after forgetting the cyclic label structure of the capsule. These are different comparison regimes.

Proposition 4.9forgetful functor

Processions minimize indeterminacy

After labels are hidden enough to compare different D-prime-strips, IUT I introduces processions to reduce the resulting label indeterminacy without giving up the symmetry needed for comparison. This is the first appearance of the procession discipline used later in IUT III.

Proposition 4.11processions

Global label classes

Proposition 4.7 constructs a unique global cusp class whose images identify the global label-class torsor with the capsule index set. This replaces a fragile local label choice by data native to the global side of the bridge.

Proposition 4.7LabCusp

Torsor/uniqueness split

Proposition 4.8 locates ambiguity precisely: D-NF bridge isomorphisms and compatible gluings form F_l^*/±-torsors, while D-Theta bridge and D-ThetaNF theater isomorphism sets have cardinality one. The wiki treats this as a formalization boundary.

Proposition 4.8ambiguity
Sources: IUT I, Definition 4.1 and Definition 4.6 IUT II, Definition 4.9 IUT III, Definition 1.1 Related: base bridges/evaluation sections Hodge theaters theta/log links Corollary 3.12

IUT I Section 4: Base Bridges and Evaluation Sections

Section 4 develops the multiplicative combinatorial Teichmuller theory used before thetaNF-Hodge theaters are built. It defines D-prime-strips, label-class torsors, model base-NF and base-theta bridges, bad-prime evaluation sections, and processions that reduce label indeterminacy.

Base bridge route Definition 4.1 through Corollary 4.12

D-prime-strips

Definition 4.1 defines a holomorphic base-prime-strip D = {D_v}_{v in V}. At nonarchimedean places the entries are categories equivalent to the local D-data; at archimedean places they are Aut-holomorphic orbispaces. Its mono-analyticization is a D^⊢-prime-strip.

Definition 4.1(i),(iii)prime strip

Label classes of cusps

Definition 4.1(ii) reconstructs the cusp label classes of each local D_v. Each LabCusp(D_v) is an F_l^* / {±1}-torsor and contains the canonical element determined by the initial cusp epsilon.

Definition 4.1(ii)LabCusp

Global label identification

Proposition 4.2 canonically identifies the local LabCusp(D_v) across all valuations by requiring compatibility with the canonical epsilon classes and the torsor structures. This yields LabCusp(D) and a natural bijection with F_l^* / {±1}.

Proposition 4.2canonical labels

Why X_v, not only C_v

Remark 4.2.1 warns that if good-place D_v were defined from C_v instead of the arrow-decorated X_v data, the canonical system of label bijections would fail for suitable decomposition groups.

Remark 4.2.1source choice

Model base-NF bridge

Example 4.3 constructs the model D_j -> D from the automorphism subgroup fixing epsilon. The quotient Aut(C_K)/Aut_epsilon(C_K) is identified with F_l^* / {±1}, producing the label poly-action on the capsule.

Example 4.3NF side

Local analytic sections

Remark 4.3.1 interprets these D-prime-strips as arithmetic collections of local analytic sections of Spec(K) -> Spec(F). They simulate a global multiplicative subspace, but they do not extend to ring homomorphisms, prime-tree splittings, or global Galois sections.

Remark 4.3.1no global section

Bad-place evaluation points

Example 4.4(i) labels cusps of C_v by |F_l| and translates them by the two-torsion point mu_-. These translated cusps are the evaluation points of X_v.

Example 4.4(i)bad places

Theta values and j^2

At a bad-place evaluation point with label j, the value of the Frobenioid-theoretic theta function lies in the mu_{2l}-orbit of q_v^{j^2}. The points above these evaluation points in the theta cover are defined over K_v.

Example 4.4(i)j squared

Evaluation sections

The K_v-rational points over evaluation points determine sections G_v -> Pi_v. Example 4.4 records that these evaluation sections are group-theoretically reconstructible from isomorphs of Pi_v.

Example 4.4(i)sections

Model base-theta bridge

Example 4.4(ii)-(iv) uses the evaluation sections at bad places and full poly-isomorphisms at good places to build the poly-morphism D_j -> D_>. The target label classes in D_> are held fixed by arbitrary automorphisms.

Example 4.4(ii)-(iv)theta side

Transport of label classes

Example 4.5 composes the base-NF and base-theta bridge restrictions to obtain phi_j^LC : LabCusp(D) -> LabCusp(D_>). The initial epsilon class is characterized as the unique class whose evaluations recover the assignments j -> j.

Example 4.5transport

No artificial synchronization

Remark 4.5.1 analyzes independent conjugacy indeterminacies in local and global groups. At bad places, Corollary 2.5 supplies the needed factorization uniqueness without choosing a synchronization that would amount to an impossible global prime-tree splitting.

Remark 4.5.1indeterminacy

D-ThetaNF theater

Definition 4.6 packages a base-NF bridge D <- D_J, a base-theta bridge D_J -> D_>, and a shared capsule index set. Isomorphisms must induce the same bijection on the bridge capsule indices.

Definition 4.6bridge pair

The zeta bijection

Proposition 4.7 constructs the bijection zeta : LabCusp(D) -> J, identifying global cusp labels with capsule indices. Remark 4.7.2 interprets its inverse as a combinatorial Kodaira-Spencer morphism relating arithmetic labels to geometric cusp data.

Proposition 4.7Kodaira-Spencer

Forgetful symmetries

Proposition 4.9 separates three outputs: the D-NF bridge with cyclic F_l^* / {±1}-symmetry, the holomorphic capsule with full symmetric-group freedom after labels are forgotten, and the mono-analytic capsule with the same label-indeterminacy behavior.

Proposition 4.9forget labels

Processions

Definition 4.10 and Proposition 4.11 replace a bare capsule by nested subcapsules 1 <= ... <= j <= ... <= l*. This reduces the label indeterminacy from the possibilities of Proposition 4.9(ii) to l*!, without abandoning comparison symmetry.

Definition 4.10, Proposition 4.11procession

D-ThetaNF etale-picture

Corollary 4.12 defines the D-ThetaNF link by passing a D-ThetaNF theater to its mono-analytic target strip D_>^⊢. Chains of D-linked D-ThetaNF theaters then share this mono-analytic core and admit arbitrary permutation symmetries among spokes.

Corollary 4.12etale-picture
Section 4 objectSource roleWhy it matters later
LabCusp(D) Canonical torsor of label classes, synchronized by epsilon. Prevents labels from becoming arbitrary names.
D <- D_J NF bridge: local analytic sections of the global number-field side. Simulates the missing global multiplicative subspace.
D_J -> D_> Theta bridge: evaluation-section side, especially at bad primes. Feeds Hodge-Arakelov evaluation and later theta values.
zeta Identifies global cusp labels with capsule indices. Supplies globalizability of labels without local ad hoc choices.
Procession Nested subcapsules of the bridge capsule. Minimizes label indeterminacy before log-volume comparison.
Formalization warning: Section 4 should not be modeled as a generic labeled family of prime-strips. The source distinguishes the NF bridge, theta bridge, evaluation sections, global label-class torsor, and procession operation.
Sources: IUT I, Section 4 Related: prime strips and bridges theta evaluation route Galois theta evaluation additive base bridges multiplicative/additive bridges Corollary 3.12 corridor

IUT I Section 6: Additive Base Bridges

Section 6 starts the additive combinatorial Teichmuller theory. Before the Frobenioid lift of Definition 6.11, Mochizuki defines signed additive label structures, D-Theta± bridges, D-Thetaell bridges, their theater, and the functorial route back to ordinary D-Theta bridges.

Additive base route Definition 6.1 through Corollary 6.10
Source itemDefinition or resultFormalization consequence
Definition 6.1(i) F_l^± = F_l ⋊ {±1}. An F_l^±-group is a signed additive group object; an F_l^±-torsor is acted on by affine maps z -> ±z + lambda. The group/torsor distinction is typed data. Translations, signs, and positive automorphisms are separate interfaces.
Definition 6.1(iii) LabCusp±(D_v) has a zero element and a ±-canonical element. Removing zero and quotienting by ± recovers LabCusp(D_v). The zero label is not cosmetic. It must remain present until the explicit nonzero/quotient step.
Definition 6.1(v) D± = B(X_K)^0. From this category one reconstructs the relevant automorphism quotients, the F_l^± torsor of ±-label classes, and V±; Remark 6.1.1 identifies V± = V±un. The additive target is global elliptic geometry, not an arbitrary relabeled D-prime-strip.
Examples 6.2 and 6.3 The model D-Theta± bridge uses positive +-full maps D_t -> D≻. The model D-Thetaell bridge maps D_t -> D± and carries the natural F_l^± poly-action. The two bridge models have the same capsule but different target logic: signed positive maps on one side, elliptic torsor equivariance on the other.
Definition 6.4 A D-Theta±ell theater is the compatible span D≻ <- D_T -> D±. The construction also forms the quotient capsule D_|T| and the nonzero subcapsule used later. A formal model needs the full capsule, the ± quotient, and the nonzero subcapsule as related but distinct objects.
Propositions 6.5 and 6.6 Proposition 6.5 transports ±-label classes through both bridges. Proposition 6.6 identifies the isomorphism and gluing torsors: {±1} x {±1}^V, F_l^±, {±1}, and F_l^± x {±1}^V. These torsor sizes are audit targets: they locate exactly which indeterminacy is supposed to remain.
Proposition 6.7 From a D-Theta± bridge one functorially constructs an ordinary D-Theta bridge by passing to the nonzero quotient part, identifying D_0 and D≻, and replacing +-full maps by the appropriate full/evaluation maps. This is the formal hinge between additive and multiplicative bridge theory; it cannot be replaced by a bare forgetful map.
Propositions 6.8-6.9, Corollary 6.10 Forgetful functors expose F_l^± and symmetric group symmetries, processions reduce the (l±)^(l±) ambiguity to l±!, and D-Theta±ell links produce an etale picture with a mono-analytic core. The wiki keeps labels, quotient capsules, processions, and mono-analytic cores as separate pages because later comparison arguments use different forgetful levels.

Group versus torsor

In Definition 6.4, T is an F_l^±-group for the D-Theta± bridge, but an F_l^±-torsor for the D-Thetaell bridge. The theater uses the torsor structure determined by the group structure.

Definition 6.4typed labels

Zero label

The zero label is the point that lets Section 6 compare zero-labeled and nonzero-labeled prime-strips before the nonzero labels are matched with the multiplicative Section 4 system.

Definition 6.1zero/nonzero

Functorial hinge

Proposition 6.7 is the step that turns the signed additive bridge into a D-Theta bridge. It uses both the quotient capsule and the evaluation maps of Section 4 at bad places.

Proposition 6.7bridge conversion

Additive processions

Proposition 6.9 builds processions from the ordered subsets {0,...,t-1} of |F_l|. This is the additive counterpart of the Section 4 procession discipline.

Proposition 6.9l±!

Additive etale-picture

Corollary 6.10 sends linked D-Theta±ell theaters to full poly-isomorphisms between the associated mono-analytic D_>^⊢ cores. The spokes retain distinct holomorphic structures.

Corollary 6.10mono-analytic core

Modeling warning

A generic signed capsule misses the source distinction between positive +-full maps, the global D± target, the F_l^± torsor action, and the later nonzero quotient used by Proposition 6.7.

formalization boundary
Sources: IUT I, Section 6 Related: multiplicative/additive bridges Theta±ell bridges 6.11 Theta±ellNF 6.13 base bridges/evaluation sections Theta±ell relay

IUT I Definition 6.1: Signed Additive Labels

Definition 6.1 introduces the additive label structure used in Section 6: signed affine motion by F_l^± = F_l ⋊ {±1}, ±-label classes of cusps, positive and negative automorphisms, and the global double cover D± = B(X_K)^0.

Signed additive label data Definition 6.1(i)-(vii)
ClauseSource contentWiki/formalization consequence
6.1(i) F_l^± is the semidirect product F_l ⋊ {±1}. Groups use signed linear coordinates; torsors use signed affine coordinates. Group and torsor pages must not be merged. The bridge definitions use both structures for different purposes.
6.1(ii)-(iv) A D-prime-strip determines D_v^±; LabCusp±(D_v) carries zero, ±-canonical class, and positive/negative automorphism data. The zero label and sign quotient are part of the mathematical object, not merely display conventions.
6.1(v)-(vii) D± = B(X_K)^0 reconstructs automorphism quotients, cusp classes, valuations, and archimedean Aut-holomorphic morphism targets. The global target page must cite elliptic-curve reconstruction and the category-theoretic origin of the torsor, not just label arithmetic.
Guardrail: Definition 6.1 is not a generic additive labeling device. It packages signed affine motion, cusp reconstruction, the zero/nonzero transition, and the global D± target that later receives the Thetaell bridge.
Sources: IUT I, Definition 6.1 Related: Section 6 atlas Theta±/Thetaell models D-Theta±ell base theater zero/nonzero relation

IUT I Examples 6.2-6.3: Model Theta± and Thetaell Bridges

Examples 6.2 and 6.3 give the model objects for the two additive bridge directions. The same capsule D_T is sent upward to a signed D-prime-strip target D≻ and downward to the global elliptic target D±.

Two model bridge directions Examples 6.2 and 6.3
ExampleConstructionWhat must remain visible
6.2(i) The model base-Theta± bridge uses copies D_t indexed by F_l and positive +-full poly-isomorphisms D_t -> D≻. Positivity is not decoration: it controls which signed automorphisms survive in the model.
6.2(ii)-(iii) The order-two operation -1_{F_l} sends t to -t and uses negative local signs; {±1}^V acts by local signed +-full automorphisms. The page separates the global sign operation from placewise sign choices.
6.3 The model base-Thetaell bridge maps the capsule to D±, after fixing a torsor identification LabCusp±(D±) ≃ F_l. The target is the global elliptic object. Its automorphism quotient is identified with F_l^± only after the torsor choice.
Sources: IUT I, Examples 6.2-6.3 Related: additive labels D-Theta±ell base theater Theta±ell Frobenioid lift

IUT I Definition 6.4 and Propositions 6.5-6.6: D-Theta±ell Base Theaters

Definition 6.4 turns the two model bridge directions into the base D-Theta±ell theater. Propositions 6.5 and 6.6 record the label transport and the torsors of isomorphisms and gluing choices.

Compatible additive span Definition 6.4, Proposition 6.5, Proposition 6.6
SourceStatementAudit target
Definition 6.4(i)-(iii) A D-Theta± bridge is D_T -> D≻; a D-Thetaell bridge is D_T -> D±; a D-Theta±ell theater is the compatible span D≻ <- D_T -> D±. Compatibility means both bridge sides induce the same capsule poly-isomorphism, not just the same index cardinality.
Proposition 6.5 The two bridge sides transport ±-label classes by well-defined bijections compatible with F_l^±-group or torsor structures. Label transport is a theorem-level structure for later pages, not an implicit consequence of naming labels the same way.
Proposition 6.6 Isomorphisms and gluing choices form the stated torsors over {±1} x {±1}^V, F_l^±, {±1}, and F_l^± x {±1}^V. These torsors are the additive indeterminacy ledger. They must remain explicit when the Frobenioid lift is introduced.
Sources: IUT I, Definition 6.4 and Propositions 6.5-6.6 Related: additive labels model bridges additive to D-Theta Theta±ell bridges 6.11

IUT I Propositions 6.7-6.10: From Additive Bridges to D-Theta Etale-Pictures

Propositions 6.7-6.10 are the hinge from the additive Section 6 bridge system back to the multiplicative Section 4 D-Theta bridge system. The construction passes through the nonzero quotient capsule, processions, and a mono-analytic etale-picture core.

Additive bridge conversion Proposition 6.7 through Corollary 6.10
ResultMathematical moveWhy this page is separate
Proposition 6.7 A D-Theta± bridge functorially gives a D-Theta bridge by replacing the capsule with the nonzero quotient, identifying D_0 with D≻, and switching to the full/evaluation morphisms required by Section 4. This is not mere forgetting. It is the explicit source bridge between additive and multiplicative combinatorial Teichmuller theories.
Proposition 6.8 Forgetful functors expose F_l^± symmetry on D-Thetaell outputs and S_{l±} symmetry on holomorphic or mono-analytic capsules. The wiki tracks the symmetry level before and after label forgetting.
Proposition 6.9 Additive processions are nested subcapsules indexed by S_t^± = {0,1,...,t-1}. They reduce the displayed ambiguity from (l±)^(l±) to l±!. Processions are the finite combinatorial discipline that makes the bridge comparison auditable.
Corollary 6.10 D-Theta±ell links are full poly-isomorphisms between the mono-analyticized D_>^⊢ cores; the resulting etale-picture admits arbitrary permutation symmetries among spokes. This is the additive analogue of the Section 4 etale-picture and the immediate precursor to the Frobenioid lift of Definition 6.11.
Sources: IUT I, Propositions 6.7-6.9 and Corollary 6.10 Related: D-Theta±ell base Section 4 processions multiplicative/additive bridge comparison Theta±ell bridges 6.11

Multiplicative and Additive Bridge Theory

IUT I separates two combinatorial directions before the later theta-value estimates are attempted. Section 4 develops a multiplicative F_l-label theory for D-ThetaNF theaters. Section 6 develops an additive F_l^±-label theory for D-Theta±ell theaters and then glues the additive and multiplicative sides into Theta±ellNF theaters.

Bridge split in IUT I multiplicative labels, additive labels, and the glued theater
LayerSymmetryBridge/theater objectWhat is synchronized
Multiplicative side F_l-symmetry on labels 1,...,l. D-NF bridge, D-Theta bridge, D-ThetaNF-Hodge theater. Capsule index set, global cusp labels, and number-field/theta-side D-prime-strips.
Additive side F_l^±-symmetry on signed labels including the zero label. D-Theta± bridge, D-Thetaell bridge, D-Theta±ell-Hodge theater. ± cusp labels, elliptic/torsion-side structure, and geometric basepoint data.
Glued side Compatibility of F_l and F_l^± labels. Theta±ellNF-Hodge theater and D-Theta±ellNF-Hodge theater. Nonzero signed labels ±t are identified with the corresponding multiplicative label.
Forgetful side Coarser symmetric-group actions after label structure is hidden. Unlabeled capsules and processions. Enough combinatorics is retained to compare objects without full holomorphic label history.
IUT I source item Data or conclusion Why the wiki separates it
Definition 4.6(i): D-NF bridge A poly-morphism D_J -> D, where D is equivalent to B(C_K)^0 and D_J = {D_j}_{j in J} is a finite capsule of D-prime-strips, modeled on phi^NF. This is the number-field bridge; its isomorphism set is an F_l-torsor, so it carries controlled label ambiguity.
Definition 4.6(ii): D-Theta bridge A poly-morphism D_J -> D>, where D> is a D-prime-strip and D_J is a finite capsule modeled on phi^Theta. This is the theta-side bridge; unlike the D-NF bridge, isomorphisms between D-Theta bridges are unique once the source data is fixed.
Definition 4.6(iii): D-ThetaNF theater A compatible span D <- D_J -> D> whose two arrows are the D-NF and D-Theta bridges and whose morphisms induce the same bijection on capsule indices. Compatibility is not just sharing a domain object; it synchronizes the two bridge histories through the same capsule index set.
Proposition 4.7 The D-Theta side determines a bijection chi : J ~= F_l; the two bridge sides determine isomorphisms of label-class torsors LabCusp(D) ~= LabCusp(D>); a unique global cusp class [epsilon] then identifies LabCusp(D) with J. This is the first precise synchronization theorem: global cusp labels, local capsule indices, and theta labels become one controlled structure.
Proposition 4.8 D-NF bridge isomorphisms and compatible gluings form F_l-torsors; D-Theta bridge and D-ThetaNF theater isomorphisms are unique. The torsor/uniqueness split tells a formalization where label ambiguity lives and where it has already been rigidified.
Additive source item Data or conclusion Why it is not the same as the multiplicative side
Definition 6.1(i): F_l^± F_l^± is the semidirect product F_l ⋊ {±1}. An F_l^±-group has a natural F_l-module structure; an F_l^±-torsor is acted on by affine maps z -> ±z + lambda. The additive side has signs and translations. Its automorphisms include positive automorphisms and full signed automorphisms, not only the multiplicative F_l-torsor action.
Definition 6.1(iii): ±-label classes LabCusp±(D_v) contains a zero class and a ±-canonical class; after removing zero and quotienting by ±, it maps to LabCusp(D_v). The zero label is real data. Forgetting it collapses the additive bridge to a multiplicative-looking object too early.
Definition 6.4(i): D-Theta± bridge A poly-morphism D_T -> D≻, where T is an F_l^±-group. It also defines D_|T| by quotienting indices by ± and D_T from the nonzero part. The quotient capsule keeps track of the passage from signed additive labels to the nonzero unsigned labels used by later comparison with the multiplicative side.
Definition 6.4(ii): D-Thetaell bridge A poly-morphism D_T -> D±, where T is an F_l^±-torsor and D± = B(X_K)^0. This is the elliptic side; its target is tied to the double-cover and additive cusp-label structure rather than to C_K alone.
Definition 6.4(iii): D-Theta±ell theater A compatible span D≻ <- D_T -> D± whose two arrows are the D-Theta± and D-Thetaell bridges and whose morphisms induce the same poly-isomorphism of capsules. The compatibility condition is stronger than sharing labels: it ties the signed group structure and the torsor structure through one capsule.
Propositions 6.5 and 6.6 Proposition 6.5 transports ±-label classes through the two additive bridges. Proposition 6.6 locates the remaining ambiguity: D-Theta± bridge isomorphisms form a {±1} × {±1}^V-torsor, D-Thetaell bridge isomorphisms form an F_l^±-torsor, and D-Theta±ell theater isomorphisms form a {±1}-torsor. These torsors explain why the additive bridge cannot be formalized by reusing the multiplicative D-ThetaNF interface with an extra sign flag.

Multiplicative side: D-ThetaNF

IUT I Definition 4.6 defines a D-NF bridge, a D-Theta bridge, and the D-ThetaNF-Hodge theater that combines them over the same capsule of D-prime-strips. Proposition 4.7 extracts a label bijection J ≃ F_l and a global cusp-label bijection LabCusp(D) ≃ J.

The mathematical role of this side is synchronization: one global object D = B(C_K)^0 prevents independent relabeling of each capsule component. Mochizuki explicitly contrasts this with nonsynchronized label choices in Remark 4.9.2.

IUT I, Definition 4.6Proposition 4.7

Forgetful functors and comparability

Proposition 4.9 records three outputs: the underlying D-NF bridge with simply transitive F_l-symmetry, the unlabeled capsule with full symmetric-group freedom, and the mono-analytic capsule. The point is not to forget information casually, but to expose exactly which comparisons become available after labels are hidden.

Proposition 4.11 then organizes the unlabeled capsule into processions. Processions retain enough order to reduce independent label ambiguity to a factorial procession ambiguity, while preserving the comparisons needed for later log-volume computations.

Proposition 4.9Proposition 4.11

Additive side: D-Theta±ell

IUT I Definition 6.1 introduces F_l^±-groups, F_l^±-torsors, positive automorphisms, and ±-label classes of cusps. Definition 6.4 uses this language to define D-Theta± bridges, D-Thetaell bridges, and D-Theta±ell-Hodge theaters.

Proposition 6.5 supplies the additive analogue of Proposition 4.7: the capsule index set is tied to global ±-label classes of cusps. Proposition 6.8 gives the corresponding F_l^±-symmetry and the coarser symmetric-group symmetry after labels are forgotten.

Definition 6.1Definition 6.4Proposition 6.5

Gluing: Theta±ellNF

Proposition 6.7 constructs a D-Theta bridge from a D-Theta± bridge by a functorial algorithm. Definition 6.13 then glues a Theta±ell-Hodge theater to a ThetaNF-Hodge theater along the associated Theta bridges; the gluing is unique in the sense cited in Remark 6.12.2.

Remark 6.12.6 describes the result as a relay between geometric and arithmetic basepoints. This relay is why the wiki treats the bridge theory as structural, not as peripheral notation.

Proposition 6.7Definition 6.13basepoints
For the IUT formalization project, this page marks a concrete boundary: labels, torsors, capsules, processions, and mono-analyticization must be represented as separate interfaces. A Lean model that identifies them too early cannot express the distinction between synchronized F_l/F_l^± data and unlabeled symmetric-group data.
Sources: IUT I, §§4, 6 Related: prime strips theta/log links log-theta lattice Corollary 3.12 corridor

Theta±ell Bridges in IUT I 6.11

Definition 6.11 is the Frobenioid lift of the additive D-bridge formalism developed earlier in Section 6. It defines Theta±-bridges, Thetaell-bridges, and Theta±ell-Hodge theaters, then Corollary 6.12 reads their isomorphism theory back through the associated D-bridges.

Definition 6.11 additive Frobenioid lift Theta± bridge + Thetaell bridge = Theta±ell-Hodge theater
Source itemDefinition or theoremFormalization consequence
Definition 6.11(i) A Theta±-bridge is a poly-morphism psiTheta± : F_T -> F_≻, where T is an F_l^±-group, lifting a D-Theta± bridge. The quotient capsule F_|T| is obtained by quotienting labels by {±1}. The signed additive labels and the quotient-by-sign capsule must both be represented; the quotient constituents are only well-defined up to positive automorphism.
Definition 6.11(ii) A Thetaell-bridge is a D-Thetaell bridge F_T -> D±, where T is an F_l^±-torsor and D± is equivalent to the additive D± category. Unlike the Theta± bridge, the target is D-level additive geometry, so the bridge is not an F-prime-strip target on both sides.
Definition 6.11(iii) A Theta±ell-Hodge theater is the compatible span F_≻ <- F_T -> D±. Its associated D-data must form a D-Theta±ell-Hodge theater. Compatibility means the two bridge morphisms induce the same poly-isomorphism between their F-prime-strip capsules.
Corollary 6.12(i) Isomorphisms of Theta±-bridges, Thetaell-bridges, and Theta±ell-Hodge theaters map bijectively to isomorphisms of the associated D-bridges and D-theaters. Once the D-level additive bridge data is fixed, the Frobenioid lift has no extra hidden isomorphism choice.
Corollary 6.12(ii) Compatible capsule-+-full gluings of a Theta±-bridge and a Thetaell-bridge form a torsor over F_l^± x {±1}^V. The first factor corresponds to the induced isomorphism of F_l^±-torsors. The gluing ambiguity is larger than the Section 5 F_l-torsor: it includes signed affine label motion and place-indexed sign choices.

Theta± bridge

The Theta± bridge is the F-prime-strip lift of the additive D-Theta± bridge. It is indexed by an F_l^±-group and lands in F_≻.

Def. 6.11(i)signed labels

Thetaell bridge

The Thetaell bridge uses an F_l^±-torsor indexed F-prime-strip capsule but maps to the additive D-category D±.

Def. 6.11(ii)elliptic side

Theta±ell theater

The theater is not a single bridge; it is the compatible gluing of the Theta± and Thetaell bridges over the same F-prime-strip capsule.

Def. 6.11(iii)gluing

Quotient capsules

The notation F_|T| records quotienting labels by the {±1} action; the nonzero subcapsule is the part later compared with the multiplicative label system.

Def. 6.11(i)± quotient

Isomorphism bijection

Corollary 6.12(i) is the additive analogue of the Section 5 rigidity: isomorphisms of lifted bridges are read from the associated D-level bridge isomorphisms.

Cor. 6.12(i)rigidity

Gluing torsor

Corollary 6.12(ii) identifies the gluing choices as an F_l^± x {±1}^V-torsor, not merely an F_l-torsor.

Cor. 6.12(ii)torsor
Sources: IUT I Related: Theta±ell relay multiplicative/additive bridges ThetaNF Frobenioid lift Gaussian Frobenioids

IUT I Definition 6.11 and Corollary 6.12: Theta±ell Frobenioid Lift

Definition 6.11 lifts the additive D-Theta±ell bridge package to F-prime-strips. Corollary 6.12 then says that the lifted isomorphism theory is read bijectively from the associated D-bridges, with gluing choices forming an F_l^± x {±1}^V-torsor.

Lift from D-bridges to F-prime-strip bridges Definition 6.11 and Corollary 6.12
Source itemContentFormalization note
Definition 6.11(i) The Theta± bridge is a poly-morphism F_T -> F_≻ lifting a D-Theta± bridge. It forms F_|T| by quotienting the label set by {±1} and also forms the nonzero subcapsule. The quotient capsule is part of the lifted structure, not a later convenience. Its positive-automorphism ambiguity must be explicit.
Definition 6.11(ii) The Thetaell bridge keeps an F-prime-strip capsule but maps to D±; its associated D-prime-strip capsule is the D-Thetaell bridge. The right-hand target remains D-level. A wiki or Lean model that forces both bridge targets to be F-prime-strips changes the definition.
Definition 6.11(iii) A Theta±ell-Hodge theater is the compatible span F_≻ <- F_T -> D±, whose associated D-data forms a D-Theta±ell-Hodge theater. Compatibility is checked through induced poly-isomorphisms of the F-prime-strip capsules.
Corollary 6.12 Lifted isomorphism sets map bijectively to D-level isomorphism sets, and compatible gluings form an F_l^± x {±1}^V torsor. This is the additive lift analogue of Section 5 rigidity: the new information is the signed gluing torsor, not hidden extra lift choices.
Sources: IUT I, Definition 6.11 and Corollary 6.12 Related: D-Theta±ell base Proposition 6.7 hinge Theta±ellNF gluing

Theta±ellNF Hodge Theaters in IUT I 6.13

Definition 6.13 glues the additive Theta±ell-Hodge theater of Definition 6.11 to the multiplicative ThetaNF-Hodge theater of Definition 5.5. The gluing is unique in the sense of Remark 6.12.2, and the same definition has a D-level version using D-Theta±ell and D-ThetaNF theaters.

Definition 6.13 combined theater additive Theta±ell side glued to multiplicative ThetaNF side
Definition 6.13 itemDataWhy it matters
6.13(i)(a) A Theta±ell-Hodge theater, i.e. the additive theater from Definition 6.11(iii). Supplies the signed additive label system, the zero/nonzero comparison interface, and the geometric basepoint side.
6.13(i)(b) A ThetaNF-Hodge theater, i.e. the multiplicative Frobenioid lift from Definition 5.5(iii). Supplies the NF/theta bridge pair, the number-field construction, and the multiplicative label system.
6.13(i)(c) The necessarily unique gluing isomorphism between these two theaters, cited back to Remark 6.12.2. The combined theater is not a free product of structures; the two sides are connected through the functorial Theta± to Theta algorithm.
6.13(ii) The D-Theta±ellNF-Hodge theater is the analogous triple of a D-Theta±ell-Hodge theater, a D-ThetaNF-Hodge theater, and the unique D-level gluing isomorphism. The D-level shadow must be available because later rigidity and multiradial constructions repeatedly read lifted data through D-theater isomorphisms.

Additive side

The Theta±ell side carries the F_l^± additive symmetry, including the zero label and the sign quotient structure.

Def. 6.13(i)(a)additive

Multiplicative side

The ThetaNF side carries the Section 5 Frobenioid package: the NF bridge, the theta bridge, and their common F-prime-strip capsule.

Def. 6.13(i)(b)multiplicative

Unique gluing

Remark 6.12.2 says the relevant gluing isomorphism is unique, using Proposition 6.7 and the isomorphism rigidity of Corollary 5.6.

Rem. 6.12.2unique

D-level version

Definition 6.13(ii) repeats the construction at D-level. This is the version used when later arguments work only with mono-analytic or D-prime-strip structure.
