Def. 6.13(ii)D-theater

Frobenioid inventory

Figure 6.6 lists the Frobenioids appearing in the combined theater: local F-prime-strip data at places, tempered Frobenioids over D_> at bad places, and global Fmod and F layers.

Fig. 6.6inventory

Relay meaning

Remark 6.12.6 reads the combined theater as a relay between geometric basepoints for theta evaluation and arithmetic basepoints for constructing Fmod.

Rem. 6.12.6(v)relay
Sources: IUT I Related: Theta±ell bridges 6.11 ThetaNF Frobenioid lift Theta±ell relay Gaussian Frobenioids

IUT I Definition 6.13: Theta±ellNF Gluing

Definition 6.13 combines the additive Theta±ell theater with the multiplicative ThetaNF theater. The gluing is along the Theta±/Theta bridge relation supplied by Proposition 6.7 and Remark 6.12.2, and is described by Mochizuki as a relay between geometric and arithmetic basepoints.

Combined additive/multiplicative theater Definition 6.13 with Remarks 6.12.2 and 6.12.6
SourceStatementInterpretive constraint
Remark 6.12.2(i) A Theta bridge portion can be regarded as constructed from a Theta± bridge by the functorial algorithm parallel to Proposition 6.7. The final gluing depends on the explicit additive-to-multiplicative bridge conversion, not on an arbitrary identification of label sets.
Remark 6.12.2(ii) Gluing a Theta±ell-Hodge theater to a ThetaNF-Hodge theater along their associated Theta±/Theta bridges yields the Theta±ellNF-Hodge theater; the gluing isomorphism is unique. The combined theater has one additive component, one multiplicative component, and a specified unique compatibility witness.
Definition 6.13(i)-(ii) The Theta±ellNF theater and its D-level analogue are triples consisting of the two component theaters and their unique gluing isomorphism. The F-level and D-level versions should be linked but not conflated. Later arguments may read only D-level or mono-analytic information.
Remark 6.12.6(v) The combined theater is presented as a relay between geometric basepoints suited to theta evaluation and arithmetic basepoints suited to constructing Fmod. This page records why both additive and multiplicative sides are present before later Hodge-Arakelov and Gaussian Frobenioid material is read.
Sources: IUT I, Remark 6.12.2, Remark 6.12.6, Definition 6.13 Related: Theta±ell lift ThetaNF Frobenioid lift Theta±ell relay Theta±ellNF atlas

Theta±ell Relay

The additive side of IUT I is not merely the multiplicative bridge with signs attached. Section 6 introduces signed additive torsors, zero-labeled prime-strips, global ±-synchronization, and a geometric basepoint tied to X_K. These features are later needed for Hodge-Arakelov theta evaluation.

Additive and multiplicative basepoints Section 6 separates the geometric and arithmetic roles
IUT I source item Precise role Formalization consequence
Definition 6.1(i) F_l ⋊ {±1} acts on F_l by affine maps z -> ±z + lambda. Positive automorphisms are the translation part; full signed automorphisms also include the sign. The additive symmetry should be typed as an affine signed action, not as a cyclic multiplicative torsor with an added Boolean.
Definition 6.1(iii) LabCusp±(D_v) has a zero element and a ±-canonical element. Removing zero and quotienting by ± recovers the ordinary cusp-label set. The zero label must remain explicit until the theory deliberately passes to nonzero/quotiented labels.
Definition 6.1(v) The global additive object is D_o± = B(X_K)^0, a finite etale double cover of D_o = B(C_K)^0. Its automorphisms reconstruct the relevant signed affine structure using the distinguished cusp. Additive labels are anchored in the elliptic cover and its cusp data, while multiplicative labels are anchored in the orbicurve/field-of-moduli side.
Examples 6.2-6.3 The model D-Theta± bridge carries signed full poly-isomorphisms, while the D-Thetaell bridge carries a poly-action of F_l ⋊ {±1} on both the capsule and D_o±. The additive bridge has two interacting layers: signs at places and affine translations of labels.
Remark 6.12.4 Synchronization of labels comes from a single global component/basepoint, via bijections between capsule indices and global ±-label classes of cusps. Independent local relabelings are not source-accurate. A model must express the global origin of the label bijections.
Remarks 6.12.5-6.12.6 The additive symmetry relates the zero-labeled strip to nonzero-labeled strips, but this also means it does not insulate the nonzero labels from the zero label. The glued theater relays between this geometric basepoint and the arithmetic basepoint used to construct F_mod. The later Gaussian layer must remember that nonzero theta values depend on both the additive relation to zero and the multiplicative number-field structure.

Signed affine action

The additive symmetry acts by z -> ±z + lambda. Positive automorphisms are translations; negative automorphisms include the sign reversal.

Def. 6.1(i)affine

±-label classes

A ±-label class is a cusp class of a D-prime-strip lying over a single cusp of the additive cover. It remembers both a zero class and a ±-canonical class.

Def. 6.1(iii)cusps

Global additive object

D_o± = B(X_K)^0 replaces the multiplicative B(C_K)^0 base. The choice of cusp supplies the signed affine structure on global cusp labels.

Def. 6.1(v)X_K

Global ± synchronization

Remark 6.12.4 says the ±-indeterminacies over the various v in V are globally synchronized. This is a prerequisite for the additive translation action to compare labels coherently.

Rem. 6.12.4global sign

Zero/nonzero relation

Remark 6.12.5 identifies the zero label as the feature that lets the additive network compare zero-labeled and nonzero-labeled prime-strips. It is also the source of a later insulation problem for Gaussian data.

Rem. 6.12.5zero label

Gaussian insulation issue

The Gaussian Frobenioids of IUT II use nonzero theta values. The additive theory can relate zero and nonzero strips, but the later multiplicative theory is needed to keep the nonzero Gaussian package from being confused with the zero strip.

Rem. 6.12.5IUT II input

Geometric basepoint

The additive side uses one geometric basepoint: a single connected component of the local elliptic object. This is why it is suited to Hodge-Arakelov theta evaluation.

Rem. 6.12.6(ii)theta evaluation

Arithmetic basepoint

The multiplicative side uses an arithmetic basepoint and F_l^*/± symmetry. This is why it is suited to constructing F_mod.

Rem. 6.12.6(iii)F_mod

Combinatorial resolution of Z

Remark 6.12.6 summarizes the glued theater as a relay between additive and multiplicative basepoints, or as a global combinatorial resolution of the two operations on the ring Z.

Rem. 6.12.6(v)add/mult split
Source warning: the nonzero labels used by the multiplicative side are not simply a subset of the additive theory in ordinary set-theoretic memory. Section 6 relates them through signed torsors, quotienting by ±, and a later gluing to the ThetaNF theater.
Sources: IUT I, Section 6 and Remarks 6.12.4-6.12.6 Related: multiplicative/additive bridges ThetaNF Frobenioid lift Gaussian Frobenioids theta/log links

Mono-Theta Environments in IUT II 1.1

Definition 1.1 of IUT II starts from a mod-N mono-theta environment MTheta. From this object one functorially recovers the topological groups, geometric kernels, interior and exterior cyclotomes, and the cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism needed for Kummer-compatible theta evaluation.

Definition 1.1 recovery package mono-theta data controls both group and cyclotome layers
IUT II source itemContentWhy it matters
Definition 1.1(i) From MTheta one functorially constructs Pi_Y, Pi_X, G, the kernels Delta_MTheta, Delta_Y, Delta_X, the interior object l·DeltaTheta, and Pi_mu. Theta evaluation is not attached directly to labels; it is mediated by a recovered group-and-cyclotome package.
Definition 1.1(ii) The interior cyclotome is l·DeltaTheta; the exterior cyclotome is Pi_mu. The mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism identifies their mod- N reductions. This is the first explicit Kummer bridge in IUT II between the interior theta-section side and the exterior cyclotomic side.
Remark 1.1.1 The rigidity isomorphism may be reconstructed as the difference between the theta-section splitting and an algebraic commutator splitting; the construction is stable under the relevant F_l^± symmetry. The source separates line-bundle-style mono-theta rigidity from the rational-function Kummer classes used earlier for Frobenioids.
Proposition 1.2 Mono-theta environments can be constructed functorially either from a topological group isomorphic to Pi_X^tp or from the tempered Frobenioid associated to the curve. This gives both the etale-like and Frobenioid-like access routes that the later Kummer square compares.
Proposition 1.3 The mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism agrees with the corresponding base-field/Galois cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism. It prevents the mono-theta bridge from introducing an incompatible cyclotome convention.
Propositions 1.4-1.5 The etale theta functions of standard type and projective systems of mono-theta environments give the pro-level version used by later theta-value evaluation. The projective system supplies a stable cyclotome and theta-function package across moduli.

Mono-theta environment

A mod-N mono-theta environment is the input object of IUT II §1. Its structure determines the topological group Pi_MTheta and the theta-section data used for cyclotomic rigidity.

Def. 1.1MTheta

Interior/exterior cyclotomes

The interior cyclotome is the theta-side subquotient l·DeltaTheta; the exterior cyclotome Pi_mu is the kernel of the map to Pi_Y.

Def. 1.1(ii)cyclotomes

Commutator splitting

Remark 1.1.1 reconstructs the rigidity isomorphism from two sections: the theta-section splitting and the algebraic commutator splitting.

Remark 1.1.1splittings

Two construction routes

Proposition 1.2 supplies both a group-theoretic construction from Pi_X^tp and a category-theoretic construction from the tempered Frobenioid.

Prop. 1.2two routes

Cyclotomic compatibility

Proposition 1.3 says the mono-theta and base-field/Galois cyclotomic rigidity isomorphisms coincide under the stated correspondences.

Prop. 1.3compatibility

Projective system

Proposition 1.5 passes to projective systems of mono-theta environments, producing a stable exterior cyclotome and rigidity isomorphism for the later pro-level theta theory.

Prop. 1.5pro-system
Source warning: the mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism is not the same construction as the Frobenioid rational-function Kummer rigidity of IUT I Example 5.1. IUT II explicitly contrasts the line-bundle/curvature-style mono-theta route with the rational-function route.
Sources: IUT II Related: theta evaluation route mono-theta rigidity Theta±ellNF theaters Gaussian Frobenioids multiradiality

IUT II Propositions 1.2-1.5: Mono-Theta Construction Routes

After Definition 1.1, Mochizuki records two ways to construct the mono-theta package, checks cyclotome compatibility, isolates the etale theta functions of standard type, and passes to projective systems. These results are the local input for the later multiradial theta-evaluation machinery.

From local data to projective mono-theta environments Propositions 1.2-1.5
Source resultConstructionWiki/formalization consequence
Proposition 1.2(i) A functorial group-theoretic algorithm constructs a mod N mono-theta environment from a topological group Pi isomorphic to Pi_X^tp, with only the stated mu_N-conjugacy indeterminacy. The group route is not a placeholder for geometry; it is a named reconstruction algorithm with controlled automorphism ambiguity.
Proposition 1.2(ii) A tempered Frobenioid category C associated to X_k also functorially constructs a mono-theta environment, recovering the base tempered category. The wiki separates group and Frobenioid access routes because later Kummer squares compare etale-like and Frobenioid-like structures.
Proposition 1.3 The mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism agrees with the base-field/Galois cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism under the Frobenioid/MLF-Galois pair correspondences. This fixes the cyclotome convention before theta evaluation begins. Two routes are compatible because both coincide with the ordinary scheme-theoretic identification in the model case.
Proposition 1.4 and Remark 1.4.1 From Pi one constructs Pi_Ydd(Pi), lDeltaTheta(Pi), and the set of etale theta functions of standard type. Standard type is characterized by the value condition at the pointed inversion data over mu-. The normalization data is part of the construction. It cannot be replaced by an arbitrary ring-dependent unit choice.
Proposition 1.5 A projective system MTheta_* is uniquely determined up to isomorphism, has constant Pi_X, and yields a projective exterior cyclotome and the exterior theta package theta_env, infty theta_env. Later pages should refer to the projective system when working with pro-level Kummer classes and constant-multiple rigidity.
Sources: IUT II, Propositions 1.2-1.5 Related: mono-theta environment cores and cuspidalizations mono-theta rigidity Galois theta evaluation

IUT II Proposition 1.6: Cores and Elliptic Cuspidalizations

Proposition 1.6 recalls the group-theoretic reconstruction of the core group and elliptic cuspidalizations from a local tempered group. These constructions are later used to recover evaluation points and decomposition groups without choosing a scheme-theoretic coordinate system.

Group-theoretic elementary operations Proposition 1.6 and Remark 1.6.1
Source itemConstructionUse downstream
Proposition 1.6(i) From Pi, construct a topological group Pi_C(Pi) containing Pi as an open subgroup and augmented to Pi/Delta. In the model case this is the core inclusion Pi_X^tp into Pi_C^tp. This gives the core group needed for comparing local curve data through the group alone.
Proposition 1.6(ii) For each positive integer N, construct Pi_U_N(Pi) with a surjection to Pi; in the model case this is the elliptic cuspidalization associated to N-torsion points. This is later used in the theta-evaluation route to reconstruct the decomposition groups attached to evaluation points.
Remark 1.6.1 The core algorithm is described in terms of centralizers of images of open subgroups in outer automorphism groups. Cuspidalization is described through compatible outer isomorphisms between subquotients of Delta. The page treats these as specific group-theoretic algorithms, not as an abstract existence assertion. This matters for later formalization.
Sources: IUT II, Proposition 1.6 Related: mono-theta construction routes Galois theta evaluation evaluation decomposition groups radial/coric framework

IUT II Examples 1.7-1.9: Radial, Coric, and Multiradial Data

Examples 1.7-1.9 introduce the language used to say when constructions can be transported across alien arithmetic holomorphic structures through a shared mono-analytic core. This is the formal source of the later multiradial/uniradial distinction.

Radial data over a common coric core Examples 1.7-1.9 and Remarks 1.7.1, 1.9.1, 1.9.2
Source itemDefinition or exampleWhy it matters
Example 1.7(i)-(ii) A radial environment is a category of radial data R, a category of coric data C, and an essentially surjective functor Phi : R -> C. It is multiradial when Phi is full, and uniradial otherwise. Fullness is the precise condition that the radial algorithm loses no rigidity relevant to gluing over the coric core.
Example 1.7(iii)-(v), Remark 1.7.1 Multiradiality allows switching and parallel transport between fibers over the same coric data. Mochizuki compares this to a fibration equipped with a connection. The wiki uses this page as the reference for "parallel transport across alien spokes"; it is not informal metaphor but source terminology.
Example 1.8 Concrete radial environments include the basic (Pi,G,alpha) -> G environment, TM-pair and unit-pair versions, cyclotomic and co-cyclotomic quotients O^mu, O^xmu, and log-shell versions. These are the actual coric interfaces later used by Corollaries 1.10-1.12 and by the IUT III log-shell machinery.
Example 1.9 A functorial group-theoretic algorithm can be represented by its graph. Depending on the radial environment chosen, the same algorithm may be regarded tautologically as multiradial or as uniradial. This explains why Mochizuki later emphasizes the content of an algorithm, not merely the existence of some reconstruction theorem.
Remarks 1.9.1-1.9.2 The purpose is to compute which output data depends essentially on an arithmetic holomorphic structure and which portion depends only on the mono-analytic core, possibly after explicit indeterminacies are allowed. This is the source-level framework for the debated "history-erasure" or "alien comparison" issues: forgetting is only allowed with a specified radial/coric account of what survives.
Guardrail: a multiradial statement is not a claim that all radial structures are identical. It is a claim that the relevant gluing isomorphisms over the coric core can be lifted or transported in the specified category, with the specified indeterminacy.
Sources: IUT II, Examples 1.7-1.9 Related: mono-theta rigidity multiradiality log-theta lattice Corollary 3.12 corridor

IUT II Section 1: Mono-Theta Rigidity

Corollaries 1.10 through 1.12 turn mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity and constant-multiple rigidity into multiradial data. This is the local source for the distinction between an evaluation route that is already compatible with alien spokes and a base-field route that remains uniradial until log-shells enter.

Rigidity route from radial input to coric unit-quotient data
Source itemStatementWhy it matters
Example 1.7 and Remark 1.9.1 A functor from radial data to coric data is multiradial when it is full; otherwise it is uniradial. The point is to compute which outputs depend essentially on a spoke's arithmetic holomorphic structure and which can be described through the mono-analytic core. This supplies the formal meaning of "multiradial" used throughout the later theta and log-volume arguments.
Corollary 1.10 The mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism sends the interior cyclotome l·DeltaTheta(Pi) to the exterior cyclotome Pi_mu of the mono-theta package and gives a multiradially defined functor relative to the coric G O×mu(G) environment. Roots of unity disappear in the unit quotient O×mu, so mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity is compatible with sharing this coric object across spokes.
Remark 1.10.2 The reason for Corollary 1.10 is not a generic identification: the exterior cyclotome maps trivially into O×mu(G). The formal model should distinguish a genuine coric quotient from a direct equality of cyclotomes across holomorphic structures.
Corollary 1.11 The base-field/Galois cyclotomic rigidity route can be represented multiradially only after allowing specified Aut(G)- or Gamma-orbit indeterminacies. This foreshadows why base-field numerical access is useful but not automatically compatible with alien-spoke comparison.
Corollary 1.12(i)-(ii) Pointed inversion automorphisms and restriction to the subgroup D yield invariant theta subsets and splittings after quotienting by torsion: M_TM×mu x {theta-invariants / torsion}. This is the constant-multiple rigidity input used later to control scalar ambiguity in theta-value comparison.
Remark 1.12.2 The multiradiality depends on constructing theta functions up to a mu_2l-indeterminacy, not up to a larger ring-dependent unit such as a chosen value 2 = 1+1. This is a source-level guardrail: a normalization tied to one ring structure would be uniradial and would fail at the log-shell stage.
Remark 1.12.4 A Kummer-compatible theta-evaluation algorithm must extend to coverings; functoriality then forces it to arise from a section G -> Pi_Ydd(Pi). This is the Galois-evaluation principle used in IUT II Section 2.

Coric unit quotient

Corollary 1.10 works because O×mu(G) has already divided out roots of unity. The exterior cyclotome can be rigidified without reintroducing a spoke-specific unit choice.

Cor. 1.10O×mu

Orbit indeterminacy

Corollary 1.11 permits the cyclotomic comparison through specified orbits. This is weaker than strict rigidity, but it is the price for a multiradial formulation of the base-field route.

Cor. 1.11orbits

Pointed inversion

A pointed inversion automorphism is reconstructed group-theoretically and isolates theta subsets invariant modulo torsion. This is the input for the direct-product splittings of Corollary 1.12.

Cor. 1.12(i)invariants

Constant-multiple split

The split separates a coric component M_TM×mu from a radial theta-orbit component. This is how scalar ambiguity is controlled before theta evaluation.

Cor. 1.12(ii)splitting

mu_2l normalization

The source normalization uses values over the two-torsion point mu- and gives only mu_2l-indeterminacy. A larger ring-dependent unit normalization would not be multiradial.

Rem. 1.12.2normalization

Galois evaluation

Remark 1.12.4 explains why the later evaluation at torsion points is expressed through Galois groups and sections, not through an external analytic choice of values.

Rem. 1.12.4sections
Formalization warning: mono-theta rigidity should be modeled as a package of algorithms, quotient choices, and permitted indeterminacies. A single field named "rigidity isomorphism" would hide the source distinction between multiradial mono-theta rigidity and the initially uniradial base-field route.
Sources: IUT II, Corollaries 1.10-1.12 Related: mono-theta environments Galois theta evaluation theta evaluation route multiradiality

Galois-Theoretic Theta Evaluation in IUT II 2

IUT II Section 2 develops the group-theoretic algorithm for evaluating the etale theta function at l-torsion labels. The route passes through tempered covering reconstruction, subgraph decomposition groups, signed cusp labels, two-torsion translates, theta-value orbits, and the single-basepoint conjugate synchronization needed for simultaneous Kummer theory.

Section 2 evaluation pipeline from tempered coverings to synchronized theta-value orbits
Source itemConstructionConceptual role
Proposition 2.1 Reconstructs the diagram of tempered covering groups Pi_Ydd -> Pi_Y -> Pi_X and its lower-level companion diagram functorially from the local tempered group Pi_v. Supplies the covering ladder on which theta functions and theta values are evaluated.
Remark 2.1.1 Describes the dual-graph chain, the inversion automorphisms, the finite tree Gamma_X, and the one-vertex subgraph Gamma_X^bullet. Identifies the connected subgraphs that later give the single evaluation basepoint.
Proposition 2.2 Constructs decomposition groups Pi_v^bullet subset Pi_vbar subset Pi_v and the inversion-invariant theta-function orbit theta_iota(Pi_v). Turns graph-theoretic components into group-theoretic domains for the evaluation algorithm.
Definition 2.3 Defines the ambient groups Pi_v, Pi_v^±, Pi_v^cor, signed cusp-label classes, and the induced F_l^±-torsor structure. Provides the label system that later matches the additive Theta±ell symmetry.
Corollary 2.4 From a signed cusp label t, constructs the inertia group I_t, decomposition group D_t, the two-torsion decomposition group D_mu-, and the translated evaluation group D_t,mu-, compatibly with F_l^±-symmetry. This is the group-theoretic replacement for choosing torsion-shifted analytic evaluation points.
Corollary 2.5 Restricts etale theta functions to the subgraph group and then to D_t,mu-, producing mu_2l- and mu-orbits theta_|t|(Pi_vdd^gamma) indexed by |t| in |F_l|. This is the actual group-theoretic theta evaluation algorithm.
Remark 2.5.1 Relative to the standard cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism, the label j in |F_l| gives the mu_2l-orbit of the theta value with exponent j^2. This is the source of the square-weighted value profile used in the later Gaussian and log-volume constructions.
Corollary 2.6 Defines M_TM^x on the subgraph and gives zero-label splittings after quotienting by torsion. Supplies the constant-multiple control needed before passing to mono-theta and Gaussian data.
Corollary 2.8 Uses mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity to replace l·DeltaTheta by Pi_mu, producing mono-theta-theoretic theta evaluation and compatible splittings. Converts group-theoretic evaluation into the multiradial mono-theta route.
Corollary 2.9 Uses base-field-theoretic cyclotomes instead of the mono-theta exterior cyclotome. Useful for base-field access, but Remark 2.9.1 says this route is only uniradial until later log-shell modifications.

Tempered covering diagram

Proposition 2.1 reconstructs the covering ladder Pi_Ydd -> Pi_Y -> Pi_X at a bad place. Both squares in the diagram are cartesian, and the construction is functorial in the topological group.

Prop. 2.1coverings

Subgraph decomposition groups

Proposition 2.2 uses the finite tree and one-vertex subgraph of the dual graph to define Pi_vbar and Pi_v^bullet, up to the stated conjugacy.

Prop. 2.2subgraphs

Signed cusp labels

Definition 2.3 recovers LabCusp± from cuspidal inertia groups and equips the relevant label set with its F_l^±-torsor structure.

Def. 2.3labels

Two-torsion translates

Corollary 2.4 constructs the decomposition group for the mu--translate of the cusp that gives rise to I_t. This group is the image of the evaluation section used by theta evaluation.

Cor. 2.4evaluation point

Theta-value orbits

Corollary 2.5 produces orbits theta_|t|(Pi_vdd^gamma) after restriction to the translated decomposition groups. The result depends only on |t|, not the sign representative.

Cor. 2.5orbits

Square-weight value

Remark 2.5.1 identifies the theta-value orbit for j with the orbit of the value whose exponent is j^2. This is source-level data, not later notation.

Rem. 2.5.1j²

Independent conjugacy

Remark 2.5.2 explains why conjugacy indeterminacies acting on cusp inertia groups and on the subgraph groups are independent. Figure 2.1 visualizes the two unsynchronized translation lines.

Rem. 2.5.2indeterminacy

Conjugate synchronization

Remark 2.6.1 says all theta values for |t| in |F_l| are computed relative to one Galois group and one cyclotome arising from one basepoint. This is what allows simultaneous Kummer theory for all theta values.

Rem. 2.6.1single basepoint

Theta±ell compatibility

Remark 2.6.2 identifies the same single-basepoint phenomenon with the basepoint behind the F_l^±-symmetry of D-Theta±ell-Hodge theaters.

Rem. 2.6.2F_l^±

Mono-theta evaluation

Corollary 2.8 is the multiradial route: it applies mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity to express the evaluated theta data through Pi_mu.

Cor. 2.8multiradial

Base-field evaluation

Corollary 2.9 gives the base-field cyclotome version. Remark 2.9.1 marks it as uniradial in this form, so it cannot simply replace the mono-theta route in multiradial arguments.

Cor. 2.9uniradial
Formalization warning: the single-basepoint condition is not cosmetic. The simultaneous Kummer description of all theta values depends on one shared Galois group and one shared cyclotome; independent local evaluations would lose conjugate synchronization.
Sources: IUT II Related: mono-theta environments distinguished subgraph theta evaluation route Theta±ell bridges Gaussian Frobenioids

IUT II Propositions 2.1-2.2: Tempered Covering Ladder and Subgraph Groups

The first step in IUT II Section 2 reconstructs the tempered covering diagram and the subgraph decomposition groups that support theta evaluation. This fixes the connected graph-theoretic basepoint before signed labels and theta values enter.

Covering groups and graph subgroups Proposition 2.1, Remark 2.1.1, Proposition 2.2
Source itemConstructionRole
Proposition 2.1 Reconstructs the diagram of open injections Pi_Ydd -> Pi_Y -> Pi_X and its companion lower row from the tempered group Pi_v. Supplies the covering ladder on which theta functions and theta values are evaluated.
Remark 2.1.1 The special-fiber dual graph is a chain; inversion fixes the central label 0. The finite connected subgraph Gamma_X and the one-vertex subgraph Gamma_X^bullet are reconstructed. This graph segment is the geometric source of the later single-basepoint constraint.
Proposition 2.2 Constructs Pi_vbullet subset Pi_vbar subset Pi_v and an inversion automorphism iota, up to the stated conjugacy indeterminacy, then extracts theta_iota(Pi_v) and infty theta_iota(Pi_v). Converts the graph subobjects into group-theoretic domains and inversion-invariant theta-function orbits.
Sources: IUT II, Proposition 2.1 through Proposition 2.2 Related: Section 2 atlas signed cusp labels theta evaluation orbits

IUT II Definition 2.3: Signed Cusp Labels and Ambient Groups

Definition 2.3 builds the ambient local groups, cusp inertia classes, and signed label torsors used by the theta-evaluation algorithm. It is the local IUT II version of the additive label discipline introduced in IUT I.

Local signed cusp-label package Definition 2.3
ClauseDefinitionDownstream use
2.3(i) Defines the local diagram Delta_v -> Delta_v± -> Delta_vcor and Pi_v -> Pi_v± -> Pi_vcor, with profinite completions and conjugacy conventions. Establishes the ambient containers in which cusp labels and conjugacy indeterminacies live.
2.3(ii)-(iii) Reconstructs cuspidal inertia groups and defines LabCusp±(Pi) via commensurators in the larger signed group. Makes signed labels group-theoretic rather than analytic choices of cusps.
2.3(iv) A label t determines a unique vertex and hence a decomposition group Pi_vbullet,t subset Pi_vbar subset Pi_v. Connects the signed label to the subgraph on which evaluation will occur.
2.3(v) For signed ambient groups, the quotient Pi_cor / Pi_± acts on labels and determines an outer isomorphism to F_l^±. Supplies the compatibility with IUT I additive F_l^±-symmetry.
Sources: IUT II, Definition 2.3 Related: IUT I additive labels tempered covering ladder theta evaluation orbits

IUT II Corollaries 2.4-2.6: Theta Evaluation Orbits and Synchronization

Corollaries 2.4-2.6 turn signed cusp labels into group-theoretic evaluation points, restrict the etale theta function to those points, and isolate the single-basepoint conjugate synchronization needed for simultaneous Kummer theory.

From labels to synchronized theta-value orbits Corollaries 2.4-2.6 and Remarks 2.5.1-2.6.3
Source itemConstructionConstraint
Corollary 2.4 From I_t and a compatible conjugate of the subgraph group, constructs D_t, D_mu-, and D_t,mu-, compatibly with F_l^±-symmetry. This replaces analytic evaluation-point choice by group-theoretic decomposition groups.
Corollary 2.5 Restricts theta functions first to Pi_vdd and then to D_t,mu-, producing mu_2l- and mu-orbits theta_|t| and infty theta_|t|. The resulting orbit depends only on the absolute label |t|, but the label discipline is not erased.
Remarks 2.5.1-2.5.2 The theta values correspond to the special collection q_v^(j^2). Independent conjugacy indeterminacies act on inertia and subgraph-group conjugates. The construction must be modeled as two independent conjugacy actions inside one ambient container, not as a single synchronized relabeling.
Corollary 2.6 and Remarks 2.6.1-2.6.2 Zero-labeled evaluation gives splittings compatible with Corollary 1.12. Conjugate synchronization means all labels are evaluated relative to one Galois group and one cyclotome. This is why the additive F_l^±-symmetry is compatible with theta evaluation whereas the multiplicative F_l-symmetry would crush labels.
Remark 2.6.3 The connected subgraph is distinguished by qualitative constraints, by maximizing the average involving j^2, and by compatibility with the global product formula. The value set is not arbitrary sampling of theta values; the selected subgraph is tied to later global realified Frobenioids.
Sources: IUT II, Corollaries 2.4-2.6 Related: signed cusp labels distinguished subgraph tempered Gaussian monoids

IUT II Definition 2.7 and Corollaries 2.8-2.9: Mono-Theta and Base-Field Evaluation

The final part of Section 2 repeats theta evaluation after replacing the interior cyclotome by either the mono-theta exterior cyclotome or the base-field cyclotome. Mochizuki explicitly distinguishes the multiradial mono-theta route from the useful but initially uniradial base-field route.

Two cyclotome replacements Definition 2.7, Corollary 2.8, Corollary 2.9
SourceConstructionStatus
Definition 2.7 For a projective mono-theta environment MTheta_* with Pi_X(MTheta_*) ~= Pi_v, defines the inverse limit group, the subgraph inverse images, and the restricted cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism lDeltaTheta(MTheta_*dd) ~= Pi_mu(MTheta_*dd). Sets up the mono-theta replacement of the coefficient cyclotome.
Corollary 2.8 Applies the mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism to produce theta_env and infty theta_env orbits, including the zero-label splitting. This route relates group-theoretic theta evaluation to Frobenioid-theoretic theta functions from tempered Frobenioids.
Remarks 2.8.1-2.8.3 Corollaries 2.5-2.6 are the special case where MTheta_* comes from Pi_v. The chosen graph segment uses discrete rigidity and is dual to cyclotomic rigidity. Explains why the mono-theta version is the bridge to Frobenioid theta functions, not a redundant restatement.
Corollary 2.9 Replaces lDeltaTheta by the base-field cyclotome mu_Z(G_v), producing theta_bs and infty theta_bs versions of the evaluation algorithm and zero-label splittings. Useful because it gives access to the valuation map H^1(G_v,mu_Z(G_v)) -> Z.
Remark 2.9.1 The mono-theta cyclotome route has a multiradial formulation; the base-field route is only uniradial unless one later applies log-shell modifications with mild indeterminacies. This is a major source-level guardrail for the wiki: base-field numerical access and multiradial alien comparison are different interfaces.
Sources: IUT II, Definition 2.7 and Corollaries 2.8-2.9 Related: theta evaluation orbits tempered Gaussian monoids log-theta lattice theta/log links

IUT II Remark 2.6.3: Distinguished Subgraph

Remark 2.6.3 explains why the theta-value collection used later is not an arbitrary finite sample of labels. Connectedness, the zero-label splitting, independent conjugacy indeterminacy, the averaging quantity ||Gamma'||, and the product formula single out the full subgraph Gamma_Ydd.

Subgraph selection argument from local basepoint discipline to product-formula compatibility
Source itemStatementFormalization consequence
Remark 2.6.3(i) A replacement subgraph Gamma' must be connected because the theta evaluation algorithm is performed relative to a single basepoint. A formal model should not allow independent basepoints for independently selected theta labels.
Remark 2.6.3(i) The same Gamma' must contain the vertex labeled 0, since the splittings of Corollary 2.6(ii) use the zero-labeled evaluation point. Constant-multiple rigidity and theta-value sampling share the same subgraph constraint.
Remark 2.6.3(ii) Independent conjugacy indeterminacies force selection by fibers over |F_l|: one cannot restrict to a single chosen representative without the other representatives in the same fiber. The label interface must encode fiber-wise indeterminacy, not a free list of evaluation points.
Remark 2.6.3(ii)-(iii) The average ||Gamma'|| is monotone in the projected label count, reaches its maximum at l, and the infinite case adds no larger value. The later height optimization should refer to the distinguished full label collection, not to an arbitrary truncation.
Remark 2.6.3(iv)-(v) Multi-element fibers are incompatible with globalization by the product formula, so the subgraph Gamma_Ydd is singled out for the collection q_v^{j^2}. The passage from local theta evaluation to global Gaussian Frobenioids must preserve product-formula compatibility as a separate constraint.

Single-basepoint condition

The connectedness condition is not graph-theoretic decoration. It is the local geometric form of using one fundamental group, one Galois group, and one cyclotome for simultaneous Kummer theory.

Rem. 2.6.1connected

Zero-vertex condition

Corollary 2.6(ii) identifies zero-labeled theta-value orbits with the identity orbit after quotienting by torsion. Any candidate subgraph for the evaluation collection must retain this zero-labeled reference point.

Cor. 2.6(ii)zero label

Fiber indeterminacy

The source restriction is fiber-wise over |F_l|. This is how Remark 2.5.2 reappears inside the height-relevant subgraph discussion.

Rem. 2.5.2fibers

Average ||Gamma'||

The quantity averages minimum square representatives across projected label classes. Mochizuki uses its monotonicity to justify why the full projected set is optimal for the later height comparison.

Rem. 2.6.3(ii)height

Product-formula test

Globalization in IUT II Section 4 imposes product-formula compatibility. This removes the remaining fiber-rich alternatives even when the local average has already reached its maximum.

Rem. 2.6.3(iv)global

Distinguished collection

The resulting collection is the full set of values q_v^{j^2} determined by Gamma_Ydd. This is the input later treated as a Gaussian distribution, not an optional choice of sample.

Rem. 2.6.3(v)q^{j²}
Formalization warning: the subgraph argument must carry both local conditions and the later product-formula constraint. A record that only stores a finite list of labels would erase why this collection is distinguished.
Sources: IUT II, Remark 2.6.3 Related: Galois theta evaluation Theta±ell bridges Tempered Gaussian monoids Global Gaussian Frobenioids

Tempered Gaussian Monoids in IUT II 3

IUT II Section 3 converts the synchronized theta-value data of Section 2 into theta monoids, constant monoids, value-profile-indexed Gaussian monoids, and Frobenioid-theoretic Gaussian monoids. The section is also where Mochizuki explicitly separates the multiradial split-theta route from the uniradial constant-monoid route.

Section 3 monoid route theta monoids, constant monoids, and Gaussian value-profiles
Source itemConstructionWiki consequence
Proposition 3.1(i) A projective system of mono-theta environments gives split theta monoids Psi_env and infty Psi_env, together with conjugation actions and splittings up to torsion. These are the multiradial-friendly theta containers that should not be collapsed to bare numerical values.
Proposition 3.1(ii) The same system gives a constant monoid Psi_cns, naturally identified with the local base-field monoid O_Fv^triangleright. This supplies the constant part used by value-profiles, but it is the route that later carries the uniradial warning.
Example 3.2, Proposition 3.3 A tempered Frobenioid gives Frobenioid-theoretic theta monoids and constant monoids. Kummer classes identify these with the corresponding mono-theta-theoretic monoids. The wiki keeps the Frobenioid and mono-theta routes separate until the Kummer identification is invoked.
Proposition 3.4 Split theta monoids admit a multiradial formulation compatible with the O×mu-theta link. Constant monoids, as constructed here, only admit a uniradial formulation. This is the source-level boundary that later log-shell machinery must address before constant data can participate safely in multiradial comparison.
Corollary 3.5(i) The F_l^± symmetries provide symmetrizing isomorphisms among label-indexed copies, identifying t and -t while retaining the label discipline. Label synchronization is not label erasure; the value-profile product still ranges over the relevant label classes.
Corollary 3.5(ii) A value profile xi is a label-indexed element of the product of theta-value monoids. The Gaussian monoids Psi_xi and infty Psi_xi are generated from the diagonal constant part and powers or rational powers of xi. This is the local Gaussian object that later feeds global Gaussian Frobenioids and log-volume comparison.
Corollary 3.5(iii), 3.6 The zero-label/constant diagonal gives compatible splittings up to torsion. Corollary 3.6 transfers the construction to Frobenioid-theoretic Gaussian monoids Psi^{F xi}. The Frobenioid Gaussian object is not a new axiom: it is the image of the mono-theta Gaussian construction under the previous comparison data.

Split theta monoids

Proposition 3.1 constructs split theta monoids from the theta subsets and constant part of the mono-theta environment. Their splittings are only canonical after quotienting by torsion.

Prop. 3.1(i)split

Constant monoids

Constant monoids record the base-field monoid side of the same data. They are indispensable for Gaussian profiles, but Proposition 3.4 marks their initial formulation as uniradial.

Prop. 3.1(ii)constant

Frobenioid theta monoids

Example 3.2 constructs theta monoids from the Frobenioid-theoretic theta function and the base-field-theoretic hull. This is the Hodge-theater source route before group-theoretic reconstruction.

Example 3.2Frobenioid

Kummer theta-monoid isomorphisms

Proposition 3.3 uses Kummer classes to identify Frobenioid-theoretic theta and constant monoids with the mono-theta versions, compatibly with the relevant actions and splittings.

Prop. 3.3Kummer

Multiradial split-theta route

Proposition 3.4 says the split theta monoids are multiradial and compatible with the O×mu-version of the theta link.

Prop. 3.4(i)multiradial

Uniradial constant route

The constant monoids are only uniradial at this stage because they depend on the base-field cyclotomic rigidity route. Later log-shell machinery is introduced precisely to change this situation.

Prop. 3.4(ii)warning

Symmetrizing isomorphisms

Corollary 3.5 synchronizes label-indexed copies by the F_l^± action. The point is controlled symmetry, not a collapse of all labels to one undifferentiated copy.

Cor. 3.5(i)labels

Value profiles

A profile xi records one theta-value element for each absolute label class. Mochizuki records the finite cardinality of the value-profile set as (2l)^l.

Cor. 3.5(ii)profiles

Gaussian monoids

The monoids Psi_xi and infty Psi_xi are generated by the diagonal constant part and powers or rational powers of the value profile.

Cor. 3.5(ii)Gaussian

Zero-label splittings

Corollary 3.5(iii) uses the zero label and constant diagonal to obtain splittings up to torsion, compatible with the splittings from Proposition 3.1.

Cor. 3.5(iii)splitting

Frobenioid Gaussian monoids

Corollary 3.6 defines Psi^{F xi} as the Frobenioid-theoretic image of the mono-theta Gaussian construction. The diagram realizes the Galois evaluation principle from Remark 1.12.4.

Cor. 3.6Frobenioid

Post-anabelian forgetting

Remark 3.6.2 identifies the displayed comparison as the concrete realization of Galois evaluation. Only after this construction is in place does the post-anabelian forgetting of construction history become meaningful.

Rem. 3.6.2forgetful step
Formalization warning: the Section 3 route has two distinct axes. The split-theta axis is the multiradial route compatible with the O×mu-theta link; the constant-monoid axis is needed for Gaussian value-profiles but is only uniradial at this point of the paper.
Sources: IUT II, Section 3 Related: Galois theta evaluation mono-theta environments Gaussian Frobenioids theta/log links multiradiality

IUT II Proposition 3.1: Theta Monoids and Constant Monoids

Proposition 3.1 starts from a projective system of mono-theta environments and constructs two different monoid packages: split theta monoids generated by theta orbits, and constant monoids obtained from the base-field cyclotome route.

Mono-theta environment to monoids Proposition 3.1(i)-(ii)
ClauseConstructionGuardrail
3.1(i) From MTheta_*, constructs submonoids Psi_env and infty Psi_env in a direct limit of cohomology modules, generated by theta or infinite-theta subsets and the unit part M_TM^x. The theta monoids carry a natural conjugation action and splittings only up to torsion; they are not bare lists of theta values.
3.1(ii) Uses the cyclotomic rigidity isomorphisms of Corollaries 2.8 and 2.9 and the inverse image of Z subset Z to construct Psi_cns(MTheta_*), the monoid of constants. This constant monoid is naturally isomorphic to the local base-field monoid O_Fv^triangleright and is needed before Gaussian profiles can be defined.
Sources: IUT II, Proposition 3.1 Related: mono/base-field evaluation Frobenioid comparison Gaussian value profiles

IUT II Example 3.2 and Proposition 3.3: Frobenioid Theta Comparison

Example 3.2 recalls how a tempered Frobenioid constructs theta and constant monoids. Proposition 3.3 then identifies these Frobenioid-theoretic monoids with the mono-theta monoids of Proposition 3.1 by Kummer classes.

Frobenioid side and Kummer identification Example 3.2, Proposition 3.3
SourceContentWiki consequence
Example 3.2(i) A Frobenioid-theoretic theta function determines Psi_FTheta and infty Psi_FTheta inside the unit group of a birationalized characteristic pro-object. The Frobenioid route has its own theta monoids before any group-theoretic reconstruction is invoked.
Example 3.2(ii) The base-field-theoretic hull C_v determines Psi_Cv, again reconstructible category-theoretically from the tempered Frobenioid up to inner automorphism. Constant monoids have a Frobenioid source, not just a mono-theta cohomological description.
Proposition 3.3(i) Kummer classes identify the Frobenioid-theoretic theta monoids with Psi_env and infty Psi_env, compatibly with conjugation actions and splittings up to torsion. This is the first explicit bridge between Frobenioid theta functions and the mono-theta theta monoids constructed from Section 2.
Proposition 3.3(ii) Kummer classes also identify Psi_Cv with Psi_cns(MTheta_*). The constant part needed for Gaussian profiles has a controlled Frobenioid-to-mono-theta comparison.
Sources: IUT II, Example 3.2 and Proposition 3.3 Related: theta/constant monoids multiradial boundary Gaussian Frobenioid categories

IUT II Proposition 3.4: Multiradial Split Theta and Uniradial Constants

Proposition 3.4 is a source-level boundary marker. The split theta monoid algorithm is compatible with the O×mu version of the theta link and is multiradial; the constant monoid algorithm depends on base-field cyclotomic rigidity and is only uniradial at this point.

Two algorithms, different radial status Proposition 3.4 and Remark 3.4.1
ClauseAssertionFormalization warning
3.4(i) The split theta monoid algorithms are compatible with automorphisms of the pair consisting of the group action and the torsion-quotiented unit monoid (Psi_FTheta)^xmu. This is the route that may be treated as multiradially defined at this stage of IUT II.
3.4(ii) The constant monoid algorithm depends on the cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism of Corollary 1.11 and therefore fails the corresponding compatibility. The constant monoid should not be silently promoted to a multiradial object before the later log-shell modifications.
Remark 3.4.1 Mochizuki identifies this as compatibility versus incompatibility with the O×mu-version of the theta link, and points forward to log-shell algorithms that recover multiradiality at a cost. The wiki separates numerical/base-field access from alien-comparison invariance instead of merging them into one monoid interface.
Sources: IUT II, Proposition 3.4 and Remark 3.4.1 Related: Frobenioid theta comparison Gaussian value profiles theta/log links

IUT II Corollary 3.5: Gaussian Value Profiles and Label Synchronization

Corollary 3.5 applies the signed-label synchronization of Section 2 to construct Gaussian monoids. A value profile records one theta value for each absolute label class; the construction keeps the bijection with labels while using F_l^±-symmetry to synchronize the copies.

From synchronized labels to Gaussian monoids Corollary 3.5 and Remarks 3.5.1-3.5.3
SourceConstructionConstraint
3.5(i) The geometric F_l^±-outer action induces symmetrizing isomorphisms between labeled copies of G_v and Psi_cns. The resulting inner automorphism indeterminacy is independent of |t|. Synchronization is achieved without sending the set of labels to one coarse point.
3.5(ii) A value profile xi is an element of the product of theta-value monoids over absolute labels. The Gaussian monoids Psi_xi and infty Psi_xi are generated by diagonal units and integral or rational powers of xi. The Gaussian object records a synchronized distribution of theta values, not a single theta value.
3.5(iii) The zero-labeled copy of the constant monoid is identified with the diagonal constant submonoid, giving splittings up to torsion for the Gaussian monoids. The zero-label reference from Section 2 remains active inside the Gaussian construction.
Remarks 3.5.2-3.5.3 Mochizuki contrasts symmetrizing descent with naive label forgetting and states the simultaneous requirements: symmetrizing isomorphisms, bijective link with labels, and one basepoint. This is a direct source guardrail for the wiki: label synchronization must never be modeled as label erasure.
Sources: IUT II, Corollary 3.5 and Remarks 3.5.1-3.5.3 Related: theta evaluation orbits theta/constant monoids Gaussian Frobenioid categories

IUT II Corollary 3.6: Frobenioid-Theoretic Gaussian Monoids

Corollary 3.6 transports the Gaussian monoids of Corollary 3.5 back to the tempered Frobenioid side. The comparison keeps label synchronization, diagonal constants, and splittings up to torsion compatible across the Frobenioid, mono-theta, and Gaussian descriptions.

Frobenioid Gaussian transfer Corollary 3.6(i)-(iii)
ClauseConstructionConstraint
3.6(i) The comparison of Proposition 3.3 gives labeled morphisms from Frobenioid constants to Psi_cns and carries the same F_l^±-symmetrizing isomorphisms. The inner automorphism ambiguity is still independent of the label.
3.6(ii) For each value profile, defines Psi_Fxi(F_v) and infty Psi_Fxi(F_v) as the Frobenioid-side images of the Gaussian monoids of Corollary 3.5. The chain is Frobenioid theta monoid -> mono-theta theta monoid -> mono-theta Gaussian monoid -> Frobenioid Gaussian monoid.
3.6(iii) The diagonal Frobenioid constant submonoid gives the zero-label identification and splittings up to torsion for Psi_Fxi(F_v). The Frobenioid Gaussian construction inherits the zero-label discipline from Section 2 rather than rebuilding it independently.
Sources: IUT II, Corollary 3.6 Related: Gaussian value profiles Galois evaluation local Frobenioids

IUT II Remarks 3.6.1-3.6.5: Galois Evaluation and Forgetting

Remarks 3.6.1-3.6.5 explain the conceptual role of Corollary 3.6: Kummer theory compares Frobenioid theta monoids with group-theoretic theta monoids, Galois evaluation forms theta values, and a final forgetting step produces post-anabelian abstract monoids.

Kummer, evaluation, post-anabelian forgetting Figure 3.1 and Remarks 3.6.1-3.6.5
RemarkPointUse in the wiki
3.6.1-3.6.2 Corollary 3.6 realizes Galois evaluation: Kummer comparison, evaluation to theta values, then forgetting the anabelian construction to obtain post-anabelian monoids. The wiki keeps these three arrows separate because each carries different invariance information.
3.6.3 Non-ring/scheme-theoretic filters such as the theta link and log-wall produce distinct unrelated basepoints; ordinary Frobenioid power morphisms only produce inner automorphism ambiguity. Basepoint management is mathematical content, not implementation decoration.
3.6.4 The mono-theta environment is a narrow bridge. For theta-link comparison, only the first power of the reciprocal theta root crosses through cyclotomic rigidity; higher powers are formed after that crossing. This prevents the wiki from treating arbitrary powers as equally primary comparison data.
3.6.5 Cyclotomes are treated as local skeleta of arithmetic holomorphic structures; cyclotomic rigidity and constant-multiple rigidity are local uniformization constraints. This explains why cyclotomic choices matter later at the log-wall.
Sources: IUT II, Remarks 3.6.1-3.6.5 Related: Frobenioid Gaussian monoids post-anabelian Gaussians synchronization/log-wall

IUT II Corollary 3.7: Group-Theoretic to Post-Anabelian Gaussian Monoids

Corollary 3.7 starts with the full poly-isomorphism between the group-theoretic mono-theta system and the Frobenioid-derived system. It then transports Gaussian monoids through this comparison and records that the resulting algorithms remain uniradial until the log-shell modifications of IUT III.

Group-theoretic input to post-anabelian Gaussian output Corollary 3.7 and Remark 3.7.1
ClauseContentBoundary
3.7(i) The full poly-isomorphism induces compatible isomorphisms from the group-theoretic Gaussian monoids to the Frobenioid-theoretic Gaussian monoids and their unit submonoids. This gives the post-anabelian output of the Section 3 comparison.
3.7(ii) The algorithms Pi_v -> Psi_gau and Pi_v -> infty Psi_gau depend on the cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism used in Proposition 3.1 and Corollary 3.5. As given in IUT II, they only admit a uniradial formulation.
Remark 3.7.1 Mochizuki points to IUT III log-shell theory as the modification that yields manifestly multiradial Gaussian-monoid algorithms, with mild indeterminacies. The wiki marks this as a future source step, not as already solved in Section 3.
Sources: IUT II, Corollary 3.7 and Remark 3.7.1 Related: Galois evaluation local Frobenioids log-theta lattice

IUT II Definition 3.8: Local Frobenioids of Monoid Type Z

Definition 3.8 turns the monoids with Galois action constructed in Section 3 into local Frobenioids. Constant monoids yield Frobenioids with realified divisor monoids, while theta and Gaussian monoids yield split Frobenioids whose divisor monoids are generated by the theta/Gaussian data.

Monoids with action become local Frobenioids Definition 3.8(i)-(iii), Remarks 3.8.1-3.8.2
ClauseDefinitionInterpretation
3.8(i) Constant monoids with Pi_X-action give F_cns and identify the base-field hull Frobenioid with its constructed version. The monoid comparison of Proposition 3.3 becomes an isomorphism of Frobenioids.
3.8(ii) Theta and Gaussian monoids with group action give split local Frobenioids F_env, F_gau, and their Frobenioid-theoretic counterparts. The Corollary 3.6 chain becomes a chain of split Frobenioids.
3.8(iii) Direct-product constructions produce Gaussian distributions, while diagonal embeddings produce constant distributions. This is the local categorical precursor of the global Gaussian Frobenioids of Section 4.
Remarks 3.8.1-3.8.2 Category isomorphisms are isomorphism classes of equivalences; labeled Frobenioids require label-independent inner ambiguity. The Section 3 construction sits between F_l^±- and F_l-symmetry frameworks. When label synchronization becomes ambiguous, the source recommends returning to the monoid formulation.
Sources: IUT II, Definition 3.8 and Remarks 3.8.1-3.8.2 Related: Frobenioid Gaussian monoids global Gaussian Frobenioids synchronization/log-wall

IUT II Remark 3.8.3: Conjugate Synchronization and the Log-Wall

Remark 3.8.3 states what Section 3 gains from conjugate synchronization and why this gain is needed at the log-wall. The crucial output is not just diagonal bookkeeping, but the unit isomorphism used when composing the theta link with the Kummer and Galois-evaluation comparison.

Synchronization gains needed for log-wall Kummer theory Remark 3.8.3(i)-(iii)
Remark 3.8.3 itemClaimConsequence
(i) Synchronization supplies constant-monoid isomorphisms, diagonal constant submonoids, zero-label/diagonal identifications, and the unit isomorphism in the Corollary 3.6 comparison. Mochizuki identifies the unit isomorphism as the main application for composing with the theta link.
(ii) At the log-wall, one must pass from Frobenioid-theoretic monoids to etale-like counterparts by Kummer theory, using cyclotomes in the unit groups. Without synchronization, one only gets Galois invariants or orbit sets of constants, which are insufficient for Kummer theory.
(ii)-(iii) Passing to Galois orbits is incompatible with the additive/ring structure needed inside each Hodge theater for the log-wall. This is a source-level reason the wiki treats cyclotome and label synchronization as structural data, not as cosmetic notation.
Sources: IUT II, Remark 3.8.3 Related: Galois evaluation local Frobenioids theta/log links log-theta lattice

Gaussian Frobenioid Categories in IUT II 3.6-3.8

After constructing local Gaussian monoids, Mochizuki explains the categorical status of the construction: Kummer theory compares Frobenius-like and etale-like structures, Galois evaluation produces Gaussian monoids, a post-anabelian forgetting step removes the construction history, and Definition 3.8 turns the resulting monoids with group action into local Frobenioids.

From theta monoids to Frobenioid categories Remarks 3.6.2-3.8.3 and Definition 3.8
Source itemConstruction or diagnosisWhy it matters
Remark 3.6.2 Figure 3.1 decomposes the route into Kummer comparison, Galois evaluation, and a final post-anabelian forgetting step. The wiki records all three arrows separately; a post-anabelian monoid is meaningful only after the construction path has been exhibited.
Remark 3.6.2(ii) Etale-like structures are functorial and anabelian-rigid; Frobenius-like monoids are less rigid and therefore can support non-ring/scheme-theoretic filters such as the theta link and log-wall. IUT needs both sides. Treating one side as merely redundant destroys the logic of the comparison.
Remark 3.6.2(iii) Composing the theta link with Kummer, Galois evaluation, and forgetting gives the distinguished correspondence q_v -> {q_v^(j^2)}. Mochizuki interprets this as an abstract combinatorial Frobenius lifting, not as an arbitrary exponent profile.
Remark 3.6.3 Non-ring/scheme-theoretic filters produce distinct unrelated basepoints. Ordinary Frobenioid N-th power morphisms are milder and only give inner automorphism ambiguity. Basepoint bookkeeping is part of the mathematical content, especially once the log-wall enters in IUT III.
Remark 3.6.4 The mono-theta environment is a narrow bridge between Frobenius-like and etale-like worlds. The first power of the reciprocal theta root is the power that crosses this bridge via cyclotomic rigidity. Arbitrary powers/roots occur in theta and Gaussian monoids, but the bridge used by theta-link comparison is controlled by the first-power Galois evaluation.
Corollary 3.7 Group-theoretic Gaussian monoids map to post-anabelian Frobenioid Gaussian monoids, but the resulting algorithms depend on cyclotomic rigidity in a way that is only uniradial at this stage. Remark 3.7.1 says IUT III log-shells modify these algorithms to obtain multiradial computation, with mild indeterminacy.
Definition 3.8 Monoids with Galois action give local Frobenioids of monoid type Z: constant Frobenioids F_cns, theta Frobenioids F_env, and Gaussian Frobenioids F_gau. This is the precise categorical upgrade from monoid data to local Frobenioid categories used by the global Gaussian layer.
Remarks 3.8.1-3.8.3 Category isomorphisms are isomorphism classes of equivalences; the labeled Frobenioids must be synchronized up to an inner automorphism independent of the label; Galois invariants or Galois orbits are insufficient for Kummer theory through the log-wall. This is the exact place where label synchronization, not label erasure, becomes a necessary condition for later log-wall compatibility.

Kummer, Galois, forget

Remark 3.6.2 separates the comparison into three operations: Kummer identifies Frobenius-like theta monoids with etale-like theta monoids, Galois evaluation forms theta values, and forgetting produces post-anabelian abstract monoids.

Rem. 3.6.2Fig. 3.1

Etale/Frobenius complementarity

Etale-like groups carry functoriality and anabelian rigidity; Frobenius-like monoids are less rigid and can therefore host the non-scheme-theoretic filters.

Rem. 3.6.2(ii)two sides

Combinatorial Frobenius lift

The correspondence q_v -> {q_v^(j^2)} is described as a homotopy between the identity and a highly non-scheme-theoretic Frobenius-type operation.

Rem. 3.6.2(iii)j²

Basepoints and non-ring filters

Remark 3.6.3 explains that basepoints depend essentially on ring/scheme structure. Across the theta link or log-wall, the basepoints on the two sides are distinct and unrelated.

Rem. 3.6.3basepoints

Narrow mono-theta bridge

Remark 3.6.4 says the mono-theta environment bridges tempered Frobenioids and tempered fundamental groups. For theta-link comparison, the bridge is crossed by the first power of the reciprocal theta root.

Rem. 3.6.4first power

Cyclotome as holomorphic skeleton

Remark 3.6.5 treats cyclotomes as local skeleta of arithmetic holomorphic structures. This explains why cyclotomic rigidity and constant-multiple rigidity act like local uniformization data.

Rem. 3.6.5cyclotome

Group to post-anabelian Gaussian monoids

Corollary 3.7 transports the group-theoretic Gaussian monoids through the mono-theta comparison to Frobenioid-theoretic Gaussian monoids, then records that the resulting algorithms are only uniradial in this form.

Cor. 3.7uniradial

Local Frobenioids from monoids

Definition 3.8 turns constant, theta, and Gaussian monoids with group action into local Frobenioids. Constant versions have divisor monoids isomorphic to Q>=0; theta/Gaussian split versions have divisor monoids isomorphic to N.

Def. 3.8Frobenioid

Gaussian and constant distributions

Definition 3.8(iii) interprets Gaussian monoids as embeddings into products of labeled constant Frobenioids, while constant distributions are diagonal embeddings.

Def. 3.8(iii)distributions

Label-independent inner ambiguity

Remark 3.8.1 says labeled Frobenioids are determined up to an isomorphism of the identity functor independent of |t|. If this becomes ambiguous, Mochizuki says to use the original monoid formulation.

Rem. 3.8.1labels

F_l± to F_l symmetry

Remark 3.8.2 compares the Section 3 Gaussian construction with the F_l^± symmetry of D-Theta±ell theaters and the F_l symmetry obtained after passing to the D-ThetaNF local portion.

Rem. 3.8.2symmetry

Conjugate synchronization gain

Remark 3.8.3 lists the concrete gains from synchronization: diagonal constant submonoids, zero-label identifications, and a units isomorphism needed when composing with the theta link.

Rem. 3.8.3log-wall
Source warning: without conjugate synchronization, one can at best form diagonal embeddings of Galois-invariants or sets of Galois-orbits of constant monoids. Remark 3.8.3 says this is insufficient for Kummer theory and incompatible with the ring structure needed for the log-wall.
Sources: IUT II, Remarks 3.6.2-3.8.3 Related: tempered Gaussian monoids Galois theta evaluation theta/log links Frobenioids/log-shells log-theta lattice

Good-Prime Gaussian Monoids in IUT II 4.1-4.4

Section 4 begins the globalization of Gaussian monoids by extending the bad-prime construction of Section 3 to good nonarchimedean primes and archimedean primes. The pattern is deliberately parallel: construct constant monoids, pass to mono-analytic semi-simplifications, synchronize labels, then define theta and Gaussian monoids together with a formal evaluation isomorphism.

Local good-prime extension good nonarchimedean and archimedean versions before globalization
Source itemLocal constructionRole in globalization
Proposition 4.1(i) For good nonarchimedean v, the local topological group Pi_v functorially constructs a constant monoid Psi_cns(Pi_v) with G_v(Pi_v)-action. This gives the good-place analogue of the constant monoids from bad places.
Proposition 4.1(ii) The mono-analytic semi-simplification splits the constant monoid into units and a realified value monoid R>=0, with a distinguished element log(p_v). This is the value-group component needed to write the good-prime Gaussian exponent profile.
Proposition 4.1(iii)-(iv) The F_l^± action supplies symmetrizing isomorphisms and diagonal monoids; the formal evaluation isomorphism sends log(p_v) log(Theta) to the j^2 log(p_v) profile. This is the good nonarchimedean version of the Section 3 Gaussian construction.
Remark 4.1.1 The good nonarchimedean generalization is mostly formal and tautological: it is introduced so the bad and good local theories can be glued globally. Constant monoids are uniradial, while semi-simplified, theta, and Gaussian monoids are multiradial. The page treats good-prime data as global glue, not as a second source of deep theta evaluation.
Proposition 4.2 The good nonarchimedean Frobenioid in a theta-Hodge theater yields Frobenioid-theoretic constant, theta, and Gaussian monoids compatible with the group-theoretic construction of Proposition 4.1. This transports the formal group-theoretic good-place construction into the Hodge-theater Frobenioid setting.
Proposition 4.3 At archimedean places, Aut-holomorphic orbispaces replace tempered topological groups. The same constant/semi-simplified/label/Gaussian pattern is constructed from U_v. Archimedean places require their own source type; they are not modeled as nonarchimedean groups.
Proposition 4.4 Archimedean Frobenioid data in a theta-Hodge theater yields the corresponding Frobenioid-theoretic theta and Gaussian monoids, with compatible formal evaluation isomorphisms. This completes the local place-by-place input for Corollary 4.5.

Good nonarchimedean Gaussian monoids

Proposition 4.1 constructs Psi_env(Pi_v) and Psi_gau(Pi_v) directly from the good nonarchimedean topological group, after forming constant monoids and their semi-simplified value-group part.

Prop. 4.1good v

Mono-analytic semi-simplification

The semi-simplified monoid is the product of units with a realified R>=0 component. This isolates the value-group data that will be compatible with global realification.

Prop. 4.1(ii)R>=0

Good-prime formal evaluation

The formal evaluation isomorphism maps the theta formal symbol to the Gaussian tuple with j^2-weighted log(p_v) entries, while preserving units and splittings.

Prop. 4.1(iv)j²

Good Frobenioid Gaussian monoids

Proposition 4.2 transfers the good nonarchimedean construction to the local Frobenioid of a theta-Hodge theater, using unique equivariant isomorphisms and compatible splittings.

Prop. 4.2Frobenioid

Archimedean Gaussian monoids

Proposition 4.3 constructs the archimedean analogue from Aut-holomorphic orbispaces. The constant monoid is the nonzero closed unit-ball monoid of the complex archimedean field.

Prop. 4.3Aut-holomorphic

Archimedean Frobenioid monoids

Proposition 4.4 uses the archimedean Kummer structure and the equality of the relevant Aut-holomorphic spaces to compare Frobenioid-theoretic and Aut-holomorphic Gaussian monoids.

Prop. 4.4Kummer
Source warning: the good-prime construction is intentionally formal. Its purpose is to provide compatible local data at every place so that Corollary 4.5 can build global Gaussian Frobenioids.
Sources: IUT II, Propositions 4.1-4.4 Related: tempered Gaussian monoids Gaussian Frobenioid categories IUT I Frobenioid package Gaussian Frobenioids

IUT II Proposition 4.1: Good Nonarchimedean Gaussian Monoids

Proposition 4.1 formally extends the bad-prime Gaussian monoid pattern to good nonarchimedean primes. The construction is mostly tautological, but it is needed so that the later global theory has compatible local inputs at every nonarchimedean place.

Good nonarchimedean local package Proposition 4.1 and Remark 4.1.1
ClauseConstructionStatus
4.1(i) Constructs Psi_cns(Pi_v) from G_v via the cohomological algorithm for mu_Z(G_v). This is the good-prime analogue of the constant monoids used at bad primes.
4.1(ii) Constructs R>=0(G_v), the distinguished element log(p_v), and the semi-simplified monoid Psi_ss_cns = units x R>=0. This provides the realified value-group side needed for the formal Gaussian expression.
4.1(iii)-(iv) The F_l^± action gives symmetrizing isomorphisms, diagonal constants, and the formal evaluation Psi_env(Pi_v) -> Psi_gau(Pi_v). Remark 4.1.1 says this good-prime construction is formally multiradial after semi-simplification, while the raw constants are uniradial.
Sources: IUT II, Proposition 4.1 and Remark 4.1.1 Related: Section 4 atlas good nonarchimedean Frobenioids global Gaussian Frobenioids

IUT II Proposition 4.2: Frobenioid Gaussian Monoids at Good Nonarchimedean Primes

Proposition 4.2 transfers the good nonarchimedean group-theoretic monoids of Proposition 4.1 to the Frobenioid side of a theta-Hodge theater. It is the good-prime analogue of the Corollary 3.6 Frobenioid Gaussian comparison at bad primes.

Good nonarchimedean Frobenioid transfer Proposition 4.2 and Remark 4.2.1
ClauseConstructionRole
4.2(i) A p_v-adic Frobenioid in a theta-Hodge theater determines Psi_F, uniquely identified with Psi_cns(Pi_v). Moves the good-prime constant monoid from group-theoretic to Frobenioid-theoretic language.
4.2(ii) The mono-analytic prime-strip gives unit and realified-divisor comparisons, hence a semi-simplified poly-isomorphism compatible with splittings. Supplies the realified Frobenioid side used by theta/Gaussian monoids.
4.2(iii)-(iv) The label symmetries and formal evaluation isomorphism produce Psi_FTheta and Psi_Fgau for the good nonarchimedean place. Gives the good-prime Frobenioid analogue of Corollary 3.6.
Remark 4.2.1 Extends the semi-simplified notation back to bad primes and sets infty Psi_env = Psi_env at good nonarchimedean primes because those monoids are already divisible. Aligns Section 3 and Section 4 notation for the global step.
Sources: IUT II, Proposition 4.2 and Remark 4.2.1 Related: good nonarchimedean Gaussians bad-prime Frobenioid Gaussians global Gaussian Frobenioids

IUT II Propositions 4.3-4.4: Archimedean Gaussian Monoids

Propositions 4.3 and 4.4 repeat the good-prime pattern for archimedean places, replacing tempered groups by Aut-holomorphic orbispaces and then transferring the resulting monoids to the archimedean Frobenioid data of a theta-Hodge theater.

Archimedean local analogue Propositions 4.3-4.4
SourceConstructionAnalogue
4.3(i)-(ii) Builds Psi_cns(U_v) from the archimedean Aut-holomorphic space and constructs the semi-simplified monoid units x R>=0 with distinguished log(p_v). This is the archimedean replacement for the good nonarchimedean cohomological constant monoid.
4.3(iii)-(iv) The F_l^±-symmetry of cusp-label orbits gives diagonal constants and the formal evaluation Psi_env(U_v) -> Psi_gau(U_v). The same formal Gaussian shape is obtained without a nonarchimedean tempered group.
4.4(i)-(iv) The archimedean Frobenioid data of the theta-Hodge theater identifies its constant monoid with Psi_cns(U_v) and transfers the theta/Gaussian formal evaluation to Psi_FTheta -> Psi_Fgau. This completes the local good-prime inputs before the global Frobenioid construction of Corollary 4.5.
Sources: IUT II, Propositions 4.3-4.4 Related: good nonarchimedean Gaussians good nonarchimedean Frobenioids global Gaussian Frobenioids

Global Gaussian Frobenioids in IUT II 4.5

Corollary 4.5 assembles the local Gaussian constructions into global data attached to a D-Theta±ell-Hodge theater. It builds global constant monoids, semi-simplified realified data, global F_l^±-symmetrizing isomorphisms, local theta and Gaussian monoid assignments, and finally the global realified Frobenioids Denv and Dgau connected by a global formal evaluation isomorphism.

Corollary 4.5 global assembly from a D-Theta±ell theater to Denv and Dgau
Source itemGlobal constructionFormal role
Corollary 4.5(i) From a D-prime-strip D, construct the placewise assignment Psi_cns(D): at nonarchimedean places this includes a local Galois action; at archimedean places it is the Aut-holomorphic constant monoid. This is the global list of constant monoids over all valuations.
Corollary 4.5(ii) From the mono-analyticized D-prime-strip D⊢, construct semi-simplifications Psi_ss_cns(D⊢)_v = units × R>=0, each with log(p_v), plus the global realified Frobenioid D(D⊢) with primes bijective to V. This is the realified global degree container under the later Denv and Dgau constructions.
Corollary 4.5(iii) The global label bijection ζ≻ aligns local F_l^± symmetries to produce global symmetrizing isomorphisms, diagonal monoids, and the zero-label to nonzero-diagonal isomorphism. This is the global conjugate synchronization layer. It depends on the global portion of the D-Theta±ell theater.
Corollary 4.5(iv) Construct placewise theta and Gaussian assignments Psi_env(D≻), Psi_gau(D≻), and their infinity versions, with local evaluation isomorphisms. This packages the bad, good nonarchimedean, and archimedean local Gaussian monoids into one global assignment.
Corollary 4.5(v) Construct the global realified Frobenioid Denv(D⊢≻) and the sub-Frobenioid Dgau(D⊢≻) inside a product over F_l, with divisor and rational-function monoids determined by the vector of j^2-ratios. The global formal evaluation isomorphism Denv -> Dgau is compatible with all local evaluation isomorphisms.
Remarks 4.5.1-4.5.3 Frobenioid formulations are useful for realified/global degree data but monoid formulations are better for conjugate synchronization; the F_l^± symmetry is single-basepoint and connected, unlike the relevant F_l or multi-basepoint symmetries. The global page keeps both formulations visible to avoid conflating realified degree transport with label-synchronized Kummer data.

Global constant monoids

Corollary 4.5(i) assigns a constant monoid to every valuation. The nonarchimedean entries retain group action and conjugacy ambiguity; the archimedean entries use the Aut-holomorphic monoid.

Cor. 4.5(i)all places

Global semi-simplifications

Corollary 4.5(ii) forms the unit/value split units × R>=0 at every place and packages the realified value monoids into a global Frobenioid with Prime ≃ V.

Cor. 4.5(ii)realified

Global F_l± synchronization

The local F_l^± symmetries are glued by the global portion of the D-Theta±ell theater, synchronizing the ±-indeterminacies at every valuation.

Cor. 4.5(iii)single basepoint

Local theta/Gaussian assignments

Corollary 4.5(iv) bundles the local theta and Gaussian monoids, including infinity variants, and records evaluation isomorphisms place by place.

Cor. 4.5(iv)Psi_env/Psi_gau

Denv

Denv(D⊢≻) is a copy of the global realified Frobenioid multiplied by the theta formal symbol. Its local divisor monoids match the realified theta-environment monoids.

Cor. 4.5(v)theta side

Dgau

Dgau(D⊢≻) is constructed inside the product over F_l; its divisor and rational-function monoids are determined by the vector of j^2 ratios.

Cor. 4.5(v)Gaussian side

Global formal evaluation

The isomorphism Denv -> Dgau globalizes the local formal evaluation maps and is compatible with the local realified monoid identifications.

Cor. 4.5(v)global evaluation

Frobenioid versus monoid formulation

Remark 4.5.1 says Frobenioids are technically convenient for global realified degree data, while monoids remain better for discussing conjugate synchronization.

Rem. 4.5.1formulation

Single versus multi-basepoint symmetry

Remark 4.5.3 distinguishes the single-basepoint F_l^± symmetry from multi-basepoint processions and D-Thetaell-bridge symmetries. The single-basepoint form is what supports conjugate synchronization.

Rem. 4.5.3guardrail
Formalization warning: Denv and Dgau are global realified Frobenioids, while conjugate synchronization is most naturally stated at the monoid level. A clean implementation should not force one formulation to do both jobs.
Sources: IUT II, Corollary 4.5 Related: good-prime Gaussian monoids Gaussian Frobenioid categories Theta±ell bridges Gaussian Frobenioids log-theta lattice

IUT II Corollary 4.5(i)-(ii): D-Side Global Constants and Realifications

The first half of Corollary 4.5 builds the D-side global container: a placewise constant-monoid assignment over all valuations, its mono-analytic semi-simplification, and the global realified Frobenioid whose primes are identified with the valuation set.

D-side global constant infrastructure Corollary 4.5(i)-(ii)
SourceConstructionPurpose
4.5(i) Defines the assignment Psi_cns(D) place by place: nonarchimedean places use the reconstructed Galois-action pair G_v(Pi_v) acts Psi_cns(Pi_v); archimedean places use the Aut-holomorphic constant monoid. Gives one global index set of constant monoids over all valuations.
4.5(ii) Constructs Psi_ss_cns(D⊢)_v = units x R>=0 with distinguished log(p_v), plus unit comparison isomorphisms from the D-side constants. Separates the unit part from the realified value part before global degree comparison.
4.5(ii) Constructs the global realified Frobenioid D(D⊢), with Prime(D(D⊢)) ~= V and local divisor monoid maps rho_D,v. Provides the global realified container from which Denv and Dgau are built.
Sources: IUT II, Corollary 4.5(i)-(ii) Related: global Gaussian Frobenioids D-side synchronization/evaluation Theta±ell F-side lift

IUT II Corollary 4.5(iii)-(v): D-Side Synchronization and Global Evaluation

The second half of Corollary 4.5 glues the local F_l^±-symmetries into a global synchronization system, packages local theta/Gaussian monoids, and constructs the realified Frobenioids Denv and Dgau joined by the global formal evaluation map.

Global D-side synchronization and evaluation Corollary 4.5(iii)-(v), Remarks 4.5.2-4.5.4
SourceConstructionGuardrail
4.5(iii) Uses zeta≻ and the global portion of the D-Theta±ell theater to glue local F_l^± symmetries into global symmetrizing isomorphisms. Remark 4.5.3 says this global portion synchronizes the local ±-indeterminacies.
4.5(iv) Constructs the global assignments Psi_env(D≻), Psi_gau(D≻), and their infinity versions, with compatible local evaluation isomorphisms. This bundles bad, good nonarchimedean, and archimedean local Gaussian monoids without identifying their construction mechanisms.
4.5(v) Constructs Denv(D⊢≻) and Dgau(D⊢≻), with Dgau determined by the weighted diagonal vector (..., j^2, ...). Remark 4.5.4 makes the weighted-diagonal interpretation explicit.
4.5.1-4.5.3 Frobenioids are convenient for realified global degree data; monoids are technically better for conjugate synchronization. Single-basepoint F_l^±-symmetry, not multi-basepoint symmetry, supports synchronization. The wiki keeps the realified Frobenioid and monoid synchronization stories adjacent but distinct.
Sources: IUT II, Corollary 4.5(iii)-(v) and Remarks 4.5.1-4.5.4 Related: D-side constants/realifications global F_l± synchronization Theta±ell F-side lift

IUT II Corollary 4.6: Theta±ell F-Side Lift and Currency Exchange

Corollary 4.6 transfers the D-side constructions of Corollary 4.5 to the F-prime-strip side of a Theta±ell-Hodge theater. The transfer is Kummer-theoretic locally and uses the global Frobenioid as a currency exchange device, especially for the archimedean distinguished elements.

From D-side global data to F-side global Frobenioids Corollary 4.6 and Remark 4.6.1
SourceConstructionWhy it matters
4.6(i)-(ii) Constructs F-side constant and semi-simplified assignments and identifies them with the D-side assignments by Kummer-theoretic isomorphisms. Moves from the D-side global data of Corollary 4.5 to the F-prime-strip side of a Theta±ell-Hodge theater.
4.6(iii) Transfers global F_l^±-symmetrizing isomorphisms to Psi_cns(F≻), with nonarchimedean conjugacy indeterminacy independent of the label. Preserves the conjugate synchronization discipline after the F-side lift.
4.6(iv) Builds local F-theta and F-Gaussian assignments from the glued Theta-bridge, with evaluation chains through the D-side monoids. Connects the F-side theta-Hodge theater to the local evaluation machinery already constructed on the D-side.
4.6(v) Constructs global F-side realified Frobenioids Cenv and Cgau, with evaluation Cenv -> Denv -> Dgau -> Cgau. This is the F-side global realified Gaussian object used by later theta-link variants.
