Proposition 1.3(ii)-(iv) A theater log-link lies over a D-theater poly-isomorphism, inherits the Proposition 1.2 compatibility/log-shell/Kummer facts, and generates a vertical Frobenius-picture chain. This lifts the log-link interface from prime-strips to complete Theta±ellNF-Hodge theaters.
Remarks 1.3.1-1.3.2 The construction first fixes a single D-theater isomorphism to preserve plus-minus synchronization, and the resulting log-links are compatible with F_l^{±}-symmetrizing isomorphisms. Conjugate synchronization is already a constraint at the log-link stage, before Theorem 3.11.
Definition 1.4 The log-theta lattice is the Z × Z-indexed diagram with full log-links vertically and either Theta×μ or Theta×μ_gau links horizontally. The Gaussian lattice is the §1 object used for Theorem 1.5 and the later multiradial construction.
Remark 1.4.1 Lattice squares are far from 1-commutative. Each horizontal execution obligates a subsequent vertical execution if one wants log-shells. Multiradiality must be built from vertical cores, not by pretending horizontal theta motion preserves local ring histories.
Theorem 1.5(i)-(ii) Vertical arrows induce full poly-isomorphisms between D-Theta±ellNF theaters; horizontal arrows induce full poly-isomorphisms between the associated F⊢×μ_△-prime-strips. The theorem separates vertical coricity from horizontal coricity before composing them into bi-coric objects.
Theorem 1.5(iii)-(iv) F⊢×μ_△(D⊢_△) and the corresponding mono-analytic log-shells are bi-coric across arbitrary lattice indices (n,m) and (n',m'). These are the §1 bi-cores used downstream for the multiradial LGP-monoid representation.
Theorem 1.5(v), Remark 1.5.1 The D-prime-strip poly-isomorphisms induce compatible bi-coric global realified Frobenioids, and conjugate synchronization is essential for the subscript-△ constructions. The realified Frobenioid layer is the bridge to later log-volume computation, not just a formal add-on.

Hodge-theater log-link

Proposition 1.3 constructs a log-link between Theta±ellNF-Hodge theaters from an isomorphism of their associated D-Theta±ellNF-Hodge theaters. Each constituent F-prime-strip then receives the corresponding log-link.

The construction is synchronized at the D-theater level; it is not a collection of unrelated local log choices.

Proposition 1.3(i)D-theater

Gaussian log-theta lattice

Definition 1.4 defines two log-theta lattices: non-Gaussian with Theta×μ horizontal arrows and Gaussian with Theta×μ_gau horizontal arrows. The vertical arrows in both are full log-links.

Theorem 1.5 is stated for the Gaussian lattice.

Definition 1.4Gaussian

Vertical and horizontal coricity

Theorem 1.5(i) supplies vertical coricity of associated D-Theta±ellNF-Hodge theaters along log-links. Theorem 1.5(ii) supplies horizontal coricity of F⊢×μ_△-prime-strips along Gaussian theta-links.

The distinction is structural: one direction controls D-theaters, the other controls the weakened F⊢×μ interface.

Theorem 1.5(i)-(ii)coricity

Bi-coric F⊢×μ-prime-strips

Theorem 1.5(iii) constructs F⊢×μ_△(D⊢_△) as an invariant under both lattice directions, up to the specified class of poly-isomorphisms.

The result also records compatibility with Kummer isomorphisms and ×μ-Kummer structures.

Theorem 1.5(iii)bi-coric

Bi-coric mono-analytic log-shells

Theorem 1.5(iv) transports the bi-coricity of F⊢×μ_△(D⊢_△) to the corresponding mono-analytic log-shells and their enveloping log modules.

These shells are also related to holomorphic log-shells arising from the constant monoid data used in IUT II Corollary 4.10.

Theorem 1.5(iv)log-shells

Bi-coric realified Frobenioids

Theorem 1.5(v) promotes the bi-coric D-prime-strip comparison to global realified Frobenioid data, compatible with the R>0-orbits already present in IUT II.

This is the §1 preparation for later determinant and log-volume comparisons.

Theorem 1.5(v)realified
FeatureSource claimWiki/formalization consequence
Noncommutativity Squares in the log-theta lattice are far from 1-commutative. Do not model the lattice as a commuting square of ordinary maps.
Vertical cores Multiradiality requires objects invariant with respect to vertical arrows. Distinguish data attached to a vertical line from data transported across theta links.
Z^x effects Horizontal linking data is subject to unit-quotient indeterminacy. Do not force horizontal arrows to identify full arithmetic holomorphic structures.
p-adic analogy Vertical arrows correspond to Frobenius; horizontal arrows to mixed-characteristic extension structure. Use the analogy only as orientation; the formal objects remain the IUT lattice data.
The lattice page is therefore the bridge between the Hodge-theater taxonomy and the proof-critical corridor: it explains why the later comparison must be expressed through multiradial containers and controlled indeterminacies, rather than through an ordinary transport map between two schemes.
Sources: IUT III IUT IV Mutually Alien Copies Related: indeterminacies theta and log links multiradiality Corollary 3.12

Indeterminacies

Indeterminacies are controlled ambiguities that arise because IUT compares weakened data across non-scheme-theoretic links. In the Theorem 3.11 to Corollary 3.12 corridor, the key point is that the three indeterminacies have different mathematical shapes and should not be collapsed into a single quotient.

Indeterminacy channels processions, unit-quotient automorphisms, and upper-semi log-Kummer data

Ind1

The automorphism ambiguity of the processions of D⊢-prime-strips used to form tensor packets. Concretely, this includes permutation automorphisms of the nested label containers S_{j+1}^{±} and automorphisms of the mono-analytic prime-strips in those processions.

Ind2

The non-Ind1 part of the automorphism ambiguity coming from F⊢×μ-prime-strips in the theta, Gaussian, LGP, and lgp links. At nonarchimedean places this includes the Z^x-indeterminacies acting on local O×μ copies.

Ind3

The upper-semi compatibility of tensor-packet log-shell Kummer isomorphisms along a fixed vertical line of the log-theta lattice. IUT III describes this by inclusions at nonarchimedean rational valuations and natural surjections at archimedean rational valuations.

IndeterminacySource locationEffect in estimates
Ind1 Theorem 3.11(i), processions of D⊢-prime-strips. Handled in IUT IV by allowing arbitrary automorphisms in the local estimate input.
Ind2 Theorem 3.11(i),(iii), F⊢×μ prime-strip link automorphisms. Handled together with Ind1 by automorphism-invariant containers and log-volume bounds.
Ind3 Theorem 3.11(ii), upper-semi log-Kummer compatibility. Handled by upper bounds: containers contain possible images rather than identify them by equality.
The most dangerous simplification is to turn one-sided upper-semi data into equality of possible-image regions without proving that this stronger relation is licensed by the source construction. This is precisely the distinction the wiki and the Lean corridor must keep visible.
Sources: IUT III IUT IV Dupuy-Hilado probabilistic Szpiro Higuchi 2026 Related: Corollary 3.12 multiradiality 3.12 diagnostics dispute

IUT III Definition 1.1 and Proposition 1.2: Local Log-Link Mechanism

The local log-link is the vertical move of the log-theta-lattice. At nonarchimedean places it applies the local p_v-adic logarithm to unit quotient data; at archimedean places it uses a universal-cover and torsion-quotient version of the complex logarithm. The output is not a map of full Frobenioid monoids, but a controlled route to log-shell modules.

From units to log-shells Definition 1.1, Proposition 1.2
Source itemConstructionAudit consequence
Definition 1.1(i) At nonarchimedean places, the local logarithm acts on the perfected unit quotient and gives the tautological log-link and scaled log-shell. The log-link is unit-facing; value-group data is not transported by the same formula.
Definition 1.1(ii) At archimedean places, a pointed universal cover and quotients by roots of unity produce an analogous logarithmic object. The archimedean construction has its own topological/Kummer shape.
Definition 1.1(iii) A full log-link is the tautological log-link followed by a poly-isomorphism log(F) -> F'. Multiradial use depends on the full-link formulation, not just the tautological path.
Proposition 1.2 Log-links preserve the D-side core and log-volume compatibility, but Kummer isomorphisms fail to commute elementwise with the coric comparison. This is the local source of upper-semi log-Kummer behavior.
Sources: IUT III, Definition 1.1 and Proposition 1.2 Related: theta/log links log-shell containment upper-semi Log-Kummer

IUT III Introduction: Vertical, Horizontal, and Bi-Coricity

IUT III introduces a directional vocabulary for invariance in the log-theta-lattice. Vertical coricity means invariance under log-links; horizontal coricity means invariance under theta-type links; bi-coricity means compatibility with both directions. The distinction is needed because the two directions use different pieces of arithmetic structure.

Directional invariance in the lattice IUT III Introduction
TermMeaningUse in the wiki
Vertical coricity Invariance under log-links, i.e. vertical arrows of the log-theta-lattice. Used for log-shell and LGP objects constructed along a fixed vertical line.
Horizontal coricity Invariance under theta-type links, including Gaussian, LGP, and lgp variants. Used for objects meant to be read from alien horizontal viewpoints.
Bi-coricity Coricity with respect to all arrows of the relevant log-theta-lattice. Critical for mono-analytic log-shells and containers used in Theorem 3.11.
Sources: IUT III, Introduction Related: log-theta lattice bi-coric log-shells Theorem 3.11 algorithms

IUT III Introduction: Upper-Semi Log-Kummer Compatibility

The log-link and Kummer isomorphisms do not form an elementwise commuting square. IUT III instead uses log-shells as upper containers for Kummer images from both sides of the log-link. This one-sided compatibility is the source of Ind3 in Theorem 3.11 and the later Corollary 3.12 proof route.

Compatibility by containment, not equality IUT III Introduction and Theorem 3.11(ii)
Source pointStatementFormalization consequence
Intro log-shell paragraph I_v contains the Kummer images associated to both the domain and codomain of the log-link. Model the log-shell as an upper container, not as an equality quotient.
Intro upper-semi paragraph Iterated log-links and Kummer isomorphisms give upper semi-compatibility of diagrams. Use direction-sensitive containment or surjection data.
Theorem 3.11(ii) Ind3 is expressed through local inclusion and surjection clauses in the log-Kummer correspondence. The Corollary 3.12 corridor must retain this one-sidedness.
This page is a guardrail for the Scholze-Stix/Mochizuki-sensitive corridor: replacing upper-semi containment by equality would strengthen the source claim and change the formal object being audited.
Sources: IUT III, Introduction and Theorem 3.11(ii) Related: Log-Kummer correspondences Indeterminacies Corollary 3.12 corridor

IUT III Remark 1.1.2: Tautological and Full Log-Links

Definition 1.1 first constructs a tautological log-link by applying the local logarithm inside a fixed Frobenius-like structure. Remark 1.1.2 then explains why the later multiradial theory mainly uses the full log-link: the full link replaces the specific Frobenius-dependent path by a tautologically indeterminate etale-like isomorphism of the local fundamental groups.

Two log-link modes Definition 1.1 and Remark 1.1.2
Source itemStatementWhy it matters
Definition 1.1(i),(ii) The tautological log-link constructs log(F_v) from the local logarithm and carries a specific relationship between the fundamental groups in the domain and codomain. It is the local construction that produces log-shells, but it still uses Frobenius-like monoids in an essential way.
Definition 1.1(iii) A full log-link is obtained by post-composing the tautological log-link with the full poly-isomorphism log(F) ≃ F'. This is the form used when the target must be described without a chosen Frobenius-like rigidifying path.
Remark 1.1.2(i) The tautological log-link gives a specific "rigidifying path"; the full log-link replaces it by the tautologically indeterminate isomorphism needed for etale-like multiradial algorithms. The wiki and formal models must distinguish "constructed by log" from "available up to full poly-isomorphism".
Audit rule: the full log-link is not extra decoration. It is the source device that lets later algorithms speak in etale-like terms rather than in terms of a specific Frobenius-like monoid path.
Sources: IUT III, Definition 1.1 and Remark 1.1.2 Related: local log-link mechanism Log-Kummer correspondences log-theta lattice

IUT III Proposition 1.2: Log-Shell Variants

Proposition 1.2 packages the log-link output into several log-shell forms: holomorphic log-shells attached to an F-prime-strip, mono-analytic log-shells attached to D⊢-data, and coric holomorphic log-shells constructed from a D prime-strip. The theorem also records which compatibilities are actual isomorphisms and which are only upper-semi containment phenomena.

Three log-shell roles holomorphic, mono-analytic, and coric holomorphic
ClauseContentFormalization consequence
Proposition 1.2(iii) The log-link is compatible with the relevant nonarchimedean and archimedean log-volume conventions. Volume compatibility may be modeled separately from elementwise commutation.
Proposition 1.2(iv) Kummer isomorphisms fail to be compatible with the coric comparison at the level of the diagrams (*)_non and (*)_arc. This is the precise warning against replacing the log-Kummer interface by equality.
Proposition 1.2(v) Holomorphic log-shells are compact and contain the relevant unit-side images. Remark 1.2.2 makes this concrete as p_v^{-1}log(O_k^*) nonarchimedeanly and a radius-pi archimedean container. The upper container has different local analytic forms at different place types.
Proposition 1.2(vi)-(viii) Mono-analytic log-shells are constructed functorially from D⊢-data and are poly-isomorphic to the associated F⊢×mu and holomorphic log-shells. The etale-like container side is not optional; it is the multiradial interface.
Proposition 1.2(ix),(x) Coric holomorphic log-shells are constructed from a D-prime-strip; iterated full log-links form a Frobenius-picture graph with translation symmetry but no arbitrary adjacent-vertex swaps. Coric invariants are tied to the oriented chain, not to a symmetric quotient.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 1.2 and Remark 1.2.2 Related: upper-semi Log-Kummer Log-Kummer correspondences Theorem 3.11 algorithms

IUT III Theorem 1.5: Bi-Cores of the Log-Theta-Lattice

Theorem 1.5 collects the first major invariance package of IUT III. Vertical log-links identify the associated D-Hodge theaters; horizontal Gaussian links identify the associated F⊢×mu-prime-strips; combining the two produces bi-coric prime-strips, mono-analytic log-shells, and realified global Frobenioids.

Bi-core package vertical, horizontal, and combined coricity
ClauseStatementUse downstream
Theorem 1.5(i) Vertical arrows induce full poly-isomorphisms between the associated D-Theta±ellNF Hodge theaters in a fixed column. Provides the vertical coric core later used for log-shell invariance.
Theorem 1.5(ii) Horizontal arrows induce full poly-isomorphisms between the associated F⊢×mu-prime-strips along a fixed row. Provides the horizontal coric side of the Gaussian log-theta-lattice.
Theorem 1.5(iii) Certain F⊢×mu-prime-strips constructed from D⊢-data are invariant, up to the specified poly-isomorphisms, in both lattice directions. This is the source of the bi-coric prime-strip object used in LGP construction.
Theorem 1.5(iv) The bi-coric prime-strip isomorphisms induce corresponding mono-analytic log-shell isomorphisms, compatible with the holomorphic log-shells produced from the source data. This is the log-shell layer used in tensor packets and log-volume estimates.
Theorem 1.5(v) The same poly-isomorphisms induce compatible isomorphisms of the associated global realified Frobenioid data. Feeds the global LGP/lgp Frobenioid and pilot-object pages.
Theorem 1.5 is the transition from local log-link technology to lattice-level reusable objects. It does not prove Corollary 3.12, but it supplies the bi-coric log-shell and Frobenioid objects that Theorem 3.11 later acts on.
Sources: IUT III, Theorem 1.5 Related: vertical/horizontal coricity theta monoid vertical coricity tensor packets vertically coric LGP

Log-Kummer Correspondences

IUT III uses log-Kummer correspondences to compare Frobenius-like objects carried by Hodge-theater Frobenioids with etale-like objects that form multiradial containers. The comparison is deliberately upper-semi: log-shells contain the relevant images, but the source does not assert elementwise equality of all possible images.

From two versions to an upper container Frobenius-like data, etale-like data, and log-shell containment
IUT III source item Content Consequence for the 3.12 corridor
Introduction, log-link discussion Kummer isomorphisms relate the coric version of local Pi_v ↷ O_Fv data to copies appearing on both sides of a log-link, but these isomorphisms are not compatible with coricity at element level. The log-link cannot be audited as an equality-preserving transport of all local ring data.
Definition 1.1 and Proposition 1.2 The log-shell contains images from the domain and codomain of the log-link and carries compatible log-volume data across the permitted poly-isomorphisms. The comparison target is a container with log-volume control, not the original local unit set itself.
Introduction, upper-semi paragraph Iterating log-links and Kummer isomorphisms yields diagrams that are only upper semi-compatible. This is presented as the source of Ind3. The later inequality arises from one-sided containment/interference, not from a symmetric quotient argument.
Remark 3.12.2 Multiradial containers arise from etale-like versions of objects but are applied as containers for Frobenius-like versions. The theory must track the consequences of identifying these two versions. A formal model must mark where Frobenius-like and etale-like objects are distinct labels and where they are deliberately compared.
Theorem 3.11(ii) The log-Kummer correspondence relates tensor-packet log-shells, splitting monoids, and global Frobenioids through Kummer isomorphisms; the local clauses include inclusions at nonarchimedean rational valuations and surjections at archimedean rational valuations. This is the typed source of Ind3; it should not be replaced by a generic equivalence relation.

Frobenius-like label

Frobenius-like objects are the monoidal/Frobenioid objects carried by a Hodge theater: theta monoids, LGP monoids, and global realified Frobenioids before etale readout.

Frobenioidmonoid

Etale-like label

Etale-like objects are read through Galois/Kummer constructions and supply the multiradial containers. They are comparable to Frobenius-like objects only through specified Kummer isomorphisms.

GaloisKummer

Upper container

The log-shell is large enough to contain Kummer images from both sides of a log-link. Its function in the proof corridor is containment and log-volume control.

log-shellupper bound

Nonarchimedean clause

In the source statement of Theorem 3.11(ii), the upper-semi local behavior at nonarchimedean rational valuations is expressed by inclusions of possible images into the relevant log-shell packet.

Ind3inclusion

Archimedean clause

At archimedean rational valuations, the corresponding upper-semi behavior is expressed by natural surjections. This is a different local shape from the nonarchimedean inclusion clause.

Ind3surjection

No-loop discipline

IUT III avoids vicious loops while the Frobenius-like and etale-like versions remain distinct labels. The delicate step is using etale-like containers for Frobenius-like objects without erasing that distinction.

Remark 3.12.2labels
Audit rule: any wiki or Lean formulation that turns the log-Kummer correspondence into equality of all possible images has strengthened the source. The source gives upper-semi container behavior, and that asymmetry is part of the route to Corollary 3.12.
Sources: IUT III, Introduction and Theorem 3.11(ii) Related: indeterminacies multiradiality tensor/log-volume corridor Corollary 3.12 corridor

Theta Monoid Vertical Coricity in IUT III 2.1

Proposition 2.1 globalizes the multiradial local theory of theta monoids to the Gaussian log-theta lattice. It constructs theta-environment monoids and realified Frobenioids from D-side prime-strips, identifies their Frobenioid-theoretic versions by Kummer isomorphisms, and records the bad-prime, good-prime, archimedean, and constant-monoid compatibility clauses needed before Theorem 2.2 and Corollary 2.3.

Proposition 2.1 route D-side vertical coricity, Kummer readout, and local compatibility
Source itemConstructionWhy it matters
Proposition 2.1(i) Functorial algorithms in the associated D_> and D⊢_> prime-strips construct Psi_env(D_>), ∞Psi_env(D_>), and the global realified Frobenioid Denv(D⊢_>). Vertical arrows of the Gaussian log-theta lattice induce compatible isomorphisms. This gives the theta-environment monoids vertical coricity before the §2 multiradial theta-monoid construction continues.
Proposition 2.1(ii) Frobenioid-theoretic theta-environment data Psi_Fenv, ∞Psi_Fenv, and Cenv are constructed from the Θ±ellNF-Hodge theater and then identified with the D-side environment data by Kummer isomorphisms. This is the controlled Frobenius-like to D/etale-like readout for theta-environment monoids and F-prime-strips.
Proposition 2.1(iii) At bad primes, Kummer isomorphisms are obtained through mono-theta environments arising from tempered Frobenioids and from the tempered fundamental group. The exterior-cyclotome map tensored with Q/Z maps trivially into the relevant ×μ quotient. This zero-map clause insulates mono-theta rigidity from the Z×-type indeterminacies acting on coric unit-quotient data.
Proposition 2.1(iv) At good nonarchimedean primes, the unit portion is obtained from a labelled version of the relevant MLF-Galois topological-monoid isomorphism and from Kummer poly-isomorphisms of semi-simplifications. Good-prime compatibility uses the semi-simplification route already built in IUT II §4, rather than the bad-prime mono-theta route.
Proposition 2.1(v) At archimedean primes, the unit portion is obtained from a labelled version of the archimedean topological-monoid isomorphism and its Kummer semi-simplification orbit. The archimedean clause is separate from both the bad-prime and good nonarchimedean constructions.
Proposition 2.1(vi) The unit portion of the theta monoids gives natural isomorphisms between the constant and environment F⊢×-prime-strip data, compatible with the Kummer isomorphisms and Theorem 1.5(iii). This ties the §2 theta-environment route back to the constant F⊢×μ_△ interface preserved by the modified theta links.

D-side theta environment

The D-side prime-strips reconstruct Psi_env, ∞Psi_env, and Denv. This is the vertically coric environment data transported along log-links.

Prop. 2.1(i)D-side

F-side Kummer readout

The Frobenioid-theoretic data Psi_Fenv and Cenv are identified with the D-side data by Kummer isomorphisms and read as F-prime-strip isomorphisms.

Prop. 2.1(ii)Kummer

Bad-prime zero map

The exterior-cyclotome contribution, after tensoring by Q/Z and projecting to the ×μ quotient, is the zero map.

Prop. 2.1(iii)insulation

Good-prime labelled semi-simplification

At good nonarchimedean places, the Kummer data is supplied by labelled versions of MLF-Galois topological-monoid isomorphisms and semi-simplification poly-isomorphisms.

Prop. 2.1(iv)good primes

Archimedean labelled semi-simplification

At archimedean places, the construction uses labelled versions of the archimedean topological-monoid isomorphism and the associated Kummer semi-simplification orbit.

Prop. 2.1(v)archimedean

Constant/environment compatibility

The unit part of the theta monoids identifies constant and environment F⊢× data and matches the F⊢×μ isomorphism of IUT II Corollary 4.10.

Prop. 2.1(vi)constant side

Five mono-theta rigidity properties

Remark 2.1.1 lists cyclotomic rigidity, discrete rigidity, constant multiple rigidity, isomorphism class compatibility, and Frobenioid structure compatibility as the source rigidity package used in §2.

Rem. 2.1.1rigidity

First-power cyclotomic rigidity

Remark 2.1.1 emphasizes that cyclotomic rigidity is nonlinear and distinguished at the first power. Composing it with arbitrary N-power morphisms sacrifices constant multiple rigidity.

Rem. 2.1.1(iv)first power

Discrete rigidity and positivity

Remark 2.1.1 says discrete rigidity restricts attention to Z-multiples or powers, not a Zhat-style completion that would lose positivity and confuse effective with negative divisors.

Rem. 2.1.1(v)positivity
Source warning: Proposition 2.1 is not just a generic Kummer compatibility statement. Its local clauses differ by place type, and the bad-prime mono-theta zero-map clause is the mechanism that keeps theta monoids much more exact than the later Gaussian multiradial formulation.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 2.1 and Remark 2.1.1 Related: log-theta lattice Theta×μ Frobenius-pictures F×μ Kummer prime-strips multiradiality log-Kummer correspondences

Theta Monoid Multiradiality in IUT III 2.2-2.3

Theorem 2.2 and Corollary 2.3 turn the theta-environment data of Proposition 2.1 into the first explicit multiradial theta-monoid interface. The key point is not merely that prime-strip data are isomorphic, but that bad-prime mono-theta rigidity is compatible with arbitrary automorphisms of the coric F⊢×μ side.

Theorem 2.2 to Corollary 2.3 Kummer compatibility, bad-prime insulation, and radial/coric output
Source itemFormal contentRole in the corridor
Theorem 2.2(i) Automorphism groups of F-prime-strips map to automorphism groups of the associated F×μ and F⊢×μ prime-strips. The second arrows are surjections and the route is compatible with the Kummer isomorphisms of Proposition 2.1 and Theorem 1.5. This records exactly where automorphism freedom enters the weakened prime-strip interfaces.
Theorem 2.2(ii) At each bad prime, the identity on the theta splitting objects, exterior-cyclotome torsion, projective mono-theta environments, and zero-labeled splittings is compatible with arbitrary automorphisms of F⊢×μ_△. This is the Kummer-compatible multiradiality statement: the purely radial mono-theta data are insulated from coric ×μ automorphisms.
Remark 2.2.1 Mono-theta environments amalgamate Π_v and Π_μ so that cyclotomic rigidity recovers an isomorphism, not just its Z×-orbit. This decouples geometric/radial data from base-field/coric unit quotients. This explains why the theta-monoid step is more exact than the later Gaussian-monoid multiradiality.
Remark 2.2.2 A naive correspondence q ↦ q^λ lacks the mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity and zero-labeled evaluation splittings required for the Kummer-compatible radial/coric decoupling. This is a source-level warning against replacing the theta-function construction by an arbitrary power map.
Corollary 2.3(i) The radial algorithm sends radial data to coric data and its associated functor is full and essentially surjective. The theta-monoid radial environment is multiradial in the source sense.
Corollary 2.3(ii)-(iv) Vertical arrows identify radial data in each column, horizontal arrows identify coric data across columns, the resulting etale-picture admits arbitrary spoke permutations, and the constructions are equivariant with respect to automorphisms of horizontal-arrow domains and codomains. This is the radial-spoke/common-coric-center picture used before the Gaussian monoid and log-volume stages.

Prime-strip automorphism route

Theorem 2.2(i) does not leave automorphism groups informal: it routes Aut_F through Aut_{F×μ} and Aut_{F⊢×μ}, with compatibility imposed by the preceding Kummer identifications.

Theorem 2.2(i)automorphisms

Bad-prime radial insulation

The bad-prime clause makes the identity on radial mono-theta data compatible with arbitrary coric automorphisms because the relevant torsion/exterior-cyclotome contribution maps trivially into the ×μ quotient.

Theorem 2.2(ii)insulation

Mono-theta vs MLF rigidity

Remark 2.2.1 contrasts mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity with MLF-Galois-pair rigidity: the latter recovers only a Z×-orbit once the unit quotient is involved.

Remark 2.2.1Z× orbit

No naive power map

Remark 2.2.2 says that a replacement q ↦ q^λ would not carry the theta-function machinery: no mono-theta rigidity, no zero-labeled splitting, and no compatible Kummer-class passage.

Remark 2.2.2warning

Radial/coric etale picture

Corollary 2.3 packages D-Theta theater data, theta-environment prime-strips, bad-prime rigidity data, and F⊢×μ_△ into radial data, then maps it to the coric pair (D⊢_△,F⊢×μ_△(D⊢_△)).

Corollary 2.3radial to coric

Spoke symmetry

The etale-picture identifies radial data along vertical lines and coric data horizontally. The resulting diagram has arbitrary permutation symmetries among the spokes labelled by n ∈ Z.

Corollary 2.3(ii)spokes
Use for formalization. A faithful Lean interface should keep the Theorem 2.2(i) automorphism route, the Theorem 2.2(ii) bad-prime identity-compatibility clause, and the Corollary 2.3 radial-to-coric functor separate. Collapsing these into one "multiradiality" predicate would hide the point at which the theta-function-specific Kummer machinery is used.
Sources: IUT III §2 Related: theta monoid vertical coricity multiradiality overview log-Kummer correspondences Theta×μ Frobenius-pictures Corollary 3.12 corridor

F-perp Prime-Strips and Pilot Objects in IUT III 2.4

Definition 2.4 is the short but important transition from the theta-monoid radial/coric picture of Corollary 2.3 to the Gaussian and log-volume machinery of §3. It introduces prime-strip variants built from O⊥ submonoids and records how pilot objects in global realified Frobenioids can be reconstructed from ordered real monoids.

Definition 2.4 and Remark 2.4.2 subquotient Frobenioids, pilot element, and positivity constraint
Source itemConstructionWhy it matters
Definition 2.4(i) From an F⊢-prime-strip, replace the split Frobenioid portion at bad primes by Frobenioids determined by O⊥ ⊆ O◃ and by the torsion quotient O. At good primes the unit subgroup is trivial, so O = O⊥. This isolates the subquotient Frobenioid structures used to pass from theta-monoid radial data toward Gaussian/log-shell objects.
Definition 2.4(ii) Defines F⊢⊥- and F⊢-prime-strips as local collections over V, using Frobenioids at nonarchimedean places and Frobenioid plus TM⊢ data at archimedean places. Morphisms are indexed collections of isomorphisms. This keeps the local type distinction explicit instead of treating "prime-strip" as a single untyped label.
Definition 2.4(iii) Defines F⊥- and F-prime-strips by replacing the local F⊢ portion in the usual global F-prime-strip package (C, Prime(C) ≃ V, local strip, ρ_v). These are the global prime-strip variants that carry the later pilot objects and realified Frobenioid comparisons.
Remark 2.4.1 Applying Definition 2.4(i) to the F_env(D>) prime-strips of Corollary 2.3 gives multiradiality for the associated F⊥_env(D>). At bad primes, the O⊥ submonoids sit naturally inside the ∞Ψ⊥_env(D>)_v monoids of Theorem 2.2(ii). This ties the new F⊥ interface back to the theta-monoid multiradiality already established in §2.
Remark 2.4.2(ii)-(iv) The weights r_v are defined from local degree factors and either log(p_v) at good primes or ord_v(q_v)log(p_v) at bad primes. An ordered monoid M ≃ R gives local copies M_v; a negative pilot element η_M gives submonoids with ρ_v(η_{M_v}) = r_v η_M. This is the numerical skeleton behind the pilot-object comparison and later weighted log-volume conventions.
Remark 2.4.2(v)-(vi) Taking M = Pic(C) reconstructs (C, Prime(C) ≃ V). Adding the negative pilot element reconstructs the further rigidification by local constant divisor monoids and the maps ρ_v. Distinct negative pilot choices can be compared inside the common Frobenioid container C; comparing positive and negative choices would reverse the order structure and destroy the meaningful height distinction.

Subquotient Frobenioids

Definition 2.4 starts with local split Frobenioids and replaces their relevant portions by Frobenioids attached to O⊥ and to its torsion quotient. The good-prime case is simpler because the unit subgroup is trivial.

Definition 2.4(i)O⊥

Global F⊥ and F variants

The global F⊥ and F variants keep the same global spine: a global realified Frobenioid C, a prime identification with V, local strip data, and comparison maps ρ_v.

Definition 2.4(iii)global strip

Inherited multiradiality

Remark 2.4.1 says that the theta-monoid multiradiality of Corollary 2.3 passes to associated F⊥-prime-strips. This prevents §2.4 from becoming a fresh unproved bridge.

Remark 2.4.1inheritance

Pilot-object common container

Remark 2.4.2 reconstructs the common Frobenioid container from Pic(C) as an ordered monoid. Different negative pilot elements then produce different rigidifications that remain comparable inside the same C.

Remark 2.4.2(v)-(vi)common container

Weights r_v

The local weights combine residue degree factors with either log(p_v) or ord_v(q_v)log(p_v). They make the local pilot-element images satisfy ρ_v(η_v)=r_vη.

Remark 2.4.2(ii)-(iv)weights

Positive/negative warning

Remark 2.4.2 warns that comparing positive and negative pilot choices reverses the order on M. The resulting Frobenioids have pre-steps in opposite directions; embedding them in an ambient category that inverts those pre-steps makes the height distinction meaningless.

Remark 2.4.2(vi)positivity
Formalization warning. The pilot-object discussion is not a generic "compare any two real numbers" interface. The source requires a fixed ordered-monoid orientation and negative pilot elements; allowing a positive versus negative comparison would erase precisely the arithmetic degree distinction the Frobenioid container is meant to preserve.
Sources: IUT III, Definition 2.4 and Remarks 2.4.1-2.4.2 Related: theta monoid multiradiality tensor/log-volume corridor theta/q pilot objects weighted log-volume measure Corollary 3.12 corridor

Tensor Packets in IUT III 3.1-3.3

Propositions 3.1-3.3 introduce tensor packets as the §3 amalgam of log-shells and processions. They first construct local holomorphic packets from F-prime-strip capsules, then local mono-analytic packets from D⊢-prime-strip capsules, and finally global tensor packets from labeled M_mod fields.

Local and global tensor packets holomorphic packets, mono-analytic packets, and log-linked localization
Source itemConstructionWhy it matters
Proposition 3.1 setup For an n-capsule {αF}_{α∈A}, the one-packet over v_Q is the direct sum of log(αF_v) over all v|v_Q. The n-packet is the tensor product over α∈A, equivalently a direct sum over all choices of places {v_α} over v_Q. This is the basic packet container used by the later procession and log-volume machinery.
Proposition 3.1(i) The ind-topological field structures on the factors determine an ind-topological ring structure on the packet. Decompositions as direct sums of fields are determined by that ring structure and are compatible with the local group actions on the relevant summands. The packet is not merely a formal tensor product; its ring structure recovers its field-summand decomposition.
Proposition 3.1(ii) For fixed α and v|v_Q, the summand log(A,αF_v) uses the v-summand in the α factor and all other factors. Tensoring with 1's gives log(αF_v) → log(A,αF_v), transporting integral structures to packet summands. This is the local holomorphic integral structure that later has to be compared with mono-analytic log-shell integral structures.
Remark 3.1.1 Locally, log(αF_v) behaves like an algebraic closure kbar and its integral structure like O_kbar. The remark fixes normalized weights for log-volumes over the chosen set V⊆V(K) and warns that weighted sums need not be logarithms of ordinary measures. This fixes the normalization convention before the log-volume estimates of Proposition 3.9 and Corollary 3.12.
Proposition 3.2 Repeats the packet construction for D⊢-prime-strip capsules. If the capsule comes from F-data, the poly-isomorphisms D⊢ → F⊢×μ → F induce compatible identifications with the holomorphic packets. Integral structures are built from mono-analytic log-shells, or Hermitian unit balls at archimedean places. This is the packet-level bridge from holomorphic data to the mono-analytic/log-shell side.
Proposition 3.3 For a subset A of Hodge-theater labels, the global packet (†M_mod)_A is the tensor product of the labeled global fields. Localization functors, composed with the chosen log-links, inject this global packet into the product of local holomorphic packets. This supplies the global object whose local images are measured in the packet/log-volume corridor.

Holomorphic n-packet formula

The n-packet over v_Q is a tensor over labels and a direct sum over all choices of places above v_Q. This is why packet log-volumes later require normalized sums over those choices.

Proposition 3.1local packet

Field-summand decomposition

Proposition 3.1(i) says the ind-topological ring structure determines the decomposition into direct sums of ind-topological fields. This prevents the packet from being treated as an unstructured product of modules.

Proposition 3.1(i)ring structure

Holomorphic integral structures

The injection log(αF_v) → log(A,αF_v) carries the integral structure of the factor into each field summand of the packet. This is one side of the discrepancy later estimated in IUT IV.

Proposition 3.1(ii)integers

Weighted-volume caution

Remark 3.1.1 says the weighted log-volume sum is not generally the log of an ordinary measure. Direct product regions are straightforward; an auxiliary weighted-measure construction handles broader Borel sets.

Remark 3.1.1measure

Mono-analytic packet compatibility

Proposition 3.2 makes the same packet construction for D⊢ data and identifies it with F⊢×μ and F packets when the data arise from an F-capsule.

Proposition 3.2(i)poly-isomorphism

Global packet localization

Proposition 3.3 links the global tensor packet (†M_mod)_A to local holomorphic packets by composing localization functors with the selected log-links.

Proposition 3.3global to local
Use for formalization. Tensor packets should be represented with their index set, place fibers, field-summand decomposition, and integral structures explicit. A bare tensor-product type would miss the decomposition and weighting data that are later used in log-volume comparisons.
Sources: IUT III, Propositions 3.1-3.3 and Remark 3.1.1 Related: F-perp prime-strips and pilot objects tensor/log-volume corridor local packet Frobenioids weighted log-volume measure Corollary 3.12 corridor

Local Packet Frobenioids and LGP Monoids in IUT III 3.4

Proposition 3.4 turns tensor-packet data into local packet-theoretic Frobenioids and logarithmic Gaussian procession monoids. This is the first point in §3 where the packet formalism directly produces monoids that act on packet containers and carry the splitting data later used in the log-Kummer and log-volume arguments.

Proposition 3.4 single packet monoids, processions, log-link pullback, and local LGP monoids
Source itemConstructionWhy it matters
Remark 3.3.2 Local images of global tensor packets are considered for holomorphic and mono-analytic processions. In the |F_l| processions, the zero label is identified with the diagonal label by the isomorphisms cited from IUT II Corollaries 4.6 and 4.5. These local packet images are the packet-level inputs to Proposition 3.4 and later global Frobenioid constructions.
Proposition 3.4(i) The factor-to-packet map sends the logarithmic monoid of one factor, its units, and its realification to the corresponding packet monoid, packet unit monoid, and realified packet monoid inside the selected packet summand. This converts one factor's monoid data into monoids acting on the packet summand where later LGP actions are defined.
Proposition 3.4(i), local place clauses At nonarchimedean places, these monoids carry G_v(αΠ_v)-actions and determine Frobenioids naturally isomorphic to log(αF_v). At archimedean places, the analogous data are an Aut-holomorphic orbispace and a Kummer structure. The local construction keeps the nonarchimedean and archimedean types separate while preserving the packet action interpretation.
Proposition 3.4(ii) Given a log-link of Θ±ellNF-Hodge theaters, apply the single packet monoid construction to the F-prime-strip processions associated to the corresponding Θ±-bridges, then pull back the Gaussian monoids Ψ_Fgau and ∞Ψ_Fgau along the log-link poly-isomorphisms. This produces the local logarithmic Gaussian procession monoids Ψ_FLGP and ∞Ψ_FLGP.
Proposition 3.4(ii), symmetry condition The functorial algorithm requires compatibility of the log-link with the F_l^±-symmetrizing isomorphisms. This prevents LGP monoids from being a purely formal relabeling of Gaussian monoids; the procession symmetry is part of the construction.
Proposition 3.4(ii), packet action For bad primes, the component labelled j of the Galois-invariant submonoid sits inside I_Q(S_{j+1}^±,j;‡F_v) and acts multiplicatively. For every place, the unit portion has the analogous action on the same packet piece. This is the explicit "splitting monoids of LGP-monoids acting on tensor packets" interface shown in Fig. 3.1.

Single packet monoids

Proposition 3.4(i) takes the monoid, unit submonoid, and realification from one packet factor and views their images inside log(A,αF_v). These images are the local packet monoids.

Proposition 3.4(i)packet monoids

Local place types

Nonarchimedean places use group actions and Frobenioids; archimedean places use Aut-holomorphic orbispace and Kummer data. The page keeps these source clauses separate because they are not interchangeable.

Proposition 3.4(i)local clauses

LGP monoids act on packets

The local LGP monoids and their unit portions act on rational spans I_Q(S_{j+1}^±,j;‡F_v) of the packet integral structures. This is the source-level content behind Fig. 3.1.

Proposition 3.4(ii)action

Log-link pullback

The Gaussian monoids from IUT II are pulled back through the log-link poly-isomorphisms before becoming LGP monoids. This is why the construction depends on the log-link, not only on a procession label.

Proposition 3.4(ii)log-link

F_l^± symmetry condition

Proposition 3.4(ii) explicitly invokes compatibility with the F_l^±-symmetrizing isomorphisms. This links the LGP construction back to the procession combinatorics of IUT I.

symmetryprocessions

Bad-prime splittings

At bad primes, the local LGP monoids carry splittings up to torsion. These are the local objects that Proposition 3.5 will make vertically coric and Kummer-compatible.

Vbadto Prop. 3.5
Use for formalization. A local LGP-monoid interface should store the packet action target, the place type, the log-link used to pull back Gaussian data, and the procession label. Treating it as only a named monoid would lose the action-on-packets data needed for later log-volume estimates.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 3.4 and Remarks 3.3.2-3.3.3 Related: tensor packets tensor/log-volume corridor vertically coric LGP monoids log-Kummer totality Corollary 3.12 corridor

Vertically Coric Local LGP Monoids in IUT III 3.5

Proposition 3.5 takes the local LGP monoids of Proposition 3.4 and rebuilds them in the vertically coric D-Theta±ellNF Hodge theater attached to a fixed column of the Gaussian log-theta lattice. Its central point is that compatibility with vertical log-links is upper-semi: one must use the totality of Kummer maps pre-composed with log-link iterates, not a single Kummer isomorphism.

Proposition 3.5 vertical coricity, Kummer maps, and upper semi-compatibility
Source itemConstructionAudit consequence
Proposition 3.5 setup Fix a family {n,m HT_Theta±ellNF} arising from a Gaussian log-theta lattice. For each n, use vertical coricity to determine a D-Theta±ellNF Hodge theater labelled n,◦. The target for comparison is the columnwise vertically coric object, not one of the ordinary n,m holomorphic theaters.
Proposition 3.5(i) Apply Proposition 3.4(ii) to the prime-strips F(n,◦D≻)_t and the associated full log-links, compatibly with the F_l^± symmetries, to construct vertically coric local LGP monoids Psi_LGP and infty Psi_LGP. A correct interface must remember both the prime-strip source and the log-link data used to make the construction vertical.
Proposition 3.5(i), Kummer displays For every n,m, Kummer isomorphisms compare the ordinary local FLGP monoids in the n,m theater with the vertically coric LGP monoids in the n,◦ theater. These maps are comparison inputs. They do not by themselves commute with the vertical log-links.
Proposition 3.5(ii)(a) At nonarchimedean primes, the target module contains images obtained directly from Kummer maps and images obtained after pre-composing Kummer maps with positive iterates of the column's log-links. The formal statement is a containment statement; replacing it by equality would erase the upper-semi feature.
Proposition 3.5(ii)(b) At archimedean primes, the closed unit ball contains images of unit groups and radius-pi balls. These balls contain subsets that surject onto the log-link domains for iterates. The archimedean clause is not a copy of the nonarchimedean one; it uses ball containment and surjectivity for partially defined log-link iterates.
Proposition 3.5(ii)(c) At bad primes and nonzero labels, the splitting submonoids Psi_perp and infty Psi_perp act on rational log-shell packet modules. Across the column, only root-of-unity indeterminacies in log-link domains can be related, and these become addition by zero at the next index. This is the bad-prime source of the "no new indeterminacy after the log-link" clause. It is not a global equality of all packet actions.
Final paragraph The action is not via a single Kummer isomorphism. It is via the totality of Kummer isomorphisms pre-composed with iterates of the log-links. This is the page's main audit rule for the 3.11 to 3.12 corridor: source-faithful models must encode total log-Kummer correspondence.

Columnwise target

The label n,◦ in this page denotes the vertically coric target determined by the column. It is the object whose rational log-shell packet modules receive the total correspondence.

3.5 setupvertical coricity

Kummer displays

The displayed Kummer isomorphisms compare Psi_FLGP(n,m) with Psi_LGP(n,◦), and similarly for the realified monoids. They preserve the local actions and splittings stated in 3.5(i).

3.5(i)Kummer

Single Kummer map not enough

Proposition 3.5 explicitly rejects using one Kummer isomorphism as the action. Such a map fails to be compatible with the column's log-links, so the construction uses all relevant log-link pre-composites.

3.5(ii)audit rule

Nonarchimedean containment

For v_Q in V_Q^non, the target I(S_{j+1}^±F(n,◦D≻)_{v_Q}) contains images from both direct Kummer maps and Kummer maps pre-composed with log-link iterates.

3.5(ii)(a)upper semi

Archimedean ball containment

For archimedean primes, the closed unit ball contains images of units and radius-pi balls. This is the local real-analytic version of the upper-semi containment.

3.5(ii)(b)closed balls

Root-of-unity becomes zero

At bad primes, root-of-unity ambiguity in the domain of a log-link corresponds, after the log-link, to addition by zero. The proposition uses this to state mutual compatibility of bad-prime splitting actions without turning upper-semi compatibility into equality.

3.5(ii)(c)Ind3 source
Formalization warning. A Lean structure for this page should not store only one Kummer isomorphism and a proof that it commutes with log-links. The source statement asks for containment data generated by direct Kummer maps and by their log-link iterates, with separate nonarchimedean, archimedean, and bad-prime clauses.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 3.5 Related: local LGP monoids upper semi-compatibility log-Kummer totality bad-prime splitting compatibility Theorem 3.11 Corollary 3.12 corridor

Global Packet Frobenioids and Pilot Objects in IUT III 3.6-3.8

Example 3.6 and Proposition 3.7 globalize the packet machinery. They compare two concrete models of the global Frobenioid, build single-packet and global realified Frobenioids from local packet data, and prepare Definition 3.8, where theta-pilot and q-pilot objects enter the LGP/lgp Gaussian log-theta-lattice.

3.6-3.8 global packet route FMOD/Fmod models, global LGP/lgp Frobenioids, and pilot objects
Source itemConstructionWhy it matters
Example 3.6(i) FMOD is the rational-function torsor version: objects are Fmod^×-torsors with local trivializations, and morphisms are elementary integral morphisms plus a positive Frobenius degree. This model is strictly multiplicative and is naturally isomorphic to the Frobenioid of arithmetic line bundles.
Example 3.6(ii) Fmod is the local fractional-ideal version: objects are collections {J_v} of fractional ideals, trivial almost everywhere, with integral morphisms given by elements of Fmod^×. This model represents objects additively as local modules, which is needed for later comparison by mono-analytic log-shells.
Remark 3.6.2 The union Fmod^× ∪ {0} has a natural additive structure. This fails for the rational-function monoids of the perfection or realification, and for arbitrary subgroups not coming from subfields. A formal model must not replace Fmod by an arbitrary multiplicative subgroup when additive object-level comparison is required.
Proposition 3.7(i),(ii) For each packet label alpha, construct (FMOD)_alpha from the global packet image, and construct (Fmod)_alpha from the number field plus local monoid invariants inside the packet integral structures. The packet construction now has global Frobenioid objects, not only local monoids acting on tensor packets.
Proposition 3.7(iii),(iv) Applying realified Frobenioid isomorphisms to the Gaussian source prime-strip constructs C_LGP and C_lgp, together with tautological F-prime-strip isomorphisms from F_gau. This is the global realified target in which later pilot objects and log-volume computations live.
Proposition 3.7(v) The global realified LGP and lgp Frobenioids embed into products of the labeled realified FMOD and Fmod copies; local bad-prime monoid generators construct global objects compatibly with local isomorphisms. This is the bridge from bad-prime splitting monoids to global objects whose log-volumes can be compared.
Definition 3.8(i) A theta-pilot object is determined by generators, up to torsion, of the bad-prime splitting monoids in the LGP/lgp side. A q-pilot object is determined in the global realified Frobenioid C_delta by the bad-prime q_v. This supplies the two pilot objects whose log-volume comparison is central in the later Theorem 3.11 to Corollary 3.12 route.

Two global models

FMOD is multiplicative and torsor-based. Fmod is fractional-ideal based and carries the additive object representation. They are isomorphic as Frobenioids, but their roles in later comparison are not interchangeable.

Example 3.6FMOD/Fmod

Additive structure warning

Remark 3.6.2 says the additive structure belongs to Fmod^× ∪ {0}, not to arbitrary realified or subgroup replacements. This is a source-level constraint on any formalization that tries to simplify the global Frobenioid layer.

Remark 3.6.2do not collapse

Single packet Frobenioids

Proposition 3.7(i),(ii) constructs one global Frobenioid for each packet label alpha. The construction is tied to the global packet image and to local packet monoid invariants.

Proposition 3.7(i),(ii)packet global

Global realified LGP/lgp

The C_LGP and C_lgp Frobenioids are constructed functorially in a log-link of Theta±ellNF Hodge theaters and carry F-prime-strip isomorphisms from the Gaussian source.

Proposition 3.7(iii),(iv)realified

Bad-prime data to global objects

Proposition 3.7(v) uses local fractional ideals generated by elements of bad-prime monoids to construct global objects in the LGP/lgp Frobenioids. This is the object-level globalization of the splitting monoid data.

Proposition 3.7(v)globalization

Theta and q pilots

Definition 3.8(i) distinguishes the theta-pilot object, built from bad-prime splitting generators on the LGP/lgp side, from the q-pilot object determined by the bad-prime q_v in the delta-side global realified Frobenioid.

Definition 3.8(i)pilot objects
Formalization consequence. The global layer should distinguish the multiplicative FMOD route, the additive fractional-ideal Fmod route, and the realified product-embedding route. The pilot-object comparison downstream depends on this separation.
Sources: IUT III, Example 3.6, Proposition 3.7, Definition 3.8 Related: vertically coric LGP global packet Frobenioids theta/q pilot objects LGP Gaussian log-theta lattice Corollary 3.12 corridor

LGP and lgp Gaussian Log-Theta Lattices in IUT III 3.8

Definition 3.8(ii)-(iii) defines the horizontal Theta×mu_LGP and Theta×mu_lgp links as full poly-isomorphisms of F×mu-prime-strips, then places them with full vertical log-links into two Gaussian log-theta lattices. The following remarks state which earlier results transfer formally and why unit portions cannot be replaced by log-shells alone.

Definition 3.8(ii)-(iii) horizontal LGP/lgp links plus vertical full log-links
Source itemStatementWiki/formalization consequence
Definition 3.8(ii) From a log-link ‡HT -> †HT and a target Hodge theater *HT, form the associated F×mu-prime-strips *F_delta, †F_LGP, and †F_lgp. The horizontal links are defined relative to the vertical log-link; they are not free-standing identifications of Hodge theaters.
Definition 3.8(ii) The Theta×mu_LGP link and Theta×mu_lgp link are full poly-isomorphisms from †F×mu_LGP or †F×mu_lgp to *F×mu_delta. A page or Lean interface should store which prime-strip variant supplies the horizontal arrow: LGP and lgp are related but distinct.
Definition 3.8(iii) Arrange Theta±ellNF Hodge theaters in a Z×Z-indexed grid. Vertical arrows are full log-links; horizontal arrows are the LGP or lgp links defined relative to the preceding vertical log-link. The diagram is a routed lattice of typed arrows, not merely a rectangular array of copies.
Remark 3.8.1 The LGP and lgp lattices are closely related. The later theory mainly uses the LGP lattice, but computation of the Theta×mu_LGP link uses the Theta×mu_lgp link and related theta-pilot objects. Both link types map theta-pilots to q-pilots. The lgp side is auxiliary but essential; it should not be dropped from the explanatory map.
Remark 3.8.2 Theorem 1.5, Proposition 2.1, Corollary 2.3, and Proposition 3.5 generalize formally to the LGP and lgp Gaussian log-theta lattices. Earlier vertical coricity and upper-semi pages can be reused here, but only because their substantive portions are unaffected by the LGP/lgp transition.
Remark 3.8.3 It is not sufficient to replace the unit portions of the F×mu-prime-strips by the associated log-shells. The nontrivial local Galois action on unit portions is necessary for the Kummer theory of the local and global objects contained by log-shells. This is a source-level anti-simplification rule: log-shells are multiradial containers, not substitutes for the Kummer-theoretic unit data.

Theta×mu_LGP link

The LGP horizontal arrow is the full poly-isomorphism of F×mu-prime-strips from the global realified LGP construction to the delta target, relative to the fixed log-link.

Definition 3.8(ii)LGP

Theta×mu_lgp link

The lgp horizontal arrow is built analogously from the lgp prime-strip. Remark 3.8.1 says this link is needed in the computation of the LGP link.

Definition 3.8(ii)lgp

Horizontal arrows depend on vertical log-links

In Definition 3.8(iii), the horizontal arrow from n,m to n+1,m is defined relative to the full log-link from n,m-1 to n,m.

Definition 3.8(iii)grid

LGP versus lgp

The later theory is mainly organized around the LGP lattice, while the lgp lattice supplies related objects needed for computation. The wiki keeps both names because their source roles differ.

Remark 3.8.1do not merge

Formal transfer

Vertical coricity, theta-monoid Kummer theory, multiradiality, and upper-semi compatibility transfer formally to these lattices according to Remark 3.8.2.

Remark 3.8.2reuse

Log-shells alone are not enough

Remark 3.8.3 blocks a common simplification: trivial Galois action on log-shell containers cannot replace the nontrivial Galois action on unit portions required by Kummer theory.

Remark 3.8.3Kummer data
Audit rule. In the 3.11 to 3.12 corridor, any simplification that keeps log-shell containers but discards the unit portions of the F×mu-prime-strips has lost source data that Mochizuki explicitly calls necessary for Kummer theory.
Sources: IUT III, Definition 3.8 and Remarks 3.8.1-3.8.3 Related: global packet Frobenioids theta/q pilot objects LGP Gaussian log-theta lattice log-shells warning Corollary 3.12 corridor

Packet and Procession Log-Volumes in IUT III 3.9

Proposition 3.9 defines the log-volume layer for the tensor-packet and LGP/lgp constructions. It fixes local packet normalizations, transfers them to mono-analytic log-shell packets, forms global sums, and records compatibility with vertical log-links. The following remarks explain why the procession average is forced and why the result must remain real-valued, not valued modulo a dilation period.

Proposition 3.9 local packets, mono-analytic transfer, global sum, and log-link compatibility
Source itemDefinition or assertionAudit consequence
3.9(i), nonarch. On nonempty compact open subsets of the direct-sum fields in IQ(alpha F), IQ(AF), and IQ(A,alpha F), the local holomorphic integral structures have log-volume zero, and multiplication by the local prime adds -log(p_v). The sign convention is part of the source definition and must be preserved when later upper-ray inequalities are stated.
3.9(i), arch. On compact closures of nonempty open subsets of the direct-sum complex fields, radial log-volumes are summed over direct summands; local holomorphic integral structures have log-volume zero, and multiplication by e adds 1. The archimedean clause has its own normalization and object class for measurable regions.
3.9(i), processions For processions, log-volumes on products of packet-region spaces are normalized by averaging over the capsules under consideration. The procession-normalized number is not an arbitrary weighted average.
3.9(ii) The packet-normalized log-volumes are transferred to mono-analytic D⊢ packets by applying the relevant p-adic or radial log-volumes to log-shells and adjusting for the holomorphic versus mono-analytic integral-structure discrepancy. The same construction is available for F⊢×mu. A source-faithful interface must store the integral-structure discrepancy, not only the final real number.
3.9(iii) The global log-volume is obtained by summing local log-volumes over v_Q, with all but finitely many terms zero. It is invariant under multiplication by the labeled global field and under permutations of packet labels; on fractional-ideal objects it agrees with the arithmetic line-bundle degree up to normalization. This connects the packet/log-shell numerical layer to the global Frobenioid degree layer.
3.9(iv) In an LGP-Gaussian log-theta lattice, these log-volumes apply to the local/global LGP and lgp monoids/Frobenioids relative to the log-link n,m-1 -> n,m, and are compatible along that log-link at the level of rational log-shell spans. This is the numerical compatibility needed later when 3.5's log-Kummer correspondence is used in Theorem 3.11.
Remark 3.9.1 The discrepancy between holomorphic and mono-analytic integral structures is computable from the initial theta data: p-adically via p_v^*-scaled logarithmic unit lattices, and archimedeanly via Hermitian metric comparisons such as C tensor_R C -> C plus C. The discrepancy is not an unspecified error term; it is source-level data that should eventually be formalized.
Remark 3.9.3 General weighted averages over capsules are disallowed because the same log-shell label can contribute to different capsule index sets. Equal capsule weights are forced by compatibility with procession inclusions. This prevents changing the final numerical estimate by choosing convenient capsule weights.
Remark 3.9.4 Log-link/log-volume compatibility can be formulated using realified semi-simplifications of measure spaces. If a log-link only preserved volumes up to a nontrivial dilation factor, log-volumes would be defined only modulo Z log(lambda), which is unacceptable for the later real-valued estimates. The compatibility requirement is not cosmetic; it rules out circular or periodic ambiguity in the Corollary 3.12 numerical comparison.

Packet normalization

Proposition 3.9(i) defines local packet log-volumes with integral structures normalized to zero. The nonarchimedean sign convention is multiplication by p_v adding -log(p_v).

3.9(i)local packets

Mono-analytic transfer

The same log-volume layer is transported to D⊢ and F⊢×mu log-shell packet objects after correcting the holomorphic versus mono-analytic integral-structure discrepancy.

3.9(ii)log-shells

Global degree link

The global packet log-volume is a finite-support sum over v_Q. For fractional-ideal objects, it recovers the arithmetic line-bundle degree, up to the normalization specified in the source.

3.9(iii)degree

Forced procession average

Remark 3.9.3 gives a specific no-go statement: unequal capsule weights would conflict with the way the same log-shell label appears in different procession capsules.

Remark 3.9.3no weighted choice

Realified semi-simplification

Remark 3.9.4 packages measure spaces into commutative monoids with R_>0-action and then into R_>=0. This is the formal layer used to compare log-volumes across log-links.

Remark 3.9.4Rss

No log-volume period

If log-links introduced a nontrivial volume dilation lambda, the numbers would live in R / Z log(lambda). The source rejects this because Corollary 3.12 requires real-valued estimates.

Remark 3.9.4(ii)real-valued
Formalization consequence. The 3.9 interface should not be just a function from packet regions to real numbers. It should include the local place type, packet normalization, procession averaging rule, holomorphic/mono-analytic discrepancy data, finite-support global summation, and log-link compatibility evidence.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 3.9 and Remarks 3.9.1-3.9.4 Related: tensor packets LGP/lgp lattice packet log-volume card procession average card Corollary 3.12 corridor

Global Kummer Theory and Non-Interference in IUT III 3.10

Proposition 3.10 globalizes the log-Kummer correspondence from local LGP monoids to number fields, Frobenioids, and F-prime-strips. Its central non-interference statement says that global multiplicative elements that lie in all local packet integer monoids are exactly torsion elements, i.e. roots of unity. The following remarks explain why this gives precise compatibility for FMOD/LGP but only upper-semi-compatible behavior for the additive Fmod/lgp side.

Proposition 3.10 vertical global objects, roots-of-unity non-interference, and FMOD/Fmod bias
Source itemConstruction or statementAudit consequence
Proposition 3.10 setup Work in an LGP-Gaussian log-theta lattice and, for each column n, use vertical coricity to obtain n,◦HT_D-Theta±ellNF. Global comparisons are made against a columnwise vertically coric target, not against a single ordinary theater selected ad hoc.
Proposition 3.10(i) Applying the global Frobenioid constructions of Proposition 3.7 to the F(n,◦D≻)_t prime-strips constructs vertically coric number fields, monoids, Fmod/FMOD Frobenioids, and Gaussian/LGP/lgp F-prime-strips. The 3.7 global packet objects now exist in a vertically coric form suitable for comparison across all horizontal indices.
Proposition 3.10(i), Kummer displays For every n,m, Kummer isomorphisms compare the ordinary global fields, monoids, Frobenioids, and F-prime-strips in n,m with the vertically coric n,◦ target. These are the global counterparts of the local Kummer comparisons of Proposition 3.5.
Proposition 3.10(ii) The intersection of the global multiplicative group MMOD_alpha with the product of local packet integer monoids Psi_log(A,alpha F_v) is the finite torsion subgroup M_mu_MOD, i.e. roots of unity. This is the non-interference fact: any overlap between global multiplicative action and all local integer constraints is harmless torsion.
Proposition 3.10(ii), final paragraph The global groups act on the vertically coric IQ(S_{j+1}^±F(n,◦D≻)_{V_Q}) not by one Kummer isomorphism but via all Kummer pre-composites with log-link iterates, producing a global log-Kummer correspondence. The totality principle from Proposition 3.5 remains mandatory at the global number-field level.
Proposition 3.10(iii) The global log-Kummer correspondence induces log-link-compatible isomorphisms of FMOD Frobenioids and realified FMOD Frobenioids, and also compatible isomorphisms of associated F⊥-prime-strips. This is the precise Kummer-detachment side used later in Theorem 3.11.
Remark 3.10.1(i) The precise Frobenioid isomorphism works for FMOD because its construction uses the global multiplicative group and valuation subquotients. It does not work for Fmod because Fmod also depends on local packet monoids subject to upper-semi compatibility. Do not state a log-link-compatible global Frobenioid isomorphism for Fmod where the source only gives one for FMOD.
Remark 3.10.1(ii),(iii) FMOD/LGP is rigid and suited to Kummer-detachment indeterminacies. Fmod/lgp is distorted by upper-semi and holomorphic/mono-analytic discrepancies, but its additive log-shell relation makes it suited to explicit log-volume estimates. The two sides have different proof roles; merging them would lose the reason for using LGP in the main lattice while still consulting lgp/Fmod for estimates.
Remark 3.10.2 The F×mu-prime-strips used in the Theta×mu_LGP and Theta×mu_lgp links are constructed from the environmental prime-strip; lower horizontal arrows are obtained by conjugating upper etale-like arrows by Kummer isomorphisms. Horizontal LGP/lgp arrows are not primitive labels; they are built from environmental data and Kummer conjugation.

Vertically coric global objects

Proposition 3.10(i) constructs vertically coric versions of the global fields, monoids, Frobenioids, and LGP/lgp prime-strips introduced in Proposition 3.7.

3.10(i)global

Roots-of-unity non-interference

The intersection statement in 3.10(ii) reduces simultaneous global/local interference to torsion. This is why adjacent log-link ambiguity is described as harmless identity indeterminacy.

3.10(ii)torsion

Global totality

As in 3.5, the action is not through a single Kummer isomorphism. It uses the totality of Kummer maps pre-composed with vertical log-link iterates.

3.10(ii)totality

FMOD/Fmod bias

FMOD is multiplicative and admits precise log-Kummer compatibility; Fmod is additive and estimate-friendly but only upper-semi-compatible at the relevant local monoids.

Remark 3.10.1Fig. 3.2

Kummer detachment

Remark 3.10.1 says the precise FMOD/LGP compatibility is what makes Kummer-detachment indeterminacies disappear for the global portion of the Theta×mu_LGP link.

Remark 3.10.1(ii)LGP

Kummer-conjugated arrows

Remark 3.10.2 reviews the commutative diagram in which the lower horizontal LGP/lgp arrows are obtained by conjugating corresponding upper etale-like arrows by the relevant Kummer isomorphisms.

Remark 3.10.2F×mu
Audit rule. Do not upgrade the Fmod side to the precise log-link-compatible behavior of FMOD. The paper explicitly separates the two: FMOD/LGP is rigid and Kummer-detachment-friendly, while Fmod/lgp is distorted but suited to log-volume estimates.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 3.10 and Remarks 3.10.1-3.10.2 Related: packet log-volume global packet Frobenioids LGP/lgp lattice global Kummer card FMOD/Fmod bias card Theorem 3.11

Theorem 3.11: Multiradial Algorithms via LGP-Monoids/Frobenioids

Theorem 3.11 is the main construction theorem of IUT III. It starts from initial theta data and an LGP-Gaussian log-theta lattice, fixes the vertically coric D-theater n,◦HT_D-Theta±ellNF for each column, constructs the multiradial representation n,◦R_LGP, and then records how this representation is related to every n,m Hodge theater by log-Kummer correspondences and horizontal Theta×mu_LGP-link compatibility.

Theorem 3.11 vertical coric input, multiradial output, log-Kummer comparison, and horizontal compatibility
Source itemConstruction or statementWhat the wiki must preserve
Theorem 3.11 setup Fix initial theta data and a family of distinct Theta±ellNF-Hodge theaters n,mHT. For each column n, use vertical coricity to obtain n,◦HT_D-Theta±ellNF. The theorem is columnwise: the vertically coric object is determined only up to isomorphism from all theaters in that column.
Theorem 3.11(i), procession Apply the natural functor of IUT I Proposition 6.9(ii) to obtain the procession of D⊢-prime-strips Prc(n,◦D⊢_T). The multiradial representation is built from the procession, not from a free list of labels.
Theorem 3.11(i)(a) Use the mono-analytic tensor packets and Q-spans of Proposition 3.2(ii), equipped with the procession-normalized mono-analytic log-volumes of Proposition 3.9(ii). The local log-shell packet layer and its log-volume are part of the same theorem interface.
Theorem 3.11(i)(b) At bad primes, include the LGP splitting monoid of Proposition 3.5(ii)(c) as a subset of the relevant packet Q-spans and with its multiplicative action on the product of those packet modules. Bad-prime splitting monoids are active operators on the packet containers, not merely labels attached to them.
Theorem 3.11(i)(c) For each j∈F_l, include the global number field/monoid data and the associated FMOD/Fmod and realified Frobenioids whose global degrees are computed by the log-volumes from (a). The global Frobenioid layer is tied back to the packet log-volume layer.
Ind1 and Ind2 The representation n,◦R_LGP is taken up to automorphisms of the D⊢-prime-strip procession and independent local automorphism actions on tensor-product summands. These indeterminacies act before the log-Kummer comparison with ordinary n,m theaters.
Theorem 3.11(i), final paragraph n,◦R_LGP is constructed by an algorithm functorial for isomorphisms of processions, and etale-picture permutation symmetries induce compatible poly-isomorphisms between columns. The construction is algorithmic and functorial; cross-column comparison uses etale-picture symmetries.
Theorem 3.11(ii)(a) Kummer isomorphisms compare the vertically coric local mono-analytic tensor packets and Q-spans with the corresponding local packets in each n,m Hodge theater, compatibly with log-volumes. This is the local packet comparison; it is the place where Ind3 later appears.
Theorem 3.11(ii)(b),(c) The same log-Kummer framework compares bad-prime LGP splitting monoids, number fields, global FMOD/Fmod Frobenioids, and realified Frobenioids. The comparison has three layers: local packets, bad-prime splitting monoids, and global Frobenioids.
Theorem 3.11(ii), final paragraph As m varies, splitting monoids and MOD-subscripted global Frobenioids are mutually compatible up to roots of unity; the local packet isomorphisms are only upper-semi-compatible, by inclusions at nonarchimedean places and surjections at archimedean places. This is the precise split between harmless torsion, exact MOD behavior, and Ind3 upper-semi packet behavior.
Theorem 3.11(iii) Kummer isomorphisms satisfy compatibility with the horizontal Theta×mu_LGP links: for F⊢×mu prime-strips, environment prime-strips, mono-theta projective systems, poly-isomorphisms, and evaluation maps. Horizontal compatibility is stated as stabilization/equivariance/ functoriality under arbitrary automorphisms of the horizontal-arrow domains and codomains, modulo the named indeterminacies where applicable.

Data (a),(b),(c)

The theorem's multiradial representation has three visible layers: local mono-analytic tensor packets with log-volumes, bad-prime LGP splitting monoids acting on packet modules, and global number-field/ Frobenioid data with degrees measured by the packet log-volumes.

3.11(i)source interface

Ind1, Ind2, Ind3

Ind1 comes from automorphisms of the procession. Ind2 comes from independent automorphism actions on tensor-product summands. Ind3 is not another quotient of global Frobenioids; it is the upper-semi compatibility of local packet Kummer isomorphisms as the horizontal index varies.

3.11(i),(ii)indeterminacy

MOD versus mod

Theorem 3.11(ii) explicitly transfers exact mutual compatibility from roots-of-unity non-interference to the MOD global Frobenioids, while warning that this does not apply in the same way to mod-subscripted isomorphisms.

3.11(ii)FMOD/Fmod

Horizontal compatibility

Part (iii) says that the Kummer constructions, projective systems, and evaluation maps are compatible with horizontal Theta×mu_LGP arrows in the stabilized, equivariant, functorial sense specified by the source.

3.11(iii)horizontal arrows

IPL, SHE, APT

Remark 3.11.1 recasts the theorem as an algorithm whose input is an F×mu-prime-strip, whose output is linked to that input by full poly-isomorphisms, and whose construction is simultaneously executable relative to the domain and codomain holomorphic structures. Its transport is algorithmic, not a direct map of set-theoretic regions.

Remark 3.11.1IPL/SHE/APT

Label discipline

Remark 3.11.2 emphasizes that the label transitions require all labels in the relevant F_l and |F_l| systems. The procession index sets used in the multiradial representation are naked finite sets; they are not arbitrary omissions of arithmetic labels.

Remark 3.11.2labels
The source theorem does not identify adjacent Hodge theaters by a shared ring structure. Its comparison route is: vertically coric target, multiradial representation modulo Ind1/Ind2, log-Kummer correspondence with Ind3 for local packets, and horizontal link compatibility in the stabilized/equivariant/ functorial sense.
Sources: IUT III, Theorem 3.11 and Remarks 3.11.1-3.11.4 Related: global Kummer 3.10 multiradiality overview tensor/log-volume corridor 3.11 representation card 3.11 log-Kummer card Theta-link compatibility card Corollary 3.12

Multiradiality, SHE, IPL, APT

Multiradial algorithms are meant to make sense from the viewpoint of an alien arithmetic holomorphic structure. In IUT III this is achieved by replacing holomorphic prime-strip data with mono-analytic processions, tensor packets of log-shells, and controlled indeterminacies.

From labeled theta data to multiradial output processions, tensor packets, LGP monoids, and source obligations
IUT III §2 source item Multiradial theta-monoid content Formal consequence
Proposition 2.1(i) From the D- and D⊢-prime-strips of a Theta±ellNF-Hodge theater, functorial algorithms construct Psi_env(D>), infty Psi_env(D>), and D_env(D⊢_>). Vertical log-link poly-isomorphisms transport these data. Theta-environment monoids are vertically coric before the §3 tensor-packet construction starts.
Proposition 2.1(ii) Kummer isomorphisms identify the Frobenioid-theoretic Psi_Fenv(HT_Theta), infty Psi_Fenv(HT_Theta), and C_env(HT_Theta) with the D-theoretic environment data. This is the Frobenius-like to etale-like passage for theta-environment monoids.
Proposition 2.1(iii)-(vi) At bad primes, mono-theta Kummer classes and exterior cyclotome isomorphisms yield a composite map to Psi(F⊢_△)×μ that is zero; good and archimedean places use labeled Kummer semi-simplification isomorphisms. These are compatible with constant-monoid identifications. The zero-map/torsion insulation is the local mechanism that keeps mono-theta rigidity compatible with F⊢×μ automorphisms.
Remark 2.1.1 The proof-critical mono-theta properties are cyclotomic rigidity, discrete rigidity, constant multiple rigidity, isomorphism class compatibility, and Frobenioid structure compatibility. The wiki treats "rigidity" as a five-part source package, not a single informal stability assertion.
Theorem 2.2(i) Automorphism groups of F-prime-strips map to automorphisms of associated F×μ and then F⊢×μ prime-strips, compatibly with the Kummer isomorphisms of Proposition 2.1 and Theorem 1.5. Multiradiality must account for the exact automorphism route through the weakened prime-strip interfaces.
Theorem 2.2(ii) At bad primes, the identity automorphism on the theta-splitting objects, exterior cyclotome torsion, mono-theta environments, and zero-labeled splittings is compatible with arbitrary Aut(F⊢×μ_△) actions. This is the local Kummer-compatible multiradiality statement for theta monoids.
Corollary 2.3 A radial environment packages D-Theta±ellNF data, the associated F_env(D>), bad-prime rigidity data, F⊢×μ_△(D⊢_△), and the full poly-isomorphism from environment to constant side. The radial algorithm maps this to coric data and is full and essentially surjective. This is the §2 etale-picture of radial data: spokes are radial data and the center is the common coric F⊢×μ_△(D⊢_△) output.

Vertical coric theta monoids

Proposition 2.1(i) reconstructs theta-environment monoids and global realified Frobenioids from D-theater data and shows that vertical log-link poly-isomorphisms transport them compatibly.

This is the §2 reason the theta monoid layer can be read along a vertical line of the log-theta lattice.

Proposition 2.1(i)vertical coric

Theta-monoid Kummer isomorphisms

Proposition 2.1(ii) supplies Kummer isomorphisms from the Frobenioid-theoretic theta-environment monoids to the D-theoretic environment monoids, including the associated global realified Frobenioids.

These isomorphisms are the controlled Frobenius-like to etale-like passage for the theta environment.

Proposition 2.1(ii)Kummer

Bad-prime zero map

Proposition 2.1(iii) and Theorem 2.2(ii) isolate the bad-prime mechanism: torsion/exterior-cyclotome data maps trivially into the relevant ×μ quotient, so the identity on mono-theta rigidity data remains compatible with arbitrary F⊢×μ_△ automorphisms.

This is the source-level insulation of cyclotomic rigidity from the Z^×-type indeterminacies.

Proposition 2.1(iii)Theorem 2.2(ii)

Mono-theta rigidity package

Remark 2.1.1 names five inputs from mono-theta theory: cyclotomic rigidity, discrete rigidity, constant multiple rigidity, isomorphism class compatibility, and Frobenioid structure compatibility.

The §2 multiradial argument depends on this package, especially on cyclotomic rigidity and isomorphism class compatibility with log-link basepoints.

Remark 2.1.1rigidity

Radial environment

Corollary 2.3 packages D-Theta±ellNF data, theta-environment F-prime-strips, bad-prime rigidity data, and the F⊢×μ_△ output into a radial environment.

The radial algorithm forgets to the coric pair (D⊢_△, F⊢×μ_△(D⊢_△)).

Corollary 2.3radial data

Etale-picture of radial data

Corollary 2.3 identifies radial data along vertical lines, then uses horizontal poly-isomorphisms to form a common coric center. The resulting etale-picture admits arbitrary permutation symmetries among spokes.

This extends IUT II's D-theater etale-picture to the theta-monoid multiradial setting.

Corollary 2.3(ii)etale-picture

Procession reduction
