IUT III replaces the full label set by nested containers S_{j+1}^{±} = {0,...,j}. This reduces the label ambiguity from all independent label choices to procession automorphisms and keeps the core label 0 insulated from later label indeterminacy.

Tensor packets

For each valuation of Q, the construction forms direct sums of log-shells over valuations above it, then tensor products over the elements of S_{j+1}^{±}. These packets are the mono-analytic containers for LGP monoids and labeled copies of F_mod^x.

LGP monoids

Logarithmic Gaussian procession monoids are the log-shell-theoretic versions of the Gaussian monoids from IUT II. Their splitting monoids act on tensor packets and later feed the log-volume estimates in Corollary 3.12.

Alien viewpoint

The final representation forgets the arithmetic holomorphic structure of a specific vertical line and is formulated in terms meaningful from a horizontally related, non-ring/scheme-theoretic Hodge theater.

TermSource meaningFormalization burden
Multiradial Constructed from mono-analytic data so it is meaningful from alien holomorphic viewpoints. Represent the processions, log-shell tensor packets, and permitted indeterminacies.
IPL Output data remains linked to the fixed input q-pilot prime-strip through full poly-isomorphisms of F-prime-strips. Prove the output/input relation, not merely a shared label or postulated comparison.
SHE The construction and its output are simultaneously valid relative to the domain and codomain holomorphic structures of the theta link. Track which operations are executable in both holomorphic structures while the q-pilot value is held fixed.
APT Algorithmic parallel transport: not a direct set-map on regions, but an algorithm valid on both sides up to controlled indeterminacy. Model the algorithmic transport and its allowable hull/Ind1/Ind2/Ind3 losses explicitly.
The source text warns against reading APT as ordinary transport of a set-theoretic region. The output may be a family of possible regions; comparison with the q-pilot requires further passage through holomorphic hulls, determinants, log-volumes, and the log-Kummer correspondence.
Sources: IUT III IUT IV 2026 formalization report Higuchi 2026 Related: RIMS Lean effort indeterminacies tensor/log-volume corridor LANA

Tensor Packets and Log-Volume Corridor

IUT III §3 converts the bridge and Gaussian machinery into containers on which log-volumes can be computed. The corridor runs through tensor packets, splitting monoids of LGP-monoids, the log-Kummer correspondence, holomorphic hulls, and finally the procession-normalized log-volume comparison of Corollary 3.12.

Theorem 3.11 interface containers, correspondences, hulls, and log-volume
IUT III sourceObject introducedPrecise role in the corridor
Proposition 3.1 Local holomorphic tensor packets from F-prime-strip capsules. Creates the local tensor containers and the holomorphic integral structures used before mono-analytic comparison.
Remark 3.1.1 Normalized weighted log-volume formalism for tensor packets. Fixes the weight conventions and warns that the weighted sum is not generally the logarithm of an ordinary measure.
Proposition 3.2 Local mono-analytic tensor packets from D⊢-prime-strip capsules. Transfers the packet construction to the log-shell side and records compatibility with holomorphic packets via poly-isomorphisms.
Proposition 3.3 Global tensor packet (†M_mod)_A. Connects global labeled fields to products of local packets through log-shifted localization maps.
Proposition 3.4 Local packet-theoretic Frobenioids and logarithmic Gaussian procession monoids. Builds the LGP monoids that act on tensor-packet containers and feed the later log-Kummer correspondence.
Proposition 3.5 Vertically coric local LGP monoids and upper semi-compatibility. Replaces a single incompatible Kummer isomorphism by the totality of Kummer isomorphisms pre-composed with vertical log-link iterates.
Example 3.6 Concrete models FMOD and Fmod for the global Frobenioid. Separates the multiplicative rational-function torsor model from the additive local fractional-ideal model.
Proposition 3.7 Global packet-theoretic Frobenioids and realified LGP/lgp Frobenioids. Constructs the global Frobenioid objects attached to packet data and embeds them into products of labeled realified Frobenioids.
Definition 3.8 and Remarks 3.8.1-3.8.3 Theta/q pilot objects and LGP/lgp Gaussian log-theta lattices. Defines the pilot objects and warns that replacing the unit portions by log-shells alone loses necessary Kummer-theoretic Galois action.
Proposition 3.9 Packet-normalized, procession-normalized, mono-analytic, and global log-volumes. Defines the numerical log-volume layer that remains compatible with tensor packets, processions, global Frobenioids, and vertical log-links.
Remarks 3.9.1-3.9.4 Integral-structure discrepancy, forced averaging, and realified semi-simplification. Explains how the holomorphic/mono-analytic discrepancy is computed from initial theta data and why log-link compatibility must avoid circular real-valued ambiguity.
Proposition 3.10 Global Kummer theory and non-interference with local integers. Globalizes the log-Kummer correspondence and separates precise FMOD compatibility from upper-semi-compatible Fmod behavior.
Remarks 3.10.1-3.10.2 FMOD/LGP versus Fmod/lgp, and the F×μ-prime-strip diagram. Explains why LGP is used for Kummer detachment, why Fmod is used for estimates, and how horizontal links are obtained by conjugating etale-like arrows by Kummer isomorphisms.
Theorem 3.11(i) Multiradial representation n,◦R_LGP. Packages mono-analytic tensor packets with procession-normalized log-volumes, bad-prime splitting monoids, global Frobenioids, and Ind1/Ind2.
Theorem 3.11(ii) Log-Kummer correspondence for local packets, splitting monoids, and global Frobenioids. Compares the vertically coric data with each n,m Hodge theater, with Ind3 as upper semi-compatibility for local packet data.
Theorem 3.11(iii) and Remarks 3.11.1-3.11.4 Θ×μ_LGP-link compatibility and the IPL/SHE/APT interpretation. States compatibility with horizontal arrows and explains the algorithm as input-prime-strip-linked, simultaneously holomorphically expressible, and not a set-theoretic transport map.

Holomorphic packet formula

For an n-capsule indexed by α∈A, Proposition 3.1 forms log(αF at v_Q) as the direct sum over v|v_Q, and forms log(AF at v_Q) as the tensor product over α∈A. The latter decomposes as a direct sum over all choices v_α | v_Q.

The ind-topological field structures on the factors determine an ind-topological ring structure on the packet, and its decomposition as a direct sum of fields is determined by that ring structure.

IUT III, Proposition 3.1(i)

Tensor-packet integral structures

Fixing α∈A and v|v_Q, Proposition 3.1 defines the direct summand log(A,αF_v) by using the v-summand in the α factor and all remaining packet factors. Tensoring with ones gives a natural injective ring homomorphism log(αF_v) → log(A,αF_v).

The integral structure Ψ_log(αF_v)^gp ∪ {0} then supplies integral structures on the packet summands. Proposition 3.2 replaces these by mono-analytic log-shell integral structures I(−) and their rational spans I_Q(−).

IUT III, Propositions 3.1(ii), 3.2(ii)

Weighted log-volume measure

Remark 3.1.1 fixes the weights used when local log-volumes are taken on packet components, including the normalization over all places above v_Q. With this normalization, multiplication by p(v_Q) changes log-volume by ±log p(v_Q), with the sign depending on the archimedean/nonarchimedean convention.

The remark explicitly separates this weighted sum from the logarithm of an ordinary measure in general. The theory later uses direct product regions and pre-regions, for which the computation is straightforward; the auxiliary E-weighted measure extends the construction to arbitrary Borel sets.

IUT III, Remark 3.1.1(ii)-(iv)normalization

Mono-analytic packet compatibility

Proposition 3.2 constructs packets log(αD⊢ at v_Q), log(AD⊢ at v_Q), and log(A,αD⊢ at v). When the capsule comes from F-data, the poly-isomorphisms of Proposition 1.2 induce compatible identifications with the corresponding F⊢×μ and holomorphic packet objects.

This is the packet-level place where log-shell integral structures enter the later comparison with holomorphic integral structures.

IUT III, Proposition 3.2(i)log-shells

Global-to-local packet image

Proposition 3.3 forms the global tensor packet (†M_mod)_A from the labeled global fields in a Θ±ellNF-Hodge theater. Localization functors, composed with the chosen log-links, give an injective homomorphism into the product of local holomorphic tensor packets.

Remarks 3.3.1 and 3.3.2 record the corresponding D-Θ±ellNF version and the use of zero-label and diagonal-label identifications in the processions.

IUT III, Proposition 3.3log-shifted localization

Local packet Frobenioids

Proposition 3.4(i) maps the monoid Ψ_log(αF_v), its units, and its realification into log(A,αF_v). At nonarchimedean places the resulting monoid with its group action determines a Frobenioid naturally isomorphic to log(αF_v); at archimedean places it gives the analogous Aut-holomorphic/Kummer data.

IUT III, Proposition 3.4(i)packet monoids

Local LGP monoids

Proposition 3.4(ii) applies the single-packet monoid construction to holomorphic processions pulled back through a log-link of Θ±ellNF-Hodge theaters. The result is a functorial algorithm producing the local logarithmic Gaussian procession monoids ΨF_LGP and their realified versions.

At bad places the component labeled j acts multiplicatively on the rational log-shell packet attached to S±(j+1), label j, and ‡F_v. The construction uses compatibility of the log-link with the F_l±-symmetrizing isomorphisms.

IUT III, Proposition 3.4(ii)LGP

Vertically coric LGP monoids

Proposition 3.5(i) fixes a Gaussian log-theta lattice and, for each vertical index n, uses the vertically coric D-Θ±ellNF-Hodge theater to construct local LGP monoids ΨLGP and their realified versions. These are built functorially from the associated F(n,◦D≻) prime-strips and full log-links.

For every horizontal index m, Kummer isomorphisms compare the ordinary local LGP monoids in n,m with this vertically coric target in n,◦.

IUT III, Proposition 3.5(i)vertical coricity

Upper-semi local clauses

Proposition 3.5(ii) states that the Kummer isomorphisms are upper semi-compatible with the vertical log-links. At nonarchimedean places, the target module contains images obtained directly by Kummer isomorphisms and images obtained after pre-composing these Kummer isomorphisms with positive iterates of the vertical log-links.

At archimedean places, the closed unit ball contains images of the unit groups and of closed balls of radius π. The statement uses the fact that the log-link concerns units and that these radius-π balls contain subsets surjecting to the domains on which log-link iterates are defined.

IUT III, Proposition 3.5(ii)(a),(b)upper semi

Bad-prime splitting compatibility

Proposition 3.5(ii)(c), for bad primes and nonzero labels, restricts to the splitting submonoids Ψ⊥ and ∞Ψ⊥. As m varies, their actions on the rational log-shell modules are mutually compatible relative to the vertical log-links.

The only portions possibly related through the log-links are root-of-unity indeterminacies in the domains. Under the log-link these correspond to addition by zero in the next theater, hence to no new indeterminacy there.

IUT III, Proposition 3.5(ii)(c)bad primes

Log-Kummer totality

The final paragraph of Proposition 3.5 is explicit: the relevant action is not via one Kummer isomorphism, because such an isomorphism fails to be compatible with the log-links of the Gaussian log-theta lattice. Instead, one uses the totality of Kummer isomorphisms pre-composed with all relevant log-link iterates.

This total correspondence acts on the invariant n,◦-labeled rational log-shell container and is the local source of the later Ind3/upper-semi directionality.

IUT III, Proposition 3.5(ii)not a single isomorphism
Proposition 3.5 single Kummer maps are replaced by a total log-Kummer correspondence

Concrete FMOD/Fmod models

Example 3.6 gives two concrete representations of the global Frobenioid Fmod. The rational-function torsor version FMOD is built from an F_mod^×-torsor plus local trivializations and is strictly multiplicative.

The local fractional-ideal version Fmod uses collections of local fractional ideals and makes the additive representation of objects available. Remark 3.6.2 says this additive representation is crucial for relating objects across the enhanced Θ×μ_gau-link by mono-analytic log-shells.

IUT III, Example 3.6additive/multiplicative

Global packet Frobenioids

Proposition 3.7 constructs packet-theoretic Frobenioids from the global tensor-packet images. The FMOD version is obtained from the image of the global field under localization; the Fmod version is obtained from local fractional ideals generated by packet monoids.

The same proposition constructs global realified LGP and lgp Frobenioids, with tautological F-prime-strip isomorphisms from the Gaussian source prime-strip.

IUT III, Proposition 3.7(i)-(iv)global LGP/lgp

Realified product embeddings

Proposition 3.7(v) gives a commutative diagram embedding the global realified LGP and lgp Frobenioids into products of the labeled realified Frobenioids. This is the global object-level route from local bad-prime generators in the splitting monoids to objects in the global categories.

The construction remains compatible with the local isomorphisms attached to the corresponding F-prime-strips.

IUT III, Proposition 3.7(v)realification

Theta and q pilots

Definition 3.8(i) defines a theta-pilot object from generators up to torsion of bad-prime splitting monoids attached to the LGP/lgp side. The q-pilot object is the corresponding object in the global realified Frobenioid from IUT II, Corollary 4.10, concretely determined by the bad-prime q_v.

Remark 3.8.1 records that both the Θ×μ_LGP and Θ×μ_lgp links map theta-pilot objects to q-pilot objects.

IUT III, Definition 3.8(i)pilot objects

LGP Gaussian log-theta lattice

Definition 3.8(ii),(iii) defines the horizontal Θ×μ_LGP and Θ×μ_lgp links as full poly-isomorphisms of F×μ prime-strips. Placing these horizontal links together with the vertical full log-links gives the LGP- and lgp-Gaussian log-theta lattices.

Remark 3.8.2 says the earlier Gaussian log-theta-lattice results, including Proposition 3.5, generalize formally to these LGP/lgp variants.

IUT III, Definition 3.8(ii),(iii)log-theta lattice

Why log-shells alone are not enough

Remark 3.8.3 rejects a tempting simplification: one cannot replace the unit portions of the F×μ prime-strips by their associated log-shells alone. Even though log-shells serve as multiradial containers, the nontrivial Galois action on the local unit portions is needed for the Kummer theory of the objects they contain.

This warning is directly relevant to any simplified reading of the Corollary 3.12 corridor: the container layer and the Kummer-theoretic unit layer cannot be silently identified.

IUT III, Remark 3.8.3simplification warning
Global packet Frobenioids multiplicative model, additive model, and pilot objects

Packet log-volume definition

Proposition 3.9(i) defines log-volumes on compact regions in the rational spans of local holomorphic tensor packets. At nonarchimedean places, multiplication by p_v adds -log(p_v); at archimedean places, multiplication by e adds 1.

In both cases the local holomorphic integral structures have log-volume zero, and the packet log-volume is invariant under permutations of the tensor labels.

IUT III, Proposition 3.9(i)packet-normalization

Mono-analytic log-volume compatibility

Proposition 3.9(ii) transports the log-volume theory to the mono-analytic log-shell packets. The construction adjusts for the discrepancy between holomorphic integral structures and mono-analytic log-shell integral structures, then remains compatible with the holomorphic side through the poly-isomorphisms of Proposition 3.2.

The same construction applies to the corresponding F⊢×μ objects.

IUT III, Proposition 3.9(ii)log-shells

Global log-volume compatibility

Proposition 3.9(iii) defines the global packet log-volume by summing the local log-volumes over v_Q, with all but finitely many terms zero. This global log-volume is invariant under multiplication by elements of the labeled global field and under permutation of packet labels.

For objects coming from the local fractional-ideal model, the global log-volume agrees, up to the stated normalization, with the degree of the corresponding arithmetic line bundle.

IUT III, Proposition 3.9(iii)global degree

Forced procession average

Proposition 3.9(i),(ii) normalizes procession log-volumes by averaging over the capsules in the procession. Remark 3.9.3 says more general weighted averages are not available: the same log-shell label can occur in multiple capsules, so compatibility with the inclusion morphisms forces equal weights.

IUT III, Remark 3.9.3no arbitrary weights

Log-link/log-volume compatibility

Proposition 3.9(iv) states that the log-volumes attached to the LGP/lgp objects are compatible along vertical log-links at the level of the rational spans of log-shells. Remark 3.9.4 reformulates this using realified semi-simplifications of measure spaces.

The point of the reformulation is to rule out a nontrivial dilation factor: otherwise the comparison would only be valued modulo a period log(λ), which would not support the real-valued estimates needed later.

IUT III, Proposition 3.9(iv)Remark 3.9.4

q-power log-volume

Remark 3.9.4(iv) interprets the bad-prime log-Kummer correspondence as passing from a local field to the submonoid O× · q^N, then to units, then through the local logarithm and back to the additive field. The normalized log-volume sends the generator of q^N to -log(q).

This is the local numerical interface between the q-pilot side and the later theta-pilot estimate.

IUT III, Remark 3.9.4(iv)q-pilot
Proposition 3.9 from packet regions to real-valued log-volume

Global Kummer non-interference

Proposition 3.10(i) constructs vertically coric global fields, monoids, Frobenioids, and LGP/lgp F-prime-strips from the vertical D-Θ±ellNF-Hodge theater. For every n,m, Kummer isomorphisms compare the ordinary global data with this vertically coric target.

Proposition 3.10(ii) then identifies the intersection of the global multiplicative field with all local packet integer monoids as the finite group of roots of unity. This is the non-interference statement that makes adjacent log-link ambiguity harmless.

IUT III, Proposition 3.10(i),(ii)roots of unity

FMOD/Fmod bias

Remark 3.10.1 distinguishes two roles. The multiplicative FMOD side admits precise log-Kummer compatibility with vertical log-links and is suited to computing Kummer-detachment indeterminacies.

The additive/local fractional-ideal Fmod side is tied to mono-analytic log-shells and log-volume estimates, but its local monoids are governed by upper semi-compatibility rather than precise equality. This is why it is suited to estimates, not to precise Kummer-detachment isomorphisms.

IUT III, Remark 3.10.1Fig. 3.2

F⊥ prime-strip compatibility

Proposition 3.10(iii) uses the global log-Kummer correspondence and the splitting-monoid part of Proposition 3.5 to obtain isomorphisms of associated F⊥-prime-strips. These isomorphisms are mutually compatible as m varies along the vertical log-links.

This precise compatibility applies to the MOD side. The source explicitly excludes the corresponding mod side from this precise conclusion.

IUT III, Proposition 3.10(iii)Kummer detachment

F×μ prime-strip square

Remark 3.10.2 reviews the commutative diagram of F×μ-prime-strips connecting the environment, Gaussian, LGP, and lgp variants in the ordinary n,m row and the vertically coric n,◦ row.

The lower horizontal arrows used in the horizontal links are obtained by conjugating the corresponding etale-like upper arrows by the vertical Kummer isomorphisms. This keeps the source of the horizontal Θ×μ links explicit.

IUT III, Remark 3.10.2F×μ
Proposition 3.10 precise Kummer compatibility versus estimate-oriented additivity

Theorem 3.11 multiradial representation

Theorem 3.11(i) constructs n,◦R_LGP from the vertically coric D⊢-prime-strip procession. Its data consist of mono-analytic tensor packets with procession-normalized log-volumes, bad-prime LGP splitting monoids acting on the relevant packet modules, and global FMOD/Fmod Frobenioids whose degrees are computed by log-volume.

The representation is taken modulo Ind1, coming from automorphisms of the procession, and Ind2, coming from independent local automorphism actions on the tensor factors. It is functorial for isomorphisms of processions and compatible with etale-picture permutation symmetries.

IUT III, Theorem 3.11(i)Ind1/Ind2

Theorem 3.11 log-Kummer correspondence

Theorem 3.11(ii) uses Kummer isomorphisms to compare the vertically coric data with the corresponding data in each n,m Hodge theater. The compared layers are local mono-analytic tensor packets, bad-prime splitting monoids, and global number-field/Frobenioid data.

For splitting monoids and the MOD-subscripted global Frobenioids, the variation in m is mutually compatible with the vertical log-links up to harmless root-of-unity ambiguity. For the local packet isomorphisms, the variation is Ind3: upper semi-compatibility by inclusions at nonarchimedean places and surjections at archimedean places.

IUT III, Theorem 3.11(ii)Ind3

Θ×μ-LGP link compatibility

Theorem 3.11(iii) states compatibility with the horizontal Θ×μ_LGP links. It compares the F⊢×μ portions, the environment prime-strips, the projective systems of mono-theta environments, and the evaluation-map portions of the log-Kummer correspondence.

The compatibility is stated as stabilization, equivariance, and functoriality with respect to arbitrary automorphisms of the domain and codomain of the horizontal arrows, modulo Ind1/Ind2/Ind3 where those indeterminacies apply.

IUT III, Theorem 3.11(iii)horizontal arrows

IPL, SHE, APT, HIS

Remark 3.11.1 recasts Theorem 3.11 as a functorial algorithm whose input is an F×μ-prime-strip and whose output is linked to that input by full poly-isomorphisms. This is the input prime-strip link property, IPL.

The same output is expressible relative to both the domain and codomain arithmetic holomorphic structures, giving SHE. The resulting parallel transport is algorithmic rather than a set-map on regions, giving APT. The theta-function machinery can be treated as hidden internal structure only when one studies the qualitative logical consequences.

IUT III, Remark 3.11.1IPL/SHE/APT

Label-to-procession transitions

Remark 3.11.2 explains that Theorem 3.11 uses all labels in the relevant F_l, |F_l|, and F_l± systems. The mono-analytic procession index sets are naked finite sets determined by cardinality, which is why they can be used in the multiradial representation.

Arbitrarily omitting labels is not available in the source argument.

IUT III, Remark 3.11.2Fig. 3.3

Log-volume coarse space

Remark 3.11.4 and Step (x) of the proof of Corollary 3.12 interpret Ind1/Ind2/Ind3 as a formal quotient. Procession-normalized log-volumes produce a coarse numerical object: they are invariant under Ind1 and Ind2, and they convert Ind3 into an inequality from above.

This is the numerical bridge from Theorem 3.11 to the Corollary 3.12 comparison, but only after the holomorphic hull and determinant/log-volume steps have made the objects comparable.

IUT III, Remark 3.11.4Cor. 3.12 Step (x)
Theorem 3.11 representation, correspondence, and horizontal compatibility

Tensor packets as containers

IUT III Propositions 3.1 and 3.2 construct local holomorphic and mono-analytic tensor packets. The mono-analytic packets associated to D⊢-prime-strips are the containers used in Theorem 3.11 for the multiradial representation.

The theorem applies the ±-procession of D-prime-strips from IUT I Proposition 6.9 and equips the resulting tensor packets with procession-normalized mono-analytic log-volumes.

IUT III, Propositions 3.1-3.2Theorem 3.11(i)(a)

LGP data inside the representation

Theorem 3.11(i) combines tensor packets with splitting monoids of LGP-monoids at bad places and with global number-field/Frobenioid data MMOD = Mmod, FMOD, and Fmod. This is the data denoted n,◦R_LGP.

The first two indeterminacies are explicit: automorphisms of the procession and independent local automorphism actions on tensor factors.

Theorem 3.11(i)Ind1/Ind2

Log-Kummer correspondence

Theorem 3.11(ii) relates vertically coric data to data in each n,m Hodge theater via Kummer isomorphisms. For tensor packets this relation is not equality across the vertical column: it is upper semi-compatible with the log-links, giving indeterminacy Ind3.

The same theorem states that these tensor-packet isomorphisms remain precisely compatible with the relevant log-volumes. This is why the one-sided packet relation can still feed a numerical estimate.

Theorem 3.11(ii)Ind3log-link

Kummer detachment before final form

The introduction to IUT III describes the Step (xi) comparison as first applying bi-coric mono-analytic log-shell theory, then Kummer theory for splitting monoids and copies of Fmod, in order to pass from Frobenius-like versions of objects in the Theta×μ-LGP link to the etale-like versions appearing in the multiradial representation.

Mochizuki calls this passage Kummer detachment. Because the log-theta lattice is far from commutative, the operation must use the entire log-Kummer correspondence along the vertical column and must account for Ind3; after detachment, Ind1 is applied again to reach the final multiradial form.

IUT III, Fig. I.8Kummer detachment

Hulls before log-volume

Remark 3.9.5 explains why the output possibilities cannot simply be replaced by their log-volumes immediately. The theory first passes through holomorphic hulls, determinant/tensor-power operations, and local/global Frobenioid comparison, so that the objects remain comparable to the q-pilot side of the Theta×μ-LGP link.

Only after this comparable-object stage does Corollary 3.12 apply the procession-normalized log-volume.

Remark 3.9.5Corollary 3.12
Audit point: the corridor is intentionally one-sided at the tensor-packet level but numerical at the log-volume level. Any wiki page, Lean interface, or informal explanation that silently upgrades upper semi-compatibility to an equality is no longer tracking Mochizuki's stated mechanism.
Sources: IUT III, §3 Related: log-Kummer correspondence Kummer detachment upper semi-compatibility holomorphic hull Corollary 3.12 corridor

Corollary 3.12 Corridor

The proof-critical corridor is the route from IUT III, Theorem 3.11 through Step (x), Step (xi), and the final signed real inequality. The core issue is how qualitative multiradial/common-container data becomes a numerical log-volume comparison without illicitly identifying histories or comparison levels.

Theorem 3.11 to Corollary 3.12 conditional interfaces made explicit
IUT III sourceContentRole in the 3.12 corridor
Corollary 3.12 statement Defines -|log(Θ)| from the holomorphic hull of possible theta-pilot images and -|log(q)| from the q-pilot image. States -|log(Θ)| ≥ -|log(q)|, equivalently C_Θ ≥ -1 for the displayed upper-bound convention.
Proof before Step (i) Introduces possible-image regions, their holomorphic hulls, and the finiteness of -|log(Θ)|. Restricts attention to possible images interpretable as F-prime-strip data before applying the later comparison.
Steps (i)-(vi) Fixes a single Θ×μ_LGP link and explains why unit/value portions must be treated through one horizontal link plus vertical log-Kummer correspondences. Accounts for bi-coric log-shell containers, Kummer detachment, and the need for Ind3.
Step (x) Interprets Ind1/Ind2/Ind3 as a formal quotient and uses procession-normalized log-volume as a coarse numerical object. Ind1/Ind2 become invariant; Ind3 becomes an inequality from above.
Step (xi) Applies IPL, SHE, APT, holomorphic hulls, determinants, and log-volume to compare theta output possibilities with the q-pilot value. Yields the membership -|log(q)| ∈ R≤-|log(Θ)|, hence the final inequality.
Remarks 3.12.1-3.12.4 Discuss generalized links, intertwining of unit/value dimensions, 0-column/1-column differences, and Teichmuller analogies. Clarifies why weakened data, distinct labels, holomorphic hulls, and log-volumes are not optional decorations.

What is formal algebra

Once Lean has q_signed <= theta_signed, 0 < -q_signed, and the displayed theta_signed <= C_Theta * (-q_signed), the lower bound -1 <= C_Theta is elementary ordered-real algebra.

What remains mathematical source work

The hard part is constructing the bridge from IUT source data to the signed-log upper-ray membership of the q-pilot, while preserving the distinctions between labels, histories, real-line copies, and hull/log-volume levels.

Corollary 3.12 statement

The source defines -|log(Θ)| as the procession-normalized mono-analytic log-volume of the holomorphic hull of the union of possible images of a theta-pilot object, subject to Ind1, Ind2, and Ind3. The q-pilot value -|log(q)| is computed from the image of a q-pilot object and is not treated as subject to those three indeterminacies.

The asserted inequality is -|log(Θ)| ≥ -|log(q)|.

IUT III, Corollary 3.12statement

Possible images and hulls

At the start of the proof, the possible theta-pilot images are collected in packet regions U; their holomorphic hulls are denoted by the corresponding barred regions. Compactness and the definition of log-volume give finiteness of -|log(Θ)|.

The proof then restricts attention to possible images that can be interpreted as F-prime-strip data.

IUT III, Cor. 3.12 proofholomorphic hull

Step (x): numerical coarse object

Step (x) says the multiradial representation is subject to Ind1, Ind2, and Ind3. Procession-normalized log-volume behaves like a coarse numerical quotient: it is invariant under Ind1 and Ind2 and converts Ind3 into an upper-bound inequality.

The log-link compatibility of Proposition 3.9(iv) keeps this numerical object compatible with the arrows of the log-Kummer correspondence.

IUT III, Cor. 3.12 Step (x)Ind3 -> inequality

Step (xi): comparison route

Step (xi) uses one Θ×μ_LGP link from (0,0) to (1,0). The theta-pilot object in the domain is related to the q-pilot object in the codomain through the value-group portion, while the unit portions are handled by bi-coric log-shells and the 0-column log-Kummer correspondence.

After applying IPL, SHE, APT, holomorphic hulls, determinants, and log-volume, the output possibilities form an upper ray R≤-|log(Θ)| that must contain the q-pilot log-volume -|log(q)|.

IUT III, Cor. 3.12 Step (xi)IPL/SHE/APT

Two computations of the q-pilot

Step (xi-g) summarizes the argument as two tautologically equivalent ways to compute the q-pilot log-volume at (1,0): via the theta-pilot side transported through the multiradial representation and via the q-pilot side in the fixed codomain arithmetic holomorphic structure.

The proof then reads membership of the q-pilot value in the theta upper ray, yielding -|log(q)| ≤ -|log(Θ)|.

IUT III, Fig. 3.8membership

Why global Frobenioids remain required

Step (xii) warns against replacing the global realified Frobenioids by only local Frobenioids. Local Frobenioid objects have endomorphisms that are not automorphisms, creating uncontrolled local scaling ambiguity such as p_v^N-multiples.

That ambiguity would make the precise log-volume computation in Step (xi) meaningless.

IUT III, Cor. 3.12 Step (xii)global data

Remark 3.12.2: intertwining

Remark 3.12.2 describes the proof as dismantling and reassembling the unit and value-group dimensions of a number field. The weakened F×μ-prime-strip data are weak enough that theta and q pilot objects cannot be distinguished there; the distinction returns only when one chooses an arithmetic holomorphic structure.

In logical form, Mochizuki describes the q-intertwining as giving a simultaneous construction of the theta-intertwining up to indeterminacies, which formally yields the Corollary 3.12 inequality after the quotient and log-volume operations.

IUT III, Remark 3.12.2intertwining

0-column and 1-column differ

Remark 3.12.2(iv),(v) stresses that both the Frobenius-like •0 and etale-like ◦ roles are essential in each column. The 0-column uses log-shells as containers for theta-side regions and therefore incurs Ind3.

The 1-column holds its arithmetic holomorphic structure fixed. There, log-shells document the logarithmic relation between ring structures, while holomorphic hulls and log-volumes absorb the remaining choices; this gives equalities on the q side rather than only upper bounds.

IUT III, Fig. 3.90/1 columns
Corollary 3.12 Source Route from possible images to the ordered-real inequality

Theta-pilot quantity

Corollary 3.12 defines -|log(Theta)| as the procession-normalized mono-analytic log-volume of the holomorphic hull of the union of possible images of a theta-pilot object, subject to Ind1, Ind2, and Ind3.

q-pilot quantity

The q-pilot quantity -|log(q)| is the procession-normalized mono-analytic log-volume of the image of a q-pilot object. In the corollary statement it is not regarded as subject to the three indeterminacies.

Stated numerical output

The asserted comparison is -|log(Theta)| >= -|log(q)|. Equivalently, for any C_Theta with -|log(Theta)| <= C_Theta * |log(q)|, the conclusion is C_Theta >= -1.

Step (xi-e)

Mochizuki describes the output possible log-volumes R_{<= -|log(Theta)|} as linked by IPL to the input q-pilot log-volume -|log(q)|, while the construction remains expressible under SHE relative to the chosen holomorphic structure.

Step (xi-f)

The formal conclusion is that the constructed output possibilities include the q-pilot log-volume. This is the exact place where a wiki or Lean model must expose the bridge from source data to numerical membership.

3.11.5 convention

Mochizuki's 2026 formalization report uses "3.11.5" for a reorganized interface that moves hull-and-determinant work out of the final algebraic comparison. The label is a formalization convention, not an original theorem number in IUT I-IV.

Audit rule for this wiki: the final inequality is not the hard part. The hard part is the source-valid membership assertion -|log(q)| in R_{<= -|log(Theta)|} after the allowed weakening, hull, determinant, log-volume, and indeterminacy operations.
Sources: IUT III Higuchi 2026 Pajo 2026 Related: dispute formalization

Corollary 3.12 Proof Steps

This page maps the proof of IUT III Corollary 3.12 at the level needed for audit. The statement is an ordered-real inequality, but the proof route passes through possible images, holomorphic hulls, one fixed Theta×mu_LGP link, bi-coric log-shell containers, log-Kummer correspondences, Kummer-detachment indeterminacies, and finally the membership assertion -|log(q)| in R_{<= -|log(Theta)|}.

Proof Skeleton from source regions to membership in an ordered-real upper ray
Proof locationSource contentAudit reading
Statement Defines -|log(Theta)| from the holomorphic hull of possible theta-pilot images in the multiradial representation, and -|log(q)| from the q-pilot image. The theta quantity is indeterminacy-sensitive; the q quantity is the fixed comparison value.
Before Step (i) Introduces regions U, their holomorphic hulls, proves finiteness under the negative case, and restricts to possible images interpretable as F-prime-strip data. The proof does not compare arbitrary subsets; it first filters to the source-compatible prime-strip interpretation.
Steps (i)-(iii) Fix one Theta×mu_LGP link from (0,0) to (1,0), with a unit portion and a value-group portion. The value portion maps theta-pilot objects to q-pilot objects, and the proof explains why unit and value portions must not be compared through different horizontal links. This is a strict single-link discipline. Splitting the unit and value comparisons across different horizontal positions would not track the source proof.
Step (iv) The desired vertical shift for units conflicts with the single-link requirement. The source resolves this by using vertically coric structures and log-Kummer correspondences, at the cost of Ind3 upper-semi compatibility. Ind3 is introduced as the price of reconciling vertical-shift needs with noncommutativity of the log-theta lattice.
Step (v) Use the unit portion of the theta-link and bi-coricity of units/log-shells to treat log-shells as multiradial mono-analytic containers for local and global value-group structures of the theta-pilot. Log-shells function as containers for theta-side regions; this is not a direct equality with the original holomorphic units.
Step (vi) Recalls that the log-Kummer correspondences for splitting monoids and global Frobenioids depend on Galois evaluation, mono-theta rigidity, basepoint compatibility, and compatible cyclotomic rigidity mechanisms. The proof relies on earlier rigidity inputs; they are not decorative references.
Steps (vii)-(ix) Separates F_l^{±} and F_l symmetries, records the role of global synchronizations, and explains how copies of Fmod translate between multiplicative Frobenioid data and additive log-shell containers. This is the source explanation of why both FMOD-style rigidity and Fmod-style estimate machinery appear.
Step (x) Kummer-detachment relates Frobenius-like structures at (0,0) to the multiradial representation but introduces Ind1, Ind2, and Ind3. Procession-normalized log-volume acts as a coarse numerical object: invariant under Ind1/Ind2 and converting Ind3 into an upper inequality. This is where the proof moves from formal quotient data to a real-valued comparison object.
Steps (xi-a)-(xi-c) The q-pilot at (1,0) is fixed in its arithmetic holomorphic structure. The multiradial algorithm produces output possibilities satisfying IPL and SHE, and these are viewed from the 1,◦ structure. The proof reads the theta output inside the codomain structure, not by identifying the two ring structures.
Step (xi-d) Formation of holomorphic hulls, determinants of higher-rank arithmetic vector bundles, and log-volume produces comparable objects: R_{<= -|log(Theta)|} and -|log(q)|. The hull/determinant/log-volume passage is the comparable-object stage.
Step (xi-e) SHE is interpreted as expressibility by operations still valid when the pilot-object log-volume of the input prime-strip is fixed to -|log(q)|. The output possible log-volumes remain IPL-linked to that q-pilot value. The fixed q-pilot value is a constraint on the construction, not a later informal substitution.
Step (xi-f) The construction of output possibilities is said to include, perhaps up to approximation from indeterminacies, the pilot-object log-volume of the input prime-strip. Thus -|log(q)| in R_{<= -|log(Theta)|}, so -|log(q)| <= -|log(Theta)|. This membership is the central audit point. Once supplied, the final ordered-real inequality is formal.
Steps (xi-g),(xi-h) The proof summarizes the argument as two equivalent ways to compute the q-pilot log-volume. It also warns that a routine extension to N-th tensor powers would give sharper false inequalities and is not available. The first-power theta-function theory is essential; tensor-power extrapolation is explicitly rejected.
Step (xii) Global realified Frobenioids cannot be replaced by local Frobenioids alone, since local Frobenioid objects have non-automorphism endomorphisms that create uncontrolled p_v^N-scale ambiguity. A local-only log-volume reconstruction would destroy the precise computation required in Step (xi).

Single theta-link rule

Steps (i)-(iii) force the proof to use one horizontal Theta×mu_LGP link for both unit and value portions. The noncommutativity of the lattice is exactly why a two-link simplification is not source-faithful.

Steps (i)-(iii)one link

Vertical-shift cost

Step (iv) says the vertical shift needed for unit-group computation is implemented through vertically coric structures and log-Kummer correspondences. The cost is Ind3, i.e. upper-semi compatibility.

Step (iv)Ind3

Rigidity inputs

Step (vi) ties the proof back to mono-theta environments, discrete rigidity, isomorphism-class compatibility, and cyclotomic rigidity. A formal model must expose these as dependencies of the log-Kummer bridge.

Step (vi)EtTh

Coarse numerical space

Step (x) is the transition from indeterminacy-laden multiradial data to real log-volumes: Ind1 and Ind2 become invariance, and Ind3 becomes an upper inequality.

Step (x)log-volume

Hull, determinant, log-volume

Step (xi-d) explains why output regions must pass through holomorphic hulls, determinants, and log-volumes before they can be compared with the q-pilot arithmetic line-bundle value.

Step (xi-d)comparable objects

Membership assertion

Step (xi-f) is the exact numerical bridge: -|log(q)| must belong to the theta-side upper ray R_{<= -|log(Theta)|}. The inequality is then immediate.

Step (xi-f)audit point
Source-faithful summaries should not replace Step (xi-f) by an assumed equality of theta and q regions. The proof route is a membership in an upper ray after the permitted weakening, hull, determinant, log-volume, and indeterminacy operations.
Sources: IUT III, Corollary 3.12 proof and Remarks 3.12.1-3.12.4 Related: 3.12 corridor Theorem 3.11 source page 3.11 log-Kummer card packet log-volume 3.12 diagnostics

Formal Diagnostics Around Corollary 3.12

Several recent papers isolate narrow formalization tasks around the 3.12 corridor. They are not substitutes for IUT III, and they do not by themselves prove or disprove IUT. Their value for this wiki is diagnostic: they identify which data must be present before a common-container or qualitative comparison can be read as a numerical ordered-real inequality.

Diagnostic bridge test what must be present before the final inequality is validly read

Initial theta data

Dupuy-Hilado isolate the hypothesis side of Corollary 3.12 by explaining initial theta data and constructing it explicitly for the elliptic curve 11a1. This supports the wiki's separation between input construction and the later multiradial log-volume comparison.

input side11a1

Step (xi-e) to (xi-f)

Higuchi formalizes a qualitative package consisting of IPL, SHE, APT, common-container passage, and output closed-half-line data, then gives a countermodel showing that this package alone does not imply the required numerical membership statement.

negative testbridge axiom

Scale-free containers

Pajo studies a typed common-container abstraction with monoid, hull, log-volume, embedding, comparison, and final numerical maps. A relative rescaling countermodel shows that scale-free container data alone does not entail the final numerical inequality.

no-goscale retention

Approximation is not enough

In the closed-half-line setting, Higuchi's note observes that approximation language does not remove the need for membership: approximate realization from below collapses to exact inclusion in the relevant upper ray.

closed raymembership

Bridge certificates

Pajo lists families of additional bridge mechanisms that would defeat the scale-free countermodel, such as numerical charts, scale-preserving transport, certified relations, interval bridges, sandwich estimates, and calibration plus comparison-realization.

audit targetcertificates

Wiki policy

The wiki treats these papers as formal lower-bound diagnostics. A page or Lean module may use their abstractions to test whether enough bridge data has been supplied, but not as a replacement for the source IUT construction.

scope controlformalization
Sources: Dupuy-Hilado initial theta data Higuchi 2026 Pajo 2026 Related: Corollary 3.12 corridor formalization IUT IV consequences

IUT IV Section 1: Local Log-Volume Estimates

IUT IV Propositions 1.1 through 1.5 are the local numerical substrate for Theorem 1.10. They estimate tensor products of local rings, different ideals, p-adic logarithms, normalized nonarchimedean log-volume, and archimedean tensor-product metrics before any Diophantine application is made.

Local estimate route from tensor products to nonarchimedean and archimedean containers
Source itemLocal statementWhy it matters downstream
Proposition 1.1 For a finite tensor product R_I of rings of integers over Z_p, normalization satisfies R_I subset (R_I)^~, and a power controlled by the different of I_* carries the normalization back into R_I. This is the first local containment used to turn tensor-product indeterminacy into explicit powers of p.
Proposition 1.2(i) The p-adic logarithm image log_p(R_i^x) lies between fractional ideals p^{a_i}R_i and p^{-b_i}R_i, with equality in the tame small ramification range. The logarithm layer becomes a bounded lattice rather than an uncontrolled analytic operation.
Proposition 1.2(ii)-(iv) Any automorphism phi of the rationalized logarithm space that preserves log_p(R_I^x) sends fractional ideal pieces into explicitly shifted logarithmic and normalized tensor-product regions. This is the source of the later automorphism-tolerant containment used when bounding log-volume under indeterminacy.
Proposition 1.3 Relative different exponents are bounded below by tame ramification terms and, under a Galois/tame intermediate hypothesis, bounded above by a wild-degree term plus a base contribution. The different terms in Theorem 1.10 are not placeholders; they come from explicit ramification estimates.
Proposition 1.4(i)-(ii) The log-volume mu_log is normalized so that mu_log(R_i)=0 and mu_log(pR_i)=-log(p); it also computes mu_log(log_p(R_i^x)) after quotienting torsion. This fixes the numerical scale used later by packet and theta-pilot log-volumes.
Proposition 1.4(iii)-(iv) Combining Propositions 1.2 and 1.3 gives explicit upper bounds for the log-volume of the images of automorphism-distorted local regions; in the unramified odd case, phi((R_I)^~) stays inside (R_I)^~. This is the nonarchimedean local estimate that feeds the Step (x) log-volume bounds in IUT III/IV.
Proposition 1.5 At archimedean places, tensor-product and direct-sum metrics differ by controlled powers of 2; primitive automorphisms preserve the decompositions and the integral ball B_I, and equal-length tensor vectors lie in lambda^{|I|}B_I. This supplies the archimedean counterpart to the p-adic hull estimates and explains the metric factors that later enter Theorem 1.10.

Tensor products and differents

Proposition 1.1 works over Z_p with finitely many local integer rings. Its output is an inclusion of the normalization back into the original tensor product after multiplying by a ceiling of the summed different exponent.

Prop. 1.1normalization

p-adic logarithm lattice

The constants a_i and b_i encode the valuation loss when passing between units and their p-adic logarithms. They are the local source of the later ceiling/floor terms.

Prop. 1.2logarithm

Automorphism-tolerant containment

The automorphism phi is arbitrary subject to preserving the logarithm submodule. The result is therefore not a calculation in one chosen coordinate system; it is a containment stable under the permitted local ambiguity.

Prop. 1.2(ii)phi

Relative different estimates

Proposition 1.3 separates tame and wild behavior. The lower bound is exact in the tame case; the upper bound uses a Galois extension over a tamely ramified intermediate field and the p-primary degree exponent.

Prop. 1.3ramification

Normalized log-volume scale

Proposition 1.4 normalizes log-volume by field degree. The basic scale is fixed by mu_log(R_i)=0 and mu_log(pR_i)=-log(p).

Prop. 1.4(i)scale

Torsion quotient

The computation of mu_log(log_p(R_i^x)) uses the quotient of units by torsion. This is why the residue-degree and p-power torsion terms appear explicitly in the local estimate.

Prop. 1.4(ii)units

Nonarchimedean hull bound

Proposition 1.4(iii) gives the explicit log-volume bounds for automorphism images of fractional ideal regions. This is the local numerical version of "upper-semi" containment.

Prop. 1.4(iii)upper bound

Unramified odd stability

In the case p>2 and all ramification indices equal one, the automorphism image of the normalized tensor product remains inside the same normalized region and the log-volume is zero.

Prop. 1.4(iv)unramified

Direct-sum/tensor metric

Proposition 1.5 compares C tensor_R C with C plus C. The Chinese-remainder isomorphism is metric-compatible up to the stated factor, and primitive automorphisms preserve the relevant decomposition.

Prop. 1.5(i),(ii)archimedean

Integral ball B_I

For multiple copies, the topological ring decomposition gives 2^{|I|-1}|V|^{|I|} copies of C. Their unit balls define B_I, preserved by primitive automorphisms.

Prop. 1.5(iii)B_I

Equal-length tensor containment

If each component vector has length lambda, their tensor lies in lambda^{|I|}B_I. This is the archimedean counterpart of the local p-adic hull containment.

Prop. 1.5(iv)lambda
Formalization warning: these propositions should not be replaced by a single "local estimate" axiom. The different exponent, logarithm lattice, torsion quotient, automorphism action, normalization scale, and archimedean metric factor are separate pieces of numerical data used later by Theorem 1.10.
Sources: IUT IV, Propositions 1.1-1.5 Related: IUT IV overview theta-pilot estimate IUT III packet log-volume tensor/log-volume corridor

IUT IV Section 1: Prime and Averaging Input

Propositions 1.6 and 1.7 supply the elementary global numerical tools that connect the local log-volume estimates to Theorem 1.10: a prime-number estimate producing eta_prm, and a weighted-average identity for packet-normalized log-volumes.

Global averaging route from local weights to packet-normalized theta-pilot estimates
Source itemStatementUse in Theorem 1.10
Proposition 1.6 From the prime number theorem, for sufficiently large n, the n-th prime satisfies n <= 4p_n/(3 log(p_n)). This turns prime counting into an explicit reciprocal-log estimate that can be inserted into the final theta-pilot bound.
Proposition 1.6 There is a positive real threshold eta_prm such that the weighted sum over primes p <= eta is at least one for all eta >= eta_prm. The constant eta_prm is later referenced as a concrete large-prime cutoff, not as an unspecified analytic assumption.
Proposition 1.7 For a finite set E, positive weights lambda_e, and real values beta_e, the weighted average over E^n of the tuple sum beta_vec_e equals n beta_avg. This is the elementary identity that lets packet-normalized local log-volumes be averaged over repeated label choices.
Remark 1.7.1 When using the normalized log-volumes of Proposition 1.4, the weights from IUT III Remark 3.1.1 must be replaced by local degree weights [(F_mod)_v : Q_vQ]. This prevents mixing normalized and non-normalized log-volume conventions in Theorem 1.10.
Remark 1.7.1 The proposition is applied with E the set of valuations over a fixed rational place, n=|A|, lambda_e the local degree, and beta_e a normalized local log-volume. The abstract averaging formula is tied to the actual valuation-indexed packet data used in the IUT IV estimate.

Prime-number bound

Proposition 1.6 uses only the standard prime number theorem, but fixes an explicit inequality strong enough for the later proof. The wiki records it because it is where analytic prime distribution first enters IUT IV.

Prop. 1.6PNT

eta_prm threshold

The threshold eta_prm packages the sufficiently large range of the prime-number estimate. Later estimates can refer to this number instead of reopening the analytic proof.

Prop. 1.6large bound

Finite weight set

Proposition 1.7 starts with a finite nonempty set E, positive weights lambda_e, and real values beta_e. This abstraction is deliberately elementary, so the later packet averaging is a transparent calculation.

Prop. 1.7finite set

Tuple weight product

For a tuple vec e in E^n, the product lambda_Pi_vec_e multiplies the component weights, while beta_vec_e sums the component values. The identity computes their normalized average.

Prop. 1.7E^n

Normalized-weight replacement

Remark 1.7.1 is a convention checkpoint: because IUT IV uses normalized log-volume, the degree weights used in IUT III's non-normalized setting must be replaced by normalized local degree weights.

Rem. 1.7.1normalization

Packet log-volume application

In Theorem 1.10, E is the set of local valuations over a rational place, and beta_e is a normalized log-volume of a compact open subset. This is where the elementary average becomes a packet-normalized log-volume computation.

Rem. 1.7.1packets
Formalization warning: the averaging formula is elementary, but its use is convention-sensitive. A formal model must state whether log-volumes are normalized as in Proposition 1.4; otherwise the weights imported from IUT III Remark 3.1.1 have the wrong scale.
Sources: IUT IV, Propositions 1.6-1.7 and Remark 1.7.1 Related: local estimates weighted averages atlas card prime-number estimate atlas card theta-pilot estimate

IUT IV Proposition 1.8: Elliptic Torsion Input

Proposition 1.8 collects the elliptic-curve facts needed before the explicit theta-pilot estimate: torsion detects automorphisms, descent data is expressed by sections over Galois subgroups, rational torsion forces uniqueness and semistability, 2-torsion gives the Legendre form, and reduction type controls ramification on torsion.

Elliptic input route torsion, descent, Legendre form, and ramification constraints
Source itemStatementRole in IUT IV
Proposition 1.8(i) For l >= 3, the action of automorphisms of a polarized abelian variety on l-torsion is injective. This makes torsion-level structure rigid enough to control models and descent in the later application.
Proposition 1.8(ii) For an elliptic curve over a perfect field, automorphisms over the algebraic closure fit into an exact sequence over G_k; models over extensions are equivalent to sections over the corresponding closed subgroups. The proposition translates model/descent questions into Galois-section data, matching the anabelian style of the surrounding IUT machinery.
Proposition 1.8(iii) If the geometric automorphism group is {+/-1}, then the 2-torsion representation factors through the field-of-moduli Galois image. This isolates the 2-torsion information used to pass to a Legendre-form model in the application setup.
Proposition 1.8(iv) If two descents to k have all l-torsion rational over k, then the descended elliptic curves are isomorphic over k. Rational torsion eliminates a model ambiguity that would otherwise affect the initial-theta-data construction.
Proposition 1.8(v) Over a complete discrete valuation field, rational l-torsion with l >= 3 and l invertible implies semistable reduction. This supplies the reduction-type control required by the later bad/good place estimates.
Proposition 1.8(vi) If the field-of-moduli image is all of G_k and the 2-torsion action is trivial, then the curve descends to a Legendre-form model; over a discrete valuation field, semistability is reached after an extension of degree at most two. This is the local source for the lambda-line/Legendre model used in IUT IV Corollary 2.2.
Proposition 1.8(vii) For torsion prime to the residue characteristic, good reduction gives unramified Galois action on torsion, while bad multiplicative reduction gives tame ramification. These ramification alternatives feed the explicit prime and different estimates in Theorem 1.10 and Section 2.

Serre criterion

The first assertion is a rigidity statement: a nontrivial automorphism of a polarized abelian variety cannot act trivially on l-torsion for l >= 3.

Prop. 1.8(i)torsion

Descent via sections

A model over an extension field is the same as a section of the automorphism-to-Galois map over the corresponding subgroup. This keeps descent explicit instead of treating the model as a label.

Prop. 1.8(ii)sections

Field-of-moduli image

The finite extension determined by the Galois image is the field generated by the j-invariant. This is why the later field-of-moduli notation is tied to actual descent data.

Prop. 1.8(ii)j

2-torsion factorization

When the only geometric automorphisms are +/-1, their action on 2-torsion is trivial, so the 2-torsion representation descends to the field-of-moduli Galois image.

Prop. 1.8(iii)E[2]

Rational torsion uniqueness

If all l-torsion is rational, the section is forced by lying in the kernel of the torsion representation. This gives uniqueness of the descended model.

Prop. 1.8(iv)unique model

Semistable reduction from level

Full rational l-torsion for l >= 3 gives an integral point on a proper level cover of the compactified elliptic moduli stack, hence semistable reduction.

Prop. 1.8(v)level

Legendre-form descent

Trivial 2-torsion action gives rational 2-torsion, hence a Legendre equation after at most a quadratic twist. This is the source for the lambda-line model in the IUT IV application.

Prop. 1.8(vi)Legendre

Good reduction

For torsion order invertible in the valuation ring, good reduction makes the Galois action on torsion unramified. This becomes a clean local input at good places.

Prop. 1.8(vii)unramified

Multiplicative reduction

Bad multiplicative reduction gives a tamely ramified torsion action. This separates the bad multiplicative places from the good ones in the arithmetic estimates.

Prop. 1.8(vii)tame
Formalization warning: Proposition 1.8 is not just background elliptic-curve folklore. It supplies named gates for uniqueness of models, field-of-moduli descent, Legendre-form input, and ramification cases used by the later construction of suitable initial theta data.
Sources: IUT IV, Proposition 1.8 Related: prime/averaging input arithmetic divisors theta-pilot estimate IUT IV output route

IUT IV Definition 1.9: Arithmetic Divisors

Definition 1.9 fixes the global bookkeeping language used in Theorem 1.10: arithmetic divisors on a number field, support and effectivity, the degree and normalized degree maps, and the localized normalized E-degree over a rational valuation.

Arithmetic divisor bookkeeping global valuation sums and base-extension-invariant degrees
Source itemDefinitionUse in IUT IV
Definition 1.9 setup For a number field F, valuations split into nonarchimedean and archimedean parts. At nonarchimedean v, the notation includes ord_v, ramification index e_v, and residue cardinality q_v. This is the valuation language used by the later different, conductor, and q-parameter divisors.
Definition 1.9(i) An R-arithmetic divisor is a finite formal sum sum c_v v over valuations. Its support is where c_v != 0; it is effective if all coefficients are nonnegative. The theorem can speak uniformly about finite and archimedean height data without leaving the divisor formalism.
Definition 1.9(i) The degree map weights nonarchimedean valuations by log(q_v) and archimedean valuations by 1. The normalized degree divides by [F:Q]. This fixes the global scale for the explicit estimates in Theorem 1.10 and Section 2.
Definition 1.9(i) For every finite extension K/F, normalized degree is invariant under pullback: deg(a|K)=deg(a). This allows the proof to move through the field extensions appearing in initial theta data without changing the normalized degree scale.
Definition 1.9(ii) If E is a nonempty set of valuations of F above one rational valuation v_Q, then a_E is the supported part and deg_E(a) is the normalized E-degree. This is the local-over-global degree used when estimates are localized at a chosen rational place.
Definition 1.9(ii) The localized degree is also stable under finite base extension: deg_{E|K}(a|K)=deg_E(a). This preserves the local contribution when passing from F to larger fields in the theta-data tower.

Valuation split

Definition 1.9 starts by separating nonarchimedean and archimedean valuations. Only nonarchimedean valuations carry ord_v, e_v, and q_v; archimedean valuations enter through the degree weight 1.

Def. 1.9V(F)

Finite formal sum

Arithmetic divisors are finite sums over all valuations, not ideals alone. This is why the same object can carry finite q-parameter data and archimedean contributions.

Def. 1.9(i)ADiv

Support and effectivity

Support records the valuations with nonzero coefficient. Effectivity is coefficientwise nonnegativity and later applies to q-parameter and conductor-like divisors.

Def. 1.9(i)effective

Degree map

The degree map sends nonarchimedean v to log(q_v) and archimedean v to 1. The normalized version divides by [F:Q].

Def. 1.9(i)degree

Base-extension invariance

Pulling an arithmetic divisor from F to a finite extension K preserves normalized degree. This is a scaling convention, not an automatic property of unnormalized degree.

Def. 1.9(i)pullback

Localized E-degree

The E-degree isolates the contribution from valuations above a fixed rational valuation. It is the bookkeeping device for localizing global divisor estimates.

Def. 1.9(ii)local
Formalization warning: arithmetic divisors in Definition 1.9 combine finite and archimedean valuation data and use normalized degrees. A formalization should not silently replace them by fractional ideals or by unnormalized divisor degrees.
Sources: IUT IV, Definition 1.9 Related: elliptic torsion input theta-pilot estimate IUT IV overview IUT IV output route

IUT IV Theorem 1.10: Theta-Pilot Estimate

Theorem 1.10 is the explicit numerical meeting point of IUT III Corollary 3.12 and the local estimates of IUT IV Section 1. It starts with initial theta data satisfying the Corollary 3.12 hypotheses, adds torsion and field-of-moduli assumptions, and converts the theta-pilot log-volume bound into a concrete inequality for log(q).

Theta-pilot estimate route Corollary 3.12 plus local numerical estimates
Theorem 1.10 itemPaper contentWiki/formalization consequence
Hypotheses The theorem assumes initial theta data in the situation of IUT III Corollary 3.12, with good reduction at good nonarchimedean places not dividing 2*ell, and with specified torsion-field conditions. The page is downstream of the Corollary 3.12 corridor; it does not independently prove that corridor.
Tripodal field The field tower includes Fmod, the tripodal field Ftpd = Fmod(E[2]), and F, together with 2, 3, and 5 torsion conditions used in the explicit estimates. Field data and torsion data must be represented before the inequality can be read as a Diophantine estimate.
Divisor inputs For an intermediate field F' between Fmod and K, Galois over Fmod, the theorem uses arithmetic divisors d_ADiv^F', q_ADiv^F', and f_ADiv^F' for differents, q-parameters, and bad-place conductor support. These are Definition 1.9 objects with normalized degrees, not informal height labels.
Theta-pilot target Step (iv) estimates the procession-normalized mono-analytic log-volume of the holomorphic hull of possible images of the theta-pilot object, relative to the Kummer isomorphisms of IUT III Theorem 3.11 and subject to Ind1, Ind2, and Ind3. This is the bridge from the IUT III comparison corridor to the explicit IUT IV numerical calculation.
Local cases Step (v) handles distinguished nonarchimedean valuations using Proposition 1.4(iii); Step (vi) gives zero contribution at nondistinguished nonarchimedean valuations; Step (vii) applies Proposition 1.5 to archimedean valuations. The local estimates should remain as three separate formal gates, because their hypotheses and error terms differ.
Averaging and primes Step (viii) sums the local bounds using the weighted-average formalism and absorbs prime-counting terms by Proposition 1.6 and eta_prm. The final constant is a composed estimate, not a single unexplained numerical axiom.
Final inequality Taking the displayed value of CTheta and using CTheta >= -1 yields an upper bound for (1/6) log(q) by normalized different/conductor degrees and the explicit e*_mod ell + eta_prm term. The formal target is a theorem transforming certified Corollary 3.12 input plus local Section 1 estimates into this explicit inequality.

Field tower

The theorem works over Fmod, Ftpd, F, and K. The tripodal intermediate field is generated by 2-torsion, while the 3- and 5-torsion hypotheses enter the explicit bound through the chosen initial theta data.

Thm. 1.10field data

Divisor inputs

The theorem's d, q, and f terms are normalized arithmetic-divisor degrees. This keeps ramification, bad places, and theta q-parameters on the same global scale.

Def. 1.9normalized degree

Theta-pilot hull

The object estimated is a holomorphic hull of possible theta-pilot images under the multiradial comparison machinery of Theorem 3.11. Ind3 is used as an upper-semi containment, matching the sign-sensitive log-volume convention.

Step (iv)theta-pilot

Distinguished local case

At distinguished nonarchimedean valuations, the proof applies Proposition 1.4(iii) to bound automorphism-distorted local regions by different, q-parameter, prime, and ramification terms.

Step (v)Prop. 1.4

Nondistinguished case

At nondistinguished nonarchimedean valuations, Proposition 1.4(iv) gives an exactly stable normalized tensor-product region, hence zero upper contribution to the relevant log-volume.

Step (vi)zero bound

Archimedean case

At archimedean valuations, Proposition 1.5 bounds the tensor-product region by a power of the integral ball B_I, giving the archimedean term used in the total estimate.

Step (vii)metric

Procession averaging

The proof averages over the packet/procession indices using Proposition 1.7, so the local log-volume bounds become a single procession-normalized global estimate.

Step (viii)weighted average

Prime absorption

Proposition 1.6 controls the prime-sum contribution by eta_prm. This is where the elementary analytic estimate enters the otherwise arithmetic divisor calculation.

Prop. 1.6eta_prm

Final inequality

The theorem derives (1/6)log(q) bounded by a multiple of log(d)+log(f) plus the explicit torsion/prime term. The sign-sensitive input is |log(q)| = (1/(2*ell))log(q) in the IUT convention.

outputlog(q)
Formalization warning: Theorem 1.10 is not a substitute proof of IUT III Corollary 3.12. It assumes the Corollary 3.12 lower bound CTheta >= -1 and then performs the explicit local, averaged, and prime-counting estimates needed to turn that lower bound into a Diophantine numerical inequality.
Sources: IUT IV, Theorem 1.10 Related: Corollary 3.12 Theorem 3.11 algorithms local estimates prime/averaging input elliptic torsion input arithmetic divisors suitable initial theta data IUT IV output route

IUT IV Corollary 2.2: Suitable Initial Theta Data

Corollary 2.2 is the application gate. It starts from the lambda-line P^1 - {0,1,infty}, imposes compact-boundedness and a 2-adic j-invariant condition, removes controlled exceptional points, chooses a large auxiliary prime ell, and produces initial theta data satisfying the hypotheses of Theorem 1.10.

Corollary 2.2 application gate lambda-line data to Theorem 1.10 hypotheses
Corollary 2.2 itemPaper contentWiki/formalization consequence
Lambda line The source curve is P^1_Q with divisor {0,1,infty}, viewed as the lambda-line for the Legendre equation y^2=x(x-1)(x-lambda). The application is first proved in the concrete thrice-punctured-line setting before GenEll transfers it to general hyperbolic curves.
Compact set A compactly bounded subset KV is chosen with support containing the prime 2. The extra condition (*j-inv) requires the 2-adic j-invariant image to be bounded. This is a real application hypothesis, not a stylistic restriction: it controls the comparison of all-prime and odd-prime q-parameter heights.
BD identity Part (i) identifies, up to bounded discrepancy on KV, the normalized q-parameter height away from 2, the all-prime q-height, the height at infinity, and ht_{omega_X(D)}. The output is an equality of bounded-discrepancy classes, not pointwise equality of functions.
Exceptions Part (ii) removes a finite set depending on KV, d, and the chosen error parameter. It contains points whose elliptic curves have automorphisms of order greater than 2. The formal object should record which constants are uniform in KV and which depend on it.
Prime ell The proof chooses a prime ell with sqrt(h) <= ell and an explicit upper bound, avoiding divisibility by local height terms and avoiding l-cyclic subgroup pathologies after enlarging the exceptional set. This is the arithmetic source of condition (C1), and it explains the square-root scale later visible in the error term.
Theta data The prime-choice, bad multiplicative place, and torsion-image properties give initial theta data satisfying IUT I Definition 3.1 and the hypotheses of Theorem 1.10. This page is the link from explicit Diophantine input to the inter-universal machinery; it is not part of the Corollary 3.12 proof itself.
C2 bound Applying Theorem 1.10 yields the displayed q-height bound by (1+epsilon_E) times log-diff + log-cond plus a constant depending on KV. Corollary 2.2 is the immediate numerical precursor to Corollary 2.3.

Lambda line

The point lambda classifies a Legendre elliptic curve. The removed points 0,1,infty are exactly the degenerations excluded from the open curve.

Cor. 2.2Legendre

2-adic j condition

The condition (*j-inv) controls the image of KV under the j-invariant at the prime 2. This is what makes the away-from-2 and all-prime q-height functions equivalent up to bounded discrepancy on KV.

conditionprime 2

BD-class identity

The identity (1/6)log(q_2) ~= (1/6)log(q_all) ~= ht_{omega_X(D)} is the translation from q-parameter data to the Vojta height language.

part (i)BD class

Prime choice

Proposition 2.1 supplies a prime outside a finite prohibited set. Its size is controlled by the q-height and degree parameter, which is why ell is comparable to a square root of the height up to logarithmic factors.

Prop. 2.1large ell

Galois image gate

After excluding an l-cyclic subgroup case and ensuring a bad multiplicative place remains, GenEll gives the required large image condition on ell-torsion.

SL2(F_ell)torsion

C1/C2 output

Condition (C1) records the prime range. Condition (C2) records the q-height estimate by log-different and log-conductor terms, with epsilon_E controlled after enlarging the exceptional set.

part (ii)output
Formalization warning: Corollary 2.2 mixes ordinary arithmetic gates with inter-universal input. The prime-choice, exceptional-set, BD-class, and torsion-image arguments should remain separate from the assumed Theorem 1.10 estimate, which itself is downstream of Corollary 3.12.
Sources: IUT IV, Corollary 2.2 Related: Theorem 1.10 Diophantine inequalities IUT IV output route initial theta data elliptic torsion input IUT IV overview

IUT IV Corollary 2.3: Diophantine Inequalities

Corollary 2.3 is the stated Diophantine output of IUT IV. It takes the suitable-theta-data estimate from Corollary 2.2, applies the reduction theorem from GenEll, and states a bounded-discrepancy Vojta-type inequality for hyperbolic curves. In Mochizuki's presentation, the abc and Szpiro consequences are reached through this output route.

Corollary 2.3 dependency route Corollary 2.2 plus GenEll gives the Vojta-form inequality
Corollary 2.3 itemPaper contentWiki/formalization consequence
Curve Let X be a smooth, proper, geometrically connected curve over a number field, let D be a reduced divisor, and set U_X = X \\ D. The output is not only a lambda-line statement; GenEll transfers the lambda-line application to general hyperbolic curves.
Hyp. The line bundle omega_X(D) must have positive degree. This is the hyperbolic-curve condition in the statement. A formal page should keep the hyperbolicity hypothesis explicit rather than folding it into the word "curve".
Degree d The inequality is stated on U_X(Q)_{<=d}, the points defined over extensions of degree at most d. Bounded degree is part of the theorem's quantifier structure and must be represented separately from compact-boundedness.
BD bound The conclusion is an inequality of bounded-discrepancy classes: ht_{omega_X(D)} is bounded by (1+epsilon)(log-diff_X+log-cond_D), up to an additive bounded term. The conclusion is not pointwise equality; it is a lower bound for the difference between the right-hand side and the height.
GenEll The proof says the statement is exactly GenEll Theorem 2.1(i), so it suffices by GenEll to verify the lambda-line case of Theorem 2.1(ii). The wiki records this as a dependency boundary: Corollary 2.3 relies on GenEll plus Corollary 2.2, not on a fresh IUT construction.
Lambda case In the reduced case, one assumes X=P^1_Q, D={0,1,infty}, a compactly bounded KV whose support includes 2, and the (*j-inv) condition. This is exactly where Corollary 2.2 supplies the needed bounded-discrepancy class equality and C2 estimate.
Consequences Mochizuki identifies the Vojta statement for hyperbolic curves, abc, and Szpiro-type statements as consequences of this Diophantine inequality route. These consequences remain downstream of the Corollary 3.12 corridor through Theorem 1.10 and Corollary 2.2.

Hyperbolic input

The statement applies to U_X=X-D when deg(omega_X(D)) > 0. This includes the thrice-punctured projective line and the hyperbolic curves to which GenEll reduces the problem.

Cor. 2.3hyperbolic

Bounded-degree points

The quantifier d fixes the degree of the number fields over which the points are defined. The bounded-discrepancy constant may depend on the theorem's fixed parameters.

domainU_X(Q)_{<=d}

Bounded discrepancy

The notation records a comparison up to an additive bounded term. In Corollary 2.3, this means the right-hand side minus the height is bounded below on the bounded-degree domain.

BD classnot pointwise

GenEll boundary

The proof invokes the equivalence in GenEll Theorem 2.1. Therefore the IUT IV contribution at this stage is to verify the lambda-line statement via Corollary 2.2, then let GenEll propagate it.

GenEllreduction

Compact-bounded correction

Remark 2.3.1 clarifies that a compactly bounded subset of an open curve must have its local compact domains contained in the open complement, both archimedean and nonarchimedean.

Remark 2.3.1domain control

Coefficient one

Remark 2.3.2 says the coefficient 1 in the displayed Corollary 2.3 inequality cannot be improved in the framework under discussion, referring back to the asymptotic examples and the theta-power discussion near Corollary 3.12.

Remark 2.3.2sharpness

Faltings comparison

Remark 2.3.3 compares Corollary 2.3 with Faltings' proof of Mordell: Faltings stays scheme-theoretic and arithmetically holomorphic, whereas IUT claims to work by dismantling that structure into multiradial and mono-analytic data.

Remark 2.3.3Mordell

Bogomolov and Parshin

Remarks 2.3.4 and 2.3.5 compare the IUT output with Bogomolov's geometric Szpiro proof and Parshin's function-field Mordell proof, emphasizing the real-analytic versus holomorphic contrast.

Remarks 2.3.4-2.3.5analogies

abc and Szpiro output

The wiki treats abc and Szpiro as downstream consequences of the Vojta-form bounded-discrepancy inequality, not as separate proofs detached from the IUT III/IV corridor.

outputabc/Szpiro
Formalization warning: Corollary 2.3 has two distinct dependency layers. The inter-universal layer supplies Corollary 2.2 through Theorem 1.10 and Corollary 3.12; the propagation from the lambda-line statement to arbitrary hyperbolic curves is delegated to GenEll Theorem 2.1. These should be separate modules or theorem interfaces in a proof assistant.
Sources: IUT IV, Corollary 2.3 and Remarks 2.3.1-2.3.5 Related: suitable theta data Theorem 1.10 IUT IV output route Bogomolov-Szpiro analogy probabilistic Szpiro route Scholze-Stix and responses

IUT IV and Consequences

IUT IV converts the IUT III log-volume corridor into explicit estimates and then into Diophantine inequalities. Section 1 is the numerical engine: local different estimates, normalized nonarchimedean log-volume, archimedean metric estimates, prime-number-theorem input, weighted averages, arithmetic divisors, and finally Theorem 1.10 for theta-pilot objects. Section 2 applies this output to suitable initial theta data and then to Vojta, abc, and Szpiro-type consequences.

IUT IV estimate pipeline from the Corollary 3.12 corridor to Diophantine inequalities

Local estimate layer

Propositions 1.1-1.5 estimate tensor products of local rings, different ideals, p-adic logarithms, normalized nonarchimedean log-volume, and archimedean metric containers. This is the local numerical substrate under the global theta-pilot estimate.

Props. 1.1-1.5local fields

Averaging layer

Proposition 1.6 supplies the prime-number-theorem estimate used to sum over primes in the explicit computation, while Proposition 1.7 supplies the weighted-average formalism for packet-normalized log-volumes.

Props. 1.6-1.7global sums

Arithmetic divisors

Definition 1.9 packages finite and archimedean valuation data as arithmetic divisors. The theorem then expresses the q-parameter and theta-pilot estimates in terms of degrees, supports, and height-like quantities.

Definition 1.9heights

Theorem 1.10

Theorem 1.10 estimates the procession-normalized mono-analytic log-volume of the holomorphic hull attached to theta-pilot objects. It is the direct numerical continuation of IUT III's Corollary 3.12 setting.

theta-pilotlog-volume

Suitable initial theta data

Corollary 2.2 constructs initial theta data from points on the thrice punctured projective line, outside controlled exceptional loci, so that the hypotheses needed for Theorem 1.10 are available in Diophantine applications.

Corollary 2.2application input

Diophantine inequalities

Corollary 2.3 applies the estimate to Vojta-type inequalities for hyperbolic curves; the IUT series identifies abc and Szpiro-type statements as consequences through this route.

Corollary 2.3Vojta/abc/Szpiro
IUT IV stepRole in the wiki mapNearby concept pages
Propositions 1.1-1.5 Local estimates for differents, logarithms, and nonarchimedean/archimedean log-volume containers. local estimate route, different estimates, normalized log-volume
Propositions 1.6-1.7 Analytic and averaging input used to assemble local estimates into global bounds. prime/averaging route, prime number theorem input, weighted averages
Definition 1.9 and Theorem 1.10 Arithmetic divisors and the explicit theta-pilot log-volume estimate. elliptic torsion input, arithmetic divisor route, arithmetic divisors, theta-pilot estimate
Corollaries 2.2-2.3 Construction of suitable initial theta data and extraction of Diophantine inequalities. suitable initial theta data, Diophantine inequalities, lambda-line setup, prime choice, GenEll reduction
Section 3 Set-theoretic language of species, mutations, and copies used to describe inter-universal movement. species, mutation, set-theoretic copies
Reading rule: IUT IV does not replace the disputed Corollary 3.12 corridor. It assumes the earlier log-volume comparison machinery and computes its numerical consequences. A faithful wiki therefore keeps the estimate pipeline linked back to Corollary 3.12 rather than presenting the Diophantine inequalities as independent elementary estimates.
Sources: IUT IV Explicit Estimates WRAP version Related: IUT IV atlas tensor/log-volume corridor source library

IUT IV Output Route

IUT IV Section 2 is the application layer. It converts the explicit theta-pilot estimate of Theorem 1.10 into bounded-discrepancy Diophantine inequalities by constructing suitable initial theta data and then invoking the reduction theorem from GenEll.
